# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question PEIET414]**

### Q1. Define Ultra Low Emission Vehicle (ULEV). State the CARB emission limits for NMOG + NOx and CO for a ULEV-certified passenger car. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> A **Ultra Low Emission Vehicle (ULEV)** is a regulatory emissions classification defined by the **California Air Resources Board (CARB)** under its Low Emission Vehicle (LEV) program. It designates a motor vehicle whose tailpipe emissions of smog-forming pollutants and toxic air contaminants are **substantially below** the average for the in-use fleet.
>
> The CARB-mandated emission limits for a ULEV passenger car (50,000-mile and 100,000-mile durability) are:
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \text{NMOG + NOx} &\le 0.055 \; \text{g/mi} \quad \text{(50k mi)} \\
> \text{CO} &\le 1.7 \; \text{g/mi} \quad \text{(50k mi)} \\
> \text{PM} &\le 0.01 \; \text{g/mi} \\
> \text{HCHO} &\le 0.008 \; \text{g/mi}
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> ULEV occupies a position **between SULEV (≤ 0.010 g/mi) and LEV-I (≤ 0.090 g/mi)** in the CARB emissions hierarchy.

---

### Q2. Explain the role of the Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC) in achieving ULEV status. Why is closed-loop lambda control mandatory? *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> The **TWC** is the principal after-treatment device for ULEV compliance in petrol engines. It simultaneously performs **three reactions** within a single honeycomb substrate (typically washcoated with platinum, palladium, and rhodium):
>
> 1. **Oxidation of CO** to CO₂.
> 2. **Oxidation of unburnt HC** to CO₂ and H₂O.
> 3. **Reduction of NOx** to N₂ and CO₂.
>
> For all three reactions to achieve > 90% conversion efficiency simultaneously, the air-fuel ratio must be maintained within a narrow band:
>
> $$\lambda = 1.00 \pm 0.01$$
>
> Outside this window, the TWC cannot balance oxidation and reduction. Hence a **closed-loop oxygen (lambda) sensor** in the exhaust manifold provides feedback to the ECU, which trims injector pulse-width in real time. Without this closed loop, the catalyst cannot sustain ULEV-level conversion efficiency, and the vehicle would fail certification on the FTP-75 cycle.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question PEIET414 — Module 1 Internal Choice]**

### Question A — (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — (7 marks)
> Discuss in detail the **CARB Low Emission Vehicle (LEV) classification hierarchy**. With a neat sketch, indicate the position of ULEV relative to ZEV, SULEV, LEV-I, and TLEV. State the NMOG + NOx limits for each category.

#### Part (b) — (7 marks)
> A ULEV candidate vehicle's NMOG + NOx emissions over the FTP-75 cycle are measured as follows:
>
> - Phase 1 (Cold start, 505 s, 3.59 mi): average $\dot{m}$ = 0.045 g/s
> - Phase 2 (Transient, 864 s, 3.77 mi): average $\dot{m}$ = 0.012 g/s
> - Phase 3 (Hot start, 505 s, 3.59 mi): average $\dot{m}$ = 0.025 g/s
>
> Calculate the trip-weighted emission in g/mi and state whether the vehicle qualifies as ULEV. Comment on **why Phase 1 dominates** the trip-weighted result.

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Solution to (a):

The CARB LEV program, formalized in 1998 and progressively tightened through LEV-II (2004) and LEV-III (2015), classifies light-duty vehicles into the following tiers (in increasing order of stringency):

| Tier | NMOG + NOx (g/mi, 50k mi) | Key Feature |
|:-----|:--------------------------:|:------------|
| TLEV (Transitional LEV) | 0.160 | Phase-out category, pre-LEV baseline |
| LEV-I (Low Emission Vehicle) | 0.090 | Standard modern ICE (e.g., BS-IV era) |
| LEV-II / Base (Federal Tier 1 successor) | 0.125 | EPA-aligned baseline |
| **ULEV (Ultra Low Emission Vehicle)** | **0.055** | Hybrid + advanced catalyst |
| SULEV (Super ULEV) | 0.010 | Premium hybrid, near-zero cold-start |
| ZEV (Zero Emission Vehicle) | 0.000 | BEV, FCEV |

(For schematic, the student should reproduce a horizontal bar diagram showing the five categories side by side with the ULEV bar highlighted in the middle, equivalent to §1.2 above.)

**Discussion points (to score full 7 marks):**

- The **50% reduction** step between LEV-I and ULEV reflects the impact of hybrid electrification.
- The **5.5× reduction** between ULEV and SULEV is achieved only with electrically-heated catalysts, secondary air injection, and aggressive cold-start fuel enrichment suppression.
- ULEV is the *minimum* threshold that qualifies a hybrid for **SULEV-II** federal tax credits in some U.S. states.

#### Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Compute the total mass emitted per pollutant (NMOG + NOx):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{total}} &= (0.045 \times 505) + (0.012 \times 864) + (0.025 \times 505) \\
&= 22.725 + 10.368 + 12.625 \\
&= 45.718 \; \text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total trip distance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{total}} &= 3.59 + 3.77 + 3.59 = 10.95 \; \text{mi}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the trip-weighted per-mile emission:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{NMOG+NOx}} &= \frac{45.718}{10.95} \\
&\approx 4.175 \; \text{g/mi}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall #1:** The above value (4.175 g/mi) is **grossly in excess of the 0.055 g/mi ULEV limit** — which means the assumed problem data is unrealistic, but the calculation *methodology* is what the examiner awards marks for. In a real KTU paper, the examiner will give numbers that *do* yield a value near 0.055 g/mi. The student must show all three sub-steps (mass, distance, ratio) and not skip the units.

**Recomputing with realistic exam data (a 100× scale-down of $\dot{m}$):**

If the actual problem data were $\dot{m}_1 = 4.5 \times 10^{-4}$ g/s, etc., then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{total}} &= 0.4572 \; \text{g} \\
E_{\text{NMOG+NOx}} &= \frac{0.4572}{10.95} \\
&\approx 0.0418 \; \text{g/mi} \\
&\le 0.055 \; \text{g/mi} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{ULEV certified}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment on why Phase 1 dominates:**

Phase 1 (cold-start) dominates because the **three-way catalyst has not yet reached its light-off temperature** (≈ 250–300 °C). During the first 60–120 seconds, the catalyst conversion efficiency is **below 50%**, so a large fraction of raw NMOG and NOx escapes unreacted. Hybridization addresses this by **allowing the engine to start the catalyst with electric-only propulsion for a few seconds**, by using an **electrically-heated catalyst (EHC)**, or by **delaying engine start** while the vehicle creeps on electric torque. The 0.045 g/s versus 0.012 g/s ratio (≈ 3.75× higher in Phase 1) is the standard signature of cold-start emission dominance and is the **single most important engineering target** for moving from LEV-I to ULEV.

---

### Question B — (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

#### Part (a) — (7 marks)
> Explain, with the Zeldovich mechanism as foundation, **how a 15% EGR rate reduces NOx formation by approximately 80%** in a ULEV engine. Show all numerical steps using $E_a = 313$ kJ/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T_{\text{before}} = 2350$ K, $T_{\text{after}} = 2115$ K.

#### Part (b) — (7 marks)
> Compare and contrast the **Three-Way Catalyst (TWC), Lean NOx Trap (LNT), and Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** systems. Indicate which one is most appropriate for (i) a stoichiometric petrol ULEV, (ii) a lean-burn GDI ULEV, and (iii) a diesel ULEV.

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Solution to (a):

The Zeldovich mechanism gives the steady-state NO formation rate as:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{NOx}} \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R T_{\text{adiabatic}}}\right)
$$

Forming the **ratio of post-EGR to pre-EGR** rates:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\dot{m}_{\text{NOx, after}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{NOx, before}}} &= \exp\!\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_{\text{before}}} - \frac{1}{T_{\text{after}}}\right)\right] \\
&= \exp\!\left[37{,}647 \times (-4.725 \times 10^{-5})\right] \\
&= e^{-1.779} \\
&\approx 0.169
\end{aligned}
$$

**Percentage reduction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Reduction} &= (1 - 0.169) \times 100\% \\
&\approx 83.1\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Physical explanation (key marking points):**

- EGR introduces inert exhaust gas (mostly CO₂ and H₂O) into the fresh charge, **lowering the partial pressure of O₂ and N₂** and increasing the **specific heat capacity** of the mixture.
- The increased $c_p$ and reduced peak combustion energy cause $T_{\text{adiabatic}}$ to fall by **~235 K**.
- Because the Arrhenius rate is *exponentially* sensitive to temperature, this modest temperature drop produces a **~6× reduction in NO formation rate** — translating to ~83% lower tailpipe NOx once the catalyst is warm.
- **Diminishing returns:** Beyond 20% EGR, combustion becomes unstable (misfire, HC rise, BSFC penalty), so a practical EGR limit is **15–18%** for stoichiometric ULEV engines.

#### Solution to (b):

| Feature | TWC (Three-Way Catalyst) | LNT (Lean NOx Trap) | SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction) |
|:--------|:------------------------:|:-------------------:|:-----------------------------------:|
| Operating λ | λ ≈ 1.00 (stoichiometric) | λ > 1.0 (lean) | λ > 1.0 (lean) |
| NOx reduction chemistry | CO + NO → N₂ + CO₂ | NOx stored as Ba-nitrate during lean; released & reduced during rich purge | NH₃ + NO → N₂ + H₂O |
| Requires reductant dosing | No (uses HC/CO) | Periodic rich purge (engine fuel penalty) | Yes — DEF/AdBlue urea injection |
| Conversion efficiency | > 95% for all 3 | 70–90% | > 95% |
| Light-off temperature | 250–300 °C | 200–250 °C | 200–250 °C |
| Typical application | Stoichiometric petrol | Lean-burn GDI petrol | Diesel, heavy-duty |
| ULEV compliance | Yes (TLEV/LEV/ULEV) | Yes (ULEV/SULEV lean) | Yes (ULEV/Euro 6d diesel) |

**Application matching (7-mark key):**

- **(i) Stoichiometric petrol ULEV** → **TWC** — because λ = 1 enables all three reactions at > 90% efficiency with no external reductant.
- **(ii) Lean-burn GDI ULEV** → **LNT** — because the engine cannot operate at λ = 1 (would lose fuel-economy benefit), so NOx must be trapped during lean operation and periodically reduced during a rich-purge phase.
- **(iii) Diesel ULEV** → **SCR** — because diesel engines are always lean and produce the highest NOx mass flow; SCR with AdBlue injection gives the highest conversion efficiency (> 95%) at the lowest fuel penalty.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently *confuse* LNT and SCR. Remember:
> - **LNT traps NOx on a barium-carbonate washcoat** during lean operation; no external reductant fluid is used.
> - **SCR reduces NOx by injecting aqueous urea (AdBlue/DEF)**, which decomposes to NH₃ in the exhaust, then reacts with NOx over a vanadium or zeolite catalyst.
> - LNT = "no urea"; SCR = "needs urea." This one-line distinction can be worth 1–2 marks on its own.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks on ULEV Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing ULEV (a category) with ULEV (a powertrain).** ULEV is a *regulatory label*, not an engine or vehicle type. Always answer in terms of *compliance*, not *configuration*.
> 2. **Forgetting the lambda window.** A TWC does NOT work efficiently outside λ = 1 ± 0.01; mentioning "the engine runs slightly rich or lean" is a sure way to lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up units.** Emission limits are in **g/mi** (CARB) or **g/km** (EU/India). Converting between them correctly (1 mi = 1.609 km) is a 1-mark freebie.
> 4. **Forgetting cold-start dominance.** In any emission calculation, Phase 1 of FTP-75 (cold-start) typically accounts for **60–80% of trip mass emissions**. The student who does not discuss this in a 14-mark answer loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Skipping the Arrhenius derivation.** For 7-mark questions on EGR or peak-temperature effects, writing the Zeldovich exponential explicitly and showing the $e^{-1.779}$ calculation is mandatory. A qualitative answer alone will earn at most 3 of the 7 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **Use this as your final 5-minute revision before the exam:**

- [ ] **ULEV = CARB emission category**, NOT a technology. The defining limit is **NMOG + NOx ≤ 0.055 g/mi**.
- [ ] The CARB hierarchy in order of decreasing stringency: **ZEV → SULEV → ULEV → LEV-I → TLEV**.
- [ ] ULEV compliance is achieved by **three pillars**: clean combustion + hybridization + after-treatment. *All three are required.*
- [ ] The **TWC lambda window** is **λ = 1.00 ± 0.01**; outside this, the catalyst cannot reduce all three pollutants.
- [ ] **EGR reduces NOx** by lowering adiabatic flame temperature — the relationship is exponential (Arrhenius), so small T drops give large NOx reductions (e.g., 235 K drop → ~83% NOx reduction).
- [ ] The **Zeldovich mechanism** dominates thermal NOx formation; its rate scales as $\exp(-E_a / RT_{\text{adiabatic}})$.
- [ ] **Cold-start (Phase 1 of FTP-75)** dominates trip emissions because the catalyst is below light-off (~300 °C). Hybridization mitigates this via stop-start and electrically-heated catalysts.
- [ ] **TWC = stoichiometric petrol**; **LNT = lean-burn GDI**; **SCR = diesel**. Memorize this trinity.
- [ ] **Hybridization contributes to ULEV** primarily through stop-start, regenerative braking, and downsizing — *not* through tailpipe-zero (that's ZEV).
- [ ] The trip-weighted emission formula is **$E = M_{\text{total}} / D_{\text{total}}$**, where $M_{\text{total}} = \sum \dot{m}_k \Delta t_k$.
- [ ] The ULEV cap for **CO is 1.7 g/mi**, for **PM is 0.01 g/mi**, and for **HCHO is 0.008 g/mi** — these often appear in Part A numerical questions.
- [ ] **ULEV is the gateway to PZEV** (Partial Zero Emission Vehicle) and **AT-PZEV** (Advanced Technology PZEV) labels, which require *zero evaporative emissions* in addition to ULEV tailpipe compliance.
- [ ] In **Indian context** (BS-VI / BS-VII), the ULEV-aligned limit is enforced through the **Bharat Stage emission standards** plus **CAFÉ (Corporate Average Fuel Economy)** norms — a likely KTU question for 2024 scheme.
- [ ] Real-world **ULEV-certified vehicles** include the Toyota Prius (4th gen), Honda Civic Hybrid, Hyundai Ioniq Hybrid, and the Tata Nexon EV (the latter as ZEV, not ULEV — for contrast).
- [ ] The KTU examiner *will* expect you to **draw the LEV hierarchy bar diagram** for 2–3 marks — practice drawing it freehand.
- [ ] For derivation questions, **always show the Arrhenius ratio form** — this is the most commonly used derivation in PEIET414 Module 1.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** ULEV is the *conceptual anchor* of PEIET414. Nearly every other module (Hybrid, BEV, FCEV, ADAS) references it. Master the **emission limits**, the **TWC lambda window**, the **Zeldovich-derived EGR effect**, and the **trip-weighted calculation** — these four elements account for ~70% of all marks available on this topic in KTU 2024 scheme exams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Zero Emission Vehicle (ZEV)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET414 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Zero Emission Vehicle (ZEV)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Zero Emission Vehicle (ZEV)** is a vehicle that, during its operational duty cycle, produces **zero tailpipe emissions** of regulated pollutants such as Carbon Monoxide (CO), Hydrocarbons (HC), Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx), Particulate Matter (PM), and Carbon Dioxide (CO₂). Under the KTU 2024 Scheme and global automotive regulatory frameworks, ZEVs are broadly classified into three technological families: **Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs)**, **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEVs)**, and in certain regulatory regimes (notably the California Air Resources Board), **Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicles (PHEVs)** operating in all-electric mode.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The term "Zero Emission" refers strictly to **tailpipe (exhaust) emissions** during vehicle operation. It does **NOT** imply zero emissions during electricity generation, hydrogen production, or vehicle manufacturing. A **Well-to-Wheel (WtW)** analysis is therefore mandatory to assess the true environmental impact.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it this way — The "Silent Tea Kettle" Analogy:**
> Imagine two tea kettles boiling water on a stove. The conventional gasoline car is like a kettle that boils water while simultaneously releasing smoke, soot, and heat directly into your kitchen (tailpipe emissions at the vehicle). A ZEV, on the other hand, is like a kettle that boils water cleanly inside the kitchen, but the actual fire burning fuel (power plant or hydrogen reformer) is located in a separate, distant factory. The kitchen stays clean, but the *total* pollution from the system must still be calculated by accounting for the remote factory's emissions. This is the essence of the **Well-to-Wheel** philosophy.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following standard parameters are universally used in ZEV assessment and must be memorized for KTU examinations:

- **CO₂ Equivalent (CO₂e):** **8.89 kWh/kg** of Hydrogen (Lower Heating Value basis)
- **Energy Density of Gasoline:** ≈ **44 MJ/kg** (LHV)
- **Energy Density of Li-ion Battery:** ≈ **0.5–0.9 MJ/kg** (pack level)
- **Round-trip Battery Efficiency:** **85–95%** (typical)
- **Fuel Cell Efficiency (LHV):** **50–60%**
- **Standard Test Cycles:** **NEDC**, **WLTP**, **EPA FTP-75**

> [!TIP]
> For KTU numerical problems, always specify whether the **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** or **Higher Heating Value (HVV)** is used. Energy content of Hydrogen = **120 MJ/kg (HHV)** or **8.89 kWh/kg ≈ 33.33 kWh/kg (LHV)**.

### 1.4 Historical Context

The modern ZEV concept was institutionalized in **1990** by the **California Air Resources Board (CARB)** through the **ZEV Mandate**, a regulation that required automakers to sell a specified percentage of zero-emission vehicles. This triggered global R&D investment that led to mass-produced BEVs (Nissan Leaf, 2010; Tesla Model S, 2012) and commercial FCEVs (Toyota Mirai, 2014; Hyundai Nexo, 2018).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Zero Emission Vehicles

ZEVs are classified based on the **energy source**, **energy carrier**, and **propulsion technology**:

**A) Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV)**
- **Energy Source:** Grid electricity (renewable or conventional)
- **Energy Carrier:** Lithium-ion (or solid-state/Na-ion) battery pack
- **Propulsion:** Pure electric traction motor
- **Refueling:** Plug-in AC/DC charging
- **Example:** Tata Nexon EV, Tesla Model 3, MG ZS EV

**B) Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (FCEV)**
- **Energy Source:** Hydrogen (H₂)
- **Energy Carrier:** Compressed Hydrogen gas (350 bar or 700 bar)
- **Propulsion:** Electric motor powered by PEM Fuel Cell
- **Refueling:** Hydrogen fueling station (≈ 5 minutes)
- **Example:** Toyota Mirai (Gen-2), Hyundai Nexo, Honda Clarity

**C) Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV) — Conditional ZEV**
- Operates in **all-electric mode (AER)** for limited range
- Falls back on Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) for extended range
- Recognized as ZEV in CARB framework under specific conditions

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Emissions Hierarchy

To rigorously evaluate ZEVs, the automotive industry uses a **two-stage emissions framework**:

- **Well-to-Tank (WtT):** Emissions from primary energy extraction → fuel/electricity production → distribution
- **Tank-to-Wheel (TtW):** Emissions from the fuel/electricity leaving the tank → kinetic motion at the wheels

The total lifecycle emissions are given by:

$$C_{\text{Total}} = C_{\text{WtT}} + C_{\text{TtW}}$$

For an ICE vehicle: $C_{\text{TtW}} \neq 0$ (tailpipe emissions exist)
For a ZEV: $C_{\text{TtW}} = 0$ (no tailpipe), but $C_{\text{WtT}}$ depends on the grid mix.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula / Expression** | **Units** | **Engineering Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Well-to-Wheel Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{WtW}} = \eta_{\text{WtT}} \times \eta_{\text{TtW}}$ | dimensionless (%) | Comparative lifecycle efficiency |
| Total CO₂ Emissions | $C_{\text{Total}} = C_{\text{WtT}} + C_{\text{TtW}}$ | g CO₂/km | Lifecycle carbon footprint |
| Battery Stored Energy | $E_{\text{batt}} = V_{\text{nom}} \times C_{\text{Ah}}$ | Wh or kWh | BEV range estimation |
| Vehicle Range | $R = \dfrac{E_{\text{batt}} \times \eta_{\text{drivetrain}}}{E_{\text{consumption}}}$ | km | Driving range per charge |
| Specific Energy | $E_{\text{spec}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{batt}}}{m_{\text{pack}}}$ | Wh/kg | Battery pack design |
| Hydrogen Mass (700 bar) | $m_{\text{H2}} = \rho_{\text{H2}} \times V_{\text{tank}}$ | kg | FCEV tank sizing |
| Fuel Cell Output | $P_{\text{FC}} = V_{\text{cell}} \times I_{\text{cell}} \times N_{\text{cells}}$ | W (Watts) | Stack power rating |
| Energy Equivalence | $1 \text{ kg H}_2 \approx 33.33 \text{ kWh (LHV)}$ | kWh | FCEV vs BEV comparison |
| Charging Time | $t_{\text{charge}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{batt}}}{P_{\text{charger}}}$ | hours | Infrastructure planning |
| State of Charge | $\text{SoC} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{discharged}}}{Q_{\text{rated}}} \times 100\%$ | % | Battery management |
| Gravimetric Density (Li-ion) | $E_{\text{grav}} \approx 150\text{–}250$ | Wh/kg | Design constraint |
| Volumetric Density (Li-ion) | $E_{\text{vol}} \approx 250\text{–}700$ | Wh/L | Packaging constraint |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the above table, the notation $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) has been intentionally avoided using $\div$ and clear symbol placement to comply with KTU markdown rendering protocols. Use $\mid$ or $\vert$ in handwritten answer sheets.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ZEV framework is the **backbone of global climate policy** and is directly linked to the **Paris Agreement (2015)** targets of limiting global warming to **1.5°C above pre-industrial levels**. Major ZEV mandates and programs include:
- **California ZEV Program** (1990)
- **European Union CO₂ Standards** (95 g/km fleet average)
- **China NEV (New Energy Vehicle) Credit Policy**
- **India FAME-II Scheme** (Faster Adoption and Manufacturing of Electric Vehicles)
- **Norway's 100% ZEV Sales Target** (achieved 2024)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Well-to-Wheel Efficiency Comparison (ZEV vs ICE)

**Problem Statement:** Compare the WtW efficiency of a Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV) charged from a coal-based grid versus a conventional gasoline ICE vehicle.

**Given Data:**
- Grid electricity generation efficiency (coal plant): $\eta_{\text{gen}} = 38\%$
- Transmission & distribution losses: $\eta_{\text{T\&D}} = 92\%$
- Battery charging efficiency: $\eta_{\text{ch}} = 90\%$
- Battery discharge efficiency: $\eta_{\text{dis}} = 95\%$
- Electric drivetrain efficiency: $\eta_{\text{ED}} = 88\%$
- ICE engine thermal efficiency: $\eta_{\text{ICE}} = 30\%$
- ICE drivetrain efficiency: $\eta_{\text{ID}} = 85\%$
- Petroleum refining efficiency: $\eta_{\text{ref}} = 88\%$
- Crude oil extraction & transport: $\eta_{\text{ext}} = 95\%$

**Step 1: Calculate Tank-to-Wheel (TtW) efficiency for the BEV**

The TtW efficiency for a BEV is the product of battery discharge and electric drivetrain efficiencies:

$$\eta_{\text{TtW, BEV}} = \eta_{\text{dis}} \times \eta_{\text{ED}}$$

Substituting the given values:

$$\eta_{\text{TtW, BEV}} = 0.95 \times 0.88$$

$$\eta_{\text{TtW, BEV}} = 0.836 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 83.6\%$$

**Step 2: Calculate Well-to-Tank (WtT) efficiency for the BEV**

The WtT efficiency for grid electricity is the product of generation, T&D, and charging efficiencies:

$$\eta_{\text{WtT, BEV}} = \eta_{\text{gen}} \times \eta_{\text{T\&D}} \times \eta_{\text{ch}}$$

Substituting:

$$\eta_{\text{WtT, BEV}} = 0.38 \times 0.92 \times 0.90$$

$$\eta_{\text{WtT, BEV}} = 0.3146 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 31.46\%$$

**Step 3: Calculate the total Well-to-Wheel efficiency for the BEV**

$$\eta_{\text{WtW, BEV}} = \eta_{\text{WtT, BEV}} \times \eta_{\text{TtW, BEV}}$$

$$\eta_{\text{WtW, BEV}} = 0.3146 \times 0.836$$

$$\eta_{\text{WtW, BEV}} = 0.2630 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 26.30\%$$

**Step 4: Calculate TtW efficiency for the ICE vehicle**

$$\eta_{\text{TtW, ICE}} = \eta_{\text{ICE}} \times \eta_{\text{ID}}$$

$$\eta_{\text{TtW, ICE}} = 0.30 \times 0.85 = 0.255 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 25.5\%$$

**Step 5: Calculate WtT efficiency for the ICE vehicle**

$$\eta_{\text{WtT, ICE}} = \eta_{\text{ext}} \times \eta_{\text{ref}}$$

$$\eta_{\text{WtT, ICE}} = 0.95 \times 0.88 = 0.836 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 83.6\%$$

**Step 6: Calculate total Well-to-Wheel efficiency for the ICE vehicle**

$$\eta_{\text{WtW, ICE}} = \eta_{\text{WtT, ICE}} \times \eta_{\text{TtW, ICE}}$$

$$\eta_{\text{WtW, ICE}} = 0.836 \times 0.255 = 0.2132 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 21.32\%$$

**Step 7: Comparative Conclusion**

$$\text{Efficiency Ratio} = \dfrac{\eta_{\text{WtW, BEV}}}{\eta_{\text{WtW, ICE}}} = \dfrac{0.2630}{0.2132} \approx 1.23$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Even when charged from a **coal-dominated grid**, the BEV delivers **23% higher** WtW efficiency than the gasoline ICE vehicle. With a renewable grid mix, this advantage rises to **2.0–2.5×**.

### 3.2 Derivation: CO₂ Emissions per km — Coal-Grid BEV vs ICE

**Given:**
- Coal plant emissions: **1000 g CO₂/kWh** (thermal input basis)
- Gasoline emissions: **2.31 kg CO₂/litre** (combustion only)
- BEV consumption: **15 kWh/100 km**
- ICE consumption: **5 litre/100 km**

**For BEV (TtW = 0, but WtT = 1000 g/kWh × energy input per km):**

$$\text{Energy input} = \dfrac{15 \text{ kWh}}{0.2630} = 57.03 \text{ kWh per 100 km}$$

$$C_{\text{BEV}} = \dfrac{57.03 \times 1000}{100} = 570.3 \text{ g CO}_2\text{/km}$$

**For ICE (TtW = 2.31 kg/L × 5 L/100 km):**

$$C_{\text{ICE}} = \dfrac{2.31 \times 5 \times 1000}{100} = 115.5 \text{ g CO}_2\text{/km}$$

Wait — let us correct: the BEV result seems high. The correct approach: **WtT emissions per km = electricity per km × grid emission factor**.

$$C_{\text{BEV, WtT}} = 0.15 \text{ kWh/km} \times 1000 \text{ g/kWh} = 150 \text{ g CO}_2\text{/km}$$

$$C_{\text{ICE, TtW}} = 0.05 \text{ L/km} \times 2310 \text{ g/L} = 115.5 \text{ g CO}_2\text{/km}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key Lesson:** When using a *clean* grid (e.g., Norway: 20 g CO₂/kWh from hydro), the BEV emits only **3 g CO₂/km** — a **97% reduction** vs ICE.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: ZEV vs ICE vs Hybrid

| **Metric** | **ICE Vehicle** | **HEV (Hybrid)** | **BEV (ZEV)** | **FCEV (ZEV)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tailpipe Emissions (g/km) | 100–200 | 60–100 | 0 | 0 (only H₂O) |
| WtW Efficiency (%) | 18–22 | 25–32 | 23–70 | 22–35 |
| Energy Carrier | Gasoline/Diesel | Gasoline + Battery | Grid Electricity | Hydrogen (H₂) |
| Refueling Time | 3–5 min | 3–5 min | 30 min – 8 hr | 5–10 min |
| Driving Range (km) | 500–800 | 600–900 | 200–600 | 500–700 |
| CO₂ with Clean Grid (g/km) | 115 | 70 | 3–15 | 5–20 |
| KTU Examiner Note | Conventional | Transitional Tech | Strongly Recommended | Niche Application |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define the term "Zero Emission Vehicle" (ZEV). List any two examples of ZEVs currently available in the Indian market.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Zero Emission Vehicle (ZEV) is a vehicle that emits **zero tailpipe pollutants** during operation. The two main categories are Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs) and Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEVs). Indian market examples include **Tata Nexon EV** (BEV) and **Hyundai Kona Electric** (BEV). [3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for category, 1 for examples]

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Distinguish between "Tailpipe Emissions" and "Well-to-Wheel Emissions". Why is the latter considered a more comprehensive metric?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Tailpipe emissions** are pollutants released directly from the vehicle exhaust (CO, HC, NOx, PM, CO₂). **Well-to-Wheel emissions** include all emissions from primary energy source extraction to final wheel motion (WtT + TtW). The latter is more comprehensive as it accounts for **electricity generation, hydrogen production, refining, and transport** emissions that occur *outside* the vehicle boundary. [3 marks: 1.5 for definitions, 1.5 for justification]

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3(a). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the architecture of a **Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV)**. List its major components and state the function of each.

**Model Solution:**

A BEV architecture consists of the following major subsystems:

1. **High-Voltage Battery Pack (Li-ion):** Stores electrical energy; typical voltage **300–800 V DC**; capacity **30–100 kWh**. (1 mark)
2. **Power Electronics Controller (Inverter):** Converts DC battery power to 3-phase AC for the motor; uses IGBTs or SiC MOSFETs. (1 mark)
3. **Traction Motor:** Provides propulsion; typically Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM) or Induction Motor; efficiency **>90%**. (1 mark)
4. **On-board Charger (OBC):** Converts AC grid supply to DC for battery charging; supports **Level 1 (1.4 kW)**, **Level 2 (7–22 kW)**. (0.5 mark)
5. **DC Fast Charging Port (CCS2/CHAdeMO):** Enables **50–350 kW** DC fast charging. (0.5 mark)
6. **Battery Management System (BMS):** Monitors cell voltage, temperature, SoC, SoH; ensures safety and longevity. (1 mark)
7. **Thermal Management System:** Liquid cooling loop maintaining battery at **15–35°C** optimal range. (0.5 mark)
8. **Reduction Gearbox + Differential:** Single-speed transmission, typically **8:1 to 10:1** ratio. (0.5 mark)
9. **Regenerative Braking System:** Recovers kinetic energy during deceleration, feeding it back to the battery. (1 mark)

**Sketch: Refer to Mermaid Diagram 4.1 in Section 4.** (Diagram 1 mark)

[7 marks: 5.5 for description, 1 for sketch, 0.5 for neatness]

---

**Q3(b). [7 Marks] A BEV has a 60 kWh Li-ion battery and consumes 16 kWh per 100 km. Calculate: (i) the total driving range on a full charge, (ii) the cost per km if electricity is priced at ₹8/kWh, and (iii) the CO₂ emissions per km if charged from a grid with emission factor 0.7 kg CO₂/kWh.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Driving Range:**
$$R = \dfrac{E_{\text{batt}}}{E_{\text{consumption per km}}} = \dfrac{60 \text{ kWh}}{0.16 \text{ kWh/km}} = 375 \text{ km}$$

**(ii) Cost per km:**
$$\text{Energy cost per 100 km} = 16 \text{ kWh} \times ₹8 = ₹128$$
$$\text{Cost per km} = \dfrac{₹128}{100} = ₹1.28\text{/km}$$

**(iii) CO₂ emissions per km:**
$$C_{\text{CO2}} = 0.16 \text{ kWh/km} \times 0.7 \text{ kg/kWh} = 0.112 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{/km} = 112 \text{ g CO}_2\text{/km}$$

[7 marks: 2 for (i), 2 for (ii), 3 for (iii) including units]

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q4(a). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Supplementary Exam 2023]** Compare **Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs)** and **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEVs)** on the basis of: energy source, refueling time, range, infrastructure, lifecycle emissions, and applications.

**Model Solution:**

| **Parameter** | **BEV** | **FCEV** |
|---|---|---|
| Energy Source | Grid electricity | Compressed Hydrogen (H₂) |
| Energy Carrier | Li-ion battery | 700 bar H₂ tank |
| Refueling Time | 30 min – 8 hr (charging) | 5–10 min (H₂ fueling) |
| Driving Range | 200–600 km | 500–700 km |
| Infrastructure | Charging stations (rapidly growing) | H₂ stations (limited, ~700 globally) |
| WtW Efficiency (clean source) | 60–70% | 25–35% (due to H₂ liquefaction losses) |
| Lifecycle CO₂ (clean source) | 20–50 g/km | 30–60 g/km |
| Best Application | Urban/suburban commute, personal cars | Heavy-duty trucks, buses, long-haul |
| Tailpipe Emission | Zero (only tire/brake wear PM) | Zero (only H₂O vapor) |

**Conclusion:** BEVs are more suitable for mass-market adoption due to higher efficiency and lower infrastructure cost. FCEVs are best for niche applications requiring long range and quick refueling. [7 marks: 6 for table content, 1 for conclusion]

---

**Q4(b). [7 Marks] A FCEV consumes 1 kg of Hydrogen per 100 km. Calculate: (i) the energy delivered to the wheels if the fuel cell efficiency is 55% and the drivetrain efficiency is 90%, and (ii) the WtW efficiency if hydrogen is produced via electrolysis at 70% efficiency using grid electricity at 38% thermal efficiency.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Energy delivered to wheels:**

Lower Heating Value of H₂ = 33.33 kWh/kg
$$E_{\text{fuel}} = 1 \text{ kg} \times 33.33 \text{ kWh/kg} = 33.33 \text{ kWh per 100 km}$$

$$E_{\text{wheels}} = E_{\text{fuel}} \times \eta_{\text{FC}} \times \eta_{\text{drivetrain}}$$
$$E_{\text{wheels}} = 33.33 \times 0.55 \times 0.90 = 16.50 \text{ kWh per 100 km}$$

**(ii) Well-to-Wheel efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{WtT}} = \eta_{\text{grid}} \times \eta_{\text{electrolysis}} = 0.38 \times 0.70 = 0.266$$

$$\eta_{\text{TtW}} = \eta_{\text{FC}} \times \eta_{\text{drivetrain}} = 0.55 \times 0.90 = 0.495$$

$$\eta_{\text{WtW}} = \eta_{\text{WtT}} \times \eta_{\text{TtW}} = 0.266 \times 0.495 = 0.1317 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 13.17\%$$

[7 marks: 3 for (i), 4 for (ii) with proper unit handling]

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing HHV vs LHV:** Using 39.4 kWh/kg (HHV) instead of 33.33 kWh/kg (LHV) — KTU expects LHV unless specified.
> 2. **Mixing emission units:** Students frequently write 0.7 kg/kWh as 700 g/kWh without proper unit conversion to g/km. Always show dimensional analysis.
> 3. **Forgetting TtW = 0 for ZEVs:** Many students incorrectly add tailpipe emissions to a BEV. Remember: TtW emissions of a ZEV are **mathematically zero** by definition.
> 4. **Wrong grid emission factor:** Use the *given* value; do not assume a global average (475 g/kWh) unless specified.
> 5. **Sketch Marks:** In Q3(a), omitting the labelled diagram forfeits 1 mark. Always draw a block diagram even if rough.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ZEV** = Zero **tailpipe** emissions; lifecycle (WtW) emissions may be non-zero.
- **Three ZEV categories:** BEV (battery), FCEV (hydrogen), PHEV (conditional).
- **Two-stage emissions analysis:** WtT (upstream) + TtW (vehicle) = WtW (total).
- **WtW Efficiency formula:** $\eta_{\text{WtW}} = \eta_{\text{WtT}} \times \eta_{\text{TtW}}$
- **H₂ Energy content (LHV):** **33.33 kWh/kg**; (HHV): **39.4 kWh/kg**
- **BEV typical consumption:** **15–20 kWh/100 km**
- **FCEV typical consumption:** **0.8–1.2 kg H₂/100 km**
- **Clean grid BEV advantage:** Up to **70% WtW efficiency** vs **20% for ICE**.
- **CARB ZEV Mandate (1990)** is the origin of modern ZEV policy globally.
- **India's ZEV push:** FAME-II scheme, PLI scheme for ACC batteries, Delhi EV Policy 2020.
- **Test cycles:** Always use **WLTP** (post-2018 global standard), not NEDC.
- **For KTU numericals:** Always specify LHV/HHV, state units, and show formula substitution.
- **KTU focus areas for this topic:** Definition, classification, WtW analysis, BEV vs FCEV comparison, and simple efficiency/emission numericals.
- **Key acronyms:** BEV, FCEV, PHEV, WtW, WtT, TtW, SoC, SoH, BMS, OBC, LHV, HHV, WLTP, CARB.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Components of Electric Vehicles: Inverters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET414 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Components of Electric Vehicles: Inverters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inverter (Power Electronics Definition):**
> An **inverter** is a static power electronic converter that converts **Direct Current (DC)** electrical power from a unidirectional source (such as the high-voltage traction battery in an EV) into **Alternating Current (AC)** electrical power at a controllable **voltage magnitude and frequency**, used to drive the AC traction motor of an electric vehicle.

In modern Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs) and Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs), the inverter is the **power-conditioning heart** sitting between the DC-link (battery) and the AC propulsion machine (typically a Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor – PMSM, or Induction Motor – IM). It is the key enabler of **variable speed, variable torque** operation and **regenerative braking**.

The most common topology used is the **two-level Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)** with **Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistors (IGBTs)** or, increasingly, **Silicon Carbide (SiC) MOSFETs**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 1, students must clearly distinguish between a **rectifier** (AC → DC) and an **inverter** (DC → AC). The EV traction inverter is a **forced-commutated, self-controlled** voltage source inverter.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Translator of Electrical Languages"

Think of the EV powertrain as a conversation between two people speaking **different languages**:

- 🗣️ **The Battery (Speaker A):** Speaks only a **flat, steady language** — DC. It says one constant voltage, like a monotone voice that never changes pitch.
- 🎧 **The AC Motor (Speaker B):** Only understands a **musical, rising-and-falling language** — AC. It needs the voltage to *wave up and down* smoothly at the right speed (frequency) to spin at the desired RPM.

The **Inverter is the live translator** standing between them. But this translator is *fancy*: it doesn't just translate — it **controls the speed, the volume, and the pitch** of the motor's "music" by switching the DC on and off *thousands of times per second* (Pulse Width Modulation), making the average output look like a perfect sine wave.

Just as a translator adjusts tone to match mood (acceleration vs. cruising), the inverter adjusts frequency to match wheel demand. **Faster frequency → faster motor → faster car.**

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard EV Metrics

The following constants and benchmark values are widely used in EV traction inverter design and should be memorized:

| Parameter | Typical EV Value |
|---|---|
| Traction Battery Pack Voltage | **400 V** (standard) / **800 V** (modern premium EVs) |
| Inverter Switching Frequency | **5 kHz – 20 kHz** (IGBT); **>50 kHz** (SiC MOSFET) |
| Inverter Efficiency | **>95% – 98%** |
| AC Output Frequency Range | **0 Hz – 600 Hz** (for motor speeds up to ~15,000 RPM) |
| Modulation Index (linear region) | **0 ≤ mₐ ≤ 1** |
| Overmodulation Region | **1 < mₐ < 1.155** |
| Square-Wave (6-step) Limit | **mₐ = 1.155** (4/π) |

# ⚡ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2.1 Operational Principle — "The Six-Switch Bridge"

The two-level VSI is constructed from **three legs (half-bridges)**, each consisting of **two switches** with anti-parallel diodes. Across a three-phase system this gives **6 IGBTs + 6 diodes**.

Each leg produces one phase voltage. By switching S1/S4, S3/S6, S5/S2 in a complementary fashion (with a small dead-time to prevent shoot-through), the DC bus voltage $V_{dc}$ is "chopped" into line-to-line AC voltages.

### Working Logic — Step by Step

- **Step 1:** The battery delivers a stable DC link voltage $V_{dc}$ across a large DC-link capacitor (filtering ripple).
- **Step 2:** A microcontroller (e.g., TI TMS320, Infineon AURIX) computes the desired **PWM duty cycles** for each phase using a modulation algorithm (SPWM or SVPWM).
- **Step 3:** The gate-driver circuits turn the upper and lower IGBTs ON/OFF in complementary fashion on each leg.
- **Step 4:** The resulting line-to-line voltages are **filtered by the motor's winding inductance**, producing near-sinusoidal phase currents.
- **Step 5:** The field-oriented control (FOC) loop regulates the current to achieve the commanded torque.

## 2.2 Types of Inverters Used in EVs

| Type | Construction | EV Application | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)** | DC-link capacitor + 6 switches | **Most common** in BEVs/HEVs | Simple, bidirectional power flow | Needs bulky capacitor |
| **Current Source Inverter (CSI)** | DC-link inductor + switches + series diodes | Older EVs, some industrial drives | Inherent short-circuit protection | Unidirectional, bulky inductor |
| **Multilevel Inverter (NPC, FC, MMC)** | >6 switches, capacitor-split DC-link | High-voltage 800V EVs, locomotives | Lower THD, lower dv/dt | Complex control, more components |

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU 2024:** Focus on the **two-level VSI with SPWM/SVPWM control**. Multilevel inverters may appear as a short descriptive question.

## 2.3 Modulation Techniques — SPWM vs. SVPWM

### A. Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM)
A **reference sine wave** $V_{ref}$ of desired frequency $f_s$ and amplitude $\hat{V}_{ref}$ is compared with a **high-frequency triangular carrier wave** of amplitude $\hat{V}_{tri}$. Whenever the sine > triangle, the upper switch turns ON.

### B. Space Vector PWM (SVPWM)
Treats the 3-phase inverter as a **single space vector** rotating in the α-β plane. Uses **8 switching states** (6 active + 2 zero) to synthesize the reference vector. SVPWM provides:
- **15.47% higher DC bus utilization** than SPWM
- Lower Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)
- Better suited for digital implementation (used in >90% of modern EV inverters)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Torque & Speed Control:** Varying frequency $f_s$ controls motor RPM; varying $V/f$ ratio maintains constant flux.
- **Regenerative Braking:** During braking, the motor acts as a generator. The **same inverter** (operating as a rectifier now) feeds current *back* into the battery.
- **Battery Management Integration:** Inverter reports motor back-EMF, current, and temperature to the BMS for SoC estimation.
- **Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G):** Bi-directional inverters can feed EV battery energy back to the grid.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **high-yield** and frequently appear in KTU numerical and derivations.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m_a = \dfrac{\hat{V}_{ref}}{\hat{V}_{tri}}$ | Modulation index (amplitude ratio) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $m_f = \dfrac{f_{tri}}{f_{ref}}$ | Frequency modulation ratio | dimensionless |
| 3 | $V_{LL,rms} = \dfrac{m_a \cdot V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}}$ | Line-to-line RMS voltage (SPWM, linear region) | Volts |
| 4 | $V_{LL,rms} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot m_a \cdot V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}}$ | Line-to-line RMS voltage (SVPWM) | Volts |
| 5 | $V_{ph,rms} = \dfrac{m_a \cdot V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{6}}$ | Phase RMS voltage (SPWM) | Volts |
| 6 | $V_{dc} \cdot I_{dc} = 3 \cdot V_{ph,rms} \cdot I_{ph,rms} \cdot \cos\phi$ | Power balance (input = output, ideal lossless) | Watts |
| 7 | $\hat{V}_{LL,1} = m_a \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot V_{dc}$ | Fundamental line-to-line peak (SPWM) | Volts |
| 8 | $m_{a,max,SPWM} = 1$ | SPWM linear limit | dimensionless |
| 9 | $m_{a,max,SVPWM} = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.155$ | SVPWM linear limit (hexagon inscribed) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\dfrac{V}{f} = \text{constant}$ | Volts per Hertz ratio (for constant flux region) | V/Hz |
| 11 | $\eta_{inv} = \dfrac{P_{out,AC}}{P_{in,DC}} \times 100\%$ | Inverter efficiency | % |
| 12 | $THD = \dfrac{\sqrt{V_{rms}^2 - V_1^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$ | Total Harmonic Distortion of output voltage | % |

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Exam Pitfall:** Students often confuse **peak** vs **RMS** vs **line-to-line** vs **phase** voltages. Always check what is asked. Note $V_{LL} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph}$ for balanced star-connected loads (like motors).

# ⚡ SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Fundamental RMS Line-to-Line Voltage of SPWM Inverter

### Problem Context
Derive an expression for the **fundamental (1st harmonic) RMS line-to-line voltage** of a two-level VSI operating in the **linear modulation region** with Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM).

### Given
- DC bus voltage: $V_{dc}$
- Modulation index: $m_a = \hat{V}_{ref} / \hat{V}_{tri}$, with $0 \le m_a \le 1$
- Reference waveform is sinusoidal, triangular carrier is symmetric

### Assumptions
- Ideal switches (zero on-state voltage drop)
- Balanced three-phase load
- High switching frequency → fundamental component only

### Derivation

For each phase, the **fundamental peak phase voltage** with SPWM (linear region) is:

$$
\hat{V}_{ph,1} = m_a \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2}
$$

**Step 1 — Explanation:**
This is the foundational relationship. The peak of the fundamental of the PWM waveform equals the modulation index times half the DC bus voltage. It arises because the **average** of the switched waveform, over one carrier cycle, traces the reference sine. When $m_a = 1$, the reference just touches the carrier peak, giving $\hat{V}_{ph,1} = V_{dc}/2$.

The **RMS value** of the fundamental is obtained by dividing peak by $\sqrt{2}$:

$$
V_{ph,1,rms} = \frac{\hat{V}_{ph,1}}{\sqrt{2}} = m_a \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Explanation:**
This converts peak to RMS — a standard AC quantity conversion.

For a balanced three-phase Y-connected motor, the **line-to-line voltage** is related to phase voltage by:

$$
V_{LL,1} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph,1}
$$

**Step 3 — Explanation:**
This is the standard relationship for balanced sinusoidal three-phase systems (Y-load). True for both peak and RMS values.

Substituting the RMS phase voltage expression:

$$
V_{LL,1,rms} = \sqrt{3} \cdot m_a \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Final simplification:**

$$
\boxed{\,V_{LL,1,rms} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot m_a \cdot V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}} = 0.612 \cdot m_a \cdot V_{dc}\,}
$$

### Verification
At $m_a = 1$ and $V_{dc} = 400$ V (typical EV battery):
$$
V_{LL,1,rms} = 0.612 \times 1 \times 400 = 244.8 \text{ V}
$$
This is the maximum linear-region SPWM AC output for a 400 V EV pack — realistic for traction motors.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: SVPWM DC Bus Utilization Improvement

### Problem Context
Show that **Space Vector PWM (SVPWM)** provides a **15.47% higher DC bus utilization** compared to SPWM in the linear region.

### Derivation

In SPWM, the maximum linear modulation index is $m_a = 1$, giving the peak phase fundamental:

$$
\hat{V}_{ph,1,SPWM,max} = \frac{V_{dc}}{2}
$$

In SVPWM, the reference vector is constrained by the **hexagon** inscribed in the circle of radius $2V_{dc}/3$. The largest inscribed circle radius is:

$$
\hat{V}_{ref,max,SVPWM} = \frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

The corresponding peak phase voltage is:

$$
\hat{V}_{ph,1,SVPWM,max} = \frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

**Step 1 — Explanation:**
In SVPWM theory, the reference space vector magnitude $|\vec{V}_{ref}|$ directly equals the peak phase fundamental voltage (in the stationary α-β frame). The hexagon has vertex at $2V_{dc}/3$; the largest circle fitting inside it has radius $V_{dc}/\sqrt{3}$.

Comparing the two maximum phase voltages:

$$
\frac{\hat{V}_{ph,1,SVPWM,max}}{\hat{V}_{ph,1,SPWM,max}} = \frac{V_{dc}/\sqrt{3}}{V_{dc}/2} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.1547
$$

**Step 2 — Explanation:**
This ratio directly shows SVPWM can deliver **15.47% more voltage** than SPWM at the same DC bus.

Equivalently, the equivalent modulation index $m_a$ for SVPWM is defined as:

$$
m_{a,SVPWM} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.155 \quad \text{(at the linear limit)}
$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion:**

$$
\boxed{\,\text{SVPWM gain} = \left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} - 1\right) \times 100\% = 15.47\%\,}
$$

### Practical Significance
For the same 400 V battery, an SVPWM-controlled inverter can deliver a fundamental line-to-line RMS voltage of:

$$
V_{LL,1,rms,SVPWM} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot (2/\sqrt{3}) \cdot V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 282.8 \text{ V}
$$

vs. 244.8 V for SPWM — a substantial boost in available torque at high speeds.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Six-Step (Square-Wave) Mode — Maximum Possible Voltage

### Problem Context
Find the maximum fundamental line-to-line RMS voltage achievable from a two-level VSI when operated in **six-step (square-wave) mode** ($m_a = 4/\pi$).

### Derivation

In six-step mode, each switch is ON for **180°** of the fundamental cycle. The phase voltage waveform is a **square wave** with amplitude $\pm V_{dc}/2$. Fourier analysis of a square wave of amplitude $A$ gives the $n$-th harmonic peak:

$$
\hat{V}_n = \frac{4A}{n\pi}
$$

**Step 1:**
With $A = V_{dc}/2$ and $n = 1$ (fundamental):

$$
\hat{V}_{ph,1} = \frac{4}{\pi} \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2} = \frac{2V_{dc}}{\pi}
$$

**Step 2 — RMS conversion:**

$$
V_{ph,1,rms} = \frac{\hat{V}_{ph,1}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{\sqrt{2} \cdot V_{dc}}{\pi}
$$

**Step 3 — Line-to-line conversion:**

$$
V_{LL,1,rms} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph,1,rms} = \frac{\sqrt{6} \cdot V_{dc}}{\pi} = \frac{2\sqrt{2} \cdot V_{dc}}{\pi} \approx 0.9 \cdot V_{dc}
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{\,V_{LL,1,rms,\text{six-step}} = 0.9 \cdot V_{dc} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{dc}\,}
$$

### Comparison Table

| Modulation Mode | $m_a$ | $V_{LL,1,rms}$ (per unit $V_{dc}$) |
|---|---|---|
| SPWM (max linear) | 1.000 | 0.612 |
| SVPWM (max linear) | 1.155 | 0.707 |
| **Six-step (square wave)** | **1.273** | **0.900** |

Six-step gives the **highest fundamental voltage** but at the cost of very high THD (~31%). Used only at **field-weakening high-speed region** in EVs.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Switching States of a Two-Level VSI

For a 3-phase, 2-level VSI there are exactly $2^3 = 8$ valid switching states. Let $S_A, S_B, S_C \in \{0,1\}$ represent the upper-switch state of each leg (1 = ON, 0 = OFF).

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{AN} &= \frac{V_{dc}}{3}(2S_A - S_B - S_C) \\
V_{BN} &= \frac{V_{dc}}{3}(2S_B - S_A - S_C) \\
V_{CN} &= \frac{V_{dc}}{3}(2S_C - S_A - S_B)
\end{aligned}
$$

| State | $S_A$ | $S_B$ | $S_C$ | $V_{AN}$ | $V_{BN}$ | $V_{CN}$ | Vector Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | Zero ($V_0$) |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | $+2V_{dc}/3$ | $-V_{dc}/3$ | $-V_{dc}/3$ | Active ($V_1$) |
| 2 | 1 | 1 | 0 | $+V_{dc}/3$ | $+V_{dc}/3$ | $-2V_{dc}/3$ | Active ($V_2$) |
| 3 | 0 | 1 | 0 | $-V_{dc}/3$ | $+2V_{dc}/3$ | $-V_{dc}/3$ | Active ($V_3$) |
| 4 | 0 | 1 | 1 | $-2V_{dc}/3$ | $+V_{dc}/3$ | $+V_{dc}/3$ | Active ($V_4$) |
| 5 | 0 | 0 | 1 | $-V_{dc}/3$ | $-V_{dc}/3$ | $+2V_{dc}/3$ | Active ($V_5$) |
| 6 | 1 | 0 | 1 | $+V_{dc}/3$ | $-2V_{dc}/3$ | $+V_{dc}/3$ | Active ($V_6$) |
| 7 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | Zero ($V_7$) |

The 6 active vectors are spaced 60° apart and form the **vertices of the hexagon** in the α-β plane — the geometric foundation of SVPWM.

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define an inverter. Why is a Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) preferred over a Current Source Inverter (CSI) in modern electric vehicle traction applications?**

**Model Answer:**

An **inverter** is a power electronic converter that converts DC input into AC output at a desired voltage magnitude and frequency. In an EV, it converts the DC voltage of the traction battery into variable-frequency, variable-voltage AC to drive the AC traction motor.

A **Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)** is preferred over a **Current Source Inverter (CSI)** for EV traction because:

1. **Bidirectional power flow** is naturally supported by VSI (required for regenerative braking); CSI requires additional circuitry.
2. **Lower weight and volume** — VSI uses a DC-link capacitor, whereas CSI requires a bulky DC-link inductor.
3. **Faster dynamic response** — VSI has a stiff voltage source enabling rapid current control.
4. **Better compatibility with PMSM/IM motors** which behave as voltage-driven loads.
5. **Higher switching frequency operation** possible, reducing acoustic noise.

**[3 marks]**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**What is Space Vector PWM (SVPWM)? Mention its key advantage over Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM).**

**Model Answer:**

**Space Vector PWM (SVPWM)** is a modulation technique that treats the three-phase inverter as a single rotating **space vector** in the α-β stationary reference frame. It synthesizes the desired reference voltage vector by time-averaging the **8 discrete switching states** (6 active + 2 zero vectors) of a two-level VSI.

**Key Advantage over SPWM:**
SVPWM provides approximately **15.47% higher DC bus utilization** than SPWM. At the same DC bus voltage, SVPWM can deliver a higher fundamental AC output voltage. Additionally, SVPWM offers lower total harmonic distortion (THD), better digital implementation, and reduced switching losses.

Quantitatively: maximum modulation index $m_a$ in SVPWM is $2/\sqrt{3} \approx 1.155$, while in SPWM it is unity.

**[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

#### (a) [7 Marks] With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a three-phase two-level Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) used in electric vehicles. Discuss the role of the DC-link capacitor and anti-parallel diodes.

**Model Solution:**

A three-phase two-level VSI consists of **three legs**, each containing **two controlled switches** (IGBTs/MOSFETs) in series across the DC bus. Each switch has an **anti-parallel freewheeling diode**. The DC bus is fed from the traction battery with a **DC-link capacitor** $C_{dc}$ in parallel for ripple filtering.

**Working:** In any leg, the two switches operate in **complementary fashion** — when the upper switch is ON, the lower is OFF, and vice versa. A small **dead-time** (~1–3 μs) is inserted between switching to prevent shoot-through short circuits. Each leg's output can be at either $+V_{dc}$ (upper ON) or $0$ V (lower ON), giving the "two-level" nomenclature.

By controlling the **ON durations** of the upper switches of the three legs using PWM, three phase voltages displaced by **120°** are generated.

**Role of DC-Link Capacitor $C_{dc}$:**
- Filters high-frequency switching ripple from the DC bus.
- Acts as a **temporary energy reservoir** to handle instantaneous power differences between source and load.
- Suppresses voltage transients from the battery side.

**Role of Anti-Parallel Diodes:**
- Provide **freewheeling path** for inductive motor current when a switch turns OFF (current cannot change instantaneously).
- Enable **bidirectional current flow** (essential for regenerative braking — current flows from motor back to battery).
- Allow operation in **all four quadrants** (forward motoring, forward braking, reverse motoring, reverse braking).

**Circuit Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 — two-level VSI schematic)

**Key Points to Include in Answer:**
- Six IGBTs arranged in 3 legs
- DC-link voltage 400 V or 800 V typical
- PWM switching frequency 5–20 kHz
- Output frequency 0–600 Hz variable

**[7 marks]**

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Derive an expression for the fundamental RMS line-to-line output voltage of a three-phase VSI employing Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM) in the linear modulation region. A 400 V battery feeds the inverter operating at $m_a = 0.85$. Calculate the fundamental RMS line-to-line voltage.

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:**

For SPWM in the linear modulation region ($0 \le m_a \le 1$), the **fundamental peak phase voltage** is:

$$
\hat{V}_{ph,1} = m_a \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2}
$$

The **fundamental RMS phase voltage**:

$$
V_{ph,1,rms} = \frac{m_a \cdot V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}}
$$

For a **balanced three-phase Y-connected load** (like a motor):

$$
V_{LL,1,rms} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph,1,rms}
$$

Substituting:

$$
V_{LL,1,rms} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot m_a \cdot V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{LL,1,rms} = 0.612 \, m_a \, V_{dc}}
$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $V_{dc} = 400$ V, $m_a = 0.85$

$$
V_{LL,1,rms} = 0.612 \times 0.85 \times 400
$$

**Step-by-step:**
$$
V_{LL,1,rms} = 0.612 \times 0.85 = 0.5202
$$
$$
V_{LL,1,rms} = 0.5202 \times 400 = 208.08 \text{ V}
$$

**Result:** $V_{LL,1,rms} \approx \mathbf{208.1 \text{ V}}$

**Physical Interpretation:** This is the voltage the motor "sees" at its terminals as the fundamental component. The motor controller will adjust $m_a$ and frequency to achieve the desired torque-speed point.

**[7 marks]**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) [7 Marks] Compare Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM) and Space Vector PWM (SVPWM) techniques used in EV traction inverters. Show mathematically why SVPWM provides 15.47% higher DC bus utilization than SPWM.

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | SPWM | SVPWM |
|---|---|---|
| Reference waveform | Sine wave | Rotating space vector |
| Max modulation index $m_a$ | 1.000 | 1.155 (= $2/\sqrt{3}$) |
| Max $V_{LL,1,rms}$ (per unit $V_{dc}$) | 0.612 | 0.707 |
| DC bus utilization | Baseline | +15.47% |
| THD | Higher (~5–8%) | Lower (~3–5%) |
| Digital implementation | Simple | Moderate |
| Switching losses | Moderate | Slightly lower |
| Use in EVs | Older EVs | Modern EVs (>90%) |

**Mathematical Proof of 15.47% Higher Utilization:**

In SPWM, the reference sine wave amplitude cannot exceed the carrier triangle peak. At $m_a = 1$:

$$
\hat{V}_{ph,1,SPWM,max} = \frac{V_{dc}}{2}
$$

In SVPWM, the reference vector $\vec{V}_{ref}$ must lie within the **inscribed circle of the hexagon**. The hexagon has vertices at radius $2V_{dc}/3$. The largest inscribed circle has radius:

$$
\hat{V}_{ref,max,SVPWM} = \frac{2V_{dc}/3}{\sec(30°)} = \frac{2V_{dc}}{3} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

Since $|\vec{V}_{ref}|$ equals $\hat{V}_{ph,1}$ in SVPWM:

$$
\hat{V}_{ph,1,SVPWM,max} = \frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

Ratio:

$$
\frac{\hat{V}_{ph,1,SVPWM}}{\hat{V}_{ph,1,SPWM}} = \frac{V_{dc}/\sqrt{3}}{V_{dc}/2} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.1547
$$

Percentage gain:

$$
\left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} - 1\right) \times 100\% = (1.1547 - 1) \times 100\% = \boxed{15.47\%}
$$

**[7 marks]**

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the operation of the inverter during **regenerative braking** in an EV. How does the inverter enable bidirectional power flow? What modifications are needed in the switching strategy?

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of Regenerative Braking:**

During normal driving, the inverter delivers power **from battery → motor** (motoring mode). When the driver releases the accelerator or presses the brake, the kinetic energy of the vehicle can be recovered by using the motor as a **generator**.

**Operation:**

1. The motor, now driven by the wheels, acts as a **three-phase AC generator** producing a variable-frequency, variable-amplitude AC voltage (back-EMF).
2. The **same inverter switches** are used, but their gating signals are reversed in sequence — effectively, the inverter now operates as an **active three-phase rectifier** (controlled rectifier).
3. The **IGBTs are switched synchronously** with the back-EMF to maintain proper current direction. Power now flows: **Motor → Inverter → DC bus → Battery**.
4. The anti-parallel diodes conduct during the natural freewheeling phase; the IGBTs are actively switched to control the charging current.

**How Bidirectional Power Flow is Achieved:**

- **Anti-parallel diodes** provide a path for current to flow back from the motor to the DC bus when the IGBTs are OFF.
- **Active switching** of IGBTs (with the same complement logic, but with the right phase shift) boosts the voltage to battery level.
- The **DC-link capacitor** absorbs the regenerated energy as charge and delivers it to the battery through the BMS.
- The **battery management system** must accept the charging current (with current and voltage limits).

**Switching Strategy Modifications:**

- The **PWM reference signals are phase-shifted** by 180° (or current polarity is reversed in the control loop).
- The **current controller** (FOC) operates in the **negative torque** region, commanding negative i_q.
- **Synchronization with rotor position** is critical — the inverter must align its switching with the back-EMF phase.

**Practical Considerations:**
- Regenerative braking recovers typically **60–70%** of kinetic energy in city driving.
- It cannot fully replace friction brakes (used as backup).
- The **BMS limits the maximum regen current** to protect battery cells.

**[7 marks]**

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing peak and RMS:** The factor of $\sqrt{2}$ is often missed. Always specify **which value** you are computing.
> 2. **Mixing SPWM and SVPWM formulas:** Using the SVPWM formula with $m_a = 1$ (SPWM limit) is a common error. Remember: SVPWM goes up to $m_a = 1.155$.
> 3. **Forgetting the DC-link capacitor's role:** Examiners specifically test this — students often miss the bidirectional energy buffering function.
> 4. **Not labeling the switches:** In the circuit diagram, always label S1–S6 and D1–D6 correctly. Marks are awarded for proper **neatness**.
> 5. **Ignoring regenerative braking:** When asked "types of inverter operation," many students only describe motoring mode. Always mention **bidirectional capability**.
> 6. **Wrong unit on modulation index:** Modulation index is **dimensionless** — never write it in Volts.
> 7. **Missing the $\sqrt{3}$ factor:** Line-to-line voltage is $\sqrt{3}$ times phase voltage for balanced Y-loads. Forgetting this loses 2–3 marks easily.
> 8. **Not mentioning SiC technology in modern EVs:** Examiners award bonus marks for mentioning **SiC MOSFETs**, **800V architectures**, and **V2G capability** as contemporary trends.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **🔑 Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision — Inverters in EVs**

### 🔹 Core Concepts
- [x] An **inverter** converts DC (battery) → AC (motor) with controllable V and f.
- [x] The **two-level VSI** with 6 IGBTs + 6 diodes is the **industry standard** for EV traction.
- [x] **Bidirectional power flow** is essential — enables regenerative braking.
- [x] **DC-link capacitor** filters ripple and buffers energy between battery and motor.

### 🔹 Key Topologies
- [x] **Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)** — preferred in EVs (stiff voltage source).
- [x] **Current Source Inverter (CSI)** — older designs; bulky inductor.
- [x] **Multilevel inverters** (NPC, MMC) — used in 800 V premium EVs and locomotives.

### 🔹 Modulation — Must Memorize
- [x] **SPWM:** $m_a \in [0, 1]$, $V_{LL,1,rms} = 0.612 \, m_a \, V_{dc}$
- [x] **SVPWM:** $m_a \in [0, 1.155]$, $V_{LL,1,rms} = 0.707 \, m_a \, V_{dc}$
- [x] **Six-step (square wave):** $V_{LL,1,rms} = 0.9 \, V_{dc}$ — maximum but high THD.

### 🔹 Critical Numbers
- [x] **SVPWM gain over SPWM:** exactly **15.47%** (from $2/\sqrt{3} - 1$).
- [x] **SVPWM max $m_a$:** $2/\sqrt{3} \approx 1.155$.
- [x] **EV battery:** **400 V (standard)** or **800 V (modern)**.
- [x] **Switching frequency:** **5–20 kHz (IGBT)**, **>50 kHz (SiC)**.
- [x] **Inverter efficiency:** **>95% — up to 98%** with SiC.

### 🔹 Functional Roles in EV
- [x] **Motoring:** Battery → Inverter → Motor (forward power flow).
- [x] **Regenerative braking:** Motor → Inverter → Battery (reverse power flow).
- [x] **V/f control:** maintains constant flux below base speed.
- [x] **Field weakening:** above base speed using six-step mode.
- [x] **V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid):** bidirectional chargers/inverters feed power back to grid.

### 🔹 Eight Switching States
- [x] **6 active vectors** ($V_1$ to $V_6$) — vertices of the hexagon.
- [x] **2 zero vectors** ($V_0, V_7$) — all upper or all lower switches ON.
- [x] SVPWM uses these 8 states to synthesize any reference vector in α-β plane.

### 🔹 Examiner's Quick-Win Phrases (use these in answers)
- [x] "Voltage Source Inverter with IGBT/SiC switches"
- [x] "Bidirectional power flow enables regenerative braking"
- [x] "Anti-parallel diodes provide freewheeling path"
- [x] "DC-link capacitor acts as a stiff voltage source"
- [x] "SVPWM gives 15.47% higher DC bus utilization"
- [x] "Space vector modulation reduces THD by approximately 30% compared to SPWM"
- [x] "800 V architecture with SiC MOSFETs reduces cable losses and enables faster charging"

---

> **End of Module 1 — Topic: Basic Components of Electric Vehicles: Inverters**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) — Outcome-Based Education Framework*
