
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** *Define the term "Electric Wiring Harness" and list any **four advantages** of replacing a conventional point-to-point wiring harness with a CAN Bus in a modern automobile.*

**Model Answer:**
The Electric Wiring Harness is a pre-assembled bundle of wires, terminals, connectors, and protective sleeves that distributes electrical power and signals throughout a vehicle. Replacing the conventional harness with a CAN Bus provides:
1. **Reduced harness weight** (up to 40–60% lighter) and easier packaging.
2. **Simplified diagnostics** via OBD-II and UDS protocols.
3. **Real-time data sharing** between multiple ECUs without dedicated wires.
4. **Lower cost and improved reliability** due to fewer connectors and solder joints.

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q2.** *With a neat sketch, explain the significance of the **120 Ω termination resistor** in a CAN Bus network. What happens if it is omitted?*

**Model Answer:**
The **120 Ω resistor** is connected between CAN-H and CAN-L at **both physical ends** of the bus. Its value matches the **characteristic impedance** ($Z_0$) of the twisted-pair cable (~120 Ω), thereby absorbing incident wave energy and **suppressing signal reflections** that would otherwise cause ringing and bit errors at high speeds. If the termination is omitted, the bus behaves as an open-ended transmission line; reflections corrupt the bit waveform, especially at 1 Mbps, leading to **CRC errors and bus-off states** in the ECUs.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** *With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture of a CAN Bus-based vehicle network**. Discuss the role of each major block — ECU, transceiver, bus lines, and termination. ***(7 marks)***

**(b)** *Explain the **bit-wise non-destructive arbitration** mechanism of CAN Bus. Show, with an example, how two ECUs transmitting simultaneously resolve contention based on message IDs. ***(7 marks)***

#### **Model Solution (a):**

A typical CAN Bus vehicle network consists of the following functional blocks (refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1):

- **ECUs (Electronic Control Units):** Microcontroller-based nodes (e.g., engine ECU, ABS ECU, BCM). Each contains a **CAN controller** that handles protocol logic (framing, arbitration, error handling) and a **CAN transceiver** (e.g., TJA1050, MCP2551) that converts digital logic levels into differential voltages on the physical bus.
- **Twisted-Pair Bus:** Two physical wires — **CAN-H and CAN-L** — twisted together to reject electromagnetic interference (EMI) common in automotive environments. Differential signalling provides common-mode noise rejection.
- **120 Ω Termination Resistors:** Placed at both ends, matching the cable's characteristic impedance to prevent reflections.
- **Topology:** Strictly **linear bus** (no stars, no stubs longer than ~0.3 m at 1 Mbps), as per ISO 11898-2.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

CAN uses **CSMA/CD+AMP** (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection and Arbitration on Message Priority). The procedure:

1. **Carrier Sense:** An ECU wishing to transmit checks if the bus is idle (recessive for 3 bit times).
2. **Simultaneous transmission:** Suppose **ECU-A (ID = 0x110 = 001 0001 0000)** and **ECU-B (ID = 0x108 = 001 0000 1000)** start transmitting at the same instant.
3. **Bit-by-bit comparison:** They transmit ID bits MSB-first. Up to bit 7, both send the same pattern. At bit 8, ECU-A sends `1`, ECU-B sends `0`.
4. **Dominant override:** The bus becomes dominant (`0`). ECU-A, expecting recessive but reading dominant, realizes it has **lost arbitration** and immediately switches to **receive mode** without corrupting ECU-B's message.
5. **ECU-B wins**, continues transmitting with higher priority (lower ID = 0x108 < 0x110).

This is **non-destructive** because no time, no bandwidth, and no message is lost — the higher-priority message simply continues unimpeded.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** *Draw and explain the **CAN 2.0A Data Frame structure** field-by-field. What is **bit stuffing** and why is it mandatory in CAN? ***(7 marks)***

**(b)** *A CAN Bus operates at **250 kbps**. The maximum allowable bus length per ISO 11898-2 guideline is governed by $L_{max} \cdot f_{baud} \le 25 \times 10^6$ (m·bits/s). Calculate **(i)** the maximum cable length, **(ii)** the bit time $t_{bit}$, and **(iii)** the worst-case frame transmission time for an 8-byte data frame including stuffing. ***(7 marks)***

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**CAN 2.0A Data Frame Fields** (refer to Section 4.2):
1. **SOF (1 bit, dominant):** Marks frame start; synchronizes all receivers.
2. **Arbitration Field (12 bits):** 11-bit ID + RTR bit (dominant for data frames).
3. **Control Field (6 bits):** IDE (r0), reserved bit, and 4-bit DLC (Data Length Code, 0–8).
4. **Data Field (0–64 bits):** The payload, up to 8 bytes.
5. **CRC Field (16 bits):** 15-bit CRC + 1-bit delimiter for error detection.
6. **ACK Field (2 bits):** Any receiver that accepted the frame writes a dominant bit in the ACK slot.
7. **EOF (7 bits, recessive):** Marks end of frame.
8. **IFS (3 bits):** Inter-frame space before next transmission.

**Bit Stuffing:** After every **5 consecutive identical bits**, the transmitting node automatically inserts a **stuff bit of opposite polarity**. The receiving node de-stuffs them. This guarantees **sufficient edges on the bus** for clock resynchronization — without stuffing, a long run of recessive or dominant bits would prevent the receivers from maintaining bit-time synchronization, causing errors.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Maximum cable length:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{max} &= \dfrac{25 \times 10^6}{250 \times 10^3} \\
&= 100\ \text{metres}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Bit time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{bit} &= \dfrac{1}{250 \times 10^3} \\
&= 4\ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Worst-case frame time (8-byte payload):**

From Section 3.1 derivation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{nominal} &= 47 + 64 = 111 \text{ bits} \\
N_{stuff,max} &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{111}{5} \right\rfloor = 22 \\
L_{max} &= 111 + 22 = 133 \text{ bits} \\
t_{frame,max} &= 133 \times 4\ \mu s = 532\ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong stuffing placement:** Stuff bits are inserted *during transmission*, not at the end. The stuffed bits themselves are also counted in the denominator for the next 5-bit window.
> 2. **Confusing dominant = 1:** Examiners will deduct marks if you write "dominant = logic 1". Always write **dominant = 0, recessive = 1**.
> 3. **Forgetting fixed overhead bits:** Many students calculate only the data bytes (64 bits) and forget the 47 fixed bits. The correct total is 111 bits minimum.
> 4. **Using nominal length instead of worst-case:** For real-time guarantees, always use the **worst-case (stuffed)** frame length.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **CAN Bus** = Controller Area Network, multi-master, message-broadcast serial protocol (Bosch 1986, ISO 11898).
- ✅ **Dominant bit = 0** (overrides); **Recessive bit = 1** (yields).
- ✅ **Two-wire differential bus** (CAN-H, CAN-L) twisted; **120 Ω termination** at both ends.
- ✅ **Two ID formats:** CAN 2.0A (11-bit ID), CAN 2.0B (29-bit ID). **Lower ID = Higher Priority.**
- ✅ **Non-destructive bit-wise arbitration** via CSMA/CD+AMP — no collision loss.
- ✅ **Bit stuffing** after every 5 identical bits → guarantees clock edges for synchronization.
- ✅ **Frame types:** Data, Remote, Error, Overload.
- ✅ **Data frame max length** (CAN 2.0A): 47 fixed bits + 64 data bits + ≤22 stuff bits = **133 bits worst-case**.
- ✅ **Bus length vs bit rate trade-off:** $L_{max} \cdot f_{baud} \le 25 \times 10^6$ m·bits/s.
- ✅ **Bus load <30%** recommended for real-time headroom.
- ✅ **Termination omission** → reflections → bit errors → bus-off.
- ✅ **Topology:** Strictly linear bus; **no star topology** in high-speed CAN.
- ✅ **Application domains:** Automotive, industrial automation, aerospace (CANaerospace), marine (NMEA 2000), medical, agriculture (ISOBUS).
- ✅ **Sampling point (SP)** typically 75–87.5% of bit time for reliable decoding.
- ✅ **CAN FD** (Flexible Data-rate) extends payload to 64 bytes and dual bit rate (data phase faster than arbitration phase).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiplex Wiring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET414 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Multiplex Wiring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Multiplex Wiring** (also called *Automotive Multiplexing* or *In-Vehicle Networking*) is a method of transmitting multiple digital signals, sensor data, and control commands between various Electronic Control Units (ECUs) using a **single shared communication medium** (typically a twisted-pair cable or optical fiber). It replaces the traditional point-to-point wiring harness, in which each switch and sensor required a dedicated copper wire running back to a central controller.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *Multiplex wiring is a data communication technique in which multiple input and output signals are transmitted simultaneously over a common data bus using serial protocols such as CAN, LIN, FlexRay, MOST, or Automotive Ethernet.*

Key terms an examiner expects:
- **ECU** – Electronic Control Unit (the microcomputer node).
- **Bus** – The shared physical communication channel.
- **Protocol** – The set of rules (CAN, LIN, etc.) governing message exchange.
- **Node** – Any ECU connected to the bus.
- **Message Frame** – A standardized packet of data containing ID, data, and CRC.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Office Telephone Party Line" Analogy**
>
> Imagine an old office where every desk had its own private copper wire to every other desk. With 20 employees, you would need **190 separate wires**! Now imagine instead that they all share **one single line**, and at the start of every sentence, each speaker announces *who* the message is for and *what* it is about. Only the intended listener "pays attention." 
>
> That shared line is the **multiplex bus**, the announcement is the **message ID**, and the desks are the **ECUs**. The wiring count drops from 190 to just 1 — saving copper, weight, and complexity.

### 1.3 Why It Matters — Hard Engineering Metrics

- A modern premium car contains **80–150 ECUs**.
- Conventional wiring harness length: **~3 km with ~1,500 wires and ~3,000 connectors**.
- Multiplex architecture reduces this to typically **< 1 km and < 30 wires**, cutting harness weight by **~30–50 %** and improving reliability dramatically.
- The bandwidth is standardized: Classic CAN operates at **125 kbps – 1 Mbps**; CAN FD goes up to **5 Mbps**; Automotive Ethernet reaches **100 Mbps – 1 Gbps**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Exams:** *Multiplex = "Many messages + One wire"*. Whenever you see the term, your brain should immediately picture: **ECU → Bus → ECU**, not a spaghetti of copper.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — Structured Logic

Multiplex wiring operates on the principle of **time-division and identifier-based bus arbitration**. The logical steps are:

1. **Sensor / Switch activation** — A physical event (e.g., brake pedal pressed) is detected by an ECU.
2. **Message packaging** — The ECU packages the data into a *frame* (ID + data + CRC).
3. **Bus arbitration** — If two ECUs transmit simultaneously, the one with the **lower ID** (dominant bit = 0) wins, and the other backs off.
4. **Broadcast on the bus** — The winning frame is broadcast to **all** nodes; only the intended one(s) act on it.
5. **Acknowledgement & error check** — Receiving nodes perform **CRC verification** and send an ACK bit. Errors trigger automatic retransmission.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why one shared line?** Reduces harness mass, eliminates connector count, enables central diagnostics via **OBD-II**.
- **Why a unique ID?** Allows multiple ECUs to "listen" selectively (e.g., the instrument cluster listens for vehicle-speed messages but ignores airbag data).
- **Why a CRC?** Automotive environments have severe **EMI from ignition, motors, and solenoids** — the Cyclic Redundancy Check detects corrupted bits.

### 2.3 Multiplexing Techniques Used in Vehicles

| Technique | Domain | Strength | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **LIN (Local Interconnect Network)** | Body electronics (mirrors, seats) | Very cheap, single wire, < 20 kbps | Master-slave, low speed |
| **CAN (Controller Area Network)** | Powertrain, chassis | Robust, prioritized arbitration, 1 Mbps | Limited bandwidth |
| **CAN FD** | Modern powertrain | Up to 5 Mbps, larger payload | Partial backward compat |
| **FlexRay** | Chassis, ADAS | 10 Mbps, deterministic, time-triggered | Expensive |
| **MOST (Media Oriented Systems Transport)** | Infotainment | 25–150 Mbps, supports audio/video | Optical fiber, costly |
| **Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1)** | ADAS, autonomous driving, infotainment | 100 Mbps – 1 Gbps, IP-based | Newer, EMI-sensitive design |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **KTU-board-exam-relevant**. Memorize the units and boundary conditions.

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Notes / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bus Utilization Efficiency** | $\eta_{bus} = \dfrac{N \cdot L_{data}}{T_{frame} \cdot R_{bit}}$ | dimensionless | $N$ = nodes, $L_{data}$ = payload bits, $R_{bit}$ = bit rate |
| **Wires Saved (Harness Reduction)** | $W_{saved} = \dfrac{N(N-1)/2 - 1}{N(N-1)/2} \times 100$ | **%** | For $N$ ECUs: conventional = $N(N-1)/2$ wires |
| **CAN Bit Time** | $t_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{R_{bit}}$ | **seconds** | At 1 Mbps, $t_{bit} = 1\ \mu s$ |
| **CAN Frame Time** | $T_{frame} = (44 + 8 \cdot L_{data}) \cdot t_{bit}$ | **seconds** | 44 = control bits; $L_{data}$ in **bytes** (0–8) |
| **Propagation Delay (Bus)** | $t_{prop} = \dfrac{L_{bus}}{v_{prop}}$ | **seconds** | $v_{prop} \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s in twisted pair |
| **Maximum Bus Length (CAN)** | $L_{max} = \dfrac{t_{bit} \cdot v_{prop}}{2}$ | **meters** | At 1 Mbps → $L_{max} \approx 40$ m |
| **Bit Stuffing Overhead** | $N_{stuffed} = \lceil L_{raw} / 4 \rceil$ | bits | CAN inserts stuff bit after 5 equal bits |
| **CRC Polynomial (CAN)** | $x^{15} + x^{14} + x^{10} + x^{8} + x^{7} + x^{4} + x^{3} + 1$ | — | Standard CAN 2.0A |
| **Bus Voltage Levels (CAN)** | $V_{diff} = V_{CAN\_H} - V_{CAN\_L}$ | **Volts** | Dominant: $V_{diff} \geq 0.9$ V; Recessive: $V_{diff} \leq 0.5$ V |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Diagnostics** — A single OBD-II port can read *every* ECU over the CAN bus, enabling emission testing, fault code retrieval (DTCs), and firmware flashing.
- **ADAS** — Radar, LiDAR, and camera ECUs exchange data over **Automotive Ethernet at 1 Gbps**, a rate impossible on legacy CAN.
- **Functional Safety** — Time-triggered protocols (FlexRay, TSN Ethernet) satisfy **ISO 26262** ASIL-D timing guarantees for brake-by-wire and steer-by-wire.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Conventional vs. Multiplex Wiring Count

**Problem:** A car has $N = 10$ ECUs. Compute the number of wires required in a conventional point-to-point harness and in a multiplexed bus, then the % reduction.

**Step 1 — Conventional count.** Each of the $N$ ECUs needs a dedicated wire to every other ECU. This is a complete graph $K_N$ whose edge count is the combination $\binom{N}{2}$.

$$
W_{conv} = \binom{N}{2} = \dfrac{N(N-1)}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Substituting** $N = 10$:

$$
W_{conv} = \dfrac{10 \cdot 9}{2} = 45 \text{ wires}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiplexed count.** All ECUs share **one** common bus line (plus a return/ground or second differential line, but treated as a single logical channel).

$$
W_{mux} = 1
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage reduction:**

$$
\%R = \dfrac{W_{conv} - W_{mux}}{W_{conv}} \times 100
$$

**Step 5 — Substituting values:**

$$
\%R = \dfrac{45 - 1}{45} \times 100 = \dfrac{44}{45} \times 100
$$

**Step 6 — Final numeric answer:**

$$
\%R = 97.78\ \%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Insight:** For $N=10$ ECUs, ~98 % of wires vanish. This is the *single most asked* quantitative example on multiplex wiring in KTU papers.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — CAN Frame Transmission Time

**Given:** A CAN 2.0A standard frame with $L_{data} = 8$ bytes at bit rate $R_{bit} = 500$ kbps.

**Step 1 — Bit time:**

$$
t_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{R_{bit}} = \dfrac{1}{500{,}000} = 2\ \mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Frame bit count.** Standard CAN: 1 SOF + 11 ID + 1 RTR + 1 IDE + 1 r0 + 4 DLC + 0–8 data bytes (×11 bits each incl. stuff allowance) + 15 CRC + 1 CRC-del + 1 ACK + 1 ACK-del + 7 EOF = **44 + 8·$L_{data}$** (simplified model used by KTU).

$$
L_{frame} = 44 + 8 \cdot 8 = 44 + 64 = 108 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Frame time:**

$$
T_{frame} = L_{frame} \cdot t_{bit} = 108 \cdot 2\ \mu s
$$

**Step 4 — Final result:**

$$
T_{frame} = 216\ \mu s = 0.216\ ms
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Multiplex Protocols

| Parameter | LIN | CAN 2.0 | CAN FD | FlexRay | MOST 150 | 100BASE-T1 Ethernet |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Max Bit Rate** | 20 kbps | 1 Mbps | 5 Mbps | 10 Mbps | 150 Mbps | 100 Mbps |
| **Topology** | Single-master bus | Multi-master bus | Multi-master bus | Star / multi-drop | Ring | Star / full-duplex |
| **Physical Medium** | Single wire | Twisted pair | Twisted pair | Twisted pair (2 ch.) | Optical fiber | Single twisted pair |
| **Typical Application** | Window, seat | Engine, ABS | Engine, ADAS | Drive-by-wire | Infotainment | ADAS, cameras |
| **Cost / Node** | Very low | Low | Low–Medium | High | High | Medium |
| **Determinism** | Low | Event-based | Event-based | Time-triggered | Streaming | Best-effort (TSN adds T-T) |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Low | High | High | Very High | High | Moderate |

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU mistake is to **swap CAN and LIN applications**. Remember: **LIN = body (comfort)**, **CAN = powertrain & chassis**. Markers often exploit this confusion.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — In-Vehicle Multiplex Network Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    gatewayA[Gateway ECU]
    busA[CAN Powertrain Bus]
    busB[CAN Chassis Bus]
    busC[CAN Body Bus]
    busD[LIN Body Bus]
    busE[FlexRay Chassis Bus]
    busF[Ethernet ADAS Bus]
    ecmA[Engine ECU]
    tcmA[Transmission ECU]
    absA[ABS / ESP ECU]
    epsA[Electric Power Steering]
    bcuA[Body Control Module]
    clusterA[Instrument Cluster]
    hvacA[HVAC ECU]
    mirrorA[Mirror ECU LIN]
    seatA[Seat ECU LIN]
    radarA[Front Radar]
    camA[Surround Camera]
    obdA[OBD II Diagnostic Port]

    ecmA --> busA
    tcmA --> busA
    absA --> busB
    epsA --> busB
    bcuA --> busC
    clusterA --> busC
    hvacA --> busC
    mirrorA --> busD
    seatA --> busD
    radarA --> busF
    camA --> busF

    busA --> gatewayA
    busB --> gatewayA
    busC --> gatewayA
    busD --> gatewayA
    busE --> gatewayA
    busF --> gatewayA

    gatewayA --> obdA
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — CAN Frame Format (Standard 11-bit ID)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    frameStart[CAN Standard Frame]
    sofB[SOF 1 bit]
    idB[Arbitration ID 11 bits]
    rtrB[RTR 1 bit]
    ideB[IDE 1 bit]
    r0B[r0 1 bit]
    dlcB[DLC 4 bits]
    dataB[Data Field 0 to 64 bits]
    crcB[CRC 15 bits]
    ackB[ACK 2 bits]
    eofB[EOF 7 bits]
    ifsB[IFS 3 bits]

    frameStart --> sofB --> idB --> rtrB --> ideB --> r0B --> dlcB --> dataB --> crcB --> ackB --> eofB --> ifsB
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Multiplexing Decision Flow (How a Switch Reaches a Lamp)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Driver presses switch]
    inputECU[Input ECU reads switch state]
    packageA[ECU packages message: ID 0x201, data 0x01]
    txA[ECU drives bus dominant or recessive]
    arbA{Bus arbitration winner}
    busPropA[Frame propagates on CAN bus]
    listenA[All ECUs receive frame]
    matchA{ID matches my filter}
    acceptA[Accept and process]
    ignoreA[Ignore frame]
    actA[Body Control Module turns lamp ON]
    ackA[All receivers send ACK bit]
    errA{CRC check pass}
    retryA[Retransmit on error]
    doneA[Message complete]

    startA --> inputECU --> packageA --> txA --> arbA
    arbA -- Dominant wins --> busPropA
    arbA -- Lost arbitration --> retryA
    busPropA --> listenA --> matchA
    matchA -- Yes --> acceptA --> actA --> ackA --> errA
    matchA -- No --> ignoreA
    errA -- Pass --> doneA
    errA -- Fail --> retryA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The mermaid block above is a *functional architecture flow* (per the system rule, complex physical drawings are mapped as architecture blocks). The labels remain alphanumeric and no reserved keywords are used as node names.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q1a]**

**Q1. Define multiplex wiring. How is it different from conventional point-to-point wiring?**

*Model Answer (3 marks):*
Multiplex wiring is a data communication technique in which multiple digital signals are transmitted between ECUs over a **single shared serial bus** using protocols like CAN, LIN, or FlexRay.

| Aspect | Conventional | Multiplex |
|---|---|---|
| Wiring | One dedicated wire per signal | One shared bus for many signals |
| Harness weight | High | Reduced by 30–50 % |
| Diagnostics | Difficult | OBD-II over the same bus |
| Scalability | Poor | Excellent |

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for comparison.)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Q1c]**

**Q2. List any four automotive multiplex protocols and state one typical application of each.**

*Model Answer (3 marks):*
1. **LIN** → window lift, seat control.
2. **CAN** → engine management, ABS.
3. **FlexRay** → drive-by-wire, active suspension.
4. **MOST** → infotainment audio/video.
5. **Automotive Ethernet** → ADAS cameras, radar.

(3 marks: 0.5 per protocol + 0.25 per application.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q2] — OR — May 2024, Q3**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **architecture of a multiplexed automotive network** showing ECUs, bus, and gateway. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a suitable example, derive the **wiring harness reduction** achieved by multiplexing for **N = 12 ECUs**. *(7 marks)*

---

**Solution to Q2(a) — Architecture (7 marks):**

A multiplexed automotive network consists of:

1. **Multiple ECUs** (nodes) — engine ECU, transmission ECU, ABS ECU, BCM, instrument cluster, HVAC, etc.
2. **Communication buses** — typically a hierarchical structure:
   - **High-speed CAN** for powertrain (500 kbps – 1 Mbps).
   - **Low-speed CAN / LIN** for body electronics (125 kbps / 19.2 kbps).
   - **FlexRay / Ethernet** for ADAS and chassis.
3. **Central Gateway ECU** — interconnects buses, translates messages, and provides the **OBD-II diagnostic interface**.
4. **Physical medium** — twisted-pair copper (CAN, LIN, FlexRay) or optical fiber (MOST).

*Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes):* The ECU sends a message with a unique ID onto the bus; the gateway forwards it across buses as needed; the target ECU receives and acts on it.

**Mark distribution (7 marks):** 2 for listing components, 2 for explaining bus hierarchy, 2 for the gateway role, 1 for diagram/description.

---

**Solution to Q2(b) — Wiring Reduction for N = 12 (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Conventional point-to-point wires = $\dfrac{N(N-1)}{2} = \dfrac{12 \times 11}{2} = 66$ wires.

**Step 2:** Multiplexed wires = 1 bus.

**Step 3:** Reduction:

$$
\%R = \dfrac{66 - 1}{66} \times 100 = \dfrac{65}{66} \times 100 = 98.48\ \%
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation: A 12-ECU system saves 65 wires, dramatically lowering harness mass, cost, and connector count.

**Mark distribution (7 marks):** 2 for formula, 2 for substitution, 1 for answer, 2 for interpretation/justification.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Compare **CAN, LIN, and FlexRay** protocols under the headings: topology, maximum bit rate, typical application, and cost. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **standard CAN 2.0A frame format** field-by-field and state the purpose of the **CRC field**. *(7 marks)*

---

**Solution to Q2B(a) — Protocol Comparison (7 marks):**

| Feature | LIN | CAN 2.0 | FlexRay |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Topology** | Single-master, multi-slave bus | Multi-master bus | Star and/or bus (time-triggered) |
| **Max Bit Rate** | 20 kbps | 1 Mbps | 10 Mbps (each of 2 channels) |
| **Application** | Body comfort (mirrors, seats) | Engine, ABS, transmission | Drive-by-wire, ADAS |
| **Cost** | Very low | Low | High |
| **Determinism** | Event-based | Event-based (CSMA/CR) | Time-triggered (TDMA) |

(7 marks: 1.5 per feature row, 0.5 bonus for determinism line.)

---

**Solution to Q2B(b) — CAN 2.0A Frame Format (7 marks):**

| Field | Length | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **SOF** | 1 bit | Start of Frame (dominant 0) |
| **Arbitration ID** | 11 bits | Message identifier and bus priority |
| **RTR** | 1 bit | Remote Transmission Request |
| **IDE** | 1 bit | Identifier Extension (0 = standard) |
| **r0** | 1 bit | Reserved |
| **DLC** | 4 bits | Data Length Code (0–8 bytes) |
| **Data** | 0–64 bits | Payload |
| **CRC** | 15 bits | Cyclic Redundancy Check |
| **CRC Delimiter** | 1 bit | Marks end of CRC |
| **ACK** | 2 bits | Acknowledgement |
| **EOF** | 7 bits | End of Frame (7 recessive) |
| **IFS** | 3 bits | Inter-Frame Space |

**Purpose of CRC field:** The 15-bit CRC is computed by the transmitter over SOF, ID, control, and data fields. Receivers recompute it; any mismatch signals a bit error, triggering **automatic retransmission**. This is essential in the **EMI-rich automotive environment** (ignition, motor, solenoid noise).

(7 marks: 1 per critical field × 5 = 5, 1 for CRC purpose, 1 for EMI rationale.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the "RTR / IDE / r0" trio** → -1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing CRC length** (CAN 2.0A = 15 bits, not 16). One-mark penalty.
> 3. **Writing "CAN is deterministic"** — *WRONG*. CAN is event-based; only FlexRay / TSN-Ethernet are deterministic. Heavy penalty.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert units** in CAN frame time calculations → final-answer mark lost.
> 5. **In architecture diagrams**, omitting the **Gateway ECU** → lose 2 marks; the gateway is the heart of modern domain architecture.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Multiplex wiring = *one shared bus + many ECUs + serial protocol*.
- **Hierarchy rule:** Engine/Chassis → **High-speed CAN**; Body → **Low-speed CAN or LIN**; Infotainment → **MOST**; ADAS → **Ethernet**.
- **Quantitative must-knows:**
  - Wire count formula: $W = N(N-1)/2$.
  - CAN bit time at 1 Mbps = **1 µs**.
  - Standard CAN frame ≈ **44 + 8·$L_{data}$** bits.
- **Frame anatomy:** SOF → ID → RTR → IDE → r0 → DLC → Data → CRC → ACK → EOF → IFS.
- **Why multiplexing wins:** ~30–50 % harness mass saved, single OBD-II diagnostics, OTA-capable, scalable for ADAS.
- **Determinism trap:** Only **FlexRay and TSN-Ethernet** are deterministic; **CAN is NOT** (event-based, best-effort under load).
- **Safety standard:** ISO 26262 ASIL-D demands deterministic protocols for brake-by-wire, steer-by-wire.
- **Memory hook:** *LIN is slow and cheap. CAN is the workhorse. FlexRay is the time-keeper. MOST is the entertainer. Ethernet is the future.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regenerative Braking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET414 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Regenerative Braking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Regenerative Braking (RB)** is an energy recovery mechanism used predominantly in **Electric Vehicles (EVs)** and **Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs)** in which the kinetic energy of the moving vehicle—normally dissipated as heat by conventional friction brakes—is converted into **electrical energy** by reversing the operating mode of the traction motor so that it acts as a **generator**. The recovered electrical energy is then stored in the **High-Voltage (HV) traction battery** or in **supercapacitor banks** for subsequent propulsion use.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, regenerative braking is classified as one of the four pillars of EV energy efficiency, the other three being:
1. Efficient traction motor design (PMSM / BLDC / SRM)
2. Optimized Battery Management System (BMS)
3. Lightweight vehicle architecture

The standard metric used by the Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE) to grade regenerative systems is the **Regenerative Braking Efficiency (η_rb)**, which is the ratio of recovered electrical energy to the total kinetic energy available at the wheels during a braking event.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1.4):** Students must clearly distinguish between **Pure Regenerative Braking**, **Blended (Co-operative) Braking**, and **Maximum Regenerative Braking with Anti-lock Braking System (ABS) integration**. Questions are frequently asked on the energy-flow paths.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a child on a bicycle coasting down a hill. Normally, to slow down, they squeeze the brake levers on the handlebar, and the rubber brake pads rub against the wheel rims, producing friction. The bicycle slows down, but the energy is wasted as **heat** (you can often smell hot rubber!).

Now, imagine the child instead presses a button that engages a small dynamo (generator) attached to the rear wheel. The dynamo resists the wheel's rotation (providing the braking force), but in doing so, it **charges a battery** that powers the bicycle's headlight later that night. The bicycle has slowed down *and* saved energy.

> This is **exactly** how regenerative braking works in a Tesla Model 3, Tata Nexon EV, Toyota Prius, or a Hyundai Kona Electric. The electric traction motor — the very same machine that pushes the car forward — is told by the **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)** to "spin in reverse as a generator." The electromagnetic resistance inside the motor creates the braking torque, and the induced EMF charges the battery.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **η_rb (Regenerative Braking Efficiency):** typically **0.60 – 0.80 (60 % – 80 %)** for modern production EVs
- **ρ_air (Air density at STP):** **1.225 kg/m³**
- **g (Gravitational acceleration):** **9.81 m/s²**
- **Typical peak regenerative deceleration:** **0.3 g – 0.5 g** (≈ 2.94 – 4.91 m/s²)
- **Standard braking test cycle:** **WLTP (Worldwide Harmonised Light Vehicles Test Procedure) Class 3** — used for benchmarking energy recovery in km

### 1.4 System-Level Significance

| Vehicle Category | Typical η_rb Achievable | Dominant Energy Sink |
|---|---|---|
| Mild Hybrid (MHEV, 48 V) | 20 % – 40 % | Battery (small) |
| Full Hybrid (HEV) | 40 % – 60 % | Battery + Mechanical |
| Plug-in Hybrid (PHEV) | 50 % – 70 % | Battery (large) |
| Battery Electric (BEV) | 65 % – 80 % | Battery (very large) |

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters at KTU exams:** The examiner loves the phrase *"energy is neither created nor destroyed, only converted."* Whenever a regenerative braking question appears, your first line of the answer should mention the **Law of Conservation of Energy**, followed by the **electro-mechanical energy conversion principle**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The regenerative braking sequence can be broken down into **six (6) discrete operational stages**, each managed by the VCU:

1. **Driver Intention Detection (Stage 1):** The brake pedal position sensor (a Hall-effect or potentiometric sensor) detects that the driver has lifted the accelerator pedal or pressed the brake pedal. The Accelerator Pedal Position Sensor (APPS) and Brake Pedal Position Sensor (BPPS) send analog/digital signals to the VCU.

2. **Mode Arbitration (Stage 2):** The VCU evaluates **State of Charge (SOC)** of the battery. If SOC < **85 %** (typical upper threshold to prevent overcharge), regenerative mode is **enabled**. If SOC > 90 %, regeneration is **disabled** to protect the battery cells.

3. **Motor Torque Reversal (Stage 3):** The Motor Control Unit (MCU) commands the three-phase inverter to **reverse the torque direction** of the Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM) / BLDC motor. The stator winding excitation is adjusted so that the rotor's back-EMF exceeds the battery terminal voltage, forcing current to flow *backward* into the battery.

4. **Electromagnetic Braking Force Generation (Stage 4):** Per **Lenz's Law**, the induced current creates a magnetic field that opposes the original rotation. This electromagnetic opposition manifests as a **negative torque** on the driveshaft, which the wheels transfer to the road as a **deceleration force**.

5. **Energy Storage (Stage 5):** The recovered DC current passes through the **DC-DC converter** and the **Battery Management System (BMS)** before being stored in the Li-ion traction battery. The BMS monitors individual cell voltages to ensure no cell exceeds its upper cutoff (typically **4.2 V/cell** for NMC chemistry).

6. **Hydraulic / Friction Backup (Stage 6):** If the demanded deceleration exceeds the regenerative system's capacity (e.g., emergency braking or low SOC), the **Electronic Stability Control (ESC)** module activates the conventional hydraulic friction brakes via the brake-by-wire actuator. This is the **blended braking** regime.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' — Physics Foundation

| Physics Law | Role in Regenerative Braking |
|---|---|
| **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** | A conductor moving in a magnetic field induces an EMF. In a motor, the rotor (with permanent magnets) spinning inside the stator induces a back-EMF. |
| **Lenz's Law** | The induced current flows in a direction that opposes the change producing it → creates a braking torque. |
| **Newton's Second Law (F = m·a)** | The negative torque at the wheel produces a deceleration force; the vehicle's kinetic energy decreases. |
| **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** | Back-EMF > Battery Voltage → current flows from motor to battery, charging it. |
| **Conservation of Energy** | ½·m·v² (kinetic) → Electrical energy (stored) + Heat (losses) |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **high-yield equations** that account for > 90 % of marks in regenerative braking numerical questions. Memorize units carefully.

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_k = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^2$ | $E_k$ = kinetic energy (J), $m$ = mass (kg), $v$ = velocity (m/s) | Available energy to recover |
| 2 | $P_{rb} = T_{rb} \cdot \omega$ | $P_{rb}$ = regen power (W), $T_{rb}$ = regen torque (N·m), $\omega$ = angular speed (rad/s) | Instantaneous recovery power |
| 3 | $\eta_{rb} = \dfrac{E_{recovered}}{E_{kinetic,available}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | System efficiency grading |
| 4 | $F_{brake} = \mu \cdot m \cdot g$ | $F_{brake}$ = braking force (N), $\mu$ = friction coefficient, $g$ = 9.81 m/s² | Compare with regen deceleration |
| 5 | $a_{regen} = \dfrac{T_{rb} \cdot \eta_{drivetrain}}{m \cdot r_{wheel}}$ | $a_{regen}$ = deceleration (m/s²), $r_{wheel}$ = wheel radius (m) | Equivalent friction-less braking |
| 6 | $E_{recovered} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} P_{rb}(t)\,dt$ | Time-integrated power | Energy over a braking event |
| 7 | $V_{back-emf} = K_e \cdot \omega$ | $K_e$ = motor voltage constant (V·s/rad) | Voltage generated by motor as generator |
| 8 | $I_{charge} = \dfrac{V_{back-emf} - V_{battery}}{R_{internal}}$ | $R_{internal}$ = wiring + connector resistance (Ω) | Charging current into battery |
| 9 | $\omega = \dfrac{v}{r_{wheel}}$ | Wheel angular speed from linear velocity | Used to compute back-EMF |
| 10 | $SOC_{final} = SOC_{initial} + \dfrac{E_{recovered}}{E_{battery,capacity}}$ | Expressed as percentage (0 – 100 %) | State of Charge update |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **Formula E racing** (the premier electric single-seater championship), regenerative braking is so heavily exploited that drivers can complete an entire lap without using the hydraulic rear brakes. The **Mahindra M9Electro** and **Porsche 99X Electric** recover up to **350 kW** of peak regen power — equivalent to the energy used by **280 average Indian households for one hour**.

In **commercial logistics**, the **Tata ACE EV** and **Mahindra Treo** (electric three-wheelers) use aggressive regenerative tuning to extend range on congested stop-and-go Indian city routes, where frequent braking events are ideal for energy recovery.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Maximum Recoverable Energy During Braking

**Problem Statement:** A battery electric vehicle of mass **m = 1500 kg** (including battery) is traveling at **v = 90 km/h = 25 m/s**. The driver applies the brakes and decelerates uniformly to a stop. Compute the total kinetic energy available for recovery, assuming the regenerative system operates at an efficiency of **η_rb = 0.70**.

**Step 1: Identify the available kinetic energy**

The vehicle's kinetic energy at the moment of brake application is given by the classical Newtonian expression:

$$
E_k = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^2
$$

**Step 2: Substitute numerical values**

$$
E_k = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot (1500\ \text{kg}) \cdot (25\ \text{m/s})^2
$$

**Step 3: Compute the square of velocity**

$$
(25\ \text{m/s})^2 = 625\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

**Step 4: Multiply by mass and divide by 2**

$$
E_k = 0.5 \cdot 1500 \cdot 625
$$

$$
E_k = 750 \cdot 625 = 468\,750\ \text{J}
$$

**Step 5: Convert to kilojoules and kilowatt-hours for engineering readability**

$$
E_k = 468.75\ \text{kJ} = \dfrac{468\,750}{3.6 \times 10^6}\ \text{kWh} = 0.1302\ \text{kWh}
$$

**Step 6: Apply the regenerative efficiency factor**

$$
E_{recovered} = \eta_{rb} \cdot E_k
$$

$$
E_{recovered} = 0.70 \cdot 468\,750 = 328\,125\ \text{J}
$$

**Step 7: Final converted value**

$$
E_{recovered} \approx 0.0911\ \text{kWh}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** 0.0911 kWh is enough energy to power a 100 W incandescent bulb for ~55 minutes, or to propel the same 1500 kg vehicle an additional **1.2 km** at city speeds. This is why every stoplight in an EV is considered a "free refuel."

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Instantaneous Regenerative Power as a Function of Speed

**Problem Statement:** For the same 1500 kg EV with wheel radius **r_w = 0.33 m**, compute the instantaneous regenerative power at a vehicle speed of **v = 60 km/h = 16.67 m/s**, given a constant regenerative torque command of **T_rb = 250 N·m** and a drivetrain efficiency of **η_d = 0.92**.

**Step 1: Compute the wheel angular speed**

$$
\omega = \dfrac{v}{r_w} = \dfrac{16.67}{0.33} = 50.51\ \text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the mechanical power at the wheel**

The mechanical power delivered by the braking torque is:

$$
P_{mech,wheel} = T_{rb} \cdot \omega
$$

$$
P_{mech,wheel} = 250 \cdot 50.51 = 12\,627.5\ \text{W}
$$

**Step 3: Apply drivetrain efficiency to get electrical-side power**

$$
P_{rb} = \eta_d \cdot P_{mech,wheel}
$$

$$
P_{rb} = 0.92 \cdot 12\,627.5 = 11\,617.3\ \text{W}
$$

**Step 4: Express in kilowatts**

$$
P_{rb} \approx 11.62\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the equivalent deceleration**

The linear deceleration produced by this regen torque is:

$$
a_{regen} = \dfrac{T_{rb} \cdot \eta_d}{m \cdot r_w}
$$

$$
a_{regen} = \dfrac{250 \cdot 0.92}{1500 \cdot 0.33} = \dfrac{230}{495} = 0.4646\ \text{m/s}^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This deceleration is **0.0474 g** — well within comfortable passenger comfort limits (typical maximum comfortable regen is **0.3 g**), confirming that 11.62 kW of recovery feels smooth to the driver.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — SOC Increment from a Single Braking Event

**Problem Statement:** If the recovered energy from Section 3.1 (0.0911 kWh) is stored in a traction battery of total capacity **E_cap = 60 kWh**, compute the SOC increment.

**Step 1: Apply the SOC increment formula**

$$
\Delta SOC = \dfrac{E_{recovered}}{E_{cap}} \cdot 100\%
$$

**Step 2: Substitute**

$$
\Delta SOC = \dfrac{0.0911}{60} \cdot 100\%
$$

**Step 3: Final value**

$$
\Delta SOC = 0.1518\ \%
$$

> A single highway-speed stop recovers roughly **0.15 % SOC** in this vehicle. Over 50 such stops (typical daily Indian urban commute), this adds up to **7.5 % SOC** — equivalent to **~30 km of additional range** in a real-world Tata Nexon EV.

---

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Braking Type | Energy Recovery | Deceleration Limit | Pedal Feel | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pure Friction (Conventional)** | 0 % (all heat) | ~1.0 g | Hard, linear | All ICE vehicles |
| **Pure Regenerative** | 60 – 80 % | 0.3 – 0.5 g | Soft, can feel "grabby" | Two-wheeler EVs (Ola, Ather) |
| **Blended (Co-operative)** | 50 – 70 % | 0.8 – 1.0 g | Natural, mimics ICE | Tesla Model 3, Hyundai Kona |
| **One-Pedal Driving** | 70 – 85 % | 0.4 g via accelerator lift-off | Strong regen on lift-off | Nissan Leaf, BMW i3 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Regenerative Braking System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **interactions** between the major subsystems of a regenerative braking system. This is a **functional block diagram**, not a physical sketch, but it is the *exact* representation KTU examiners expect in 14-mark descriptive questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Driver Intent: Brake Pedal Sensor] --> node2{Vehicle Control Unit: VCU Mode Arbitration}
    node3[Accelerator Pedal Sensor] --> node2
    node4[Battery SOC Feedback] --> node2
    node2 -->|SOC less than 85 percent| node5[Motor Control Unit: Inverter Reverse Mode]
    node2 -->|SOC greater than 90 percent| node6[Disable Regen, Friction Only]
    node5 --> node7[PMSM Motor Acting as Generator]
    node7 -->|Three Phase AC Output| node8[Inverter: AC to DC Rectification]
    node8 -->|High Voltage DC Bus, 350 to 800 V| node9[DC DC Converter: Voltage Step Down]
    node9 --> node10[Battery Management System BMS]
    node10 --> node11[Li ion Traction Battery Pack]
    node11 -->|SOC Increment| node4
    node2 -->|Excess Demand Detected| node12[ESC Module Activates Hydraulic Brakes]
    node12 --> node13[Friction Brake Calipers at Wheels]
    node7 -->|Opposing Electromagnetic Torque| node14[Wheels: Negative Torque Applied]
    node14 --> node15[Vehicle Decelerates]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Follow the arrow from `Driver Intent: Brake Pedal Sensor` (node1) to the VCU decision diamond (node2), then to the inverter (node5), then to the motor (node7), then back to the wheels (node14). The closed loop with `node4 → node2 → node11` shows the **SOC feedback** that decides whether regen is allowed.

---

### 4.2 Sequential State Diagram of Regen Mode Transitions

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> DriveMode
    DriveMode --> RegenReady: Accelerator released
    RegenReady --> RegenActive: Brake pedal pressed and SOC acceptable
    RegenActive --> FrictionBackup: Deceleration demand exceeds regen capacity
    RegenActive --> DriveMode: Brake released
    RegenActive --> RegenDisabled: SOC greater than 90 percent
    FrictionBackup --> RegenActive: Demand falls back within regen limit
    RegenDisabled --> DriveMode: SOC stabilizes
    DriveMode --> [*]: Ignition OFF
```

**State explanations:**
- **DriveMode:** Vehicle is accelerating or cruising; motor consumes power.
- **RegenReady:** Accelerator released; mild regen may begin (≈ 0.05 g).
- **RegenActive:** Brake pedal pressed; full regen engaged.
- **FrictionBackup:** Hydraulic brakes join in to meet total demand.
- **RegenDisabled:** Battery full; regen locked out to prevent overcharge.

---

### 4.3 Energy Flow Sankey-Style Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Vehicle Kinetic Energy 100 percent] -->|Mechanical to Wheels| nodeB[Brake Demand at Wheels]
    nodeB -->|60 to 80 percent| nodeC[Regenerative Path: Motor to Inverter to Battery]
    nodeB -->|20 to 40 percent| nodeD[Friction Path: Calipers to Rotors to Heat]
    nodeC -->|5 percent| nodeE[Inverter and Wiring Losses]
    nodeC -->|3 percent| nodeF[BMS and Charging Losses]
    nodeC -->|52 to 72 percent| nodeG[Useful Stored Energy in Battery]
    nodeD -->|20 to 40 percent| nodeH[Waste Heat to Atmosphere]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked to draw an "energy flow diagram," always use **two parallel paths** (Regen + Friction) and label the percentages. This single diagram can fetch 4 – 5 marks by itself in a 14-mark question.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Define regenerative braking. State any two advantages of regenerative braking over conventional friction braking.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Regenerative braking is an energy recovery mechanism in which the electric traction motor of an EV/HEV is operated as a generator during deceleration, converting the vehicle's kinetic energy into electrical energy that is stored in the traction battery instead of being dissipated as heat.
>
> **Advantages (1 mark — any two):**
> 1. **Extended driving range** — 15 – 25 % improvement in urban WLTP cycle.
> 2. **Reduced brake wear** — friction pads last 2 – 3× longer since hydraulic brakes are used less.
> 3. **Lower operating cost** — electricity from regen is essentially "free."
> 4. **Reduced thermal load** — no brake fade on long descents.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **List the four main components of a regenerative braking system and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Traction Motor (PMSM/BLDC)** | Acts as generator during braking; converts kinetic → electrical energy |
| 2 | **Power Inverter / MCU** | Rectifies three-phase AC output of the motor to DC for the battery |
| 3 | **Battery Management System (BMS)** | Monitors SOC, cell voltage, temperature; allows/blocks charging current |
| 4 | **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)** | Arbitrates between regen and friction based on driver demand, SOC, vehicle speed |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Q3 (A).** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 1 Choice Question]`
> **(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the working principle of a regenerative braking system in a Battery Electric Vehicle. Discuss the role of the VCU, BMS, and Motor Control Unit. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** An electric vehicle of mass **1800 kg** is traveling at **72 km/h**. The driver applies brakes to bring the vehicle to rest in a distance of **40 m**. The regenerative braking system recovers **55 %** of the available kinetic energy. Compute:
> (i) Initial kinetic energy of the vehicle.
> (ii) Total energy recovered by the regenerative system.
> (iii) The energy stored in the battery as a percentage of its **75 kWh** total capacity (ΔSOC). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q3 (A):

**(a) Working Principle (7 marks):**

When the driver presses the brake pedal, the **Brake Pedal Position Sensor (BPPS)** sends a signal to the **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)**. The VCU reads the current **State of Charge (SOC)** of the traction battery from the **Battery Management System (BMS)**. If SOC is below the upper threshold (typically 85 – 90 %), the VCU commands the **Motor Control Unit (MCU)** to switch the traction motor into generator mode by reversing the torque command in the inverter.

The **three-phase inverter** rectifies the AC output of the motor into DC. This DC power flows through the **DC-DC converter** (to step down the voltage to battery level) and into the **Li-ion traction battery**. The motor's electromagnetic resistance creates a **negative torque** at the driveshaft, decelerating the vehicle.

Simultaneously, if the demanded deceleration exceeds the regenerative capacity, the VCU commands the **Electronic Stability Control (ESC)** module to apply the **hydraulic friction brakes**, resulting in **blended braking**.

**Roles:**
- **VCU:** Central brain; arbitrates between regen and friction; ensures smooth pedal feel.
- **BMS:** Protects battery from overcharge; monitors cell balancing, temperature, voltage.
- **MCU:** Controls inverter switching; performs field-weakening at high speeds; implements torque vectoring.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Initial kinetic energy:**

Convert velocity: 72 km/h = 72 × (1000/3600) = **20 m/s**

$$
E_k = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1800 \cdot (20)^2
$$

$$
E_k = 0.5 \cdot 1800 \cdot 400 = 360\,000\ \text{J} = 360\ \text{kJ}
$$

**(ii) Recovered energy:**

$$
E_{recovered} = \eta_{rb} \cdot E_k = 0.55 \cdot 360\,000 = 198\,000\ \text{J} = 198\ \text{kJ}
$$

**(iii) SOC increment:**

Convert recovered energy to kWh:

$$
E_{recovered} = \dfrac{198\,000}{3.6 \times 10^6}\ \text{kWh} = 0.055\ \text{kWh}
$$

$$
\Delta SOC = \dfrac{0.055}{75} \cdot 100\% = 0.0733\ \%
$$

> The single braking event recovers **0.055 kWh** of energy, equivalent to a **0.073 %** SOC increment.

---

> **Q3 (B).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 1 Alternative Choice]`
> **(a)** Compare **pure regenerative braking**, **blended braking**, and **one-pedal driving** modes. State one vehicle example for each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the expression for the equivalent regenerative deceleration in terms of motor braking torque $T_{rb}$, drivetrain efficiency $\eta_d$, vehicle mass $m$, and wheel radius $r_w$. An EV with $T_{rb}$ = **400 N·m**, $\eta_d$ = **0.94**, $m$ = **2000 kg**, and $r_w$ = **0.35 m** computes the deceleration. Comment on whether the value is safe for a passenger car. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q3 (B):

**(a) Comparison (7 marks):**

| Mode | Description | Deceleration Range | Pedal Feel | Example Vehicle |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pure Regenerative** | Only motor braking; no friction use below threshold | 0.2 – 0.4 g | Can feel weak or "grabby" | Ola S1 Pro (two-wheeler) |
| **Blended Braking** | Regen + friction simultaneously; VCU blends based on demand | Up to 1.0 g | Natural, mimics ICE | Tata Nexon EV, Hyundai Kona |
| **One-Pedal Driving** | Strong regen on accelerator release; brake pedal used minimally | 0.3 – 0.5 g | Distinctive, "engine braking" feel | Nissan Leaf, BMW i3 |

**(b) Derivation (7 marks):**

The torque at the wheel is:

$$
T_{wheel} = T_{rb} \cdot \eta_d
$$

The linear braking force at the tire contact patch (force = torque ÷ radius):

$$
F_{brake} = \dfrac{T_{rb} \cdot \eta_d}{r_w}
$$

By **Newton's Second Law**, F = m·a, so:

$$
m \cdot a_{regen} = \dfrac{T_{rb} \cdot \eta_d}{r_w}
$$

Therefore, the regenerative deceleration is:

$$
a_{regen} = \dfrac{T_{rb} \cdot \eta_d}{m \cdot r_w}
$$

**Numerical substitution:**

$$
a_{regen} = \dfrac{400 \cdot 0.94}{2000 \cdot 0.35} = \dfrac{376}{700} = 0.537\ \text{m/s}^2
$$

In units of g:

$$
a_{regen} = \dfrac{0.537}{9.81} = 0.0547\ \text{g}
$$

> **Comment:** The deceleration of **0.0547 g** (≈ 0.537 m/s²) is **well within the safe passenger car regenerative braking range** (typical max regen-only is 0.3 – 0.5 g). It provides smooth, comfortable deceleration without triggering ABS or causing passenger discomfort. The remaining braking demand (e.g., for emergency stops) is handled by the friction system.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost 2 – 4 Marks per Question:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Forgetting to convert km/h → m/s. Always multiply km/h by **5/18**.
> 2. **Sign confusion:** Regenerative torque is **negative** with respect to propulsion torque. Many students write "+250 N·m" instead of "−250 N·m" in energy balance equations.
> 3. **Mixing efficiency factors:** The regen efficiency η_rb applies to **energy**, while the drivetrain efficiency η_d applies to **power transmission**. Do not multiply them in the wrong place.
> 4. **Ignoring SOC limit:** Real systems disable regen when SOC > 90 %. Forgetting this loses 1 mark in descriptive answers.
> 5. **No friction backup discussion:** A complete answer to "explain regenerative braking" **must** mention blended braking and the role of ESC/hydraulic brakes.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (read before entering the exam hall):**

- **Definition:** Energy recovery by operating the traction motor as a generator during deceleration.
- **Key formula (must memorize):** $E_k = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ and $P_{rb} = T_{rb} \cdot \omega$
- **Efficiency band:** η_rb = **60 – 80 %** for modern BEVs.
- **SOC limits:** Regen enabled typically between **20 % – 85 %** SOC; disabled above 90 % to protect battery.
- **Three braking modes:** Pure regen, Blended, One-pedal — know an example vehicle for each.
- **Six operational stages:** Pedal sensing → VCU arbitration → Inverter reversal → Generator mode → Battery storage → Friction backup.
- **Key laws:** Faraday's Law, Lenz's Law, Conservation of Energy, Newton's 2nd Law.
- **Energy storage location:** Li-ion traction battery (primary) and sometimes supercapacitor bank (for high-power bursts).
- **Deceleration limit:** Regenerative-only deceleration is **0.3 – 0.5 g**; beyond this, friction brakes engage.
- **KTU's favorite question style:** "An EV of mass M kg traveling at V km/h recovers X % energy. Compute (i) kinetic energy, (ii) recovered energy, (iii) ΔSOC."
- **Conversion constant:** 1 kWh = **3.6 × 10⁶ J** = **3,600,000 J**. Memorize.
- **Real-world benchmark:** Tesla Model 3 recovers up to **~70 kW** peak regen power; Formula E cars recover up to **350 kW**.
- **Indian context:** Tata Nexon EV (₹ 15 L, 465 km range) uses 4-level regen controlled via paddle shifters.
- **Block diagram must-have components:** Driver pedal sensor → VCU → MCU/Inverter → Motor (acting as generator) → DC-DC converter → BMS → Battery.
- **Two paths of energy:** Regen path (motor → battery) and Friction path (calipers → heat). Always draw both in 14-mark answers.

---

> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes: Regenerative Braking**
> *Prepared in accordance with the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) syllabus for course PEIET414 — Modern Automotive Technologies.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors to Consider for Converting Automobiles to Electric Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET414 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Factors to Consider for Converting Automobiles to Electric Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Electric Vehicle (EV) Conversion**, also known as **Retrofitting**, is the engineering process of removing the Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) powertrain—including the engine, fuel tank, exhaust system, and related auxiliaries—from an existing vehicle and replacing it with an Electric Powertrain comprising an **Electric Motor**, **Battery Pack**, **Power Electronics Controller**, **Battery Management System (BMS)**, and **Charging Interface**, while reusing the original chassis, suspension, braking, and body structure to meet target performance, range, and regulatory specifications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Verbatim Style):** "EV conversion is a cost-effective alternative to purchasing a new electric vehicle, involving the substitution of conventional propulsion components with electrified equivalents, subject to vehicle dynamic stability, safety standards, and homologation rules prescribed by the Automotive Research Association of India (ARAI) and Central Motor Vehicle Rules (CMVR)."

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of EV conversion as a **"Heart Transplant Surgery"** for a vehicle:

- The **ICE engine** is the old heart that burns fuel (food) noisily and emits smoke.
- The **Electric Motor + Battery** is the new efficient, silent, clean heart.
- The **chassis, wheels, seats, body** remain the same (the body of the patient).
- The **doctor (engineer)** must carefully match the new heart's capacity to the body's weight, lifestyle (range), and physical limits (voltage, weight capacity).
- A successful transplant lets the "patient" run faster, cleaner, and cheaper—just like an EV conversion breathes new life into an old car.

## 1.3 Why Convert? The Strategic Context

> [!NOTE]
> India has **over 30 crore registered vehicles** as of 2024. Widespread EV conversion can dramatically reduce urban air pollution (PM2.5 and NOx), cut crude-oil imports, and extend the life of otherwise serviceable vehicles. The Government of India, through the **Ministry of Heavy Industries**, has been actively promoting retrofitting as part of the **FAME-II** and **PM E-Drive** schemes.

## 1.4 Key Engineering Standards & Metrics

The following standards and metrics govern every EV conversion in the KTU/Indian context:

- **ARAI** – Automotive Research Association of India (testing and certification)
- **CMVR** – Central Motor Vehicle Rules (homologation)
- **AIS Standards** – Automotive Industry Standards (AIS-156, AIS-038 for batteries)
- **ISO 6469** – Electrically propelled road vehicles – Safety specifications
- **UN/ECE R100** – Uniform provisions concerning the approval of vehicles with electric powertrain
- **Nominal System Voltage:** 48 V, 96 V, 144 V, 400 V architectures
- **Specific Energy Target:** **150–250 Wh/kg** for modern Li-ion cells
- **Round-trip Efficiency Target:** **85–92%** for the complete drivetrain

## 1.5 High-Level Conversion Workflow

A typical conversion proceeds through these broad phases:

1. **Donor Vehicle Assessment** – Mass, payload, GVW, chassis integrity
2. **Performance Specification** – Range, top speed, gradeability
3. **Powertrain Component Selection** – Motor, battery, controller
4. **Mechanical Integration** – Mounting, cooling, weight distribution
5. **Electrical Integration** – Wiring harness, BMS, safety interlocks
6. **Regulatory Homologation** – ARAI testing, road certification

> [!TIP]
> KTU 2024 Scheme examiners expect students to articulate **why** a factor matters, not just **list** it. Always pair a factor with its engineering consequence (e.g., "Battery weight → reduces payload → affects suspension design").
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Comprehensive List of Conversion Factors

The decision to convert, and the success of that conversion, hinges on a structured evaluation of the following engineering, economic, and regulatory factors. Each factor is broken down into its operational logic.

### Factor 1: Vehicle Mass and Payload Capacity

- **Why it matters:** Batteries are energy-dense but **heavy** (~5–8× the energy of petrol per kg). Adding 300–500 kg of batteries drastically alters vehicle dynamics.
- **How to handle:**
  - Verify Gross Vehicle Weight (GVW) limits
  - Recalculate axle load distribution
  - Upgrade suspension springs and dampers
  - Reinforce brakes (regen + friction)
- **Consequence if ignored:** Premature tire wear, brake failure, suspension bottoming, invalid insurance/registration.

### Factor 2: Range Requirements

- **Why it matters:** Defines the minimum battery energy (kWh) to be installed.
- **How to handle:** Match battery to typical daily drive cycle (city/highway/mixed), not theoretical maximum.
- **Rule of Thumb:** For Indian urban use, **100–150 km** per charge is the practical sweet spot for retrofit projects.

### Factor 3: Battery Pack Sizing and Chemistry

- **Why it matters:** Battery is **40–60% of total conversion cost** and determines weight, range, life, and safety.
- **Chemistry Options:**
  - **LFP (LiFePO₄):** Safer, longer cycle life (2000–3000 cycles), lower energy density
  - **NMC (Nickel Manganese Cobalt):** Higher energy density, used in premium conversions
  - **LTO (Lithium Titanate):** Ultra-fast charging, very long life, expensive
- **Voltage Selection Logic:**
  - < 72 V: Low-power two/three-wheelers
  - 96–144 V: Small cars, light commercial vehicles (most common retrofit)
  - 400 V: High-performance conversions (rare in retrofit)

### Factor 4: Electric Motor Selection

- **Motor Type vs Application:**

  | Motor Type | Efficiency | Power Density | Cost | Best Use |
  |------------|-----------|---------------|------|----------|
  | BLDC | 85–90% | High | Medium | Two/three-wheelers, small cars |
  | PMSM | 92–96% | Very High | High | Modern EV passenger cars |
  | AC Induction | 90–93% | Medium | Low | Commercial/heavy duty |
  | SRM (Switched Reluctance) | 85–90% | High | Low | Rugged applications |

- **Power Sizing Logic:** Peak power must overcome rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag, and provide acceptable acceleration.
- **Torque Sizing:** Continuous torque must handle worst-case grade (typically 30% gradient for Indian hill roads).

### Factor 5: Weight Distribution and Center of Gravity (CoG)

- **Why it matters:** Original ICE cars have engine weight concentrated at the **front** (front-wheel-drive) or **front + rear** (RWD). Batteries, placed low in the floor, **lower CoG by 30–50 mm**, improving stability.
- **Risk:** Top-heavy battery placement in the engine bay can cause dangerous weight transfer during braking.

### Factor 6: Charging Infrastructure

- **AC Slow Charging (3.3–7 kW):** Standard home charging, 6–8 hours for full charge
- **DC Fast Charging (15–50 kW):** Public stations, 30–60 minutes to 80% SOC
- **On-board Charger (OBC) Rating:** Must match available grid supply
- **Regenerative Braking:** Recovers 10–20% of energy in city driving

### Factor 7: Thermal Management

- Battery performance degrades above **45 °C** and below **0 °C**
- Liquid cooling > Air cooling > Passive cooling (efficiency order)
- Indian ambient conditions (40–50 °C) mandate **active cooling** for LFP/NMC packs
- Motor and controller also need thermal protection (typically 80 °C max operating)

### Factor 8: Cost and Economic Viability

- **Conversion Cost in India (2024):** ₹4–8 lakhs for a typical hatchback
- **Payback Period:** 3–5 years depending on fuel savings (₹8/km petrol → ₹1.2/km electricity)
- **Government Subsidies:** FAME-II and state EV policies may offset 20–40% of cost

### Factor 9: Legal, Safety, and Regulatory Compliance

- **AIS-156:** Phased safety requirements for traction batteries
- **CMVR Rule 126:** Crash and electrical safety testing
- **Wiring Standards:** HV cables must be **orange**, double-insulated, properly routed
- **Emergency Disconnect:** Mandatory **HVIL (High Voltage Interlock Loop)** and **pyro-fuse** for crash protection

### Factor 10: Auxiliary Systems Integration

- Original 12 V system (lights, horn, infotainment) must continue working
- Need a **DC-DC converter** to step down HV battery (e.g., 96 V → 12 V)
- Power steering, HVAC compressor may need **electric versions** (vacuum pump deleted)

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important cheat sheet** for solving EV conversion numerical problems in KTU 2024 exams. Memorize units and boundary conditions.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Parameter} &\quad \text{Formula} &\quad \text{Units} \\
\hline
\text{Battery Stored Energy} &\quad E_{batt} = V_{nom} \times Q_{Ah} / 1000 &\quad \text{kWh} \\
\text{Required Battery Capacity} &\quad Q_{Ah} = \frac{R \times E_{cons}}{\eta_{sys} \times V_{nom}} &\quad \text{Ah} \\
\text{Vehicle Range} &\quad R = \frac{E_{batt} \times \eta_{sys}}{E_{cons}} &\quad \text{km} \\
\text{Total Tractive Force} &\quad F_t = F_{roll} + F_{grade} + F_{aero} + F_{acc} &\quad \text{N} \\
\text{Rolling Resistance} &\quad F_{roll} = m \cdot g \cdot C_r \cdot \cos\theta &\quad \text{N} \\
\text{Grade Resistance} &\quad F_{grade} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta &\quad \text{N} \\
\text{Aerodynamic Drag} &\quad F_{aero} = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2 &\quad \text{N} \\
\text{Acceleration Force} &\quad F_{acc} = m \cdot a &\quad \text{N} \\
\text{Peak Motor Power} &\quad P_{peak} = \frac{F_t \cdot v}{1000 \cdot \eta_{motor}} &\quad \text{kW} \\
\text{Specific Energy Check} &\quad E_{spec} = \frac{E_{batt}}{m_{batt}} &\quad \text{Wh/kg} \\
\text{Energy Consumption} &\quad E_{cons} = \frac{P_{aux} \cdot t + E_{drive}}{R} &\quad \text{kWh/km} \\
\text{Regen Energy Recovery} &\quad E_{regen} = \eta_{regen} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \Delta h &\quad \text{kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Symbol Dictionary

- $V_{nom}$ : Nominal battery pack voltage (V)
- $Q_{Ah}$ : Battery capacity (Ampere-hours)
- $R$ : Range (km)
- $E_{cons}$ : Energy consumption per km (kWh/km)
- $\eta_{sys}$ : Overall system efficiency (0.80–0.90)
- $m$ : Vehicle mass (kg)
- $g$ : Gravitational acceleration (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $C_r$ : Coefficient of rolling resistance (0.012–0.018 for car tires)
- $C_d$ : Coefficient of drag (0.25–0.40 for cars)
- $A_f$ : Frontal area (m²)
- $v$ : Velocity (m/s)
- $\theta$ : Road gradient angle
- $\rho$ : Air density (**1.225 kg/m³** at sea level, 25 °C)
- $a$ : Acceleration (m/s²)

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are not academic exercises—they are **used daily** by:

- **Retrofit startups** (e.g., Altigreen, Cellestial E-Mobility, E-Trio) to size batteries for fleet vehicles
- **ARAI test engineers** to verify claimed range under standard **MIDC (Modified Indian Driving Cycle)**
- **Automotive OEMs** during EV product development
- **KTU/automotive engineering researchers** for simulation in MATLAB-Simulink and AVL Cruise

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every step below is fully expanded. No algebraic transition is skipped. KTU 2024 examiners award **partial marks for correct methodology**, so showing the logic earns marks even if arithmetic slips.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Required Battery Capacity for a Target Range

### Problem Setup

A Maruti 800 (curb mass 650 kg) is to be converted to an EV. Target range: **150 km**. Average speed: **45 km/h**. Energy consumption: **0.12 kWh/km** (typical for lightweight Indian car). System efficiency: **0.85**. Selected nominal voltage: **96 V**.

**Find:** Required battery capacity in **Ah** and total **kWh** stored.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — State the energy balance equation:**

The usable energy stored in the battery must equal the total energy required to cover the range, divided by the system efficiency (losses in motor, controller, transmission, and auxiliaries).

$$
E_{batt} = \frac{R \times E_{cons}}{\eta_{sys}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given numerical values:**

$$
E_{batt} = \frac{150 \times 0.12}{0.85}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator:**

$$
150 \times 0.12 = 18.0 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the quotient:**

$$
E_{batt} = \frac{18.0}{0.85} = 21.176 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert kWh to Wh for unit consistency:**

$$
E_{batt} = 21.176 \times 1000 = 21{,}176 \text{ Wh}
$$

**Step 6 — Use the energy-voltage-capacity relationship to find Ah:**

The fundamental battery equation states:

$$
E_{batt} = V_{nom} \times Q_{Ah}
$$

Rearranging for $Q_{Ah}$:

$$
Q_{Ah} = \frac{E_{batt}}{V_{nom}}
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute values:**

$$
Q_{Ah} = \frac{21{,}176}{96} = 220.6 \text{ Ah}
$$

**Step 8 — Round to commercially available rating:**

Select a **220 Ah** or **230 Ah** pack. For example, a 96 V pack using 30 cells of 100 Ah in series-parallel configuration (3 parallel strings of 10 cells = 96 V nominal, 300 Ah? No — reconfigure: 30S × 1P = 96 V, 100 Ah is insufficient).

**Correct practical pack configuration:** Use **96 V nominal** with **220 Ah** → requires either a single large LFP cell pack (3.2 V × 30 = 96 V) at 220 Ah, OR parallel cells for higher capacity. For 220 Ah, use **30S × 2P** of 110 Ah cells, or similar.

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{Q_{Ah} \approx 221 \text{ Ah}, \quad E_{batt} \approx 21.2 \text{ kWh}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Peak Motor Power from Tractive Force

### Problem Setup

A 1500 kg EV conversion (including batteries) must:
- Cruise at **60 km/h** on a flat road
- Climb a **15% gradient** (≈ 8.53°)
- Accelerate from 0–60 km/h in **10 seconds** (composite worst-case)

Given: $C_r = 0.015$, $C_d = 0.35$, $A_f = 2.2 \text{ m}^2$, $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\eta_{motor} = 0.90$.

**Find:** Peak motor power in **kW**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Convert all velocities to SI units (m/s):**

$$
v = 60 \text{ km/h} = \frac{60 \times 1000}{3600} = 16.67 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate acceleration required:**

$$
a = \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t} = \frac{16.67 - 0}{10} = 1.667 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the gradient angle:**

For 15% grade, $\tan\theta = 0.15$, therefore:

$$
\theta = \arctan(0.15) = 8.531°
$$

**Step 4 — Compute each component of tractive force:**

**(a) Rolling Resistance:**

$$
F_{roll} = m \cdot g \cdot C_r \cdot \cos\theta
$$

$$
F_{roll} = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.015 \times \cos(8.531°)
$$

$$
F_{roll} = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.015 \times 0.989 = 218.3 \text{ N}
$$

**(b) Grade Resistance:**

$$
F_{grade} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta
$$

$$
F_{grade} = 1500 \times 9.81 \times \sin(8.531°) = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.1483 = 2182.5 \text{ N}
$$

**(c) Aerodynamic Drag:**

$$
F_{aero} = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2
$$

$$
F_{aero} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.35 \times 2.2 \times (16.67)^2
$$

$$
F_{aero} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.35 \times 2.2 \times 277.9 = 131.1 \text{ N}
$$

**(d) Acceleration Force:**

$$
F_{acc} = m \cdot a = 1500 \times 1.667 = 2500.0 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Sum the tractive forces:**

$$
F_t = F_{roll} + F_{grade} + F_{aero} + F_{acc}
$$

$$
F_t = 218.3 + 2182.5 + 131.1 + 2500.0 = 5031.9 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the power required at the wheels:**

$$
P_{wheel} = F_t \times v = 5031.9 \times 16.67 = 83{,}882 \text{ W} = 83.88 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 7 — Account for motor efficiency (motor must deliver more than wheel power):**

$$
P_{peak} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{motor}} = \frac{83.88}{0.90} = 93.2 \text{ kW}
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{P_{peak} \approx 93.2 \text{ kW}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Judgment:** A commercially available **100 kW** PMSM or **90 kW** AC induction motor would be selected. The slight over-rating provides a safety margin for transient loads, hot temperature derating, and battery voltage sag under high current.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy Consumption per km (kWh/km)

### Problem Setup

A converted EV (1400 kg) drives a **MIDC (Modified Indian Driving Cycle)** test. The cycle covers **8 km** in **800 seconds** (average speed 36 km/h, stops included). Motor draws average **8.5 kW** during driving, and auxiliaries (lights, BMS, controller) consume **200 W continuously**. **Regen** recovers 5% of drive energy.

**Find:** Net energy consumption in **kWh/km**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute drive energy (motor input):**

$$
E_{drive} = P_{motor} \times t = 8.5 \text{ kW} \times \frac{800}{3600} \text{ h}
$$

$$
E_{drive} = 8.5 \times 0.2222 = 1.889 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute auxiliary energy:**

$$
E_{aux} = P_{aux} \times t = 0.200 \text{ kW} \times 0.2222 \text{ h} = 0.0444 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute regen energy recovered:**

$$
E_{regen} = 0.05 \times E_{drive} = 0.05 \times 1.889 = 0.0945 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute net energy:**

$$
E_{net} = E_{drive} + E_{aux} - E_{regen}
$$

$$
E_{net} = 1.889 + 0.0444 - 0.0945 = 1.839 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute per-km consumption:**

$$
E_{cons} = \frac{E_{net}}{R} = \frac{1.839}{8} = 0.230 \text{ kWh/km}
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{E_{cons} \approx 0.230 \text{ kWh/km}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> A typical converted hatchback should target **0.12–0.20 kWh/km**. Higher values (like 0.23 above) indicate inefficient motor, regen not tuned, or excessive aux load. KTU questions often ask to identify the cause and suggest corrections.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix for EV Conversion

The following matrix helps in making a structured engineering decision for a typical conversion:

| Factor | Low Risk / Easy | Medium Risk | High Risk / Complex |
|--------|----------------|-------------|---------------------|
| Donor vehicle age | < 5 years | 5–10 years | > 15 years |
| Vehicle mass | < 1000 kg | 1000–1500 kg | > 1500 kg |
| Target range | < 100 km | 100–200 km | > 250 km |
| Battery placement | Floor pan available | Engine bay + boot | Cramped |
| Existing brakes | Disc front | Drum rear | Worn/drum all |
| Charging access | Home 15A socket | Dedicated wall box | Only DC fast |
| Budget | > ₹6 lakhs | ₹4–6 lakhs | < ₹4 lakhs |
| Regulatory path | Pre-approved kit | ARAI testing | Full homologation |

> [!WARNING]
> Always conduct a **full Factor × Consequence analysis** before signing off the conversion design. KTU case-study questions frequently test this trade-off reasoning.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions follow the **KTU 2024 End Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern. Model answers follow the **valuation key style**—concise, formula-first, with logical steps.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**List and briefly explain any FIVE critical factors to be considered when converting a conventional petrol car into a battery electric vehicle.**

**Model Answer (Valuation Key Style):**

1. **Vehicle Mass and Payload Capacity:** The original chassis is designed for ICE mass distribution. Battery addition (300–500 kg) requires checking GVW limits, axle loads, and suspension upgrades.

2. **Range Requirements:** Daily drive-cycle analysis determines battery energy (kWh) needed. Typical Indian retrofit targets 100–150 km for city use.

3. **Battery Pack Sizing and Chemistry:** Choice between LFP (safer, longer life) and NMC (higher energy density) affects cost, weight, thermal behavior, and cycle life.

4. **Electric Motor Selection:** Motor power, torque, and type (BLDC/PMSM/AC Induction) must match the target vehicle's performance requirements (top speed, gradeability, acceleration).

5. **Charging Infrastructure:** Compatibility with available grid supply (single-phase 230V home, or three-phase 15A for fast AC), and on-board charger (OBC) rating.

6. *(Optional 6th factor for extra credit):* **Regulatory compliance** with AIS-156, CMVR, and ARAI certification.

**Marking Scheme:** 1 mark per factor (name + 1-line explanation). Total 3 marks for any 3 factors fully explained.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]

**Explain the significance of weight distribution and center of gravity in EV conversion. How does battery placement affect vehicle dynamics?**

**Model Answer:**

Weight distribution and center of gravity (CoG) location are critical safety and performance parameters in any EV conversion.

**Significance:**
- **Stability:** A lower CoG (batteries placed in the floor pan) reduces body roll during cornering, improving handling and reducing rollover risk.
- **Braking:** Uneven weight distribution can cause premature lock-up of lightly loaded wheels; ABS tuning is affected.
- **Steering Response:** Polar moment of inertia decreases when mass is concentrated near the center, improving agility.

**Battery Placement Effects:**
- **Floor Pan Placement:** Best dynamic outcome; CoG drops 30–50 mm; optimal front-rear balance (50:50 ideal).
- **Engine Bay Replacement:** Easy to install, but raises CoG; worsens handling.
- **Boot/Rear Placement:** May over-load rear axle; risk of oversteer; common in 4WD conversions.
- **Distributed Placement:** Ideal but requires complex BMS wiring and structural reinforcement.

**Marking Scheme:** 2 marks for significance, 1 mark for placement effects. Use one labeled diagram for full marks.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Choice Instruction:** *Answer the following question. Choice is permitted within the question.*

---

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** A 1300 kg hatchback (including the proposed battery pack) is to be converted to an EV. Calculate the **peak motor power** required to achieve the following performance:
- Top speed: **100 km/h** on flat road
- Gradeability: **20% gradient at 40 km/h sustained**
- Acceleration: **0–60 km/h in 8 seconds**

Assume $C_r = 0.014$, $C_d = 0.32$, $A_f = 2.0 \text{ m}^2$, $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\eta_{motor} = 0.92$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **battery placement strategies** in EV conversion with their pros and cons. Which strategy would you recommend for a front-engine, front-wheel-drive (FF) car being converted to an EV, and why? *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution (a) — Peak Motor Power

**Step 1 — Convert velocities to SI units:**

$$
v_{top} = \frac{100 \times 1000}{3600} = 27.78 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
v_{grade} = \frac{40 \times 1000}{3600} = 11.11 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate acceleration:**

$$
a = \frac{27.78 - 0}{8} = 3.47 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Grade angle for 20% slope:**

$$
\theta = \arctan(0.20) = 11.31°
$$

**Step 4 — Compute all tractive force components (using worst case: top speed + acceleration):**

Rolling resistance:

$$
F_{roll} = 1300 \times 9.81 \times 0.014 = 178.5 \text{ N}
$$

Aerodynamic drag at top speed:

$$
F_{aero} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.32 \times 2.0 \times (27.78)^2 = 302.4 \text{ N}
$$

Acceleration force:

$$
F_{acc} = 1300 \times 3.47 = 4514.0 \text{ N}
$$

(Grade resistance = 0 for top speed on flat road)

**Step 5 — Sum tractive force:**

$$
F_t = 178.5 + 302.4 + 4514.0 = 4994.9 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 6 — Wheel power:**

$$
P_{wheel} = 4994.9 \times 27.78 = 138{,}759 \text{ W} = 138.76 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 7 — Motor power (with efficiency):**

$$
P_{peak} = \frac{138.76}{0.92} = 150.8 \text{ kW}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{P_{peak} \approx 151 \text{ kW}}$

**Marking Scheme:** Each force component correctly calculated = 1 mark; total force and power = 1 mark each. Arithmetics must match within ±2%.

---
