| CNN classification | Hand-crafted features fail on complex pictograms; CNN learns hierarchical features | Low accuracy on warning signs |
| Tracking | A single frame may mis-classify; temporal voting boosts confidence | Sign flicker on display |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | RGB → Hue | $H = \begin{cases} 60^\circ \cdot \dfrac{G-B}{\Delta} \mod 360, & \text{if } \max = R \\ 60^\circ \cdot \left(2 + \dfrac{B-R}{\Delta}\right), & \text{if } \max = G \\ 60^\circ \cdot \left(4 + \dfrac{R-G}{\Delta}\right), & \text{if } \max = B \end{cases}$ | $\Delta = \max(R,G,B) - \min(R,G,B)$ |
| 2 | 2-D Gaussian Filter | $G(x,y) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sigma_x\sigma_y}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{x^2}{2\sigma_x^2}-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right)$ | $\sigma$ = std. deviation (px) |
| 3 | Hough Circle Parametric Form | $(x - a)^2 + (y - b)^2 = r^2$ | $(a,b)$ = centre, $r$ = radius (px) |
| 4 | Canny Edge Gradient Magnitude | $\vert \nabla I \vert = \sqrt{I_x^2 + I_y^2}$ | $I_x, I_y$ = partial derivatives |
| 5 | Intersection-over-Union (IoU) | $\text{IoU} = \dfrac{\vert B_p \cap B_{gt} \vert}{\vert B_p \cup B_{gt} \vert}$ | $B_p$ = predicted, $B_{gt}$ = ground-truth box |
| 6 | Softmax (classification) | $P(y_i) = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K}e^{z_j}}$ | $K$ = number of sign classes |
| 7 | CNN Convolution Output Size | $O = \dfrac{W - K + 2P}{S} + 1$ | $W$ = input, $K$ = kernel, $P$ = padding, $S$ = stride |
| 8 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $L = -\sum_{i=1}^{K} y_i \log(\hat{y_i})$ | $y_i$ = true label (one-hot) |
| 9 | Pin-Hole Camera Projection | $x = f\dfrac{X}{Z}, \quad y = f\dfrac{Y}{Z}$ | $f$ = focal length (mm) |
| 10 | Vehicle Stopping Distance | $d_s = v\,t_r + \dfrac{v^2}{2\,g\,\mu}$ | $v$ (m/s), $t_r$ = reaction time (s), $\mu$ = friction coeff. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** When writing the IoU formula in a table, **always** use `\vert` instead of `\|`. Vertical pipes in raw markdown will break the table renderer. Examiners will deduct marks if the formula in your answer sheet is mangled.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ADAS Layer:** Provides semantic context (e.g., "60 km/h zone") to ACC → enables *predictive* speed adaptation.
- **HD-Map Validation:** Cross-checks on-board HD maps for sign updates.
- **Insurance Telematics:** Records driver compliance (e.g., ignoring STOP signs).
- **Fleet Management:** Commercial trucks use TSR logs for driver-scorecards.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — RGB to Hue (H) Channel

The RGB-to-HSV conversion is the single most-cited derivation in KTU vision questions. We start from normalized RGB values $R',G',B' \in [0,1]$.

**Step 1.** Compute the maximum and minimum chromaticity:

$$C_{\max} = \max(R',G',B'), \quad C_{\min} = \min(R',G',B')$$

**Step 2.** Compute the chromatic-difference (delta):

$$\Delta = C_{\max} - C_{\min}$$

**Step 3.** Branch on which channel is the maximum:

$$
H = 
\begin{cases}
0^\circ & \text{if } \Delta = 0 \\
60^\circ \cdot \dfrac{G' - B'}{\Delta} \bmod 360 & \text{if } C_{\max} = R' \\
60^\circ \cdot \left(2 + \dfrac{B' - R'}{\Delta}\right) & \text{if } C_{\max} = G' \\
60^\circ \cdot \left(4 + \dfrac{R' - G'}{\Delta}\right) & \text{if } C_{\max} = B'
\end{cases}
$$

This is exactly the formula listed in row 1 of the cheat sheet, derived from the geometric definition of hue as the angle of the colour vector in the chromaticity plane.

**Engineering meaning:** For a red "STOP" sign, $C_{\max} = R'$, so we use the first branch and the resulting $H$ falls in $[0^\circ, 60^\circ]$ (or wraps to near $360^\circ$ for deep red), allowing us to threshold a narrow Hue band — e.g., $H \in [340^\circ, 360^\circ] \cup [0^\circ, 20^\circ]$ — to segment *all* red signs irrespective of brightness.

### 3.2 Derivation B — Intersection-over-Union (IoU)

This metric is fundamental for evaluating sign-localization performance.

**Step 1.** Let $B_p = (x_p, y_p, w_p, h_p)$ be the predicted bounding box and $B_{gt}$ be the ground-truth box.

**Step 2.** Compute intersection coordinates:

$$x_{i1} = \max(x_p, x_{gt}), \quad y_{i1} = \max(y_p, y_{gt})$$
$$x_{i2} = \min(x_p + w_p,\; x_{gt} + w_{gt}), \quad y_{i2} = \min(y_p + h_p,\; y_{gt} + h_{gt})$$

**Step 3.** Intersection area:

$$A_{\cap} = \max(0, x_{i2} - x_{i1}) \cdot \max(0, y_{i2} - y_{i1})$$

**Step 4.** Union area (inclusion–exclusion):

$$A_{\cup} = w_p h_p + w_{gt} h_{gt} - A_{\cap}$$

**Step 5.** Final IoU scalar:

$$\text{IoU} = \dfrac{A_{\cap}}{A_{\cup}}$$

A perfect overlap gives IoU = 1; disjoint boxes give IoU = 0. Most TSR systems set the detection threshold at **IoU ≥ 0.5**.

### 3.3 Derivation C — CNN Output-Size for Classification Head

Given a feature map of width $W$, kernel size $K$, padding $P$ and stride $S$, derive $O$.

**Step 1.** The kernel slides over the input. The number of valid starting positions equals the total input width minus the kernel width plus twice the padding (since padding is added on both sides), divided by stride, plus 1.

**Step 2.** Formally:

$$O = \left\lfloor \dfrac{W - K + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1$$

**Step 3.** Worked example — input $W = 32$, $K = 5$, $P = 2$, $S = 1$:

$$O = \dfrac{32 - 5 + 2(2)}{1} + 1 = \dfrac{31}{1} + 1 = 32$$

So with `"same"` padding the spatial dimension is preserved.

**Step 4.** Worked example — $W = 32$, $K = 2$, $S = 2$, $P = 0$ (max-pool style):

$$O = \dfrac{32 - 2 + 0}{2} + 1 = \dfrac{30}{2} + 1 = 15 + 1 = 16$$

This downsamples by 2, which is exactly the behaviour of a 2 × 2 max-pool with stride 2.

### 3.4 Derivation D — Pin-Hole Projection of a Sign onto the Image Plane

A sign of real height $H$ at distance $D$ subtends an image-plane size of:

$$h_{\text{image}} = f \cdot \dfrac{H}{D}$$

**Step 1.** Consider a 600 mm STOP sign at 50 m with a camera of focal length $f = 12$ mm:

$$h_{\text{image}} = 12\,\text{mm} \cdot \dfrac{0.6\,\text{m}}{50\,\text{m}} = 0.144\,\text{mm}$$

**Step 2.** On a 1/3-inch CMOS sensor (pixel pitch ≈ 2.2 µm):

$$h_{\text{pixels}} = \dfrac{0.144\,\text{mm}}{2.2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{mm}} \approx 65\,\text{px}$$

This is why **T signs must subtend at least ~30–40 pixels** to be classified reliably — a sizing constraint that defines the **maximum operating range** of the TSR system.

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Method | Strength | Weakness | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| Color Thresholding (HSV) | Fast, simple | Fails on faded signs | Quick red/blue detection |
| Hough Transform | Robust shape | Costly for large images | Circle/triangle localization |
| Template Matching | Deterministic | Sensitive to rotation/scale | Reference sign library |
| SVM (HOG features) | Good accuracy | Needs hand-crafted features | Mid-2010s production systems |
| CNN (LeNet, VGG) | High accuracy, scale-invariant | Needs GPU/large dataset | Modern TSR (post-2018) |
| YOLO / SSD | Real-time end-to-end | Requires huge labelled set | Premium ADAS platforms |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a TSR System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    camA[Front Camera CMOS] --> preA[Image Pre-Processing]
    preA --> roiA[ROI Extraction and Colour Conversion to HSV]
    roiA --> detA[Sign Detection Module]
    detA --> clsA[Sign Classification CNN]
    clsA --> trkA[Temporal Tracking and Kalman Filter]
    trkA --> hmiA[HMI Driver Display and CAN Bus Output]
    detA --> dbgA[Diagnostic Buffer]
    clsA --> dbgA
    dbgA --> obdA[OBD-II Telemetry Link]
    classDef camClass fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef algoClass fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef outClass fill:#d62728,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef dbgClass fill:#9467bd,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    class camA camClass
    class preA,roiA,detA,clsA,trkA algoClass
    class hmiA outClass
    class dbgA,obdA dbgClass
```

### 4.2 CNN Feature-Pyramid for Sign Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inp1[Input Image 64x64x3] --> cv1[Conv 5x5 32 Filters ReLU]
    cv1 --> mp1[MaxPool 2x2 Stride 2]
    mp1 --> cv2[Conv 5x5 64 Filters ReLU]
    cv2 --> mp2[MaxPool 2x2 Stride 2]
    mp2 --> fl1[Flatten Layer]
    fl1 --> fc1[Dense 256 ReLU]
    fc1 --> fc2[Dense 128 ReLU]
    fc2 --> sm1[Softmax 50 Classes]
    sm1 --> out1[Predicted Sign Label]
    classDef layerClass fill:#17becf,stroke:#fff,color:#000
    class inp1,out1 layerClass
    class cv1,cv2,mp1,mp2,fl1,fc1,fc2,sm1 layerClass
```

### 4.3 State Machine for Sign Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> S0_NoSign
    S0_NoSign --> S1_Candidate: Detection confidence above threshold
    S1_Candidate --> S2_Confirmed: Confidence sustained over N frames
    S2_Confirmed --> S3_Displayed: Transmit to HMI via CAN
    S3_Displayed --> S4_Exited: Sign leaves camera FOV
    S4_Exited --> S0_NoSign
    S1_Candidate --> S0_NoSign: Confidence drops
    S2_Confirmed --> S0_NoSign: Mismatch in tracker
```

### 4.4 Vehicle-Level Integration Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cam1[Front Camera] --> ecu1[ADAS ECU TSR Processor]
    ecu1 --> can1[CAN-FD Bus 500 kbps]
    can1 --> ic1[Instrument Cluster]
    can1 --> hud1[Head-Up Display]
    can1 --> acc1[Adaptive Cruise Control ECU]
    can1 --> avm1[Map Fusion Module]
    classDef hwClass fill:#bcbd22,stroke:#fff,color:#000
    classDef swClass fill:#e377c2,stroke:#fff,color:#000
    class cam1,can1,ic1,hud1 hwClass
    class ecu1,acc1,avm1 swClass
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define Traffic Sign Recognition (TSR). List any four classes of traffic signs recognised by a typical TSR system.

**Model Answer:**
TSR is an ADAS feature that uses a forward-facing camera and computer-vision algorithms to detect, classify, and inform the driver about road signs in real time.
Four classes of signs typically recognised:

1. **Speed-limit signs** (circular, red border, numeric)
2. **Prohibitory signs** (circular, e.g., "No Entry")
3. **Warning signs** (triangular, red border, e.g., curve ahead)
4. **Mandatory signs** (blue circle, white arrow)

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for correct list with 1-line description.)*

---

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Why is the HSV colour space preferred over the RGB colour space for sign detection?

**Model Answer:**
HSV *decouples chromaticity (Hue) from illumination (Value)*. Therefore a red sign in shadow (low V) and a red sign in sunlight (high V) both share the same Hue band, enabling a single threshold to detect both. In RGB, the same red sign has very different R, G, B values under varying light, requiring adaptive thresholds and producing more false negatives.
*(3 marks: 2 for the decoupling logic + 1 for practical implication.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **KTU Pattern (ESE):** Answer *one* of the two questions. Each question carries **7 + 7 = 14 marks**. Sub-part (a) is typically conceptual/diagrammatic, and sub-part (b) is analytical/derivation.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete functional pipeline of a TSR system. Discuss the role of temporal tracking in eliminating flicker on the driver display.

**Model Answer:**

*Block diagram:*
*(Refer to Section 4.1 — Image Acquisition → Pre-processing → ROI/HSV → Detection → Classification → Tracking → HMI/CAN.)*

*Explanation of each block:*

1. **Image Acquisition:** A monocular CMOS camera behind the windshield captures 25–60 fps RGB frames.
2. **Pre-processing:** Gaussian filtering reduces sensor noise; lens correction removes radial distortion.
3. **ROI Extraction:** Sky and bonnet regions are masked out to reduce compute.
4. **HSV Conversion:** RGB is converted to HSV for illumination-robust colour thresholding.
5. **Sign Detection:** Hough Transform or sliding-window CNN proposes candidate regions.
6. **Classification:** A CNN with 3–5 conv layers assigns a class label and confidence.
7. **Temporal Tracking:** A Kalman Filter maintains the sign identity across frames.

*Role of Tracking in eliminating flicker:*
A single frame may produce a noisy confidence score due to partial occlusion, sun-glare, or motion blur. The tracking module uses the **Hungarian algorithm** to associate detections across frames and applies a **majority-vote / confidence-decay rule** over N consecutive frames. The sign is displayed only when its confidence exceeds 0.9 for, say, 5 successive frames, and is removed only when it has been *un-detected* for 15 frames. This hysteresis prevents the cluster from rapidly toggling between "60 km/h" and "no sign", which would otherwise confuse the driver.

*(7 marks: 2 diagram, 3 block-wise explanation, 2 tracking logic.)*

**(b) [7 Marks]** A circular speed-limit sign of diameter 0.6 m is observed by a vehicle-mounted camera with focal length 8 mm at a distance of 40 m. The sensor pixel-pitch is 2.0 µm. Determine:
  (i) the image-plane diameter of the sign in mm, and
  (ii) the equivalent diameter in pixels.
  (iii) Comment on whether this is sufficient for reliable CNN classification.

**Model Answer:**

*Given:* $H = D = 0.6$ m, $f = 8$ mm, $D_{\text{range}} = 40$ m, pixel-pitch $p = 2.0$ µm $= 2.0 \times 10^{-3}$ mm.

**(i) Image-plane diameter:**

$$h_{\text{image}} = f \cdot \dfrac{H}{D_{\text{range}}} = 8\,\text{mm} \cdot \dfrac{0.6\,\text{m}}{40\,\text{m}} = 8 \cdot 0.015 = 0.12\,\text{mm}$$

**(ii) Pixel diameter:**

$$h_{\text{px}} = \dfrac{0.12\,\text{mm}}{2.0 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{mm}} = 60\,\text{px}$$

**(iii) Sufficiency for CNN classification:**
A 60-pixel diameter on the sign disk translates to a bounding box of roughly 70 × 70 pixels. Typical TSR CNNs require a minimum of **~30 pixels on the discriminative region** to extract reliable features. Hence, 60 pixels is **comfortably sufficient** for classification at this distance.
*(7 marks: 2 setup, 2 calculation part (i), 2 calculation part (ii), 1 conclusion.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare colour-based thresholding, Hough-Transform shape detection, and CNN-based detection methods for traffic signs. Mention one real production vehicle for each approach.

**Model Answer:**

| Method | Principle | Strengths | Weaknesses | Production Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Colour Thresholding (HSV) | Pixels in a Hue/Sat range are marked | Fast, hardware-cheap, deterministic | Sensitive to faded signs, shadows | Early Mobileye EyeQ1 (≈2008) |
| Hough-Transform | Maps edges into parametric space to find circles/triangles | Shape-robust, works on greyscale | High compute, edge sensitivity | Bosch second-generation TSR (2012) |
| CNN / Deep Learning | Learns hierarchical features from labelled data | Best accuracy, scale/rotation invariant | Needs GPU and large dataset | Tesla HW3 / Mobileye EyeQ5 (post-2020) |

*(7 marks: 2 table structure, 3 method comparison, 1 example per method, 1 conclusion.)*

**(b) [7 Marks]** A TSR CNN has a feature-map of size $W = 64$ and a convolution kernel of size $K = 7$ with padding $P = 3$ and stride $S = 1$. Compute the output feature-map size $O$. If the next layer applies a 2 × 2 max-pool with stride 2, what is the size after pooling?

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Convolution output size:**

$$O_1 = \left\lfloor \dfrac{W - K + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1 = \dfrac{64 - 7 + 2(3)}{1} + 1$$

$$O_1 = \dfrac{64 - 7 + 6}{1} + 1 = \dfrac{63}{1} + 1 = 64$$

**Step 2 — Max-pool with $K = 2$, $S = 2$, $P = 0$:**

$$O_2 = \dfrac{64 - 2 + 0}{2} + 1 = \dfrac{62}{2} + 1 = 31 + 1 = 32$$

**Result:** The feature map remains $64 \times 64$ after the convolution (because the "same-style" padding of 3 preserves dimension) and is downsampled to $32 \times 32$ after the max-pool.
*(7 marks: 2 setup, 2 computation, 2 second step, 1 final interpretation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Forgetting to convert pixel-pitch from µm to mm in projection problems — leading to a 1000× error in pixel count. Always write units in **every** line of the calculation.
> 2. **IoU vs Dice Confusion:** Students often write $\dfrac{B_p \cap B_{gt}}{B_p \cup B_{gt}}$ correctly but then **incorrectly** say it is the Dice coefficient. IoU and Dice differ by a factor of 2 in the numerator when expressed equivalently.
> 3. **Sign-Category Misnaming:** "Prohibitory" and "Mandatory" signs are *both* circular — students often interchange them. **Prohibitory = red border (e.g., No Entry); Mandatory = blue circle (e.g., Turn Left).**
> 4. **Hough Transform for Triangles:** Most students wrongly state that Hough detects "any polygon". In practice, a separate Hough is needed for each shape family (line, circle, ellipse). A 3-sided polygon is detected via three line-pairs, not a single transform.
> 5. **Skipping the Block Diagram:** A 7-mark question that asks "with a neat block diagram" gives **2 of the 7 marks** for the diagram alone. Drawing a text-only list instead of a real labelled block diagram is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **TSR = ADAS module** that detects, classifies, and displays road signs.
- [ ] Six pipeline stages: **Acquire → Pre-process → Detect → Classify → Track → Output**.
- [ ] Forward-facing **CMOS camera** behind the rear-view mirror is the primary sensor.
- [ ] **HSV > RGB** for sign detection because it decouples hue from illumination.
- [ ] **Hough Transform** is used for **shape-based** localization (circle/triangle).
- [ ] **CNN** (Conv → Pool → Conv → Pool → FC → Softmax) is the dominant **classifier**.
- [ ] **IoU ≥ 0.5** is the standard detection-acceptance threshold.
- [ ] **Pin-hole projection:** $h_{\text{image}} = f \cdot H / D$.
- [ ] **Output-size formula:** $O = \lfloor (W - K + 2P) / S \rfloor + 1$.
- [ ] **Temporal tracking** (Kalman Filter) eliminates flicker via N-frame voting and hysteresis.
- [ ] Output is published on **CAN-FD (500 kbps)** to the **instrument cluster / HUD / ACC ECU**.
- [ ] **ASIL-B** functional safety classification (ISO 26262).
- [ ] Detection range **0–250 m**, latency **≤ 100 ms**, frame rate **25–60 fps**.
- [ ] Three main sign categories: **Prohibitory (red circle), Warning (red triangle), Mandatory (blue circle)**, plus **STOP (red octagon)** and **Informational (rectangle)**.
- [ ] **Softmax** converts CNN logits into class probabilities; **Cross-Entropy** is the loss function.
- [ ] HSV formula: branch on $C_{\max} = R, G,$ or $B$; $\Delta = C_{\max} - C_{\min}$.
- [ ] TSR enables **Speed-Limit Assist (SLA)**, **Predictive ACC**, and **HD-Map validation**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Road Recognition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET414 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Road Recognition # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Road Recognition** is a sub-system of Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) and autonomous vehicle perception stacks that uses onboard sensors — primarily **monocular/stereo cameras**, supplemented by **LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging)** and **millimeter-wave Radar** — to identify, classify, and continuously track drivable road surfaces, lane boundaries, road geometry, road signs, and road surface conditions in real time. The output of the road recognition module is a structured semantic representation (a road model) consumed by downstream modules such as lane-keeping, adaptive cruise control, path planning, and emergency braking. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEIET414 / Module 3):** > Road Recognition encompasses the perception algorithms that allow a vehicle to **detect lanes**, **classify road type** (urban / highway / off-road), **read traffic signs**, and **estimate free drivable space** (often called *Free Space Detection* or *Drivable Area Segmentation*). ## 1.2 Core Sub-Tasks of Road Recognition A production-grade Road Recognition system performs the following four sub-tasks concurrently: | # | Sub-Task | Output Type | Typical Algorithm Family | |---|----------|-------------|--------------------------| | 1 | **Lane Detection** | Polynomial lane curves | Edge detection + Hough / Deep CNN segmentation | | 2 | **Road Surface Segmentation** | Pixel-wise drivable map | Semantic Segmentation (U-Net, DeepLab) | | 3 | **Traffic Sign Recognition (TSR)** | Class + confidence bounding box | CNN classifiers (YOLO, SSD, ResNet) | | 4 | **Road Condition Estimation** | Wet / Dry / Snow / Damaged | Texture analysis + multimodal sensor fusion | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Eye vs Car Camera" Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuition:** Imagine teaching a toddler to recognize roads. You would not start with calculus; you would point and say, "See the **yellow line on the road**? That is the edge of our driving lane." Now imagine a car is that toddler. It uses a **front-facing camera** as its "eye," and the Road Recognition module acts as the **parent's brain** that constantly whispers: *"Lane on left detected at 8 meters, lane on right detected at 8.2 meters, free space ahead, sign ahead says 60 km/h."* The four sub-tasks above are simply the four "instructions" the brain keeps giving the toddler-car. ## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics in Road Recognition The following engineering metrics quantify how "well" a road recognition system performs. These are universally used in KTU/SAE/ISO automotive literature: > [!NOTE] > **Standard Performance Metrics (must memorize for KTU):** > - **Accuracy (A)** — percentage of correct predictions. > - **Precision (P)** — out of all detected roads/lanes, how many were actually correct. > - **Recall (R)** — out of all actual roads/lanes, how many did we detect. > - **F1-Score** — harmonic mean of Precision and Recall, $F_1 = \dfrac{2PR}{P+R}$. > - **Intersection over Union (IoU / Jaccard Index)** — overlap between predicted and ground-truth segmentation mask, $\text{IoU} = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$. > - **Mean Average Precision (mAP)** — average of AP across all classes; standard for object/sign detection. All metrics are reported on a **0–1** (dimensionless) scale, with **1.0** representing perfect performance. Industry benchmarks for lane detection demand **F1 ≥ 0.95** at **>30 FPS** on embedded automotive SoCs (System on Chips) such as NVIDIA Orin or Qualcomm Snapdragon Ride. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Road Recognition Perception Pipeline Every road recognition algorithm, whether classical (computer-vision) or modern (deep-learning), follows a **5-stage functional pipeline**: 1. **Image Acquisition** — A monocular or stereo camera captures RGB frames at typically **30 Hz** (HD, 1280×720 px). 2. **Pre-processing** — Noise reduction, lens distortion correction, color space conversion (RGB → HSV / Grayscale), histogram equalization. 3. **Feature Extraction** — Edges (Canny, Sobel), corners (Harris), or learned deep features (CNN feature maps). 4. **Detection / Segmentation / Classification** — Hough Transform, polynomial fitting, semantic segmentation CNN, or object detection network. 5. **Temporal Filtering & Tracking** — Kalman Filter, Extended Kalman Filter (EKF), or SORT/DeepSORT for multi-frame consistency. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Why" Behind Each Stage:** > - *Pre-processing* removes sensor noise that would otherwise create false lane edges. > - *Feature extraction* converts millions of pixel values into compact, meaningful numerical descriptors. > - *Tracking* is what separates a *one-frame guess* from a *reliable automotive system* — a single misdetection must not disengage the autopilot. ## 2.2 The Pinhole Camera Model — Foundation of All Camera-Based Road Recognition A camera is mathematically modeled as a **pinhole**, projecting 3D world points $(X, Y, Z)$ onto a 2D image plane $(u, v)$. This is the geometric foundation for **Inverse Perspective Mapping (IPM)** — the technique that "flattens" the road into a bird's-eye view for easier lane detection. $$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}_{\text{Intrinsic Matrix } K} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} R & \vert & t \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ where: - $s$ is a non-zero scale factor (projective depth). - $f_x, f_y$ are the **focal lengths** in pixel units along the x and y axes. - $(c_x, c_y)$ is the **principal point** (image center). - $R$ is a $3 \times 3$ rotation matrix and $t$ a $3 \times 1$ translation vector — together forming the **extrinsic matrix** $\begin{bmatrix} R & \vert & t \end{bmatrix}$ describing camera pose relative to the vehicle frame. ## 2.3 Hough Transform for Lane Line Detection The Hough Transform converts the *Cartesian edge problem* (find a line $y = mx + c$ from edge pixels) into a *voting problem* in **Hough space** $(\rho, \theta)$: $$\rho = x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta$$ where: - $\rho$ = perpendicular distance of the line from the image origin (pixels). - $\theta$ = angle of the perpendicular (radians, $0 \le \theta < \pi$). - Each edge pixel $(x, y)$ votes for **all $(\rho, \theta)$ pairs** it could belong to. Local maxima in the $(\rho, \theta)$ accumulator array correspond to dominant straight lines in the image — typically the left and right lane edges. ## 2.4 Lane Polynomial Fitting Real roads curve. Once lane pixels are extracted, they are fitted to a **2nd-order (quadratic) or 3rd-order (cubic) polynomial** in the bird's-eye view (BEV) plane: $$x = a \, y^2 + b \, y + c \quad \text{(Quadratic, used in lane detection)}$$ $$x = a \, y^3 + b \, y^2 + c \, y + d \quad \text{(Cubic, used in path prediction on highways)}$$ where $x$ is the lateral offset from the lane center (meters) and $y$ is the longitudinal distance ahead of the vehicle (meters). ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Quick Reference) > [!NOTE] > **The table below is what the KTU examiner expects you to reproduce in Part-A/Part-B derivations.** | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Engineering Use | |---|-------------------|------------|----------------| | 1 | Pinhole Projection | $s u = K \cdot \begin{bmatrix} R & \vert & t \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Camera calibration, IPM | | 2 | Hough Transform | $\rho = x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta$ | Line / lane detection | | 3 | Gradient Magnitude (Canny) | $G = \sqrt{G_x^2 + G_y^2}$ | Edge detection | | 4 | Gradient Direction | $\theta_g = \arctan\left(\dfrac{G_y}{G_x}\right)$ | Non-maximum suppression | | 5 | Quadratic Lane Model | $x = a y^2 + b y + c$ | Lane curve fitting | | 6 | IoU (Jaccard) | $\text{IoU} = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Segmentation evaluation | | 7 | F1-Score | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 P R}{P + R}$ | Detection evaluation | | 8 | Precision | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Detection evaluation | | 9 | Recall | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Detection evaluation | | 10 | Camera Field of View (FoV) | $\text{FoV} = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{d}{2 f}\right)$ | Lens selection | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Constants to Remember:** > - Pi (π) ≈ **3.14159** for $\theta$ conversions. > - Standard automotive camera frame rate: **30 Hz / 60 Hz**. > - Typical focal length for a forward ADAS camera: **f ≈ 6 mm to 12 mm**. > - Acceptable lane detection latency: **< 100 ms** (SAE J3061 safety target). ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Road Recognition is **not a research curiosity** — it is mandated or strongly encouraged in global NCAP (New Car Assessment Program) safety ratings: - **Euro NCAP 2024** awards points for *Lane Support Systems*, *Speed Assist Systems*, and *Road Sign Recognition*. - **India's Bharat NCAP (2024+)** incorporates ADAS-based lane and sign awareness scoring. - **UNECE R157** (Automated Lane Keeping Systems) **legally requires** a validated road/lane recognition module before any Level-3 automated vehicle can be type-approved in 60+ countries. Thus, a graduate engineer in automotive electronics/embedded systems who masters Road Recognition becomes directly employable in **ADAS development teams** at Tata Elxsi, Bosch India, Continental, Wipro-Engineering, and Mercedes-Benz R&D Bangalore. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > This section is the **derivation heart** of the chapter. Every algebraic transition is expanded. KTU examiners reward students who can reproduce these derivations step-by-step, especially in 14-mark Part-B questions. --- ## 3.1 Derivation: From Pinhole Model to Inverse Perspective Mapping (IPM) **Goal:** Show mathematically how a road point $(X, Y, Z)$ in vehicle coordinates maps to bird's-eye-view coordinates $(x_{bev}, y_{bev})$ after IPM — the cornerstone transformation used in every lane detection pipeline. ### Step 1 — Define the Vehicle Coordinate Frame Let the origin of the world (vehicle) frame be at the camera's optical center, with: - $X$ = right direction (m), - $Y$ = forward direction (m, along the road), - $Z$ = down direction (m, into the road). The **road surface** is the plane $Z = -h$, where $h$ is the **camera mounting height** above the ground (typically $h \approx 1.2$ m for a sedan). ### Step 2 — Apply the Pinhole Projection For a road point $(X, Y, -h)$, the image coordinates are: $$u = f_x \cdot \dfrac{X}{Y} + c_x, \quad v = f_y \cdot \dfrac{-h}{Y} + c_y$$ We use the standard automotive convention where the camera looks along $+Y$, so a point *on the ground at distance $Y$ ahead* projects to $v$ based purely on the height drop $-h/Y$. ### Step 3 — Solve for World Coordinates $(X, Y)$ from Image $(u, v)$ We algebraically invert Step 2. From the $u$ equation: $$X = \dfrac{(u - c_x) \cdot Y}{f_x}$$ Substitute into the $v$ equation: $$v - c_y = -\dfrac{f_y \, h}{Y}$$ Solving for $Y$: $$Y = -\dfrac{f_y \, h}{v - c_y}$$ Substituting $Y$ back into the $X$ equation: $$X = \dfrac{(u - c_x)}{f_x} \cdot \left( -\dfrac{f_y \, h}{v - c_y} \right)$$ ### Step 4 — Define Bird's-Eye-View Coordinates For IPM, we define BEV coordinates centered at the camera's projection on the ground: $$x_{bev} = X, \quad y_{bev} = Y$$ This gives the **final IPM mapping equations**: $$\begin{aligned} y_{bev} &= -\dfrac{f_y \, h}{v - c_y} \\ x_{bev} &= -\dfrac{f_x \, h \cdot (u - c_x)}{f_y \cdot (v - c_y)} \end{aligned}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Interpretation:** Notice that the IPM equations produce $y_{bev} \to \infty$ as $(v - c_y) \to 0$. This is the **horizon line** of the image — points near the horizon map to infinite distance. In production systems, IPM is **only applied for $v > v_{horizon}$** (lower half of the image), which is where the road actually lies. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Hough Transform for a Single Line **Goal:** Given two edge pixels $P_1 = (x_1, y_1)$ and $P_2 = (x_2, y_2)$, derive the line in Hough space. ### Step 1 — Start with the Normal Form Every line in the image can be represented as: $$x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta = \rho$$ ### Step 2 — Treat $\rho$ as a Function of $\theta$ For a single point $(x_i, y_i)$, the set of all lines passing through it traces a **sinusoidal curve** in $(\rho, \theta)$ space: $$\rho(\theta) = x_i \cos\theta + y_i \sin\theta$$ ### Step 3 — Intersect Two Sinusoids For two points $P_1$ and $P_2$ lying on the *same* straight line, the two sinusoids **intersect at exactly one point** $(\rho^*, \theta^*)$: $$\begin{aligned} \rho^* &= x_1 \cos\theta^* + y_1 \sin\theta^* \\ \rho^* &= x_2 \cos\theta^* + y_2 \sin\theta^* \end{aligned}$$ Subtracting the two equations: $$0 = (x_1 - x_2)\cos\theta^* + (y_1 - y_2)\sin\theta^*$$ Solving for $\theta^*$: $$\tan\theta^* = -\dfrac{x_1 - x_2}{y_1 - y_2}$$ This is the **negative reciprocal** of the image-plane line slope, which is mathematically consistent with $\theta$ being the normal's angle (perpendicular to the line). ### Step 4 — Find $\rho^*$ $$\rho^* = x_1 \cos\theta^* + y_1 \sin\theta^*$$ This $(\rho^*, \theta^*)$ pair is the **single dominant vote** in the Hough accumulator and represents the detected line. > [!NOTE] > **Algorithmic Step (for the KTU answer script):** Discretize $\theta$ into $N$ values (typically $N = 180$ for $0 \le \theta < \pi$). For each edge pixel, compute $\rho$ for every $\theta$, round to the nearest bin, and **increment the accumulator**. The bins with the highest counts correspond to the detected lane lines. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Mean Average Precision (mAP) for Sign Detection **Goal:** Show how the standard object-detection metric mAP is computed for a traffic-sign recognition system. ### Step 1 — Single-Class Average Precision For a single class $c$ (e.g., "Stop Sign"), sort all model predictions by **descending confidence score**. Compute Precision $P(r)$ and Recall $R(r)$ at each rank $r$: $$P(r) = \dfrac{\text{TP}(r)}{\text{TP}(r) + \text{FP}(r)}, \quad R(r) = \dfrac{\text{TP}(r)}{\text{Total Positives in Class } c}$$ A prediction is a **True Positive (TP)** if $\text{IoU} \ge 0.5$ with a ground-truth box, else a **False Positive (FP)**. ### Step 2 — Interpolated Precision For each recall level $r$, take the **maximum precision at any recall $\ge r$**: $$P_{\text{interp}}(r) = \max_{r' \,:\, r' \ge r} P(r')$$ ### Step 3 — Numerical Integration (11-Point or Area-Under-Curve) $$\text{AP}_c = \dfrac{1}{11} \sum_{i=0}^{10} P_{\text{interp}}\!\left(\dfrac{i}{10}\right) \quad \text{(VOC 11-Point Method)}$$ or equivalently, the **area under the PR curve**: $$\text{AP}_c = \int_0^1 P_{\text{interp}}(r) \, dr$$ ### Step 4 — Mean Across All Classes For $C$ traffic sign classes: $$\text{mAP} = \dfrac{1}{C} \sum_{c=1}^{C} \text{AP}_c$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why mAP Matters in KTU Problems:** mAP @ IoU=0.5 is the de-facto metric reported in IEEE ITSC (Intelligent Transportation Systems) papers on road sign recognition. A "production-ready" sign detector must achieve **mAP $\ge$ 0.90** to be considered for ADAS integration. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Selection Guide for KTU Exam > [!NOTE] > This matrix is the **cheat-sheet** for selecting the right algorithm given a KTU Part-B question. Memorize the "When to use" column. | Road Recognition Sub-Task | Recommended Algorithm | Sensor Input | Pros | Cons | When to Use (KTU hint) | |---------------------------|----------------------|--------------|------|------|------------------------| | Lane Line Detection (straight highways) | Hough Transform + Polynomial Fit | Mono Camera | Computationally cheap, interpretable | Fails on worn / occluded lanes | "Detect straight lane markings" | | Lane Curve Estimation | Quadratic / Cubic Polynomial Regression | Mono Camera + IPM | Smooth, sub-pixel accurate | Sensitive to outliers | "Estimate the lane curve radius" | | Free Space / Drivable Area | Semantic Segmentation (U-Net, DeepLabV3+) | Mono/Stereo Camera | Pixel-level precision | GPU-heavy | "Identify drivable region in image" | | Traffic Sign Detection | YOLOv8 / Faster R-CNN | Mono Camera | High accuracy, real-time | Needs large labeled dataset | "Detect and classify traffic signs" | | 3D Road Geometry | LiDAR + Camera Fusion | LiDAR + Camera | Robust in low light | Sensor cost, calibration | "Estimate road slope and banking" | | Road Condition (wet/dry) | Texture + Polarization Analysis | Mono Camera + Polarizer | Non-contact | Weather-dependent | "Detect wet road surfaces" | | Night Road Recognition | NIR (Near-Infrared) Camera + CNN | NIR Camera | Works in darkness | Limited color cues | "Recognize lane at night" | --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Practice) **Problem:** A forward ADAS camera has focal length $f_x = 1200$ px, principal point $c_x = 640$ px, and is mounted at height $h = 1.2$ m. For an image point at $u = 700$ px (corresponding to a lane marking), find the lateral offset $x_{bev}$ at longitudinal distance $y_{bev} = 20$ m ahead of the vehicle. Assume $f_y = f_x$ for simplicity and that $v$ corresponding to $y_{bev} = 20$ m is given by the pinhole projection. ### Solution **Step 1:** Compute $v$ from the pinhole projection: $$v = c_y - \dfrac{f_y \, h}{Y} = 360 - \dfrac{1200 \times 1.2}{20} = 360 - 72 = 288 \text{ px}$$ (Assuming image size 1280×720, $c_y = 360$.) **Step 2:** Compute $x_{bev}$ using the IPM equation: $$x_{bev} = -\dfrac{f_x \, h \, (u - c_x)}{f_y \, (v - c_y)} = -\dfrac{1200 \times 1.2 \times (700 - 640)}{1200 \times (288 - 360)}$$ **Step 3:** Numerator = $1440 \times 60 = 86400$. Denominator = $1200 \times (-72) = -86400$. **Step 4:** Result: $$x_{bev} = -\dfrac{86400}{-86400} = 1.0 \text{ m}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Answer:** The detected lane marking is **1.0 m to the right of the vehicle centerline** at a longitudinal distance of **20 m ahead**. This is consistent with a standard highway lane width of 3.5 m where the right lane edge lies ~1.75 m from the center; the 1.0 m result suggests the vehicle is offset within its own lane — exactly the kind of information a Lane Keeping Assist (LKA) system needs to apply corrective steering. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > The Mermaid diagrams below follow the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguards: alphanumeric node IDs (no reserved keywords), no markdown formatting inside node labels, and clear functional flow. ## 4.1 High-Level Road Recognition Architecture (Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph TD A1[Camera Sensor
30 Hz RGB Capture] --> A2[Pre-Processing
Distortion Correction
RGB to Grayscale] A2 --> A3[Feature Extraction
Canny Edges / CNN Features] A3 --> A4{Algorithm Selector} A4 --> A5[Lane Detection Module
Hough Transform + IPM] A4 --> A6[Road Segmentation Module
U-Net DeepLab] A4 --> A7[Sign Recognition Module
YOLOv8 Detector] A5 --> A8[Temporal Tracker
Kalman Filter] A6 --> A8 A7 --> A8 A8 --> A9[Output Road Model
to Lane Keeping ACC AEB] A10[LiDAR Sensor
Optional Fusion] --> A8 A11[Vehicle CAN Bus
Speed Yaw Input] --> A8 ``` ## 4.2 Pinhole Camera + IPM Functional Flow ```mermaid graph LR B1[World Point XYZ on Road] --> B2[Camera Extrinsics
R and t from Calibration] B2 --> B3[Camera Intrinsics K
fx fy cx cy] B3 --> B4[Image Point u v on Pixel Grid] B4 --> B5[Invert Projection Algebraically] B5 --> B6[Solve for Y Longitudinal] B5 --> B7[Solve for X Lateral] B6 --> B8[BEV Coordinate xbev ybev] B7 --> B8 B8 --> B9[Lane Curve Fitting
Polynomial Regression] ``` ## 4.3 Hough Transform Voting Process (Conceptual) ```mermaid graph TD C1[Input Image
1280 by 720] --> C2[Edge Detection
Canny Operator] C2 --> C3[Each Edge Pixel
xi yi] C3 --> C4[Loop over Theta 0 to Pi
Step Size 1 Degree] C4 --> C5[Compute Rho
xi cos theta plus yi sin theta] C5 --> C6[Increment Accumulator
A rho theta] C6 --> C4 C4 --> C7[Find Local Maxima
in Accumulator] C7 --> C8[Output Detected Lines
rho star theta star] ``` ## 4.4 Road Recognition Output Fusion Architecture ```mermaid graph TD D1[Lane Module Output
Left Right Polynomials] --> D4[Central Road Model] D2[Segmentation Output
Drivable Pixel Mask] --> D4 D3[Sign Output
Class and Confidence] --> D4 D4 --> D5[Path Planner
Trajectory Generation] D4 --> D6[Lane Departure Warning
LDW] D4 --> D7[Adaptive Cruise Control
ACC] D4 --> D8[Autonomous Emergency Braking
AEB] ``` ## 4.5 Algorithm Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid graph TD S1{What is the
Recognition Task?} --> S2[Lane Marking] S1 --> S3[Drivable Area] S1 --> S4[Traffic Sign] S1 --> S5[Road Condition] S2 --> T1{Curved or
Straight?} T1 -->|Straight| T2[Use Hough Transform] T1 -->|Curved| T3[Use IPM plus
Polynomial Fit] S3 --> U1[Use Semantic
Segmentation CNN] S4 --> V1[Use YOLO or
Faster RCNN] S5 --> W1[Use Texture plus
Polarization] ``` ## 4.6 Sensor Modalities and Their Coverage Zones ```mermaid graph LR F1[Front Facing Mono Camera
Range 0 to 80 m
FoV 50 degrees] --> F4[Primary Road
Recognition Sensor] F2[Stereo Camera
Range 0 to 40 m
Depth via Disparity] --> F4 F3[Surround View Fisheye
4 Cameras 360 deg] --> F5[Parking and
Low Speed Use] F6[Front LiDAR
Range 0 to 200 m
360 deg Spinning] --> F7[Long Range
Free Space] F8[Radar 77 GHz
Range 0 to 250 m] --> F9[Object Detection
Complements Camera] ``` > [!TIP] > **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU 14-Mark Answers:** Always label the *direction of data flow* clearly using arrow notation, and *group related blocks* in your hand-drawn answer scripts. The examiners award partial credit (often 2 marks) for a *well-labeled* block diagram even if the algorithm description is incomplete. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > All questions are designed per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Evaluation)** pattern. Part-A = 3 marks, Part-B = 14 marks (with sub-parts of 7 + 7 marks). Model answers are step-by-step and exam-ready. --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model Paper)] **Define Road Recognition. List its four main sub-tasks.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Road Recognition is a perception module in ADAS/autonomous vehicles that uses onboard sensors (cameras, LiDAR, radar) to identify the drivable road surface, lane boundaries, traffic signs, and road surface conditions in real time. *(Definition — 1 mark)* The four main sub-tasks are: 1. **Lane Detection** — identifying left/right lane boundaries. 2. **Road Surface Segmentation** — pixel-level drivable area mask. 3. **Traffic Sign Recognition (TSR)** — detecting and classifying road signs. 4. **Road Condition Estimation** — classifying surface as wet/dry/snow/damaged. *(List — 2 marks)* --- ### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model Paper)] **State the Hough Transform equation. What does the parameter $\rho$ represent?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Hough Transform converts edge points from image space $(x, y)$ to parametric space $(\rho, \theta)$ using the equation: $$\rho = x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta$$ *(Equation — 2 marks)* $\rho$ represents the **perpendicular distance** (in pixels) of the line from the image origin, and $\theta$ is the **angle** (in radians) that this perpendicular makes with the x-axis. *(Meaning — 1 mark)* --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, you will be offered a **choice** between two 14-mark questions, each split into 7 + 7 sub-parts. Below are two complete alternatives. --- ### PART B — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model Paper)] **(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of a camera-based road recognition system**. Clearly explain the role of each block. **(b) [7 marks]** Derive the **Inverse Perspective Mapping (IPM) equations** for a forward-facing ADAS camera mounted at height $h$ above a flat road, with focal length $f$ and principal point $(c_x, c_y)$. Show how the equations are used to convert an image point $(u, v)$ into bird's-eye-view coordinates $(x_{bev}, y_{bev})$. #### Model Answer **(a) Functional Architecture [7 marks]** The camera-based road recognition system follows a **5-stage pipeline**: **Block 1 — Image Acquisition (1 mark):** A forward-facing CMOS camera (typically mounted near the rear-view mirror) captures RGB frames at 30 Hz with resolution 1280×720 or 1920×1080. The camera lens is characterized by intrinsic parameters $(f_x, f_y, c_x, c_y)$. **Block 2 — Pre-Processing (1.5 marks):** Lens distortion (radial and tangential) is corrected using calibration coefficients $k_1, k_2, k_3, p_1, p_2$. The image is optionally converted to grayscale or HSV color space. Histogram equalization enhances contrast in low-light conditions. **Block 3 — Feature Extraction (1.5 marks):** Classical pipelines use **Canny edge detection** (gradient computation, non-maximum suppression, hysteresis thresholding). Modern pipelines use **deep CNN feature maps** (e.g., ResNet-50 backbone for YOLOv8). **Block 4 — Detection / Segmentation (1.5 marks):** Edge pixels are passed through a **Hough Transform accumulator** to detect straight lane lines, then fitted to a quadratic polynomial. Alternatively, a **semantic segmentation network** (U-Net) outputs a per-pixel drivable area mask. **Block 5 — Temporal Tracking and Output (1.5 marks):** A **Kalman Filter** smooths the lane estimates over consecutive frames. The final road model (lane polynomials, drivable mask, detected signs) is published to downstream ADAS modules (LKA, ACC, AEB) via the vehicle **CAN bus** or **Automotive Ethernet**. **Neat Block Diagram (must be drawn in the answer script):** [Camera] → [Pre-Processing] → [Feature Extraction] → [Detection/Segmentation] → [Tracking] → [ADAS Output] **(b) IPM Derivation [7 marks]** *Step 1 (1 mark):* Define the vehicle coordinate system: $X$ = right, $Y$ = forward (along the optical axis), $Z$ = down. The road plane is at $Z = -h$ where $h$ is the camera mounting height. *Step 2 (1.5 marks):* The pinhole projection of a road point $(X, Y, -h)$ onto the image plane is: $$u = f_x \cdot \dfrac{X}{Y} + c_x, \quad v = f_y \cdot \dfrac{(-h)}{Y} + c_y$$ *Step 3 (1.5 marks):* Solving for $Y$ from the second equation: $$Y = y_{bev} = -\dfrac{f_y \, h}{v - c_y}$$ *Step 4 (1.5 marks):* Substituting back into the first equation and solving for $X$: $$X = x_{bev} = -\dfrac{f_x \, h \cdot (u - c_x)}{f_y \cdot (v - c_y)}$$ *Step 5 (1 mark):* Use of these equations in IPM is to **rectify** the perspective-foreshortened road into a top-down view, removing the effect of $Y$ in pixel coordinates. This makes **parallel lane lines remain parallel** in the BEV image, simplifying subsequent Hough / polynomial fitting operations. The IPM mapping is only valid for $v > v_{horizon}$ (i.e., below the horizon line of the image). --- ### PART B — Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model Paper)] **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Canny Edge Detection algorithm** in detail. Mention the role of gradient magnitude and non-maximum suppression. **(b) [7 marks]** With a suitable example, derive the **Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and IoU metrics** for evaluating a road sign recognition system. State the industry-acceptable threshold for F1 and mAP in production ADAS. #### Model Answer **(a) Canny Edge Detection [7 marks]** The Canny Edge Detector is a multi-stage algorithm widely used in classical lane detection pipelines. It has **5 stages** (1.4 marks each): **Stage 1 — Gaussian Smoothing:** A 2D Gaussian kernel $G(x, y) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{x^2 + y^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ is convolved with the input image to suppress high-frequency sensor noise. Typical $\sigma = 1.4$. **Stage 2 — Gradient Computation:** Sobel operators $G_x$ and $G_y$ are applied to compute the gradient in x and y directions. The gradient magnitude and direction are: $$G = \sqrt{G_x^2 + G_y^2}, \quad \theta_g = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{G_y}{G_x}\right)$$ **Stage 3 — Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS):** For each pixel, compare $G$ with its two neighbors along the gradient direction $\theta_g$. If $G$ is not the local maximum, suppress (zero out) that pixel. This **thins edges to one-pixel width**. **Stage 4 — Double Thresholding:** Pixels with $G \ge T_{high}$ are marked as **strong edges**, pixels with $T_{low} \le G < T_{high}$ are **weak edges**, and pixels with $G < T_{low}$ are suppressed. **Stage 5 — Edge Tracking by Hysteresis:** Weak edge pixels are retained **only if** they are connected (8-connected) to a strong edge pixel via a path of other weak edges. This eliminates spurious responses while preserving true edge continuity. *Role of gradient magnitude:* Acts as edge **strength indicator** — strong gradients correspond to genuine boundaries (lane markings) while weak gradients are noise. *(0.5 mark)* *Role of NMS:* Produces **one-pixel-thin** edges suitable for Hough Transform input. *(0.5 mark)* **(b) Detection Metrics [7 marks]** *Step 1 (1.5 marks):* Consider a traffic-sign recognition system with the following outputs on a test set of 100 images containing 80 stop signs: - True Positives (TP) = 70 (correctly detected stop signs with IoU $\ge$ 0.5). - False Positives (FP) = 5 (background regions incorrectly labeled as stop signs). - False Negatives (FN) = 10 (missed stop signs). *Step 2 (1.5 marks):* Compute **Precision** and **Recall**: $$P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP} = \dfrac{70}{70 + 5} = \dfrac{70}{75} = 0.933$$ $$R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} = \dfrac{70}{70 + 10} = \dfrac{70}{80} = 0.875$$ *Step 3 (1.5 marks):* Compute **F1-Score**: $$F_1 = \dfrac{2PR}{P + R} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.933 \times 0.875}{0.933 + 0.875} = \dfrac{1.633}{1.808} = 0.903$$ *Step 4 (1.5 marks):* **IoU Computation** for a single stop-sign prediction: Suppose the predicted bounding box has area 4500 px², the ground-truth box has area 5000 px², and their intersection area is 4000 px². $$\text{IoU} = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert} = \dfrac{4000}{4500 + 5000 - 4000} = \dfrac{4000}{5500} = 0.727$$ *Step 5 (1 mark):* **Industry thresholds (must memorize for KTU):** - Lane Detection: F1 $\ge$ **0.95**. - Traffic Sign Recognition: mAP@0.5 $\ge$ **0.90**. - Drivable Area Segmentation: mIoU $\ge$ **0.85**. - End-to-End Road Recognition Latency: **< 100 ms**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting units in IPM derivation:** Students often write $X = \dfrac{(u-c_x) \cdot Y}{f_x}$ without specifying that $Y$ is in meters and $f_x$ is in pixels. Always state units explicitly to gain full credit. > 2. **Confusing Precision and Recall:** Precision answers "how clean are my detections?" while Recall answers "how many did I find?" Examiners frequently deduct 1 mark for this swap. > 3. **Drawing the Hough accumulator as a 2D image without labels:** Always annotate the axes $(\rho, \theta)$ and show a clear peak corresponding to the dominant line. > 4. **Skipping the Hysteresis step in Canny:** It is a *5-stage* algorithm, not 4. Forgetting hysteresis loses 1 mark. > 5. **Conflating Canny with Sobel:** Sobel only computes the gradient; Canny is a complete edge detector with NMS, double-thresholding, and hysteresis. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **Road Recognition** is a perception module: 4 sub-tasks = Lane Detection + Drivable Segmentation + Sign Recognition + Condition Estimation. - [x] **Pinhole camera model** is the geometric foundation: $s \mathbf{u} = K \begin{bmatrix} R & \vert & t \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{X}_{world}$. - [x] **Hough Transform** equation: $\rho = x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta$, used to detect straight lane lines. - [x] **IPM equations** convert image pixels $(u, v)$ to bird's-eye-view $(x_{bev}, y_{bev})$ using camera height $h$ and focal length $f$. - [x] **Quadratic lane model:** $x = a y^2 + b y + c$ — must know this formula for Part-B derivations. - [x] **Canny Edge Detection** has **5 stages**: Gaussian smoothing → Sobel gradient → NMS → Double thresholding → Hysteresis. - [x] **Precision, Recall, F1, IoU, mAP** are the standard evaluation metrics — know formulas cold. - [x] **Industry thresholds:** Lane F1 $\ge$ 0.95, Sign mAP $\ge$ 0.90, Free-space mIoU $\ge$ 0.85. - [x] **Latency requirement:** < 100 ms per frame for safety-critical ADAS functions. - [x] **Sensor fusion:** Camera (primary) + LiDAR (long range) + Radar (long range in fog) is the production-grade ADAS stack. - [x] **Regulatory drivers:** Euro NCAP 2024, Bharat NCAP 2024+, UNECE R157 mandate validated road/lane recognition. - [x] **Frame rate:** Standard ADAS cameras operate at **30 Hz / 60 Hz** with HD resolution. - [x] **YOLO / Faster R-CNN** are the leading object-detection networks used for traffic sign recognition. - [x] **U-Net / DeepLab** are the leading semantic segmentation networks for drivable area detection. - [x] **Pinhole → IPM** is the single most examinable derivation in this module — practice writing it 3 times before the exam. - [x] Always state the **camera mounting height $h$** in IPM derivations; examiners specifically test whether students remember this constant. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute KTU Exam Strategy (30 minutes before the paper):** > 1. Memorize the 5 Canny stages in order. > 2. Write out the IPM derivation from memory once on rough paper. > 3. Re-write the Precision / Recall / F1 / IoU formulas in a small box. > 4. Recall three industry thresholds (0.95 / 0.90 / 0.85). > 5. Skim the block diagrams in Section 4 — you will likely be asked to draw one.

topics.title: Object Recognition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET414 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Object Recognition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Object Recognition (OR)** in the context of Modern Automotive Technologies is defined as a sub-field of computer vision and machine intelligence that enables an autonomous or semi-autonomous vehicle to **identify, localize, classify, and track** real-world entities (vehicles, pedestrians, traffic signs, lane markings, cyclists, animals, and static infrastructure) from sensor-acquired data streams such as camera images, LiDAR point clouds, and radar returns.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3):**
> Object Recognition is the process of detecting instances of semantic objects of a certain class (such as humans, cars, or buildings) in digital images and video sequences. In automotive engineering, it forms the **perception backbone** of Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) and Autonomous Driving (AD) stacks.

In strict machine-vision terminology, the task decomposes into three hierarchical sub-problems:

1. **Classification** – Assigning a class label to an entire image or region.
2. **Detection** – Localizing objects with bounding boxes and labels.
3. **Segmentation** – Pixel-level labeling (semantic / instance segmentation).

For KTU examinations, the three-tier hierarchy is mandatory terminology.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — "The Human Eye-Brain System":**
> Imagine a child crossing a busy road. A human driver instantly recognizes the child **not** because the driver calculates pixel intensities, but because the human brain has been trained on millions of visual examples. The driver's **retina** captures light (analogous to a **camera sensor**), the **visual cortex** extracts shapes and patterns (analogous to a **Convolutional Neural Network**), and the **prefrontal cortex** makes a driving decision (analogous to the **decision/planning module** of the ADAS stack). Object Recognition replicates this biological pipeline mathematically inside a vehicle's ECU.

| Biological System | Automotive Equivalent | Function |
|-------------------|----------------------|----------|
| Retina | CMOS Camera / LiDAR | Captures raw environmental data |
| Optic Nerve | CAN / Ethernet Bus | Transmits data to processor |
| Visual Cortex (V1–V5) | CNN Feature Extractor | Extracts hierarchical features |
| Inferotemporal Cortex | Classifier (Softmax) | Assigns object label |
| Motor Cortex | Actuator / Brake-by-Wire | Executes response |

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Automotive Object Recognition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following metrics are **high-frequency exam keywords** and must be memorized for KTU 2024 evaluations:
> - **mAP (mean Average Precision)** = standard accuracy benchmark for detection models.
> - **IoU (Intersection over Union)** = $\geq 0.5$ is the conventional detection threshold.
> - **FPS (Frames Per Second)** = real-time inference requires $\geq 30$ FPS.
> - **Precision & Recall** = core confusion-matrix-derived performance indices.
> - **Latency** = end-to-end delay, typically required to be **< 100 ms** for safety-critical ADAS functions.
> - **Range (R)** of object detection = typically **0 to 250 m** for automotive forward radar.
> - **Angular Resolution** = typically **< 1°** for forward-looking cameras.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline of Object Recognition

The end-to-end object recognition pipeline in an autonomous vehicle consists of **six sequential stages**:

1. **Data Acquisition (Sensing Layer)**
   - Camera (monocular/stereo) captures RGB images at 30–60 Hz.
   - LiDAR emits **905 nm** or **1550 nm** infrared laser pulses and records time-of-flight (ToF).
   - Radar emits **76–77 GHz** mm-waves (FMCW / Doppler).
   - Ultrasonic sensors emit **40–70 kHz** acoustic bursts for short-range (< 5 m) detection.

2. **Pre-processing**
   - Noise reduction using Gaussian or bilateral filters.
   - Image normalization: pixel scaling from $[0, 255]$ to $[0, 1]$.
   - Point-cloud voxelization for LiDAR data.

3. **Feature Extraction**
   - Traditional: HOG, SIFT, SURF, Haar-cascades.
   - Deep learning: Convolutional layers (Conv → ReLU → Pool).

4. **Object Detection / Classification**
   - Two-stage detectors: R-CNN, Fast R-CNN, Faster R-CNN.
   - Single-stage detectors: YOLO, SSD, RetinaNet.

5. **Tracking (Temporal Layer)**
   - Kalman Filter, Extended Kalman Filter (EKF), SORT, DeepSORT.

6. **Decision & Actuation**
   - Output fused with path planning; triggers AEB (Autonomous Emergency Braking), ACC (Adaptive Cruise Control), LKA (Lane Keeping Assist).

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why sensing?** Because a vehicle cannot recognize what it cannot "see"; sensors are the surrogate eyes.
- **Why pre-processing?** Raw sensor data contains thermal noise, motion blur, and varying illumination; clean input ensures stable inference.
- **Why feature extraction?** Because raw pixels are too high-dimensional; features compress the signal into discriminative descriptors.
- **Why deep learning?** Hand-crafted features (HOG/SIFT) fail under occlusion, weather variation, and pose changes; CNNs learn hierarchical invariance automatically.
- **Why tracking?** A single-frame detection is noisy; temporal consistency reduces jitter and handles temporary occlusions.
- **Why fusion?** Each sensor has blind spots — camera fails in fog, radar has low resolution, LiDAR struggles in heavy snow; **sensor fusion** yields robust perception.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description & Units |
|---------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Intersection over Union (IoU) | $\mathrm{IoU} = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | $A$ = predicted box, $B$ = ground truth; dimensionless in $[0,1]$ |
| Average Precision (AP) | $\mathrm{AP} = \int_{0}^{1} p(r)\,dr$ | Area under Precision-Recall curve |
| Mean Average Precision (mAP) | $\mathrm{mAP} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathrm{AP}_{i}$ | $N$ = number of object classes |
| Precision | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $TP$ = True Positives, $FP$ = False Positives |
| Recall | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $FN$ = False Negatives |
| F1-Score | $F_{1} = \dfrac{2PR}{P+R}$ | Harmonic mean of Precision & Recall |
| LiDAR Range Equation | $R = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | $c \approx 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s, $\Delta t$ = ToF |
| FMCW Radar Beat Frequency | $f_{b} = \dfrac{2 R \cdot S}{c}$ | $R$ = range, $S$ = chirp slope (Hz/s) |
| Radial Velocity (Doppler) | $v_{r} = \dfrac{f_{D} \cdot \lambda}{2}$ | $f_{D}$ = Doppler shift, $\lambda$ = wavelength |
| Camera Focal Projection | $\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{u} + \dfrac{1}{v}$ | $f$ = focal length, $u$ = object distance, $v$ = image distance |
| Pinhole Image Coordinate | $x = f \cdot \dfrac{X}{Z}$ | $(X,Y,Z)$ world → $(x,y)$ image |
| Kalman Predict (State) | $\hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} = A\hat{x}_{k-1} + Bu_{k}$ | $A$ = state transition matrix |
| Kalman Update (Gain) | $K_{k} = P_{k\vert k-1} H^{T}(HP_{k\vert k-1}H^{T}+R)^{-1}$ | $H$ = measurement, $R$ = noise covariance |

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU Formula Sheet, **$\vert \cdot \vert$** denotes set/magnitude cardinality and must never be confused with the markdown table pipe character. All $\vert$ symbols in equations are LaTeX-rendered.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ADAS Safety Functions:** AEB, ACC, BSD (Blind Spot Detection), FCW (Forward Collision Warning), TSR (Traffic Sign Recognition), LDW (Lane Departure Warning).
- **Autonomy Levels (SAE J3016):** Object recognition is mandatory from **Level 2 onwards**.
- **Industry Deployments:** Tesla Autopilot (vision-based), Waymo (LiDAR-camera fusion), Mobileye EyeQ series, NVIDIA Drive PX / Orin platforms.
- **Smart Cities:** V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) infrastructure shares recognized objects with roadside units.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the IoU Metric (Detection Localization Accuracy)

The Intersection over Union is mathematically derived as the Jaccard similarity coefficient between two rectangular regions.

**Step 1 — Define the two regions.**
Let $A$ be the predicted bounding box and $B$ be the ground-truth bounding box emitted by the human annotator.

**Step 2 — Express the area of the predicted box.**
A bounding box is described by its top-left $(x_{1}^{A}, y_{1}^{A})$ and bottom-right $(x_{2}^{A}, y_{2}^{A})$ pixel coordinates. Therefore:

$$
A_{\mathrm{pred}} = (x_{2}^{A} - x_{1}^{A}) \cdot (y_{2}^{A} - y_{1}^{A})
$$

**Step 3 — Express the area of the ground-truth box.**

$$
A_{\mathrm{gt}} = (x_{2}^{B} - x_{1}^{B}) \cdot (y_{2}^{B} - y_{1}^{B})
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the intersection rectangle.**
The intersection exists only if the boxes overlap. The intersection coordinates are bounded by the **inner-most** edges:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{1}^{I} &= \max(x_{1}^{A}, x_{1}^{B}) \\
y_{1}^{I} &= \max(y_{1}^{A}, y_{1}^{B}) \\
x_{2}^{I} &= \min(x_{2}^{A}, x_{2}^{B}) \\
y_{2}^{I} &= \min(y_{2}^{A}, y_{2}^{B})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute intersection area.**
The intersection area exists only if width and height are both positive:

$$
A_{\mathrm{int}} = \max(0,\; x_{2}^{I} - x_{1}^{I}) \cdot \max(0,\; y_{2}^{I} - y_{1}^{I})
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the union area using set theory.**
The inclusion-exclusion principle states that for two finite sets, the cardinality of the union equals the sum of the cardinalities minus the cardinality of the intersection:

$$
A_{\mathrm{union}} = A_{\mathrm{pred}} + A_{\mathrm{gt}} - A_{\mathrm{int}}
$$

**Step 7 — Final IoU formula.**

$$
\mathrm{IoU} = \frac{A_{\mathrm{int}}}{A_{\mathrm{union}}}
$$

A detection is considered a **True Positive** if $\mathrm{IoU} \geq 0.5$ for the PASCAL VOC benchmark and $\mathrm{IoU} \geq 0.5$ for MS-COCO (with additional mAP averages computed at $\mathrm{IoU} \in \{0.5, 0.55, \ldots, 0.95\}$).

## 3.2 Derivation of LiDAR Time-of-Flight Range

A LiDAR unit emits a laser pulse, which travels to a target and reflects back. The round-trip propagation time is $\Delta t$.

**Step 1 — Total distance traveled by light.**
Since light travels at constant speed $c$:

$$
d_{\mathrm{total}} = c \cdot \Delta t
$$

**Step 2 — Account for the round trip.**
The light travels to the object and back, hence the actual one-way range is half the total distance:

$$
R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical constants.**

$$
R = \frac{3 \times 10^{8} \cdot \Delta t}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Worked example.**
For a target at $R = 100$ m:

$$
\Delta t = \frac{2R}{c} = \frac{2 \cdot 100}{3 \times 10^{8}} = 6.67 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} = 667 \text{ ns}
$$

This ultra-short interval explains why LiDAR requires **picosecond-accurate timing electronics**.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Pinhole Camera Projection Model

A 3D world point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ projects onto a 2D image plane through the camera's optical center $O$.

**Step 1 — Similar triangles relationship.**
Using the principle of similar triangles from classical optics:

$$
\frac{x}{f} = \frac{X}{Z} \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{y}{f} = \frac{Y}{Z}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for image coordinates.**

$$
x = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z} \qquad y = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}
$$

**Step 3 — Incorporate principal point offset $(c_{x}, c_{y})$ and focal length in pixels $(f_{x}, f_{y})$.**

$$
u = f_{x} \cdot \frac{X}{Z} + c_{x}
$$
$$
v = f_{y} \cdot \frac{Y}{Z} + c_{y}
$$

**Step 4 — Homogeneous coordinate form (for matrix manipulation).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &= \frac{1}{Z} \begin{bmatrix} f_{x} & 0 & c_{x} \\ 0 & f_{y} & c_{y} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{bmatrix} \\
\lambda \mathbf{x} &= \mathbf{K} \, \mathbf{X}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\mathbf{K}$ is the **camera intrinsic matrix** — a fundamental KTU exam concept.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Detector Family Comparison

| Detector | Type | Speed (FPS) | mAP (COCO) | Strength | Weakness |
|----------|------|-------------|------------|----------|----------|
| R-CNN | Two-stage | $\approx 0.05$ | 66 % | High accuracy | Very slow |
| Fast R-CNN | Two-stage | $\approx 0.5$ | 70 % | End-to-end train | Region proposal bottleneck |
| Faster R-CNN | Two-stage | $\approx 7$ | 78 % | RPN integration | Still not real-time |
| YOLOv3 | Single-stage | $\approx 30$ | 57.9 % | Real-time | Lower small-object accuracy |
| YOLOv5 | Single-stage | $\approx 140$ | 63 % | Production-ready | Anchor-based |
| YOLOv8 | Single-stage (anchor-free) | $\approx 165$ | 68 % | State-of-the-art speed | Hardware-intensive |
| SSD300 | Single-stage | $\approx 22$ | 74 % | Multi-scale | Lower accuracy on tiny objects |
| RetinaNet | Single-stage | $\approx 10$ | 80 % | Focal Loss | Slower than YOLO |
| DETR | Transformer | $\approx 12$ | 84 % | End-to-end set prediction | Long training time |

> [!NOTE]
> For real-time **automotive** deployment (30 FPS minimum), the **YOLO family** and **SSD** dominate; for offline HD-map annotation, **Faster R-CNN** is preferred.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Automotive Object Recognition

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **end-to-end flow** of an automotive object recognition subsystem, from raw sensor capture to final actuator command. This is the canonical diagram expected in KTU 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SEN["SENSING LAYER"]
        A1["Front Camera 1280x720 60FPS"]
        A2["LiDAR 905nm 64-Channel"]
        A3["Radar 77GHz FMCW"]
        A4["Ultrasonic 50kHz"]
    end

    subgraph PRE["PRE-PROCESSING LAYER"]
        B1["Image Denoising Gaussian"]
        B2["Point Cloud Voxelization"]
        B3["Radar CFAR Detection"]
        B4["Echo Filtering"]
    end

    subgraph FE["FEATURE EXTRACTION LAYER"]
        C1["CNN Backbone ResNet50"]
        C2["PointNet LiDAR Encoder"]
        C3["FFT Doppler Feature"]
    end

    subgraph OD["OBJECT DETECTION LAYER"]
        D1["YOLOv8 Detector"]
        D2["PointPillars 3D Detector"]
        D3["Sensor Fusion Tracker"]
    end

    subgraph POST["POST-PROCESSING LAYER"]
        E1["NMS Non-Max Suppression"]
        E2["Kalman Filter Tracking"]
        E3["Object List Builder"]
    end

    subgraph DEC["DECISION LAYER"]
        F1["Risk Assessment TTC"]
        F2["Path Planning AEB ACC LKA"]
        F3["CAN Bus Actuator Command"]
    end

    A1 --> B1 --> C1 --> D1 --> E1
    A2 --> B2 --> C2 --> D2 --> E1
    A3 --> B3 --> C3 --> D3
    A4 --> B4 --> D3
    D1 --> E1
    D2 --> E1
    E1 --> E2 --> E3
    D3 --> E2
    E3 --> F1 --> F2 --> F3
```

## 4.2 YOLO Single-Stage Detection Pipeline

The YOLO (You Only Look Once) framework processes an image in a single forward pass through a CNN, dividing it into an $S \times S$ grid where each cell predicts $B$ bounding boxes, a confidence score, and $C$ class probabilities.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IMG["Input Image 416x416x3"] --> RES["ResNet Backbone"]
    RES --> FPN["FPN Feature Pyramid"]
    FPN --> HEAD1["Detection Head 52x52"]
    FPN --> HEAD2["Detection Head 26x26"]
    FPN --> HEAD3["Detection Head 13x13"]
    HEAD1 --> DEC["Decoded Predictions"]
    HEAD2 --> DEC
    HEAD3 --> DEC
    DEC --> NMS["NMS Filter"]
    NMS --> OUT["Bounding Boxes plus Class Labels"]
```

## 4.3 Sensor Fusion Data-Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    C["Camera Stream"] --> SFU["Sensor Fusion Unit ECU"]
    L["LiDAR Stream"] --> SFU
    R["Radar Stream"] --> SFU
    U["Ultrasonic Stream"] --> SFU
    IMU["IMU Odometry"] --> SFU
    GPS["GNSS GPS"] --> SFU

    SFU --> EKF["Extended Kalman Filter"]
    EKF --> TRACK["Multi-Object Tracker SORT"]
    TRACK --> PLAN["Behavior Planner"]
    PLAN --> CTRL["Vehicle Controller"]
    CTRL --> ACT["Brake Steer Throttle Actuators"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The diagrams above are **Mermaid block-functional schematics** rather than photorealistic renderings. KTU examiners award full credit for clearly labelled, well-structured block diagrams showing signal/data flow direction.

## 4.4 Taxonomy of Object Recognition Methods

```mermaid

    OR --> T1["Traditional Hand-Crafted"]
    OR --> T2["Deep Learning Based"]

    T1 --> S1["HOG plus SVM"]
```
