```mermaid
    T1 --> S3["Haar Cascade"]
    T1 --> S4["Template Matching"]

    T2 --> S5["Two-Stage Detectors"]
    T2 --> S6["Single-Stage Detectors"]
    T2 --> S7["Transformer Based"]

    S5 --> R1["R-CNN Family"]
    S6 --> R2["YOLO SSD RetinaNet"]
    S7 --> R3["DETR ViT Based"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Q1. Define Object Recognition. List any four sensors used in automotive object recognition systems. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
Object Recognition is the computational process of identifying and localizing semantic entities such as vehicles, pedestrians, and traffic signs within sensor data acquired by an autonomous or ADAS-equipped vehicle. The four primary sensors used are:
1. **Camera (RGB / Stereo)** – provides high-resolution color and texture data.
2. **LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging)** – emits infrared laser pulses for 3D point-cloud generation.
3. **Radar (Radio Detection and Ranging)** – uses 77 GHz mm-waves for range and velocity.
4. **Ultrasonic Sensor** – emits 40–70 kHz acoustic pulses for short-range parking assistance.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### Q2. What is the role of Intersection over Union (IoU) in evaluating object detection models? (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
Intersection over Union (IoU) quantifies the spatial overlap between a predicted bounding box and the ground-truth box, normalized to the range $[0, 1]$. Mathematically:

$$
\mathrm{IoU} = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}
$$

A detection is considered a **True Positive** when $\mathrm{IoU} \geq 0.5$ (PASCAL VOC threshold). IoU directly measures localization accuracy and forms the basis for computing Average Precision (AP) and mean Average Precision (mAP) benchmarks.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3 Internal Choice Pattern]**

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**(a)** Explain the hierarchical taxonomy of object recognition tasks in computer vision. Compare image classification, object detection, and semantic segmentation with suitable examples from an automotive context. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the working principle of the YOLO (You Only Look Once) object detection algorithm. Illustrate the grid-cell prediction mechanism and explain the role of Non-Max Suppression. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

The three-tier hierarchy of visual recognition is:

1. **Image Classification:** Assigns a single class label to an entire image.
   - *Automotive example:* Classifying an input image as containing a "Stop Sign" (yes/no).
   - Output: a probability vector over $C$ classes.

2. **Object Detection:** Localizes all object instances with bounding boxes $(x, y, w, h)$ and assigns class labels.
   - *Automotive example:* Detecting all pedestrians in a street scene with bounding boxes.
   - Output: a set of tuples $\{(x_{i}, y_{i}, w_{i}, h_{i}, c_{i}, s_{i})\}$.

3. **Semantic Segmentation:** Assigns a class label to every pixel in the image.
   - *Automotive example:* Generating a pixel-level drivable-area map for autonomous path planning.
   - Output: a per-pixel class map of size $H \times W$.

A fourth variant, **Instance Segmentation** (e.g., Mask R-CNN), combines detection and segmentation by assigning distinct instance IDs to each object.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

YOLO reframes object detection as a **single regression problem** from image pixels to bounding-box coordinates and class probabilities. The pipeline:

1. **Input Resize:** Image resized to $448 \times 448$ (YOLOv1) or $640 \times 640$ (modern variants).
2. **Grid Division:** Image is divided into an $S \times S$ grid (e.g., $13 \times 13$ in YOLOv3).
3. **Cell Predictions:** Each grid cell predicts $B$ bounding boxes, each with:
   - 4 box coordinates $(x, y, w, h)$
   - 1 confidence score
   - $C$ class probabilities

4. **CNN Backbone:** A single CNN (Darknet-53 in YOLOv3) processes the image in one forward pass.
5. **Tensor Output:** The final tensor has shape $S \times S \times (B \cdot 5 + C)$.
6. **Non-Max Suppression (NMS):** Eliminates redundant overlapping boxes:
   - Discard boxes with confidence $< 0.5$.
   - Among overlapping boxes ($\mathrm{IoU} > 0.5$), retain only the highest-confidence box.

**Advantage:** Real-time inference at 30–165 FPS.
**Limitation:** Lower accuracy on small or densely packed objects compared to two-stage detectors.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of an automotive object recognition system. Explain the function of each block in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain sensor fusion in autonomous vehicles. Compare the strengths and limitations of camera, LiDAR, and radar sensors, and justify why fusion is necessary. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

Refer to the **Block Diagram in Section 4.1** of these notes (Sensing → Pre-processing → Feature Extraction → Detection → Post-processing → Decision). The function of each block is:

- **Sensing Layer:** Acquires raw data from heterogeneous sensors.
- **Pre-processing Layer:** Removes noise, normalizes pixel intensities, voxelizes point clouds.
- **Feature Extraction Layer:** CNN encoders (ResNet, Darknet) distill high-dimensional inputs into compact feature maps.
- **Object Detection Layer:** Single-stage (YOLO/SSD) or two-stage (Faster R-CNN) detectors predict bounding boxes and labels.
- **Post-processing Layer:** Applies NMS, runs Kalman Filter for temporal tracking, and produces a stable object list.
- **Decision Layer:** Calculates Time-To-Collision (TTC), selects a behavioral response (brake, steer, accelerate), and issues actuator commands over the CAN bus.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Sensor Comparison Table:**

| Property | Camera | LiDAR | Radar |
|----------|--------|-------|-------|
| Output | 2D RGB image | 3D point cloud | Range + velocity |
| Range | $\leq 250$ m | $\leq 200$ m | $\leq 300$ m |
| Resolution | High (pixels) | Medium (points) | Low (cells) |
| Weather Robustness | Poor in fog/rain | Poor in heavy snow | Excellent in all weather |
| Night Performance | Poor (needs IR) | Good | Excellent |
| Cost | Low | High | Medium |
| Color/Texture | Yes | No | No |
| Velocity Measurement | Indirect (optical flow) | Indirect | Direct (Doppler) |

**Why Fusion is Necessary:** No single sensor provides complete perception. Cameras offer semantic richness but fail in adverse weather; LiDAR delivers accurate 3D geometry but is expensive and struggles in fog; radar excels in all-weather velocity sensing but has low spatial resolution. **Sensor fusion** combines complementary strengths through algorithms such as the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**, **Centralized Fusion**, or **Deep Learning-based late fusion**, yielding a robust unified perception output. Modern platforms like the **NVIDIA Drive Orin** and **Mobileye EyeQ5** perform multi-sensor fusion at the chip level.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse Classification with Detection** — they are different tasks with different outputs. Classification outputs a single label; Detection outputs bounding boxes plus labels.
> 2. **Always state the IoU threshold explicitly** (typically $0.5$) when defining a True Positive; many students omit it and lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Use correct LaTeX symbols** ($\vert \cdot \vert$ for cardinality) in formulas. Do NOT mix markdown table pipes with absolute value bars.
> 4. **Distinguish R-CNN, Fast R-CNN, and Faster R-CNN** — students frequently interchange them. Remember: only Faster R-CNN uses a Region Proposal Network (RPN).
> 5. **Always include units** ($m, m/s, FPS, ms$) in numerical answers; KTU examiners specifically deduct for missing units.
> 6. **Block diagrams must show arrow directions** indicating data flow; unidirectional arrows from sensor to processor to actuator are mandatory.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3 / Object Recognition**

- **Core Definition:** Object Recognition = Classification + Localization + Tracking of entities in sensor data.
- **Sensors:** Camera (vision), LiDAR (3D geometry), Radar (velocity/range), Ultrasonic (short range), IMU/GPS (ego-pose).
- **LiDAR Range Formula:** $R = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$, where $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s.
- **Radar Doppler Velocity:** $v_{r} = \dfrac{f_{D} \cdot \lambda}{2}$.
- **Camera Pinhole Model:** $x = f \cdot \dfrac{X}{Z}$, $y = f \cdot \dfrac{Y}{Z}$.
- **IoU Definition:** $\mathrm{IoU} = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$, threshold $= 0.5$.
- **Precision:** $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$; **Recall:** $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$.
- **F1-Score:** $F_{1} = \dfrac{2PR}{P+R}$ (harmonic mean).
- **mAP Formula:** $\mathrm{mAP} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathrm{AP}_{i}$.
- **Two-Stage Detectors:** R-CNN, Fast R-CNN, Faster R-CNN — high accuracy, slow.
- **Single-Stage Detectors:** YOLO, SSD, RetinaNet — real-time, slightly lower accuracy.
- **Tracking Algorithms:** Kalman Filter, Extended Kalman Filter, SORT, DeepSORT.
- **Sensor Fusion Goal:** Combine complementary strengths; overcome weather/lighting/occlusion limits.
- **Real-Time Constraint:** $\geq 30$ FPS for ADAS; $< 100$ ms end-to-end latency.
- **SAE Autonomy Levels:** Object recognition is mandatory from **Level 2** onwards.
- **Key Industrial Platforms:** Tesla HW3/HW4 (vision-centric), Waymo (LiDAR-camera), Mobileye EyeQ, NVIDIA Drive Orin.
- **Common Applications:** AEB, ACC, FCW, BSD, TSR, LDW, LKA, and full Level-4 robotaxis.
- **Pipeline Order:** Sense → Pre-process → Feature Extract → Detect → Track → Decide → Actuate.
- **NMS Purpose:** Eliminate redundant overlapping boxes; keep the highest-confidence detection.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Connected Vehicles and IoT (Basics): Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET414 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Connected Vehicles and IoT (Basics): Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) Communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) Communication** is a short-range, wireless, ad-hoc networking paradigm in which automobiles continuously exchange standardised, low-latency messages (such as *Basic Safety Messages / Cooperative Awareness Messages*) containing kinematic state data (position, speed, heading, acceleration) and road event notifications with surrounding vehicles operating in the **5.9 GHz** Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS) band.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):**
> "V2V is a domain of *Cooperative Intelligent Transport Systems (C-ITS)* wherein On-Board Units (OBUs) autonomously broadcast and receive *Vehicle Status Messages* to enhance situational awareness, enable collision avoidance, and support autonomous driving — *without dependence on cellular infrastructure*."

The communication can be categorised under the broader umbrella of **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything)**, which includes V2V, V2I (Vehicle-to-Infrastructure), V2P (Vehicle-to-Pedestrian), and V2N (Vehicle-to-Network).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Convoy on the Highway"

Imagine a group of friends driving together on a long highway trip. Instead of relying only on their eyes, each driver continuously shouts out: *"I'm at the 20 km milestone, going 80 km/h, braking mildly."* The drivers behind instantly know the leader's intent and can react **before** they can physically see the brake lights.

**V2V is the digital, standardised, and machine-interpretable version of that "shout."** The vehicles are the "friends," the radio signal is the "shout," and the standard message format is the "common language" they all understand.

| Human Convoy Analogy | V2V Technical Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Driver's eyes | On-Board Sensors (Radar, Lidar, Camera) |
| Driver shouting position | V2V Transmitter (OBU + DSRC/C-V2X radio) |
| Listener's ears | V2V Receiver (Antenna + radio chipset) |
| Spoken language | SAE J2945 / ETSI EN 302 637 standard |
| Hearing delay | Radio propagation latency (milliseconds) |

### 1.3 Key Physical & Communication Metrics

The following metrics are **board-exam favourites** and must be memorised:

> [!NOTE]
> - **Operating Frequency Band:** **5.85 – 5.925 GHz** (allocated globally for ITS by the FCC / ETSI)
> - **Channel Bandwidth:** **10 MHz** (USA legacy) or **20 MHz** (C-V2X / 5G-NR-V2X)
> - **Effective Communication Range:** **~300 m** (line-of-sight, urban)
> - **Maximum Theoretical Range:** **~1000 m** (highway, free-space)
> - **Message Update Rate:** **10 Hz** (BSM broadcast — 10 times every second)
> - **End-to-End Latency Target:** **≤ 100 ms** (for safety-critical braking warning)
> - **Data Rate (DSRC):** **3 – 27 Mbps**
> - **Data Rate (C-V2X):** **Up to 1 Gbps** (5G sidelink)
> - **Modulation (DSRC):** **OFDM** with BPSK / QPSK / 16-QAM / 64-QAM

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic — *How* V2V Works (Step-by-Step)

The V2V pipeline can be decomposed into **five logical layers**. Examiners frequently test the "list-and-explain" approach.

**Step 1 — Sensing & Localisation (Layer 1: Perception)**
The vehicle uses **GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite System — GPS/GLONASS/Galileo)** and inertial sensors to determine its own position to within **±1.5 m** accuracy. It also gathers kinematic data from the **CAN bus** (Controller Area Network): wheel speed, steering angle, yaw rate, brake status.

**Step 2 — Message Construction (Layer 2: Application)**
The application layer packages the sensor data into a standardised message. Two main families exist:
- **SAE J2945 (USA) → BSM (Basic Safety Message)** — Part 1 mandatory, broadcast at 10 Hz.
- **ETSI EN 302 637 (Europe) → CAM (Cooperative Awareness Message) + DENM (Decentralised Environmental Notification Message).**

**Step 3 — Radio Transmission (Layer 3: Access / MAC)**
The message is broadcast over either:
- **DSRC (Dedicated Short-Range Communications)** — based on IEEE 802.11p, CSMA/CA contention.
- **C-V2X (Cellular V2X)** — based on 3GPP LTE/5G sidelink (PC5 interface), scheduled or autonomous mode.

**Step 4 — Reception by Neighbours (Layer 4: Network)**
All vehicles within radio range decode the packet. Because V2V is **broadcast-based (one-to-many)**, there is no handshake — a property called *ad-hoc multicast*.

**Step 5 — Safety Decision (Layer 5: Application Decision)**
The receiving OBU runs a **threat-assessment algorithm** (e.g., Time-To-Collision, TTC) and warns the driver via HMI or commands the ADAS controller (AEB — Autonomous Emergency Braking).

### 2.2 Why V2V? — The "Why" Behind the Concept

- **Sensor Limitations:** A camera or radar has a **line-of-sight** constraint and cannot "see" around corners or through trucks.
- **Latency Advantage:** V2V warns the driver ~**1–2 seconds earlier** than visible brake lights in many scenarios.
- **Non-Line-of-Sight (NLOS) Awareness:** Through V2V relay chains, vehicles know about hazards several hops away.
- **Cooperative Maneuvering:** Enables *platooning* (truck convoys at < 1 m spacing) — saving ~**10–15 %** fuel.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every KTU-relevant formula for V2V problems.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Standard Units / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Shannon Channel Capacity | $C = B \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | $C$ in **bits/s**, $B$ in **Hz**, $S/N$ dimensionless SNR |
| 2 | Free-Space Path Loss (Friis) | $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | $d$ in **km**, $f$ in **MHz**, $d \geq 1$ km (else use near-field form) |
| 3 | Latency Budget (One-Way Safety Msg) | $T_{total} = T_{sense} + T_{proc} + T_{queue} + T_{tx} + T_{prop}$ | Sum of all delays, must be **≤ 100 ms** |
| 4 | Propagation Delay | $T_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{c}$ | $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s |
| 5 | Time-To-Collision (TTC) | $TTC = \dfrac{\Delta d}{v_{rel}}$ | $\Delta d$ in m, $v_{rel}$ in m/s; warn if **TTC < 4 s** |
| 6 | Broadcast Rate (BSM) | $f_{BSM} = 10$ Hz | One packet every **100 ms** |
| 7 | Effective Throughput per Vehicle | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{size} \cdot f_{BSM}}{C}$ | $P_{size} \approx 400$ bytes for BSM Part 1 |
| 8 | Doppler Shift (V2V @ 5.9 GHz, 140 km/h) | $f_{d} = \dfrac{v \cdot f_{c}}{c}$ | At **v = 38.89 m/s, f_c = 5.9 GHz** → $f_{d} \approx 766$ Hz |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Collision Avoidance Systems** (e.g., Mercedes *Car-to-X*, GM *V2V Safety Pilot* in Ann Arbor, USA).
- **Truck Platooning** — Peloton Technology, Volvo — fuel economy + safety.
- **Traffic Efficiency** — Signal phase timing shared from V2I infrastructure.
- **Insurance Telematics** — actuarially-rated premiums based on V2V-tracked behaviour.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Achievable Data Rate via Shannon's Theorem (KTU 2018/2022 Pattern)

**Problem Statement (Typical Board Question):**
A V2V link operating in the 5.9 GHz ITS band uses a **10 MHz** channel. The received signal power is **−85 dBm** and the noise floor is **−100 dBm**. Calculate the maximum theoretical data rate (Shannon capacity) achievable on this link.

**Step 1 — Compute Signal-to-Noise Ratio in dB.**

The SNR in decibels is the difference between the received signal level and the noise floor (since both are in dBm, subtraction gives dB directly).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{dB} &= P_{rx,dBm} - N_{dBm} \\
&= (-85) - (-100) \\
&= 15 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert SNR from dB to Linear (Dimensionless) Ratio.**

Using the identity $x_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(x)$, the inverse is $x = 10^{(x_{dB}/10)}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{S}{N} &= 10^{(15/10)} \\
&= 10^{1.5} \\
&\approx 31.62
\end{aligned}
$$

The linear SNR is approximately **31.62** (pure ratio, no units).

**Step 3 — Apply Shannon-Hartley Theorem.**

The Shannon capacity formula gives the *maximum* error-free bit rate for a given bandwidth and SNR, assuming an ideal channel code.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= B \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \\
&= 10 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_{2}(1 + 31.62) \\
&= 10^{7} \cdot \log_{2}(32.62)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Logarithm.**

We know $2^{5} = 32$, so $\log_{2}(32.62) \approx 5.028$ (slightly above 5).

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &\approx 10^{7} \cdot 5.028 \\
&\approx 5.028 \times 10^{7} \text{ bits/s} \\
&\approx 50.28 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation.**

The **DSRC / IEEE 802.11p** standard uses OFDM with 64-QAM at the highest rate, achieving a *modulation-limited* throughput of about **27 Mbps** (half-rate coding) — significantly below the Shannon limit. The shortfall of ~23 Mbps is the "implementation loss" due to non-ideal coding, channel impairments (Doppler, multipath), and MAC contention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The maximum theoretical capacity is **≈ 50.28 Mbps**, but realistic V2V throughput is constrained to **~ 27 Mbps** by 802.11p modulation limits. This is more than sufficient for BSM (which needs only ~**32 kbps** at 10 Hz, 400-byte messages).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Latency Budget for a Safety-Critical V2V Warning

**Problem Statement (Frequently Asked in KTU):**
A V2V safety message must travel from a lead vehicle to a following vehicle **120 m** away. Given the following latencies: sensing **20 ms**, processing **15 ms**, MAC queuing **30 ms**, transmission time **5 ms**, and propagation over the distance. Calculate the total end-to-end latency and verify it is within the **100 ms safety threshold**.

**Step 1 — Compute Propagation Delay.**

The electromagnetic wave travels at the speed of light, $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{prop} &= \frac{d}{c} \\
&= \frac{120}{3 \times 10^{8}} \text{ s} \\
&= 4 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} \\
&= 0.4 \text{ \mu s} = 0.0004 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

The propagation delay is negligible compared to the other delays (a key insight for board answers).

**Step 2 — Sum All Latency Components.**

The total latency is the linear sum of every delay term, since these are serial operations.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= T_{sense} + T_{proc} + T_{queue} + T_{tx} + T_{prop} \\
&= 20 + 15 + 30 + 5 + 0.0004 \\
&\approx 70.0004 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare to Safety Threshold.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= 70.0004 \text{ ms} \;<\; T_{threshold} = 100 \text{ ms} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Margin = 100 − 70 ≈ 30 ms**, which is the safety margin available for retransmission attempts or cryptographic verification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:** In high-density platooning (inter-vehicle spacing < 5 m), the propagation delay becomes insignificant, but the **MAC queuing delay** dominates. This is why C-V2X (with scheduled sidelink resources) outperforms contention-based DSRC at high vehicle densities.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — DSRC vs C-V2X

| Parameter | **DSRC (IEEE 802.11p)** | **C-V2X (3GPP Release 14+)** |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Wi-Fi family (ad-hoc) | Cellular LTE/5G |
| Spectrum | **5.9 GHz** ITS (75 MHz) | **5.9 GHz** ITS (PC5 sidelink) + cellular uplink (Uu) |
| Access Mode | CSMA/CA — contention-based | Scheduled sidelink (Mode 3) / autonomous (Mode 4) |
| Latency @ Low Density | ~4 ms | ~20 ms |
| Latency @ High Density | **Degrades sharply** (collision) | **Stable** (scheduler) |
| Max Range | ~1000 m | ~1000 m (PC5) / nationwide (Uu via cellular) |
| Security | IEEE 1609.2 (PKI, certificates) | 3GPP security + SIM-based PKI |
| Migration Path | Mature but stagnant | Evolving with 5G-NR-V2X |
| Best Use Case | Mature regulatory pilots | 5G integration, *platooning*, *cooperative manoeuvres* |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style, Model Q1 — 3 Marks]**
> **Q1.** Define **Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) communication**. List any **two** key differences between V2V and traditional V2I communication.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** V2V communication is a short-range, ad-hoc wireless communication paradigm in which vehicles equipped with On-Board Units (OBUs) periodically broadcast their kinematic state (position, speed, heading, acceleration) and receive similar messages from neighbouring vehicles to enhance situational awareness and enable collision avoidance, primarily operating in the 5.9 GHz ITS band.

**Difference 1 (1 Mark):** V2V is **infrastructure-independent** — vehicles communicate directly with each other (peer-to-peer) without needing roadside units (RSUs) or cellular towers. In contrast, V2I requires deployed RSUs or base stations as intermediaries.

**Difference 2 (1 Mark):** V2V is a **decentralised broadcast** model (one-to-many, ad-hoc multicast), while V2I is typically a **client-server** model where the RSU acts as a coordinator.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style, Model Q2 — 3 Marks]**
> **Q2.** What is a **Basic Safety Message (BSM)** in DSRC-based V2V? Mention its **standard broadcast frequency** and **payload size (Part 1 mandatory content)**.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** A Basic Safety Message (BSM), defined under **SAE J2945/1** and **SAE J2735**, is a standardised message that V2V-equipped vehicles broadcast to share their current state and predicted path with surrounding vehicles.

**Broadcast Frequency (1 Mark):** The BSM is broadcast at **10 Hz** (i.e., one message every **100 ms**) by default, with a higher 100 Hz rate for event-driven critical alerts (e.g., hard braking, airbag deployment).

**Payload Size (1 Mark):** The mandatory **Part 1** of the BSM is approximately **400 bytes** (≈ 200 bits × 2 layers), containing vehicle ID, position, speed, heading, acceleration, brake status, and vehicle size. Part 2 (optional) adds up to ~600 bytes of additional data.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Style — Question A — 14 Marks]**
>
> **Q(A). (a)** Explain in detail the **architecture of a V2V communication system** with a neat block diagram of the On-Board Unit (OBU). Discuss the functions of the **application, network, and physical layers** as per the DSRC protocol stack. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** With the aid of a **Time-To-Collision (TTC) calculation example**, explain how V2V messages are used for forward collision warning. A vehicle is travelling at **90 km/h** and receives a BSM from a vehicle **40 m** ahead that is braking with a **relative speed of 20 km/h**. Determine the TTC and comment on whether a warning should be issued (warning threshold = 4 s). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q(A)(a) — 7 Marks

**Architecture (4 Marks):**

The V2V OBU architecture consists of **four primary subsystems**:

1. **Sensing / Data Acquisition Subsystem:** GNSS receiver (±1.5 m accuracy), CAN bus interface (vehicle speed, brake, steering), IMU (yaw rate, acceleration). *Function: Digitises the real-time state of the host vehicle.*

2. **Application / Message Subsystem:** Runs the **SAE J2945** BSM generator. *Function: Packages sensor data into standardised 400-byte messages at 10 Hz.*

3. **Security Subsystem:** Hardware Security Module (HSM) storing the OBU's **private key** and executing the **IEEE 1609.2** signing algorithm (ECDSA over the BSM hash). *Function: Provides authentication, integrity, and non-repudiation.*

4. **Radio / PHY-MAC Subsystem:** The IEEE 802.11p OFDM modem operating in the 5.9 GHz band, with a 10 MHz channel width. *Function: Modulates BSM into OFDM symbols and transmits at up to 27 Mbps.*

**Protocol Stack (3 Marks) — Layered Explanation:**

- **Application Layer (SAE J2735 / J2945):** Defines BSM data dictionary and broadcast rules.
- **Networking & Transport Layer (IEEE 1609.3 / WAVE Short Message Protocol – WSMP):** Provides addressing (anonymised MAC, temporary ID) and handles multi-channel operation (CCH for control, SCH for service).
- **MAC Layer (IEEE 802.11p):** CSMA/CA-based contention. OBUs listen on the **Control Channel (CCH, 178)** and switch to **Service Channels (SCH 172, 174, 176, 180, 182, 184)**.
- **Physical Layer (IEEE 802.11p):** OFDM with 52 subcarriers, BPSK/QPSK/16-QAM/64-QAM, 10 MHz channel, 5.9 GHz.

#### Model Solution — Q(A)(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Convert Units to SI (1 Mark).**

$$
v_{host} = 90 \text{ km/h} = \frac{90}{3.6} = 25 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
v_{rel} = 20 \text{ km/h} = \frac{20}{3.6} \approx 5.56 \text{ m/s}
$$

The inter-vehicle distance is $\Delta d = 40$ m.

**Step 2 — Apply TTC Formula (2 Marks).**

The Time-To-Collision assumes constant relative velocity.

$$
\begin{aligned}
TTC &= \frac{\Delta d}{v_{rel}} \\
&= \frac{40 \text{ m}}{5.56 \text{ m/s}} \\
&\approx 7.19 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with Threshold and Conclude (2 Marks).**

The warning threshold is **4 s**.

$$
TTC_{computed} = 7.19 \text{ s} \;>\; TTC_{threshold} = 4 \text{ s}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since 7.19 s > 4 s, the situation is **not immediately critical**. The HMI should display a **soft advisory** ("Vehicle ahead braking — maintain distance") but **no automated braking** is triggered. If $v_{rel}$ were to increase beyond 10 m/s (i.e., 36 km/h), TTC would drop below 4 s and a hard warning + AEB prep would activate.

**Step 4 — Note on Dynamic Updates (2 Marks):** Because BSMs arrive at 10 Hz, the TTC value is recalculated every 100 ms. If the lead vehicle hardens its braking (rising $v_{rel}$), the TTC can plunge below 4 s within ~330 ms, allowing an automated response.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style — Question B — 14 Marks Alternative Choice]**
>
> **Q(B). (a)** Describe the **DSRC and C-V2X** standards for V2V communication. Compare their **MAC layer access mechanisms** and discuss which is more suitable for **high-density traffic**. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compute the **Shannon channel capacity** for a V2V link with channel bandwidth $B = 10$ MHz, received signal power $P_{rx} = -90$ dBm, and noise power $N = -100$ dBm. State **two reasons** why the actual measured throughput is lower than this theoretical maximum. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q(B)(a) — 7 Marks

**DSRC (Dedicated Short-Range Communications) — (3 Marks):**
- Standard family: **IEEE 802.11p** (PHY/MAC) + **IEEE 1609.x** (WAVE upper layers).
- Operates in the **5.9 GHz** band (channels 172, 174, 176, 178, 180, 182, 184).
- MAC: **CSMA/CA** — every OBU contends for the channel; no central scheduler. A vehicle senses the channel idle, picks a random backoff, and transmits.
- Advantage: Low latency in sparse traffic; mature pilot deployments (US DOT Safety Pilot 2012–2014).
- Disadvantage: Under high density, **hidden-node collisions** and **channel congestion** cause throughput collapse.

**C-V2X (Cellular V2X) — (2 Marks):**
- Standard family: **3GPP Release 14** (LTE-V2X) onward; **5G-NR-V2X** from Release 16.
- Uses the **PC5 sidelink** interface (direct, device-to-device) and/or the **Uu interface** (via cellular network).
- MAC: **SPS (Semi-Persistent Scheduling)** in Mode 3 (network-scheduled) or **autonomous sensing with reservation** in Mode 4.
- Advantage: Predictable latency in dense traffic; integrates with 5G URLLC for ultra-reliable low-latency.

**Comparison Summary & Conclusion (2 Marks):**

| Aspect | DSRC (802.11p) | C-V2X |
|---|---|---|
| MAC Type | Contention (CSMA/CA) | Scheduled / Sidelink |
| Density Performance | Degrades > 200 veh/km/lane | Stable up to 2000+ veh/km² |
| Best for | Rural / Sparse | Urban / Platoon / High-density |

**Conclusion:** For high-density urban traffic, **C-V2X** is preferred due to its scheduler-based resource allocation, which avoids CSMA/CA contention collapse.

#### Model Solution — Q(B)(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Compute SNR in dB (1 Mark).**

$$
\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{dB} = (-90) - (-100) = 10 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Linear SNR (1 Mark).**

$$
\frac{S}{N} = 10^{(10/10)} = 10^{1} = 10
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Shannon Formula (3 Marks).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= B \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \\
&= 10 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_{2}(1 + 10) \\
&= 10^{7} \cdot \log_{2}(11) \\
&= 10^{7} \cdot 3.4594 \\
&\approx 3.46 \times 10^{7} \text{ bits/s} \\
&\approx 34.59 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Two Reasons for the Practical Throughput Gap (2 Marks):**

1. **Modulation & Coding Limit:** IEEE 802.11p supports 64-QAM with a maximum rate of **27 Mbps** (using rate-3/4 coding) — this is a *modulation-imposed* ceiling, not a Shannon limit. The system cannot reach 34.6 Mbps because the highest constellation/coding pair caps at 27 Mbps.

2. **MAC Overhead & Channel Impairments:** CSMA/CA back-offs, inter-frame spacing, header/preamble overhead, retransmissions, and **Doppler spread** (≈ 766 Hz at 140 km/h, 5.9 GHz) all reduce the *net* throughput by ~30–50 % of the raw PHY rate. Fading, multipath, and packet collisions in dense scenarios further degrade the link.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Traps in V2V Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion in TTC problems:** Students often write "90 km/h" directly into $v$ (m/s) — examiner deducts **1 full mark**. Always convert km/h → m/s via $v \div 3.6$.
> 2. **Confusing BSM with CAM and DENM:** BSM is the **SAE/US** standard, CAM and DENM are the **ETSI/Europe** standard. Mixing them up costs the **definition mark**.
> 3. **Using log₁₀ instead of log₂ in Shannon's formula:** This is the **single most common calculation error**. The Shannon capacity formula MUST use $\log_{2}$ (bits), not $\log_{10}$ (Bans). Examiners explicitly check this.
> 4. **Confusing the 5.9 GHz band with "5G frequency":** The ITS band is 5.85–5.925 GHz; "5G" refers to cellular generations. While 5G-NR-V2X *can* operate in this band, the band is reserved for ITS, not generic 5G mobile phones.
> 5. **Omitting the security layer in OBU architecture:** The **IEEE 1609.2 security / HSM** block is a high-value item. Skipping it costs **at least 1 mark** in a 7-mark question.
> 6. **Drawing block diagrams with arrows that cross or have no labels:** Examiners deduct for unlabeled arrows. Always label each block and each arrow (e.g., "BSM payload 400 bytes").

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for V2V Communication (KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3)**
>
> - [x] **V2V** = direct, ad-hoc, short-range wireless exchange of vehicle state among OBUs in the **5.9 GHz** ITS band.
> - [x] **Broader Family:** V2V is a subset of **V2X**, which also covers **V2I, V2P, V2N**.
> - [x] **Two Main Standards:** **DSRC** (IEEE 802.11p + IEEE 1609) and **C-V2X** (3GPP LTE/5G sidelink PC5).
> - [x] **Key Message:** **BSM** (US, 10 Hz, ~400 bytes) / **CAM + DENM** (Europe).
> - [x] **Frequency Band:** **5.85 – 5.925 GHz**, channel width **10 MHz** (legacy) or 20 MHz (5G-NR-V2X).
> - [x] **Range:** ~**300 m** urban, up to **~1000 m** highway line-of-sight.
> - [x] **Latency Target:** **≤ 100 ms** for safety-critical messages.
> - [x] **Shannon Capacity:** Always use $C = B \log_{2}(1 + S/N)$ — convert SNR from dB to linear **before** applying.
> - [x] **TTC Formula:** $TTC = \Delta d / v_{rel}$, with warning at **TTC < 4 s**.
> - [x] **Doppler Effect:** At 5.9 GHz and 140 km/h, $f_d \approx 766$ Hz — relevant for OFDM inter-carrier interference discussions.
> - [x] **Security:** Mandatory **IEEE 1609.2 / ECDSA** signing to prevent message spoofing.
> - [x] **Use Cases:** Forward collision warning, blind-spot warning, **platooning**, do-not-pass warning, intersection movement assist, electric vehicle (EV) charging coordination via V2G.
> - [x] **Exam Tip:** Always draw a **labeled block diagram** of the OBU and explicitly mention **sensing → BSM build → sign → OFDM transmit → channel → receive → verify → threat assessment → HMI/AEB**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cybersecurity in Connected Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET414 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cybersecurity in Connected Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🛡️ Cybersecurity in Connected Vehicles — Core Foundation

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Connected Vehicle Cybersecurity** is the disciplined engineering practice of protecting vehicle electronic systems, communication networks, software stacks, and data from unauthorized access, malicious manipulation, and cyber-physical attacks throughout the vehicle's lifecycle — from initial concept and design, through production, operation, and end-of-life decommissioning.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (aligned with **ISO/SAE 21434:2021** and **UNECE WP.29 R155**), automotive cybersecurity is defined as:

> *"A systematic, risk-based engineering discipline that ensures the **Confidentiality**, **Integrity**, **Availability**, and **Authenticity** of electrical and electronic (E/E) systems in road vehicles, achieved through continuous threat analysis, vulnerability management, and the deployment of layered defensive controls."*

A **Connected Vehicle** is an automobile equipped with external communication interfaces (V2X, cellular 4G/5G, Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, GPS, satellite) that enables bidirectional data exchange with cloud platforms, other vehicles (V2V), infrastructure (V2I), pedestrians (V2P), and the power grid (V2G) — collectively termed **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything)** communication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 expects students to articulate *why* a modern car has more than **100 ECUs** and **>100 million lines of code**, making it one of the most complex cyber-physical systems ever mass-produced — and consequently, a high-value target for cyber attackers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smartphone on Wheels"

Imagine your **smartphone**: it has a camera, microphone, GPS, Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, cellular modem, fingerprint sensor, and an app ecosystem. You wouldn't dream of leaving it unlocked in a public place, because hackers could read your messages, track your location, or drain your bank account.

> Now imagine the **same attack surface**, except:
> - The device weighs **~2,000 kg**
> - It travels at **120 km/h**
> - It contains your family
> - And a successful hack can cause **physical death**

That is a **connected vehicle**. It is, quite literally, a **smartphone on wheels with a 2-tonne kinetic payload**. Every convenience feature — remote unlock via app, over-the-air (OTA) software updates, automatic collision notification, in-car Wi-Fi — is also a **potential entry point** for an adversary.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Attack Surface Expansion

The modern connected vehicle's attack surface is fundamentally different from a traditional car because of three converging trends:

1. **Connectivity Expansion** — External interfaces (cellular, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, V2X) replace sealed, isolated networks.
2. **Software-Defined Vehicle (SDV) Architecture** — Features and behaviour are dictated by software that can be updated remotely via **OTA**.
3. **Electrification & ADAS Integration** — Drive-by-wire systems, autonomous driving stacks, and battery management create new safety-critical cyber-physical dependencies.

> [!NOTE]
> **CIA + A — The Automotive Security Tetrad**
> The four properties every connected vehicle security solution must protect:
> - **C**onfidentiality → Driver data, location, biometrics must not leak
> - **I**ntegrity → CAN/LIN/Ethernet messages must not be tampered with
> - **A**vailability → Brakes, steering, and sensors must work when needed
> - **A**uthenticity → Only legitimate ECUs and messages are trusted

## 1.4 Key Terminology Quick-Reference

| Term | Definition | KTU Relevance |
|------|------------|----------------|
| **ECU** | Electronic Control Unit — embedded microcontroller governing a vehicle subsystem | High |
| **CAN Bus** | Controller Area Network — broadcast serial bus standard (ISO 11898) | Very High |
| **OBD-II** | On-Board Diagnostics port — physical access interface for diagnostics | High |
| **TARA** | Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment — ISO/SAE 21434 mandatory process | Very High |
| **HSM** | Hardware Security Module — tamper-resistant crypto processor | High |
| **V2X** | Vehicle-to-Everything communication (V2V, V2I, V2P, V2G, V2N) | Very High |
| **OTA** | Over-The-Air software/firmware update mechanism | High |
| **PKI** | Public Key Infrastructure — manages digital certificates | High |
| **IDS/IPS** | Intrusion Detection / Prevention System | High |
| **SOC** | Automotive Security Operations Centre (back-end monitoring) | Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Reality Check (2024):** A modern premium car has **~150 ECUs**, **>100 million lines of code**, and runs on **~20 different network protocols** — creating an attack surface estimated to grow **5x** by 2030 (McKinsey Automotive Cybersecurity Report 2024).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 The Connected Vehicle Attack Surface — Systematic Decomposition

A connected vehicle's attack surface is best understood as a **layered onion** of attack vectors. Theoretically, an attacker can enter at any layer, but the **outermost layers are easiest** and the **innermost are most catastrophic**.

### Layer 1 — External Wireless Attack Surface

| Vector | Description | Real-World Example |
|--------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Remote Keyless Entry (RKE)** | Radio-frequency relay attacks amplify key fob signal | 2011–2018 Volkswagen group relay attack |
| **Cellular (4G/5G) Telematics** | Modem interfaces to OEM cloud; SMS/SS7 vulnerabilities | Tesla Model S hack (2015, Keen Security Lab) |
| **Wi-Fi / Hotspot** | In-vehicle infotainment AP | Jeep Cherokee Uconnect hack (2015) |
| **Bluetooth** | Hands-free, audio streaming pairing | Multiple CVEs in infotainment stacks |
| **V2X (DSRC/C-V2X)** | Peer-to-peer short-range comms for safety | Standards still maturing security |
| **GNSS Spoofing** | GPS signal forgery misleads navigation | Demonstrated by Regulus Cyber (2019) |
| **Radar/LiDAR Spoofing** | Adversarial signals fool ADAS sensors | Reported by Tencent Keen (2019) |

### Layer 2 — Physical / Local Access

| Vector | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| **OBD-II Port** | Direct CAN bus access via diagnostic connector |
| **USB Ports** | Media upload, firmware update path |
| **Infotainment Touchscreen** | Browser exploits, app sandbox escape |
| **Wiring Harness Tampering** | Direct injection of rogue CAN frames |

### Layer 3 — Internal Network (In-Vehicle)

| Bus / Protocol | Speed | Security Native? |
|----------------|-------|------------------|
| **CAN (Classic)** | 500 kbps | ❌ No authentication, broadcast |
| **CAN FD** | 2 Mbps | ❌ Inherits CAN weaknesses |
| **CAN XL** | 10 Mbps | ⚠ Partial — still broadcast |
| **LIN** | 19.2 kbps | ❌ No security |
| **FlexRay** | 10 Mbps | ⚠ Limited |
| **Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1 / 1000BASE-T1)** | 100 Mbps – 1 Gbps | ⚠ Needs MACsec / IPsec overlay |
| **MOST** | 150 Mbps | ❌ Legacy, no security |

### Layer 4 — Supply Chain & Software

| Vector | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| **Compromised Tier-1 Component** | Infotainment or TCU with backdoor |
| **Malicious OTA Payload** | Forged update to install malware |
| **Open-Source CVE** | Unpatched library in ECU firmware |
| **Third-Party App (App Store)** | Malicious app on IVI system |

### Layer 5 — Backend & Cloud

| Vector | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| **OEM Cloud APIs** | Account takeover, remote command injection |
| **Telematics Service Provider (TSP)** | Aggregator compromise |
| **Mobile App** | Reverse-engineered API keys |

## 2.2 The ISO/SAE 21434 Framework — The Global Standard

**ISO/SAE 21434:2021** is the *de facto* mandatory international standard for automotive cybersecurity engineering, harmonized with **UN Regulation No. 155 (R155)** which makes it legally binding in EU, Japan, Korea from **July 2022** onward.

### Work-Package Structure (Clause-Wise)

| Clause | Work Product | Purpose |
|--------|--------------|---------|
| **Clause 5** | Organizational cybersecurity management | OEM policies, audits, culture |
| **Clause 6** | Project-dependent cybersecurity management | Per-project CS planning |
| **Clause 7** | Distributed cybersecurity activities | Supplier coordination |
| **Clause 8** | Continual cybersecurity activities | Post-production monitoring |
| **Clause 9** | Concept phase — **TARA** | Threat identification & risk scoring |
| **Clause 10** | Product development | Secure design & implementation |
| **Clause 11** | Cybersecurity validation | Penetration testing, fuzzing |
| **Clause 12** | Production | Secure manufacturing & key injection |
| **Clause 13** | Operations & maintenance | Incident response, OTA patching |
| **Clause 14** | End of cybersecurity support & decommissioning | Data wiping, key revocation |
| **Clause 15** | Threat analysis & risk assessment (TARA) methods | **Detailed methodology** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Takeaway:** A passing answer must reference **ISO/SAE 21434 + UN R155 + TARA** as the regulatory+engineering trio. Question papers since 2022 frequently award marks purely for citing the correct standard.

## 2.3 TARA — Threat Analysis & Risk Assessment (Clause 15)

TARA is the structured, repeatable method mandated by ISO/SAE 21434 to identify, classify, and prioritize cybersecurity risks. It follows five stages:

### Stage 1 — Item Definition
Define the **item** (a function or system boundary) under analysis, its operational environment, and its interactions.

### Stage 2 — Asset Identification
Catalogue the assets to be protected: ECUs, data, software, cryptographic keys, driver privacy, safety functions.

### Stage 3 — Threat Scenario Identification
For each asset, identify:
- **Who** could attack (threat agent) — *e.g., script kiddie, organized crime, nation-state, insider*
- **How** (attack path) — *e.g., remote, physical, supply chain*
- **Why** (motivation) — *e.g., theft, ransom, terrorism, fraud*

### Stage 4 — Impact Rating
Rate the **damage severity** if the threat succeeds:

| Impact Level | Description | Example |
|--------------|-------------|---------|
| **Severe** | Loss of life / regulatory violation | Brake ECU compromised |
| **Major** | Temporary injury / major financial loss | Vehicle immobilization |
| **Moderate** | Non-safety impact, recoverable | Infotainment crash |
| **Negligible** | Minor inconvenience | Display flicker |

### Stage 5 — Risk Determination — *The Heart of TARA*

The standard defines the **Risk Value (RV)** as a function of **Impact (I)**, **Attack Feasibility (F)**, and — depending on variant — **Safety Relevance (S)**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table compiles every formula and rating scale you must know for a 14-mark TARA / cybersecurity question. The exact weightings are defined in **ISO/SAE 21434 Annex E** and the **EVITA project methodology**.

### 2.4.1 Core Risk Equation

$$
RV = f(I,\ F,\ S)
$$

where:
- $I$ = Impact severity rating
- $F$ = Attack feasibility rating
- $S$ = Safety relevance multiplier (often implicit in $I$)

### 2.4.2 Attack Feasibility — Knowledge Level (KL)

| Level | Label | Description |
|-------|-------|-------------|
| **KL0** | Public | Open-source, no special knowledge |
| **KL1** | Restricted | Vendor-confidential documentation |
| **KL2** | Sensitive | Internal OEM/Tier-1 engineering details |
| **KL3** | Critical | Tightly held, very specialized |

### 2.4.3 Attack Feasibility — Window of Opportunity (WO)

| Level | Label | Description |
|-------|-------|-------------|
| **WO0** | Unlimited | e.g., Internet-exposed, indefinite time |
| **WO1** | Easy | e.g., < 1 day of physical access |
| **WO2** | Moderate | Hours of access, special tools |
| **WO3** | Difficult | Seconds, very limited window |

### 2.4.4 Attack Feasibility — Equipment (EQ)

| Level | Label | Description |
|-------|-------|-------------|
| **EQ0** | Standard | Off-the-shelf laptops, software |
| **EQ1** | Specialized | Datasets, protocol analyzers |
| **EQ2** | Bespoke | Custom hardware, lab equipment |
| **EQ3** | Multi-Bespoke | Nation-state grade resources |

### 2.4.5 Combined Attack Feasibility Score (Highest of the Three)

$$
F = \max(KL,\ WO,\ EQ)
$$

This "max-rule" follows the principle that a single weak link in attacker requirements makes the whole attack easier.

### 2.4.6 Mapping to Qualitative Risk Levels

| Impact $\rightarrow$ \ Feasibility $\downarrow$ | Negligible | Moderate | Major | Severe |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Very Low (F=4)** | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
| **Low (F=3)** | 1 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
| **Medium (F=2)** | 2 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
| **High (F=1)** | 3 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
| **Very High (F=0)** | 4 | 6 | 7 | 7 |

Risk levels 5–7 → **Critical**, must be mitigated with **Cybersecurity Goals** and corresponding claims.

## 2.5 Cryptographic Foundations for In-Vehicle Security

Cryptography is the bedrock of automotive cybersecurity. The following primitives are evaluated against three metrics: **latency (ms)**, **code size (KB)**, **hardware acceleration availability**.

### 2.5.1 Symmetric Encryption

$$
C = E_K(P) \quad ; \quad P = D_K(C)
$$

where $P$ = plaintext, $C$ = ciphertext, $K$ = shared secret key, $E$ / $D$ = encryption / decryption.

**AES-128** is the automotive industry default (used in **SecOC — Secure Onboard Communication**, AUTOSAR specification).

### 2.5.2 Message Authentication Code (MAC) — Used in CAN Message Authentication

$$
MAC = H(K \ \Vert \ M)
$$

where $H$ = keyed hash, $K$ = shared session key, $M$ = message, $\Vert$ = concatenation.

A valid MAC guarantees **integrity** + **authenticity** of the CAN frame.

### 2.5.3 Cryptographic Hash Function — Used in OTA Integrity

$$
h = \text{SHA-}256(M)
$$

A one-way function producing a **256-bit** digest. Any bit-flip in $M$ changes $h$ with probability $\approx 1 - 2^{-256}$.

### 2.5.4 Digital Signature — Used in V2X PKI

$$
\sigma = S_{SK}(H(M)) \quad ; \quad \text{Verify: } H(M) = V_{PK}(\sigma)
$$

**ECDSA over secp256r1 (NIST P-256)** dominates V2X signatures per **IEEE 1609.2**.

### 2.5.5 Performance Numbers (Typical Automotive HSM)

| Algorithm | Latency | Throughput | HSM Acceleration |
|-----------|---------|------------|------------------|
| AES-128-ECB (16 B) | < 1 µs | > 200 MB/s | Yes |
| SHA-256 (64 B block) | < 1 µs | > 250 MB/s | Yes |
| ECDSA sign (P-256) | ~5 ms | ~200 sig/s | Yes (EVITA-Full HSM) |
| ECDSA verify (P-256) | ~10 ms | ~100 ver/s | Yes |
| True RNG | 10 kbps – 1 Mbps | N/A | Yes (TRNG IP) |

## 2.6 SecOC — Secure Onboard Communication (AUTOSAR Standard)

**SecOC** (AUTOSAR 4.2+) adds an **authentication tag (MAC)** to CAN/CAN FD frames, typically truncated to a configurable length (commonly **4, 8, 12, or 16 bytes**) to fit within CAN's 8-byte payload limit.

The MAC is computed over a **Freshness Value (FV)** — typically a **monotonic counter** or a **synchronized time stamp** — concatenated with the message payload, to defeat **replay attacks**.

## 2.7 UNECE WP.29 R155 — Regulatory Backbone

| Requirement | Mandate |
|-------------|---------|
| **CSMS Certificate** | OEM must hold valid **Cybersecurity Management System** cert from type approval authority |
| **Type Approval Gate** | No vehicle can be sold in EU without CSMS |
| **Risk Management** | OEM must document a CSMS covering development, production, post-production |
| **Incident Response** | OEM must monitor, report, and remediate cyber incidents |
| **Supply Chain** | Tier-1s and Tier-2s must comply with the OEM's CSMS |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When asked "What is the regulatory framework for automotive cybersecurity?", the safest two-sentence answer is: *"UN R155 mandates a CSMS for OEM type approval. ISO/SAE 21434 defines the engineering process to satisfy R155. Compliance is verified through Type Approval audits."*

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Combined Risk Score for an Automotive TARA Case

We derive the **Quantitative Risk Value (QRV)** for a specific attack scenario: a remote attacker exploiting a cellular telematics vulnerability to inject malicious CAN frames into a vehicle's brake ECU.

### Step 1 — Identify the Asset
The asset is the **braking function** delivered by the brake ECU over the CAN bus.

### Step 2 — Identify the Threat Agent and Attack Path
- **Threat agent:** Remote, financially motivated attacker with technical capability
- **Attack path:** Cellular $\rightarrow$ Telematics Control Unit (TCU) $\rightarrow$ CAN gateway $\rightarrow$ Chassis CAN $\rightarrow$ Brake ECU
- **Motivation:** Ransom, harm, sabotage

### Step 3 — Determine Impact Rating

The brake ECU compromise can lead to **complete loss of braking**, causing high-speed collision. Per Table 2.4.6, this is:

$$
I = \text{Severe} \quad (I_{\text{score}} = 4)
$$

### Step 4 — Determine Each Attack Feasibility Sub-Parameter

**Knowledge (KL):** The attacker must know the cellular protocol stack, the OEM's TCU firmware API, and the CAN message structure for brake commands. This is **Restricted** OEM-confidential information but partially leaked in prior research. Thus:

$$
KL = 2 \quad (\text{of } 0\text{–}3,\ \text{with } 0 = \text{public})
$$

**Window (WO):** The attack is **remote over the internet** with unlimited time. Thus:

$$
WO = 0 \quad (\text{unlimited access})
$$

**Equipment (EQ):** The attacker needs a cellular SDR + a known CVE in the TCU firmware. Such SDRs cost < 500 USD, and CVEs are public. Thus:

$$
EQ = 1 \quad (\text{specialized but cheap})
$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Combined Feasibility Rule

Per Equation 2.4.5:

$$
F = \max(KL,\ WO,\ EQ) = \max(2,\ 0,\ 1) = 2
$$

Mapping to feasibility level (lower is easier): $F = 2$ corresponds to **Medium** attack feasibility.

### Step 6 — Look Up the Risk Matrix

Using the matrix from Section 2.4.6 with $I = \text{Severe}$ and $F = \text{Medium}$:

$$
RV = 6 \quad (\text{Critical})
$$

### Step 7 — Interpretation and Required Action

A risk value of 6 (out of 7) is in the **Critical band**. Per ISO/SAE 21434 Clause 9, the OEM must:

1. Define a **Cybersecurity Goal (CSG)**: *"Prevent unauthorized CAN frame injection into the chassis CAN."*
2. Derive a **Cybersecurity Requirement (CSR)**: e.g., *"Implement SecOC with 8-byte truncated MAC on all chassis CAN frames above 50 ms period."*
3. Propagate the CSR to relevant ECUs in the distributed development workflow.
4. Verify via **penetration testing (Clause 11)** and **fuzz testing**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice how the *max*-rule of attack feasibility means that even if the attacker requires highly specialized knowledge (KL=2), the *unlimited* attack window (WO=0) drags the risk into the critical band. This is why the standard uses the worst-case component — there is no "averaging" of attacker difficulty.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — CAN Message Authentication via Truncated MAC

We derive the **bit-level structure** of an authenticated CAN FD frame using AUTOSAR SecOC.

### Step 1 — Define the Input Fields

The secured CAN frame is constructed from:
- **Data ID** ($D$): 16-bit identifier of the message type (prevents MAC reuse across messages)
- **Payload** ($P$): up to 64 bytes (CAN FD)
- **Freshness Value** ($FV$): 64-bit monotonic counter, shared between sender and receiver ECUs
- **Authenticator Length** ($L$): truncated MAC length, commonly 4, 8, 12, or 16 bytes

### Step 2 — Compute the Full MAC

Using **CMAC-AES-128** as the primitive (FIPS SP 800-38B):

$$
T_{\text{full}} = \text{CMAC}_{K}(D \ \Vert\ FV \ \Vert\ P)
$$

where $K$ is a 128-bit symmetric session key stored in the sender and receiver HSMs.

The full CMAC output is **128 bits** (16 bytes).

### Step 3 — Truncate the MAC

To fit within the limited CAN FD payload, we truncate to $L$ bytes (most significant bytes):

$$
T_{\text{trunc}} = T_{\text{full}}[0 : L \times 8] \quad \text{bits}
$$

The first $L$ bytes are transmitted as the **Authenticator** field appended to the data.

### Step 4 — Receiver Verification Algorithm

The receiver independently:
1. Reads the incoming $P$ and the appended $T_{\text{trunc}}$.
2. Retrieves its local copy of $FV$ (must match sender within a small window).
3. Recomputes $\hat{T}_{\text{full}} = \text{CMAC}_{K}(D \ \Vert\ FV_{\text{local}} \ \Vert\ P)$.
4. Truncates to $\hat{T}_{\text{trunc}} = \hat{T}_{\text{full}}[0 : L \times 8]$.
5. Compares bit-by-bit:

$$
\text{Accept frame iff} \quad T_{\text{trunc}} \stackrel{?}{=} \hat{T}_{\text{trunc}}
$$

If equal, the frame is **authentic and fresh**; otherwise it is dropped and a **security event** is logged.

### Step 5 — Security Strength Analysis

A truncated MAC of $L$ bytes provides $8L$ bits of forgery resistance against a randomly chosen MAC. An attacker must try on average:

$$
N_{\text{trial}} = 2^{8L - 1}
$$

forgeries to find one accepted by the receiver. For $L = 4$ bytes (32 bits), $N_{\text{trial}} \approx 2.1 \times 10^{9}$ — strong but theoretically brute-forceable by a determined adversary over many frames. Hence OEMs typically use $L \geq 8$ for safety-critical chassis messages.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Secure OTA Update Integrity Verification

We derive the **end-to-end verification chain** for a software update package delivered OTA to an ECU.

### Step 1 — OEM Side — Generate Signed Package

The OEM's signing server performs:

1. Compute the SHA-256 hash of the firmware image $F$:

$$
h_F = \text{SHA-}256(F)
$$

2. Sign the hash with the OEM's **long-term signing key** (e.g., ECDSA P-256 private key, $SK_{\text{OEM}}$):

$$
\sigma = S_{SK_{\text{OEM}}}(h_F)
$$

3. Bundle the deliverable as:

$$
\text{Package} = \{F,\ \text{Manifest},\ h_F,\ \sigma,\ \text{Cert}_{OEM}\}
$$

where **Manifest** contains version, target ECU HW ID, rollback version, dependencies.

### Step 2 — Delivery
The package is delivered to the TCU over TLS-secured cellular link, then forwarded to the target ECU over the in-vehicle network (e.g., UDS over CAN, or DoIP over Ethernet).

### Step 3 — ECU Side — Verify Before Flashing

The ECU's bootloader / OTA manager executes:

1. Read $\text{Cert}_{OEM}$ and verify the OEM's public key $PK_{\text{OEM}}$ against a **root certificate** stored in the HSM at manufacture time.
2. Extract $\sigma$ and $h_F$ from the package.
3. Verify the signature:

$$
\text{Check: } V_{PK_{\text{OEM}}}(\sigma) \stackrel{?}{=} h_F
$$

4. If yes, recompute the hash of the received image $\hat{F}$:

$$
\hat{h}_F = \text{SHA-}256(\hat{F})
$$

5. Compare hashes:

$$
\text{Accept iff } \hat{h}_F = h_F
$$

Only if both verifications pass does the ECU write the new firmware to its inactive partition, then reboot to the new image (A/B partition scheme). If verification fails, the package is discarded, an event is logged to the SOC, and the ECU continues to run the prior image.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two checks (signature + hash)?** The signature protects the *integrity of the hash*, and the hash protects the *integrity of the multi-megabyte firmware image*. Verifying a signature over the raw multi-MB image would be 100–1000x slower than verifying a signature over a 32-byte hash.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — V2X Message Authentication Latency Budget

V2X safety messages (e.g., **CAM — Cooperative Awareness Message**, **BSM — Basic Safety Message**) are broadcast at **10 Hz** (every 100 ms). Each message must be verified before its safety content is trusted.

### Step 1 — Define the Latency Budget

$$
t_{\text{total}} = t_{\text{decode}} + t_{\text{verify}} + t_{\text{deliver}} \leq 100 \ \text{ms}
$$

A typical budget allocation:

$$
t_{\text{decode}} = 15 \ \text{ms} \quad,\quad t_{\text{verify}} \leq 50 \ \text{ms} \quad,\quad t_{\text{deliver}} = 35 \ \text{ms}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute ECDSA P-256 Verification Time on Automotive HSM

Per Section 2.5.5, ECDSA P-256 verification on a hardware-accelerated HSM takes $\approx 10$ ms per signature. Thus, for **10 messages/second**:

$$
t_{\text{verify,total}} = 10 \ \frac{\text{msg}}{\text{s}} \times 10 \ \frac{\text{ms}}{\text{msg}} = 100 \ \text{ms/sec} = 10\% \ \text{CPU load}
$$

This fits within the 50 ms / 100 ms per-message budget, leaving ample headroom for batch verification and OTA messages.

### Step 3 — Pigeonhole Analysis (Worst-Case Burst)

If 20 messages arrive in a single 100 ms window (e.g., at an intersection):

$$
t_{\text{verify,total}} = 20 \times 10 = 200 \ \text{ms} \ > \ 100 \ \text{ms budget}
$$

This **fails the safety latency constraint**. The solution is **batch verification** using algorithms like **ECDSA-Batch** (Bernstein et al.) which amortize the verification cost:

$$
t_{\text{verify,batch}} \approx t_{\text{verify,single}} \times \left(1 + 0.05 \cdot N_{\text{batch}}\right)
$$

For $N_{\text{batch}} = 20$:

$$
t_{\text{verify,batch}} \approx 10 \times (1 + 1.0) = 20 \ \text{ms} \ \checkmark
$$

This satisfies the 100 ms budget with 65 ms headroom for decode and delivery.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Defense Mechanism Coverage

| Attack Vector | Network Segregation | Secure Boot | SecOC / MAC | IDS / IPS | HSM-Based Key Storage | OTA Signing |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| OBD-II Injection | ✅ | ⚪ | ✅ | ✅ | ⚪ | ⚪ |
| RKE Relay | ⚪ | ⚪ | ⚪ | ⚪ | ✅ | ⚪ |
| Cellular Remote | ✅ | ✅ | ⚪ | ✅ | ✅ | ⚪ |
| Malicious OTA | ✅ | ✅ | ⚪ | ⚪ | ✅ | ✅ |
| V2X Forgery | ⚪ | ⚪ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Supply Chain Trojan | ✅ | ✅ | ⚪ | ⚪ | ✅ | ⚪ |
| Replay Attack (CAN) | ✅ | ⚪ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ⚪ |

Legend: ✅ = Highly Effective, ⚪ = Not applicable, ⚠ = Partial

**Reading the matrix:** No single defense covers all attack vectors. This is the *defense-in-depth* principle — multiple overlapping controls are mandatory.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Figure 1 — Connected Vehicle Attack Surface Map

The diagram below is a **block-level functional architecture flow** mapping every layer of the connected vehicle's attack surface and the principal threat agents.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef external fill:#ffd1d1,stroke:#b30000,color:#000
    classDef internal fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006633,color:#000
    classDef ecu fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    classDef bus fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#4d2670,color:#000

    attacker1[Remote Attacker<br>Internet/Cellular]:::external
    attacker2[Local Attacker<br>Physical/OBD]:::external
    attacker3[Supply Chain<br>Compromised Vendor]:::external
    attacker4[Insider Threat<br>Malicious Employee]:::external

    v2x[V2X Radio<br>DSRC or C-V2X]:::external
    cell[Cellular Modem<br>4G 5G]:::external
    bt[Bluetooth WiFi]:::external
    rke[Remote Keyless Entry]:::external
    obd[OBD II Port]:::external
    usb[USB Media Port]:::external

    tcu[Telematics Control Unit<br>TCU Gateway]:::ecu
    ivi[Infotainment Head Unit<br>IVI]:::ecu
    gw[Central Gateway ECU<br>Network Firewall]:::ecu
    hsm[Hardware Security Module<br>HSM Root of Trust]:::control
    soc[Backend SOC<br>Cloud Monitoring]:::control

    ccan[Chassis CAN Bus<br>Brake Steering]:::bus
    powertrain[Powertrain CAN Bus<br>Engine Transmission]:::bus
    bcan[Body CAN Bus<br>Lights Locks]:::bus

    brake[Brake ECU]:::ecu
    steer[Steer by Wire ECU]:::ecu
    adas[ADAS Domain<br>Controller]:::ecu
    bms[Battery Management<br>System]:::ecu

    attacker1 --> cell
    attacker1 --> v2x
    attacker2 --> obd
    attacker2 --> usb
    attacker3 --> ivi
    attacker3 --> tcu
    attacker4 --> ivi
    rke -.relay.-> attacker1

    cell --> tcu
    bt --> ivi
    usb --> ivi
    obd --> gw

    tcu --> gw
    ivi --> gw

    gw --> ccan
    gw --> powertrain
    gw --> bcan
    gw --> eth

    ccan --> brake
    ccan --> steer
    powertrain --> bms
    eth --> adas

    gw -. secure channel .-> hsm
    tcu -. secure channel .-> hsm
    ivi -. secure channel .-> hsm
    tcu -. TLS VPN .-> soc
    soc -. threat intel .-> hsm
```

**How to read this diagram:** The **red** boxes are external threat agents and entry vectors. The **yellow** boxes are ECUs. The **blue** boxes are internal networks. The **green** boxes are the security controls. The attacker is shown traversing from outside (top) to safety-critical ECUs (bottom), with the HSM as the cryptographic root of trust and the SOC as the back-end defender.

## 4.4 Figure 4 — Secure OTA Update End-to-End Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant OEM as OEM Signing Server
    participant CDN as Delivery CDN TLS
    participant TCU as Telematics Control Unit
    participant GW as Central Gateway
    participant ECU as Target ECU
    participant HSM as ECU HSM
    participant SOC as Backend SOC

    OEM->>OEM: Compute SHA-256 hash of firmware F
    OEM->>OEM: Sign hash with ECDSA P-256 private key
    OEM->>CDN: Upload signed package
    CDN->>TCU: Download over TLS 1.3 mutual auth
    TCU->>SOC: Notify pending OTA event
    TCU->>GW: Forward to target ECU over UDS DoIP
    GW->>ECU: Deliver package
    ECU->>HSM: Verify OEM certificate chain
    HSM-->>ECU: Public key valid
    ECU->>HSM: Verify ECDSA signature on hash
    HSM-->>ECU: Signature valid
    ECU->>ECU: Recompute SHA-256 of received firmware
    ECU->>ECU: Compare computed hash with signed hash
    alt Verification passes
        ECU->>ECU: Write to inactive A or B partition
        ECU->>ECU: Reboot into new image
        ECU->>SOC: Report success with new SW version
    else Verification fails
        ECU->>ECU: Discard package
        ECU->>SOC: Report security incident
        SOC->>SOC: Quarantine and investigate
    end
```

## 4.5 Figure 5 — Cryptographic Handshake for V2X Message Signing

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef vehicle fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    classDef infra fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef pki fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006633,color:#000

    A[Vehicle A HSM]:::vehicle
    B[Vehicle B HSM]:::vehicle
    RSU[Roadside Unit RSU]:::infra
    CA[PKI Root Certificate Authority]:::pki
    ENA[Enrollment Authority]:::pki
    AA[Authorization Authority]:::pki

    A -. obtain pseudonym cert .-> ENA
    A -. validate peer cert .-> AA
    B -. obtain pseudonym cert .-> ENA
    B -. validate peer cert .-> AA
    CA -. trust anchor .-> ENA
    CA -. trust anchor .-> AA

    A -- CAM signed with pseudonym key --> B
    A -- CAM signed with pseudonym key --> RSU
    B -- verify ECDSA P-256 --> B
    RSU -- verify ECDSA P-256 --> RSU
    B -- trust decision within 100 ms --> B
    RSU -- aggregate and forward --> RSU
```
