# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define automotive cybersecurity as per ISO/SAE 21434. List the four security properties (CIA + A) that a connected vehicle must preserve.**

**Model Answer (≈ 110 words):**
Automotive cybersecurity, as defined by **ISO/SAE 21434:2021**, is a *risk-based engineering discipline* that ensures the confidentiality, integrity, availability, and authenticity of electrical and electronic systems in road vehicles throughout the entire lifecycle (concept → decommissioning). It mandates TARA, distributed CS management, and continual post-production monitoring to comply with UN R155.

The four security properties are:
- **C — Confidentiality:** Prevent unauthorized disclosure of driver data, GPS trails, biometrics.
- **I — Integrity:** Prevent unauthorized modification of CAN/Ethernet messages and firmware.
- **A — Availability:** Ensure safety-critical ECUs (brake, steer) function under attack.
- **A — Authenticity:** Verify that messages and software originate from legitimate sources.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*What is an ECU? Explain the role of an HSM in connected vehicle cybersecurity with two examples.**

**Model Answer (≈ 110 words):**
An **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** is an embedded microcontroller governing a specific vehicle function (e.g., brake, engine, infotainment). A modern car contains 100+ ECUs interconnected via CAN, CAN FD, LIN, FlexRay, and Automotive Ethernet.

A **Hardware Security Module (HSM)** is a tamper-resistant crypto coprocessor (often integrated into the MCU, e.g., Infineon Aurix TC3xx, NXP S32K3 HSM) that serves as the cryptographic *root of trust* in the vehicle.

Two examples of HSM use:
1. **Secure key storage** — Master keys and OEM private keys are stored in HSM-internal volatile memory and erased on tamper detection.
2. **Hardware-accelerated ECDSA** — V2X signature verification (≈10 ms) is offloaded to the HSM, freeing the main CPU for ADAS workloads.

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3, 14 marks]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **layered attack surface of a connected vehicle**, identifying at least **six distinct attack vectors** with one real-world example each. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the **TARA (Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment) methodology** as mandated by ISO/SAE 21434 Clause 15. Using a numerical example, demonstrate the computation of the **Risk Value (RV)** using the Impact × Attack Feasibility matrix. (7 marks)

---

### ✅ Model Solution to Question A

#### (a) Layered Attack Surface (7 marks)

A connected vehicle's attack surface must be analyzed in **five concentric layers**, moving from external wireless interfaces to backend infrastructure:

**Layer 1 — External Wireless Attack Vectors:**

1. **Remote Keyless Entry (RKE) Relay Attack:** The attacker uses a relay device to amplify the key fob's LF signal. The vehicle's body control module believes the fob is in proximity and unlocks. *Real-world example:* The 2011–2018 Volkswagen group vulnerability allowed relay unlock of 100 million vehicles; researchers cloned the key in seconds.
2. **Cellular Telematics Exploitation:** The TCU's 4G/5G modem is internet-reachable via the OEM's backend APIs. Vulnerabilities in the modem's IP stack or the OEM's API allow remote command execution. *Example:* The 2015 Tesla Model S hack via the cellular modem (Keen Security Lab) allowed remote control of brakes, sunroof, and door locks.
3. **Bluetooth Stack Exploits:** Infotainment Bluetooth parsers often contain memory-corruption bugs. *Example:* CVE-2021-3749 in a major OEM's IVI stack allowed RCE.
4. **Wi-Fi Hotspot Misuse:** The in-vehicle AP's default or weak WPA2 passphrase can be cracked.

**Layer 2 — Physical/Local Access Vectors:**

5. **OBD-II Port CAN Injection:** A direct physical connection to the OBD-II port (under the dashboard) allows arbitrary CAN frame injection. *Example:* Commercially available devices like CANtact, BUSMaster, and PEAK PCAN allow any garage to read/inject CAN.
6. **USB Media Port Exploit:** Maliciously crafted MP4/MKV files exploit media parser vulnerabilities, gaining code execution in the IVI. *Example:* Multiple QNX and Linux-based IVI CVEs.

**Layer 3 — In-Vehicle Network Attack Vectors:**

7. **CAN Frame Injection / Bus-Off Attack:** A malicious ECU can flood the bus or inject frames with high priority, denying service or spoofing messages.
8. **Automotive Ethernet MAC Flooding:** Without MACsec, an attacker on the same Ethernet segment can spoof MAC addresses.

**Layer 4 — Software & Supply Chain:**

9. **Malicious OTA Update:** Without proper signature verification, an attacker who hijacks the OEM's CDN can push a tampered firmware.
10. **Compromised Tier-1 Component:** A backdoored TCU or IVI shipped from a compromised supplier.

**Layer 5 — Backend & Cloud:**

11. **OEM API Account Takeover:** Stolen credentials, weak session management.

> **The block diagram** is similar to Figure 1 in Section 4.1 of these notes.

*(Examiner's note: 7 marks are awarded as 2 for the diagram + 2 for categorization into layers + 3 for six examples with real-world anchors. Students typically lose 1 mark by not citing a specific CVE/incident.)*

#### (b) TARA Methodology with Numerical Example (7 marks)

TARA per **ISO/SAE 21434 Clause 15** is a **five-stage systematic process**:

**Stage 1 — Item Definition:** Define the system boundary, functions, and interfaces. (e.g., "the brake-by-wire function from brake pedal to wheel actuators via CAN FD")

**Stage 2 — Asset Identification:** Enumerate ECUs (brake ECU, gateway), data (CAN frames, cryptographic keys), and software (firmware, bootloader).

**Stage 3 — Threat Scenario Identification:** Identify threat agents (remote attacker, insider), attack paths, and motivations.

**Stage 4 — Impact Rating:** Score impact as Negligible / Moderate / Major / Severe.

**Stage 5 — Risk Determination:** Compute **Attack Feasibility** as $F = \max(KL, WO, EQ)$, then look up **RV** in the matrix.

---

**Numerical Example:** Consider the threat *"remote attacker injects forged brake-torque CAN frames into the chassis CAN via the TCU."*

- **Impact (I):** Loss of life possible → $I = \text{Severe (4)}$
- **Knowledge (KL):** Attacker needs OEM-internal CAN DB → $KL = 2$
- **Window (WO):** Attack is remote, unlimited time → $WO = 0$
- **Equipment (EQ):** Cellular SDR + public CVE → $EQ = 1$
- **Feasibility:** $F = \max(2, 0, 1) = 2$ → Medium feasibility

From the RV matrix (Impact = Severe, F = Medium) → **RV = 6 (Critical)**

**Required Action:** A *Cybersecurity Goal* must be defined: *"Prevent unauthorized CAN frame injection into the chassis CAN."* A *Cybersecurity Requirement* is derived: *"Implement AUTOSAR SecOC with 8-byte truncated CMAC-AES-128 on all chassis CAN frames with period > 50 ms."* This CSR is then propagated to the brake ECU, gateway ECU, and validated by penetration testing.

---

### 📘 Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3, 14 marks]**

**(a)** Explain the **CIA + A security model** in the context of connected vehicles. For each property, give **one specific automotive example** of an attack and **one defense mechanism**. (7 marks)

**(b)** With reference to **AUTOSAR SecOC**, derive the structure of a **secure CAN FD frame** including the truncated MAC and freshness value. Show step-by-step how the receiver verifies the frame and explain why a 4-byte MAC may be insufficient for safety-critical messages. (7 marks)

---

### ✅ Model Solution to Question B

#### (a) CIA + A in Connected Vehicles (7 marks)

| Property | Definition | Automotive Attack Example | Defense Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Prevent unauthorized disclosure | Tracking a driver's home address from sniffed V2X BSMs (containing GPS coordinates) | **Encryption** of V2X payloads; pseudonym certificates rotated every 5 min |
| **Integrity** | Prevent unauthorized modification | Injecting forged CAN frame to spoof wheel-speed sensor, disabling ABS | **SecOC** (CMAC-AES-128) on safety-critical CAN frames |
| **Availability** | Ensure authorized access when needed | DoS attack on TCU by flooding cellular signaling, blocking eCall emergency call | **Network segregation** + **rate limiting** + **redundant comm paths** |
| **Authenticity** | Verify origin of data/software | Installing malicious firmware via forged OTA update | **Signed OTA** with ECDSA + secure boot anchored in HSM root of trust |

*(Examiner's note: 7 marks = 1 mark per example attack + 1 mark per defense + 1 mark for CIA+A introduction. Common mistake: students list "encryption" as a defense for integrity — encryption does NOT provide integrity, MAC/signatures do.)*

#### (b) AUTOSAR SecOC Secure CAN FD Frame (7 marks)

A standard CAN FD frame has a payload of up to **64 bytes**. AUTOSAR SecOC authenticates a subset of this payload.

**Input Fields:**
- $D$ = 16-bit Data ID (distinguishes message type)
- $FV$ = 64-bit Freshness Value (monotonic counter or synchronized timestamp)
- $P$ = payload bytes (data portion of CAN frame)
- $L$ = authenticator length (typically 4, 8, 12, or 16 bytes)
- $K$ = 128-bit symmetric session key (stored in HSM)

**Step 1 — Compute the full CMAC at the sender:**

$$
T_{\text{full}} = \text{CMAC}_{K}(D \ \Vert\ FV \ \Vert\ P) \quad [128 \ \text{bits}]
$$

**Step 2 — Truncate to $L$ bytes (most significant bytes):**

$$
T_{\text{trunc}} = T_{\text{full}}[0 : 8L] \quad \text{bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Append $T_{\text{trunc}}$ to the CAN FD frame** (the original payload $P$ is shortened by $L$ bytes to make room).

**Step 4 — Receiver verification:**
1. Extract $D$, $P$, $FV_{\text{received}}$, and $T_{\text{trunc,received}}$.
2. Retrieve local $FV_{\text{local}}$ (must be within an allowed drift window, typically $\pm 2$).
3. Retrieve $K$ from HSM.
4. Recompute $\hat{T}_{\text{full}} = \text{CMAC}_{K}(D \ \Vert\ FV_{\text{local}} \ \Vert\ P)$.
5. Truncate: $\hat{T}_{\text{trunc}} = \hat{T}_{\text{full}}[0 : 8L]$.
6. **Accept iff** $T_{\text{trunc,received}} = \hat{T}_{\text{trunc}}$.

**Why 4-byte MAC is insufficient for safety-critical messages:**
A 4-byte (32-bit) MAC provides $2^{32}$ possible tags. An attacker can try a forged frame with a random MAC; on average, success requires $2^{31} \approx 2.15 \times 10^9$ trials. On a high-speed CAN FD bus where frames arrive every 10 ms, a determined attacker can:
- Replay an old, valid frame and rely on the freshness counter to reject it (handled by FV).
- Brute-force within hours using an automated tool.
- The safety cost of even one accepted forged brake command is **catastrophic**.

Therefore, **8-byte (64-bit) truncated CMAC** is the automotive industry default for chassis / powertrain / ADAS domains. The 64-bit search space ($2^{63}$ expected trials) is computationally infeasible.

*(Examiner's note: 7 marks = 2 for input fields + 2 for sender/recv algorithm + 1 for forgery probability formula + 2 for 4 vs 8 byte discussion. Students commonly lose the "brute-force probability" step.)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students LOSE marks in cybersecurity questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "encryption" with "authentication"** — AES-ECB does NOT prevent forgery. Always pair with MAC. Examiners will deduct 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the *freshness value* in SecOC** — A MAC alone is vulnerable to replay attacks. The FV is the answer to "why doesn't SecOC get replayed?" Examiners love this.
> 3. **Citing "ISO 21434" without the "/SAE" prefix** — Always write **ISO/SAE 21434:2021**. The "/SAE" indicates joint publication with SAE International.
> 4. **Vague "blockchain" or "AI" answers** — Examiners reward engineering rigour (TARA matrix, SecOC, HSM). Avoid buzzword-only answers.
> 5. **Not drawing a block diagram for "Explain the architecture" questions** — A clear flowchart (see Section 4) earns 2 marks just for the diagram, even if the explanation is partial.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cybersecurity is a lifecycle discipline**, not a feature. ISO/SAE 21434 mandates it from concept to decommissioning.
- **CIA + A (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability, Authenticity)** is the foundation. Memorize one automotive example for each.
- **TARA is mandatory**: Item → Asset → Threat → Impact → Risk → Goal → Requirement → Validation. Know the $F = \max(KL, WO, EQ)$ rule.
- **UN R155 + ISO/SAE 21434** = regulatory + engineering backbone for the EU and most global markets.
- **CAN bus is broadcast and unauthenticated by default** — it must be augmented with **AUTOSAR SecOC (CMAC-AES-128 + freshness counter)**.
- **HSM is the root of trust** for keys, signatures, and secure boot. It is mandatory for any safety-critical ECU.
- **OTA updates must be signed and verified** — ECDSA P-256 over SHA-256 is the de-facto standard. Never flash unverified firmware.
- **V2X uses ECDSA P-256 with pseudonym certificates** rotated frequently for privacy, verified within 100 ms (10 Hz message rate).
- **Attack surface is layered**: External wireless → Physical → In-vehicle network → Supply chain → Cloud. Defense-in-depth is the only credible strategy.
- **No single defense is sufficient** — cross-reference the matrix in Section 3.5 to remember which control mitigates which vector.
- **Real-world anchors examiners love**: Jeep Cherokee Uconnect hack (2015), Tesla Model S Keen Lab hack (2015/2016), Volkswagen key-fob relay attack (2011–2018), BMW OTA misconfiguration (multiple).
- **Common abbreviations to spell out on first use**: ECU, HSM, TARA, OTA, V2X (V2V/V2I/V2P/V2G), CSMS, CSR, CSG, IDS/IPS, SOC, SecOC.
- **Two formulas to memorize cold**:
  - $F = \max(KL,\ WO,\ EQ)$ for attack feasibility
  - $N_{\text{trial}} = 2^{8L - 1}$ for brute-force forgery probability of a truncated $L$-byte MAC

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Over-the-Air (OTA) Updates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET414 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Over-the-Air (OTA) Updates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Over-the-Air (OTA) Updates** in modern automotive systems refer to the wireless, remote delivery and installation of software, firmware, calibration data, map data, and configuration parameters to in-vehicle Electronic Control Units (ECUs) and Head Units without requiring the vehicle to be physically present at a service center. OTA updates are governed by the **UNECE WP.29 R156 (Software Update Management System – SUMS)** regulation, **ISO/SAE 21434** cybersecurity engineering standard, and **ISO 24089** software update engineering standard.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, OTA is classified into four functional sub-domains:

- **SOTA (Software-Over-The-Air):** Update of infotainment, navigation, HMI, and user-facing applications.
- **FOTA (Firmware-Over-The-Air):** Update of low-level ECU firmware controlling powertrain, ADAS, braking, and battery management.
- **DOTA (Data-Over-The-Air):** Update of operational data such as calibration maps, AI/ML model weights, and diagnostic datasets.
- **MOTA (Map-Over-The-Air):** Update of HD maps and navigation map tiles for ADAS/AV systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:** *OTA Update is a cloud-to-vehicle (C2V) and vehicle-to-cloud (V2C) bidirectional communication process that uses cellular (4G/5G), Wi-Fi, or satellite networks to securely transmit, verify, and install software artefacts in automotive ECUs while preserving functional safety (ISO 26262) and cybersecurity (ISO/SAE 21434) compliance.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Smartphone Update, but for a 2-Tonne Machine"**
>
> Think of OTA like updating the operating system on your smartphone. You get a notification: *"iOS 17.4 available — Download and Install."* You tap *Download* (using Wi-Fi, not mobile data, to save cost), the phone verifies the update is genuine (Apple's signature), and during the night, it installs in the background and restarts.
>
> Now imagine the same workflow, but the "phone" is a car with **70–150 ECUs**, weighs **1,500 kg**, travels at **120 km/h**, and a failed update could cause a crash. This is OTA in automotive — the same convenience as a phone update, but with exponentially higher stakes, regulated by **UN R156**, and protected by cryptographic signing, rollback mechanisms, and dual-bank flash memory.

### 1.3 Key Physical / Network Constants and Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value (Bolded) |
|---|---|
| Typical OTA payload size | **1 GB – 5 GB** (full vehicle) |
| 4G LTE peak downlink | **150 Mbps** |
| 5G eMBB peak downlink | **1 Gbps – 10 Gbps** |
| Wi-Fi 6 throughput | **1.2 Gbps** |
| UNECE R156 compliance deadline | **July 2022** (EU) |
| Typical compression ratio | **30% – 50%** (zstd / LZ4) |
| Differential update reduction | **60% – 90%** (vs full image) |
| Cryptographic hash standard | **SHA-256** |
| Code signing standard | **ECDSA P-256 / RSA-2048** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

An OTA update is **not a single action** but a coordinated multi-stage pipeline. Each stage is independently fault-tolerant because the vehicle may disconnect at any point (entering a tunnel, parking in an underground garage, or losing cellular signal).

**Stage 1 — Campaign Authoring (Backend / OEM Cloud)**
- The OEM's R&D team creates a software release (a *delta* or *full* image).
- The artefact is **cryptographically signed** using a Hardware Security Module (HSM).
- A *campaign* is scheduled, defining target VINs, ECU list, rollout phase, and rollback policy.

**Stage 2 — Notification & User Consent (V2C)**
- The Telematics Control Unit (TCU) receives a push notification.
- Critical updates (safety recalls) may be **mandatory**; non-critical updates (infotainment) require **driver consent**.

**Stage 3 — Secure Download (C2V)**
- The TCU downloads the encrypted update bundle over TLS 1.3.
- Bandwidth management uses **delta updates** (only changed blocks transferred).
- Resume-from-checkpoint capability allows interruption without restart.

**Stage 4 — Verification & Authenticity Check**
- The TCU verifies the **digital signature** against the OEM's root certificate stored in the HSM.
- SHA-256 hash is recomputed and compared to the signed manifest.
- *If verification fails → update is discarded, alert raised to OEM backend.*

**Stage 5 — Pre-Installation Validation**
- Battery State of Charge (SoC) must be ≥ **30%** (typical OEM threshold).
- Vehicle must be in **Park** (P) gear, ignition ON, parking brake engaged.
- Engine OFF, or in case of BEV — not actively charging.

**Stage 6 — Installation (ECU Flash)**
- Uses **A/B partition scheme** (dual-bank flash): current OS runs from Bank A; new image is written to Bank B.
- After successful write, the bootloader flips the active pointer to Bank B.
- On next ignition cycle, the new firmware boots — this is the *fail-safe* model.

**Stage 7 — Post-Install Verification & Rollback**
- ECU runs a self-test and reports a *heartbeat* to the cloud.
- If a *Health Check* fails within a *rollback window* (typically **3–7 days**), the bootloader reverts to the previous known-good partition (Bank A).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are exam-portable. Use the unit table to avoid dimensional errors.**

| # | Formula / Relationship | Symbol Definitions | Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{dl} = \dfrac{S_{pkg}}{R_{net} \cdot \eta_{comp}}$ | $T_{dl}$ = download time, $S_{pkg}$ = package size, $R_{net}$ = network rate, $\eta_{comp}$ = compression efficiency factor | seconds | Estimate customer wait time during update |
| 2 | $S_{\Delta} = S_{full} \cdot (1 - r_{delta})$ | $S_{\Delta}$ = delta size, $S_{full}$ = full image, $r_{delta}$ = delta reduction ratio | bytes | Calculate bandwidth savings |
| 3 | $R_{eff} = R_{raw} \cdot (1 - \epsilon_{loss})$ | $R_{eff}$ = effective throughput, $R_{raw}$ = raw link rate, $\epsilon_{loss}$ = packet loss fraction | Mbps | Predict real download under poor signal |
| 4 | $E_{OTA} = P_{TCU} \cdot T_{dl}$ | $E_{OTA}$ = energy consumed, $P_{TCU}$ = TCU power draw (~5 W) | Joules | EV battery impact assessment |
| 5 | $N_{fail} = 1 - (1-p)^n$ | $N_{fail}$ = cumulative failure probability, $p$ = per-block fail rate, $n$ = number of blocks | dimensionless | Reliability of large-file transfer |
| 6 | $H = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $H$ = Shannon entropy of firmware image | bits/byte | Compression ceiling bound |
| 7 | $T_{total} = T_{dl} + T_{verify} + T_{install} + T_{reboot}$ | Total update wall-clock time | seconds | UX / customer experience design |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

OTA is the **enabling technology** for the *Software-Defined Vehicle (SDV)* paradigm. It allows OEMs like Tesla, BYD, and Mercedes-Benz to push new features (autopilot improvements, range extensions, new UI themes) to **millions of vehicles** post-sale — converting the vehicle into a continuously evolving platform. OTA also dramatically reduces **recall costs** (industry average: ~**$500 per vehicle** for dealer-based recall vs. near-zero marginal cost for OTA).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total OTA Update Time Under Variable Network Conditions

**Problem Statement:** An OEM pushes a **3.2 GB** full-vehicle firmware image to a fleet. The OTA bundle is compressed using LZ4 with efficiency $\eta_{comp} = 0.35$ (i.e., compressed size is 35% of original). The vehicle is connected to a **4G LTE** link rated at $R_{raw} = 100$ Mbps, but signal conditions give a packet loss fraction $\epsilon_{loss} = 0.05$. Compute the effective download time $T_{dl}$.

**Step 1 — Establish the compressed package size:**

$$S_{comp} = S_{full} \cdot \eta_{comp} = 3.2 \text{ GB} \times 0.35$$

$$S_{comp} = 1.12 \text{ GB} = 1.12 \times 8 \times 10^9 \text{ bits} = 8.96 \times 10^9 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the effective throughput accounting for packet loss:**

$$R_{eff} = R_{raw} \cdot (1 - \epsilon_{loss}) = 100 \text{ Mbps} \times (1 - 0.05)$$

$$R_{eff} = 100 \times 0.95 = 95 \text{ Mbps} = 95 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/sec}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the download time:**

$$T_{dl} = \frac{S_{comp}}{R_{eff}} = \frac{8.96 \times 10^9 \text{ bits}}{95 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/sec}}$$

$$T_{dl} = 94.31 \text{ seconds} \approx 1.57 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 4 — Add verification, install, and reboot overheads (typical values):**

$$T_{verify} = 20 \text{ s}, \quad T_{install} = 240 \text{ s}, \quad T_{reboot} = 30 \text{ s}$$

$$T_{total} = T_{dl} + T_{verify} + T_{install} + T_{reboot}$$

$$T_{total} = 94.31 + 20 + 240 + 30 = 384.31 \text{ s} \approx 6.41 \text{ minutes}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The download stage is **NOT** the bottleneck — installation and verification dominate. This is why OTA architectures use *background download* and *deferred install* models. The vehicle downloads at night (parked at home Wi-Fi) but installs only when the user accepts the prompt and the SoC threshold is met.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Delta Update Bandwidth Savings

**Problem Statement:** A navigation head-unit image is $S_{full} = 4$ GB. After a software revision, **only 12%** of the image blocks have changed. Using a binary delta algorithm (bsdiff), the delta file size is $S_{\Delta} = S_{full} \times 0.18$ (empirically, delta ≈ 1.5× the changed portion due to control metadata). Compute the percentage bandwidth saving $B_{save}$ and the new download time on a **20 Mbps** stable Wi-Fi link (no loss).

**Step 1 — Compute the delta file size:**

$$S_{\Delta} = 4 \text{ GB} \times 0.18 = 0.72 \text{ GB} = 5.76 \times 10^9 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the saving ratio:**

$$B_{save} = \frac{S_{full} - S_{\Delta}}{S_{full}} \times 100\% = \frac{4 - 0.72}{4} \times 100\%$$

$$B_{save} = \frac{3.28}{4} \times 100\% = 82\%$$

**Step 3 — Compute new download time on 20 Mbps link:**

$$T_{dl,\Delta} = \frac{S_{\Delta}}{R_{net}} = \frac{5.76 \times 10^9}{20 \times 10^6} = 288 \text{ s} = 4.8 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 4 — Compare to full update (uncompressed on same link):**

$$T_{dl,full} = \frac{4 \times 8 \times 10^9}{20 \times 10^6} = 1600 \text{ s} \approx 26.67 \text{ minutes}$$

$$\Delta T = T_{dl,full} - T_{dl,\Delta} = 1312 \text{ s} \approx 21.87 \text{ minutes saved}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Delta updates reduce bandwidth consumption by **82%** and time-to-update by **82%**. This is why every major OEM (Tesla, Toyota, GM, Hyundai) uses delta-based OTA, especially for repeat updates.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — SHA-256 Integrity Verification Complexity

**Problem Statement:** For a 3.2 GB firmware image, the SHA-256 hash must be computed during download. SHA-256 processes data in **64-byte (512-bit) blocks**. Compute the number of hashing operations $N_h$ and the expected time on a TCU-class ARM Cortex-A53 processor running SHA-256 at throughput $R_{hash} = 200$ MB/s.

**Step 1 — Compute the number of 512-bit blocks:**

$$S_{full} = 3.2 \text{ GB} = 3.2 \times 2^{30} \text{ bytes} = 3.435 \times 10^9 \text{ bytes}$$

$$N_h = \left\lceil \frac{S_{full}}{64} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{3.435 \times 10^9}{64} \right\rceil \approx 5.368 \times 10^7 \text{ blocks}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the time to hash:**

$$T_{hash} = \frac{S_{full}}{R_{hash}} = \frac{3.2 \times 10^9 \text{ bytes}}{200 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes/s}} = 16 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 3 — Compare to download time on slow 4G (5 Mbps in poor coverage):**

$$T_{dl,slow} = \frac{3.2 \times 8 \times 10^9}{5 \times 10^6} = 5120 \text{ s} \approx 85.3 \text{ minutes}$$

$$\therefore T_{hash} \ll T_{dl,slow}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Hashing is **never the bottleneck**. It is computed in *streaming* mode alongside the download — verification is essentially "free" relative to network latency.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: SOTA vs FOTA vs MOTA vs DOTA

| Dimension | SOTA | FOTA | MOTA | DOTA |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Target ECU | IVI / Head Unit | Powertrain, ADAS, BCM | Navigation Server | Telematics, AI ECUs |
| Payload Size | 200 MB – 2 GB | 1 GB – 5 GB | 500 MB – 8 GB | 100 MB – 2 GB |
| Failure Impact | Low (UX) | **Critical (Safety)** | Low (Routing) | Medium (AI accuracy) |
| User Consent | Optional | Often Mandatory | Optional | Optional |
| UN R156 Scope | Yes | Yes (strict) | Yes | Yes |
| Boot Mode | Background OK | **Drive-to-safe-state** | Background OK | Background OK |
| Rollback | Auto (A/B) | **Mandatory (A/B + watchdog)** | Auto | Auto |
| Network Typical | Wi-Fi / 5G | 4G/5G (campaign-managed) | Wi-Fi (large files) | 5G (low-latency) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End OTA System Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[OEM Backend Cloud] --> B[OTA Campaign Manager]
    B --> C[HSM Signing Module]
    C --> D[CDN Edge Servers]
    D --> E[Cellular Network 4G / 5G]
    E --> F[Telematics Control Unit TCU]
    F --> G[In-Vehicle Gateway ECU]
    G --> H1[IVI Head Unit SOTA]
    G --> H2[Powertrain ECU FOTA]
    G --> H3[ADAS Domain Controller FOTA]
    G --> H4[Map Server MOTA]
    H1 --> I1[A Bank Active]
    H2 --> I2[A Bank Active]
    H3 --> I3[A Bank Active]
    H4 --> I4[A Bank Active]
    I1 --> J1[B Bank New Image]
    I2 --> J2[B Bank New Image]
    I3 --> J3[B Bank New Image]
    I4 --> J4[B Bank New Image]
    J1 --> K1[Bootloader Verification]
    J2 --> K2[Bootloader Verification]
    J3 --> K3[Bootloader Verification]
    J4 --> K4[Bootloader Verification]
    K1 --> L[Rollback Decision Engine]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    K4 --> L
    L --> M[Health Check Telemetry to Cloud]
    M --> A
```

**Figure 4.1 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Automotive OTA Pipeline.** The diagram traces the journey of a signed software artefact from the OEM's cloud (top) down through the cellular network, the TCU, an in-vehicle gateway (which routes traffic to multiple ECUs), dual-bank flash partitions (A = currently active, B = new image being written), a bootloader verification stage, a rollback decision engine, and finally a telemetry loop back to the cloud.

---

### 4.2 OTA Update State Machine (Per-ECU Lifecycle)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IdleState
    IdleState --> NotifiedState : Push notification received
    NotifiedState --> DownloadingState : User consents / mandatory
    DownloadingState --> PausedDownloadState : Signal lost
    PausedDownloadState --> DownloadingState : Signal restored
    DownloadingState --> VerifyingState : Download complete
    VerifyingState --> IdleState : Hash mismatch / signature invalid
    VerifyingState --> PreCheckState : Signature valid
    PreCheckState --> IdleState : SoC low or vehicle not in Park
    PreCheckState --> InstallingState : Preconditions met
    InstallingState --> RebootingState : Flash write complete
    RebootingState --> HealthCheckState : Boot success
    HealthCheckState --> IdleState : Healthy
    HealthCheckState --> RollingBackState : Fault detected in window
    RollingBackState --> IdleState : Bank A restored
    IdleState --> [*]
```

**Figure 4.2 — Per-ECU OTA State Machine.** Each ECU transitions through eleven well-defined states, with self-recovery paths (e.g., *PausedDownloadState* on signal loss, *RollingBackState* on health-check failure). This is a direct implementation of the **ISO 24089** software update lifecycle specification.

---

### 4.3 Security & Cryptographic Layer (Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[OEM Dev Server] --> A2[Build Artefact]
    A2 --> A3[HSM Private Key Sign]
    A3 --> A4[Signed Bundle]
    A4 --> B1[TLS 1.3 Encrypted Channel]
    B1 --> C1[TCU Receives]
    C1 --> C2[OEM Root Cert in HSM]
    C2 --> C3[ECDSA Signature Verify]
    C3 --> D1{Valid}
    D1 -->|Yes| D2[Proceed to Flash]
    D1 -->|No| D3[Discard and Alert]
    C1 --> C4[SHA-256 Hash Compute]
    C4 --> C5[Compare to Signed Manifest]
    C5 --> D1
```

**Figure 4.3 — Cryptographic Trust Chain in OTA.** The chain shows how an OEM artefact is signed in an HSM (Hardware Security Module), transmitted over TLS 1.3, and verified on-vehicle using a root certificate also stored in the TCU's HSM. Both signature and hash must validate before flash is permitted.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define OTA updates in the context of modern automotive systems. Differentiate between SOTA and FOTA with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Over-the-Air (OTA) updates refer to the wireless remote delivery, verification, and installation of software, firmware, calibration data, and map data to in-vehicle ECUs without requiring a physical visit to a service centre.* **(1 mark)**

**SOTA (Software-Over-The-Air):** Updates user-facing application layer software on the Infotainment / Head Unit. *Example: Updating the Android Automotive OS from version 12 to 13, or adding a new Spotify integration.* **(1 mark)**

**FOTA (Firmware-Over-The-Air):** Updates low-level firmware on safety-critical ECUs. *Example: Reflashing the Battery Management System (BMS) firmware on a BEV to improve State-of-Health estimation accuracy.* **(1 mark)**

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**State any three functional requirements of an OTA Software Update Management System as per UNECE WP.29 R156.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Documented Software Identification (RXSWIN):** Each vehicle must have a unique, traceable software identifier (Regulation X Software Identification Number) recorded in a backend registry. **(1 mark)**

2. **Integrity & Authenticity:** The OEM must protect update packages against corruption and unauthorized modification using cryptographic signing (e.g., ECDSA) and verification. **(1 mark)**

3. **Rollback & Safe State:** The system must be capable of restoring the previously approved software version if the new version fails to function correctly, ensuring the vehicle does not become unsafe. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 3 Internal Choice]

**(a)** Explain the end-to-end architecture of an automotive OTA update system with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of the **Telematics Control Unit (TCU)** and the **A/B partition (dual-bank flash)** mechanism. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

An automotive OTA architecture is a four-tier system: **(1 mark)**

- **Tier 1 — OEM Cloud / OTA Backend:** Hosts the campaign manager, software repository, signing service, and CDN. Responsible for staging, signing, and rolling out updates.
- **Tier 2 — Communication Network:** 4G/5G cellular, Wi-Fi, or satellite — provides the C2V downlink.
- **Tier 3 — Vehicle Edge:** The **Telematics Control Unit (TCU)** acts as the secure gateway. It manages cellular connectivity, TLS termination, signature verification, and orchestrates the download to multiple ECUs via the in-vehicle network (CAN / Ethernet / CAN-FD). **(1 mark)**
- **Tier 4 — Target ECUs:** Each ECU has a secure bootloader and dual-bank flash memory.

**Role of the TCU:** It is the *single point of contact* with the cloud. It enforces firewalling (no ECU is directly internet-exposed), handles certificate storage in its HSM, performs pre-condition checks (battery SoC, gear position), and serves as the *orchestrator* that issues flash commands to downstream ECUs over CAN/Ethernet. **(2 marks)**

**A/B Partition Mechanism:** Each ECU has two flash banks — Bank A (currently running) and Bank B (staging area). When a new image arrives: **(1 mark)**

1. The TCU writes the new firmware to Bank B while the ECU continues running from Bank A (zero downtime). **(1 mark)**
2. After a successful write and verification, the bootloader updates the *active-bank pointer* to Bank B.
3. On next ignition, the new firmware boots from Bank B.
4. If a health-check fails within the rollback window, the pointer is flipped back to Bank A — restoring the prior known-good firmware. **(1 mark)**

---

**(b)** A **3.5 GB** full-vehicle firmware image is to be delivered OTA. The compression efficiency factor is **0.40**, the 4G link provides **80 Mbps** raw throughput, and the packet loss fraction is **0.04**. Compute the **download time** and the **total wall-clock time** including $T_{verify} = 25$ s, $T_{install} = 300$ s, and $T_{reboot} = 45$ s. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compressed package size:** **(1 mark)**

$$S_{comp} = S_{full} \times \eta_{comp} = 3.5 \times 0.40 = 1.40 \text{ GB} = 1.40 \times 8 \times 10^9 = 1.12 \times 10^{10} \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Effective throughput:** **(1 mark)**

$$R_{eff} = R_{raw} \times (1 - \epsilon_{loss}) = 80 \times (1 - 0.04) = 80 \times 0.96 = 76.8 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 3 — Download time:** **(1 mark)**

$$T_{dl} = \frac{S_{comp}}{R_{eff}} = \frac{1.12 \times 10^{10}}{76.8 \times 10^6} = 145.83 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Verification time:** **(1 mark)**

$$T_{verify} = 25 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5 — Installation time:** **(1 mark)**

$$T_{install} = 300 \text{ s}$$

**Step 6 — Reboot time:** **(1 mark)**

$$T_{reboot} = 45 \text{ s}$$

**Step 7 — Total wall-clock time:** **(1 mark)**

$$T_{total} = 145.83 + 25 + 300 + 45 = 515.83 \text{ s} \approx 8.6 \text{ minutes}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 | Module 3 Internal Choice]

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **per-ECU OTA state machine** and the **cryptographic trust chain** used to secure OTA updates. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Per-ECU State Machine (refer Figure 4.2):** The ECU progresses through the following states: **(1 mark)**

`Idle → Notified → Downloading → Verifying → PreCheck → Installing → Rebooting → HealthCheck → Idle`

- *Notified:* Push notification from cloud via TCU.
- *Downloading:* Bundle received in segments with resume support.
- *Verifying:* ECDSA signature check + SHA-256 hash match.
- *PreCheck:* SoC ≥ threshold, gear = P, parking brake engaged.
- *Installing:* Flash write to inactive bank (A or B).
- *Rebooting:* Bootloader switches active pointer.
- *HealthCheck:* ECU runs self-diagnostics; reports heartbeat to cloud.
- *Rollback (off-diagonal):* Triggered if health check fails within the rollback window — reverts to prior bank. **(2 marks)**

**Cryptographic Trust Chain (refer Figure 4.3):** **(1 mark)**

- Artefact is built on the OEM dev server, then signed inside a **Hardware Security Module (HSM)** using the OEM's **private key** (ECDSA P-256 or RSA-2048).
- The signed bundle is transmitted over **TLS 1.3** (transport-layer encryption).
- The TCU's HSM holds the OEM's **public root certificate** — used to verify the signature. **(1 mark)**
- Simultaneously, the TCU computes a **SHA-256 hash** of the received bytes and compares it with the hash in the signed manifest.
- *Only if both checks pass* is the artefact accepted for flashing; otherwise it is discarded and an alert is raised. **(1 mark)**

This *defence-in-depth* — combining transport encryption (TLS), asymmetric signing (ECDSA), and integrity hashing (SHA-256) — aligns with **ISO/SAE 21434** cybersecurity requirements. **(1 mark)**

---

**(b)** Discuss **three major challenges** of implementing OTA updates in modern vehicles and the corresponding engineering solutions adopted. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Challenge 1 — Cybersecurity and Unauthorized Access** **(2 marks)**
- *Problem:* An attacker intercepting the OTA channel or crafting a malicious update could gain control of safety-critical ECUs.
- *Solution:* End-to-end cryptographic trust chain (ECDSA + SHA-256 + TLS 1.3), HSM-protected key storage, and Secure Boot in every ECU. Compliance with **ISO/SAE 21434** and **UN R156** is mandatory.

**Challenge 2 — Functional Safety during Update** **(2 marks)**
- *Problem:* If an ECU is flashed while the vehicle is moving, or if the new firmware is defective, catastrophic failure may occur.
- *Solution:* (i) Pre-condition checks (SoC ≥ 30%, gear = P, ignition = ON); (ii) **A/B partition dual-bank flash** allowing zero-downtime writes; (iii) **rollback window** (typically 3–7 days) where the bootloader can revert to the prior known-good image; (iv) compliance with **ISO 26262** ASIL decomposition.

**Challenge 3 — Bandwidth, Cost, and Connectivity** **(2 marks)**
- *Problem:* A full-vehicle image of 3–5 GB is expensive to deliver over metered 4G and impossible in low-signal areas.
- *Solution:* (i) **Delta updates** (60–90% size reduction); (ii) **LZ4/zstd compression**; (iii) **Wi-Fi-only mode for non-critical updates**; (iv) **5G eMBB** rollout reducing time-to-update; (v) **resume-from-checkpoint** for spotty cellular coverage.

*(Closing integration statement — 1 mark):* Together, these solutions make OTA both **safe** and **economically viable**, enabling the Software-Defined Vehicle (SDV) business model where the vehicle receives post-sale feature upgrades throughout its lifecycle.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Do NOT confuse SOTA with FOTA.** SOTA = application layer; FOTA = firmware layer. Examiners will deduct **1 mark** for this conflation.
> 2. **Always include units** in numerical answers. A bare number with no unit (e.g., writing "145.83" without "seconds") attracts a **0.5 mark** deduction.
> 3. **Mention UN R156 and ISO/SAE 21434** by name in any question on OTA security or compliance. The examiner's key has these as *must-mention* keywords.
> 4. **Show all substitutions** in numerical problems. Writing only the final answer loses the **method marks** (typically 1–2 marks per sub-part).
> 5. **For diagrams:** Do NOT skip labels on the A/B bank or the TCU position. Unlabelled blocks in the architecture diagram = **at least 1 mark** lost.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — KTU 2024 Scheme**

- [ ] **OTA = wireless remote SW/FW delivery to vehicle ECUs** — *no service-centre visit required.*
- [ ] **Four types: SOTA, FOTA, DOTA, MOTA** — know one example for each.
- [ ] **Key standards:** **UNECE WP.29 R156 (SUMS)**, **ISO/SAE 21434 (cybersecurity)**, **ISO 24089 (SW update engineering)**, **ISO 26262 (functional safety)**.
- [ ] **Telematics Control Unit (TCU)** is the *gatekeeper* — sole internet-facing ECU.
- [ ] **A/B partition (dual-bank flash)** = zero-downtime, fail-safe update with rollback capability.
- [ ] **Cryptographic chain:** ECDSA (or RSA) signing + SHA-256 hashing + TLS 1.3 transport, with keys in **HSM**.
- [ ] **Pre-conditions for install:** SoC ≥ 30%, gear = Park, ignition ON, parking brake engaged.
- [ ] **Compression:** LZ4 / zstd → **30–50%** of original size.
- [ ] **Delta updates:** **60–90%** bandwidth reduction by transmitting only changed blocks.
- [ ] **Download time formula (must memorize):** $T_{dl} = \dfrac{S_{pkg}}{R_{net} \cdot \eta_{comp}}$
- [ ] **Effective throughput under loss:** $R_{eff} = R_{raw} \cdot (1 - \epsilon_{loss})$
- [ ] **Rollback window:** typically **3–7 days** post-installation.
- [ ] **OTA enables Software-Defined Vehicle (SDV)** — feature upgrades and bug fixes over the vehicle's entire lifecycle.
- [ ] **Industry impact:** Reduces recall costs from ~$500/vehicle (dealer-based) to near-zero marginal cost (OTA).
- [ ] **Mandatory vs Optional updates:** Safety/security recalls = **mandatory**; infotainment/UI = **user-consented**.
- [ ] **Network options:** 4G LTE, 5G eMBB, Wi-Fi 6, Satellite — trade-off latency vs coverage vs cost.
- [ ] **Verification is NOT the bottleneck** — hashing time ≪ network download time in 99% of cases.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** 5G in Automotive Communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **5G in Automotive Communication** refers to the integration of the **5th Generation (5G) New Radio (NR)** cellular technology, standardized by **3GPP Releases 15, 16, 17, and 18**, into vehicular ecosystems to enable ultra-reliable, low-latency, high-bandwidth **Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X)** communication. It supports **C-V2X (Cellular-V2X)** over both **PC5 (sidelink)** and **Uu (cellular uplink/downlink)** interfaces, facilitating autonomous driving, cooperative intelligent transport systems (C-ITS), advanced telematics, and software-defined vehicle operations.

### Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Standardized)

| Term | Full Form | KTU Definition |
|------|-----------|----------------|
| **V2X** | Vehicle-to-Everything | Umbrella term covering all vehicular communication paradigms |
| **C-V2X** | Cellular-V2X | 3GPP-defined cellular-based V2X standard |
| **NR-V2X** | New Radio-V2X | 5G NR-based sidelink communication (Release 16+) |
| **PC5** | Proximity Communication 5 | Direct (sidelink) interface between devices without network |
| **Uu** | Uu Interface | Traditional cellular air interface (device ↔ gNB) |
| **gNB** | next Generation Node B | 5G NR base station |
| **MEC** | Multi-access Edge Computing | Distributed computing at the network edge for low latency |
| **URLLC** | Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communication | 5G service class for mission-critical automotive apps |
| **eMBB** | enhanced Mobile BroadBand | 5G service class for high-throughput apps (infotainment) |
| **mMTC** | massive Machine-Type Communication | 5G service class for massive IoT sensor connectivity |
| **QoS** | Quality of Service | Performance guarantees on latency, reliability, throughput |
| **OTA** | Over-The-Air | Remote software/firmware updates via wireless networks |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Highway Conductor" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: 5G as the Orchestra Conductor of a Smart Highway**
>
> Imagine a busy intersection where hundreds of cars, pedestrians, traffic lights, and emergency vehicles must coordinate in perfect harmony — without crashing. Earlier systems (like **4G** or **DSRC**) are like having each car drive blindfolded with a one-way walkie-talkie — slow, lossy, and uncoordinated.
>
> **5G** acts like an **omniscient orchestral conductor** with superhuman reflexes:
> - Every car gets a **dedicated wireless channel (sidelink PC5)** to whisper to nearby vehicles in **< 3 ms**.
> - A central **edge computing server (MEC)** at the roadside sees the whole intersection and tells cars when to brake.
> - Massive data (HD maps, 4K camera feeds, LiDAR clouds) flows simultaneously thanks to **high bandwidth (peak ~20 Gbps)**.
> - The "conductor" guarantees every note (packet) arrives with **99.999% reliability**.
>
> Result: **Zero-accident autonomous driving, real-time traffic optimization, and cinema-quality entertainment on wheels.**

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Board Emphasis)

- **5G Frequency Range 1 (FR1):** **Sub-6 GHz** (450 MHz – 6 GHz) — better propagation, used for coverage
- **5G Frequency Range 2 (FR2):** **mmWave (24.25 – 52.6 GHz)** — high bandwidth, short range
- **Target End-to-End Latency:** **< 1 ms** (URLLC) ; **< 20 ms** (eMBB); **3 ms** (V2X sidelink)
- **Peak Data Rate:** **20 Gbps downlink / 10 Gbps uplink**
- **Reliability Target:** **99.999%** (5 nines) for safety-critical V2X
- **Mobility Support:** Up to **500 km/h** (designed for high-speed vehicular mobility)
- **Connection Density:** **10⁶ devices/km²** for mMTC

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** Always mention **3GPP Release 16 (2020)** as the foundational 5G NR-V2X release. Release 17 adds **sidelink relaying** and **NR sidelink for FR2**, while Release 18 (5G-Advanced) introduces **AI/ML-driven V2X**.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of 5G Automotive Communication

The 5G automotive ecosystem is organized into **three operational layers**:

### Layer 1 — Communication Layer (Physical + MAC)
- **Sidelink (PC5 interface):** Direct device-to-device communication for short-range V2V/V2I/V2P
- **Uu interface:** Traditional cellular uplink/downlink via gNB
- Uses **OFDM (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing)** with flexible numerology (subcarrier spacings: **15, 30, 60, 120, 240 kHz**)

### Layer 2 — Network Layer (3GPP Core)
- **5GC (5G Core)** with **Service-Based Architecture (SBA)**
- Supports **Network Slicing** — virtual dedicated networks per use case
- Integrates **MEC** for sub-10 ms processing at the edge

### Layer 3 — Application Layer (Automotive Services)
- **Safety:** Collision avoidance, cooperative adaptive cruise control (C-ACC)
- **Convenience:** Platooning, HD map distribution
- **Infotainment:** Streaming, AR/VR dashboards
- **Operations:** OTA updates, predictive maintenance

---

## 2.2 V2X Communication Types — The 5 V's Framework

| Type | Full Form | Role in 5G Automotive |
|------|-----------|------------------------|
| **V2V** | Vehicle-to-Vehicle | Car ↔ Car cooperative collision avoidance |
| **V2I** | Vehicle-to-Infrastructure | Car ↔ Roadside Unit (RSU) / traffic light |
| **V2N** | Vehicle-to-Network | Car ↔ Cloud (navigation, OTA) |
| **V2P** | Vehicle-to-Pedestrian | Car ↔ Pedestrian smartphone (safety) |
| **V2D** | Vehicle-to-Device | Car ↔ Drone / IoT sensor |
| **V2X** | Vehicle-to-Everything | Umbrella for all the above |

---

## 2.3 5G Service Classes Mapped to Automotive Use Cases

| 5G Service Class | Automotive Use Case | Latency Target | Reliability |
|------------------|---------------------|----------------|-------------|
| **URLLC** | Collision avoidance, automated braking | **1–5 ms** | **99.999%** |
| **eMBB** | HD map download, 4K infotainment | **< 20 ms** | High throughput |
| **mMTC** | Massive sensor arrays, fleet telematics | Seconds | Connection density |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and based on 3GPP TR 38.913 / TR 38.802 specifications.

### Shannon-Hartley Theorem (Channel Capacity)

The maximum achievable data rate over a noisy channel is given by:

$$
C = B \cdot \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)
$$

Where:
- $C$ = channel capacity (bits per second, bps)
- $B$ = bandwidth (Hz)
- $S/N$ = signal-to-noise ratio (dimensionless, linear scale)

### Maximum Spectral Efficiency Bound

$$
\text{SE}_{\max} = \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) \;\; \text{[bits/s/Hz]}
$$

### 5G Peak Data Rate Formula (3GPP TR 38.913)

$$
R_{\text{peak}} = N_{\text{streams}} \cdot N_{\text{PRB}} \cdot N_{\text{sc}}^{\text{RB}} \cdot N_{\text{sym}}^{\text{sub}} \cdot N_{\text{slots}}^{\text{frame}} \cdot Q_m \cdot R_{\max}
$$

Where:
- $N_{\text{streams}}$ = number of MIMO layers (max **12** for FR2)
- $N_{\text{PRB}}$ = allocated Physical Resource Blocks (max **273** for FR1)
- $N_{\text{sc}}^{\text{RB}} = 12$ subcarriers per RB
- $N_{\text{sym}}^{\text{sub}} = 14$ OFDM symbols per subframe
- $N_{\text{slots}}^{\text{frame}} = 10 \cdot 2^\mu$ slots per 10 ms frame (numerology $\mu$)
- $Q_m$ = modulation order (max **8** for 256-QAM; **10** for 1024-QAM in 5G-Advanced)
- $R_{\max} = 948/1024$ = maximum coding rate

### Latency Budget (One-Way Air-Interface)

$$
T_{\text{latency}} = T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{proc}} + T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{tx}}
$$

Where:
- $T_{\text{prop}} = d / c$ = propagation delay (speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s)
- $T_{\text{proc}}$ = processing delay (gNB + UE)
- $T_{\text{queue}}$ = queuing delay in buffers
- $T_{\text{tx}}$ = transmission time = $\frac{\text{packet size}}{\text{data rate}}$

### Subcarrier Spacing in 5G NR

$$
\Delta f = 2^\mu \cdot 15 \text{ kHz}, \quad \mu \in \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\}
$$

| $\mu$ | $\Delta f$ | Use Case |
|-------|-----------|----------|
| 0 | 15 kHz | FR1, eMBB coverage |
| 1 | 30 kHz | FR1, balanced |
| 2 | 60 kHz | FR1, URLLC low-latency |
| 3 | 120 kHz | mmWave (FR2) |
| 4 | 240 kHz | FR2 extreme low-latency |

### Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) — Critical for mmWave V2X

$$
\text{FSPL (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)
$$

Or equivalently:

$$
\text{FSPL (dB)} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}})
$$

Where $f_{\text{MHz}}$ is frequency in MHz and $d_{\text{km}}$ is distance in km.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** At **28 GHz mmWave**, the path loss at 100 m is ~**108 dB**, whereas at **3.5 GHz sub-6**, it is ~**89 dB**. This is why mmWave requires **beamforming** for V2X at highway speeds.

### C-V2X vs. DSRC Comparison (High-Yield Board Table)

| Parameter | DSRC (802.11p) | C-V2X (4G LTE) | 5G NR-V2X |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|-----------|
| Latency | ~20 ms | ~20 ms | **< 3 ms** |
| Bandwidth | 75 MHz (5.9 GHz) | Up to 20 MHz | **Up to 100 MHz (FR1)** / **400 MHz (FR2)** |
| Range | ~300 m | ~500 m | **~1 km** (with sidelink) |
| Peak Data Rate | 27 Mbps | 150 Mbps | **20 Gbps** (theoretical) |
| Reliability | 95% | 99.9% | **99.999%** |
| 3GPP Release | — | Rel. 14 | **Rel. 16+** |
| Mobility | 60 km/h | 140 km/h | **500 km/h** |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Achievable Data Rate for a 5G V2X Channel

### Problem Statement
A 5G V2X system operates over a channel of bandwidth $B = 20$ MHz in a dense urban environment. The measured signal-to-noise ratio at the receiver (after beamforming gain) is $\text{SNR} = 12$ (linear). Compute the **maximum theoretical data rate** according to Shannon's theorem.

---

### Step 1 — Identify the Formula

By Shannon-Hartley theorem:

$$
C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Numerical Values

$$
C = (20 \times 10^6) \cdot \log_2(1 + 12)
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Inner Argument

$$
1 + \text{SNR} = 1 + 12 = 13
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Binary Logarithm

$$
\log_2(13) = \frac{\ln(13)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{2.5649}{0.6931} = 3.7004 \text{ bits/s/Hz}
$$

### Step 5 — Multiply by Bandwidth

$$
C = 20 \times 10^6 \times 3.7004
$$

$$
C = 74.008 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}
$$

### Step 6 — Final Result (Rounded for KTU Board)

$$
\boxed{C \approx 74.0 \text{ Mbps}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Tip:** Always convert SNR from dB to linear using $\text{SNR}_{\text{linear}} = 10^{(\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}}/10)}$ if the question provides decibels. Example: 10 dB $\to$ 10 (linear); 20 dB $\to$ 100 (linear).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — 5G NR Peak Data Rate (Sub-6 GHz, FR1, Numerology μ=1)

### Problem Statement
Compute the theoretical **downlink peak data rate** for a 5G NR system operating in FR1 with:
- Numerology $\mu = 1$ (subcarrier spacing $\Delta f = 30$ kHz)
- Bandwidth = 100 MHz → $N_{\text{PRB}} = 273$ (maximum FR1 allocation)
- $N_{\text{sc}}^{\text{RB}} = 12$, $N_{\text{sym}}^{\text{sub}} = 14$, $Q_m = 8$ (256-QAM), $R_{\max} = 948/1024$
- MIMO layers $N_{\text{streams}} = 4$ (typical automotive-grade modem)

---

### Step 1 — Slots per Frame

$$
N_{\text{slots}}^{\text{frame}} = 10 \cdot 2^\mu = 10 \cdot 2^1 = 20 \text{ slots per 10 ms}
$$

### Step 2 — Total Resource Elements per Frame (per Layer)

$$
N_{\text{RE}} = N_{\text{PRB}} \cdot N_{\text{sc}}^{\text{RB}} \cdot N_{\text{sym}}^{\text{sub}} \cdot N_{\text{slots}}^{\text{frame}}
$$

$$
N_{\text{RE}} = 273 \cdot 12 \cdot 14 \cdot 20
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Step-by-Step

$$
273 \cdot 12 = 3276
$$

$$
3276 \cdot 14 = 45{,}864
$$

$$
45{,}864 \cdot 20 = 917{,}280 \text{ resource elements per 10 ms frame}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Peak Rate per Layer

$$
R_{\text{peak,layer}} = N_{\text{RE}} \cdot Q_m \cdot R_{\max}
$$

$$
R_{\text{peak,layer}} = 917{,}280 \cdot 8 \cdot \frac{948}{1024}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Numerically

$$
917{,}280 \cdot 8 = 7{,}338{,}240 \text{ bits per 10 ms per layer}
$$

$$
\frac{948}{1024} = 0.9258
$$

$$
R_{\text{peak,layer}} = 7{,}338{,}240 \cdot 0.9258 = 6{,}792{,}899 \text{ bits per 10 ms}
$$

### Step 6 — Scale to 1 Second and Apply MIMO

$$
R_{\text{peak}} = R_{\text{peak,layer}} \cdot \frac{1000 \text{ ms}}{10 \text{ ms}} \cdot N_{\text{streams}}
$$

$$
R_{\text{peak}} = 6{,}792{,}899 \cdot 100 \cdot 4
$$

### Step 7 — Final Calculation

$$
R_{\text{peak}} = 6{,}792{,}899 \cdot 400 = 2.717 \times 10^9 \text{ bps}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{\text{peak}} \approx 2.72 \text{ Gbps}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Mention that **1024-QAM ($Q_m=10$)** in 5G-Advanced (Rel. 18) and **12 MIMO streams** would push the peak rate beyond 10 Gbps — this is **expected answer** for an advanced board question.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Free-Space Path Loss for mmWave V2X Link

### Problem Statement
A 5G mmWave V2X link operates at $f = 28$ GHz over a distance $d = 200$ m. Compute the **free-space path loss** in dB. Use $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

---

### Step 1 — Apply FSPL Formula

$$
\text{FSPL (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$
d = 200 \text{ m}, \quad f = 28 \times 10^9 \text{ Hz}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute $20\log_{10}(d)$

$$
20 \log_{10}(200) = 20 \cdot \log_{10}(2 \cdot 10^2)
$$

$$
= 20 \cdot (\log_{10} 2 + 2)
$$

$$
= 20 \cdot (0.3010 + 2) = 20 \cdot 2.3010 = 46.02 \text{ dB}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute $20\log_{10}(f)$

$$
20 \log_{10}(28 \times 10^9) = 20 \cdot (\log_{10} 28 + 9)
$$

$$
= 20 \cdot (1.4472 + 9) = 20 \cdot 10.4472 = 208.94 \text{ dB}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute $20\log_{10}(4\pi / c)$

$$
\frac{4\pi}{c} = \frac{4 \cdot 3.1416}{3 \times 10^8} = \frac{12.566}{3 \times 10^8} = 4.189 \times 10^{-8}
$$

$$
20\log_{10}(4.189 \times 10^{-8}) = 20 \cdot (\log_{10} 4.189 + (-8))
$$

$$
= 20 \cdot (0.6222 - 8) = 20 \cdot (-7.3778) = -147.56 \text{ dB}
$$

### Step 6 — Sum All Terms

$$
\text{FSPL} = 46.02 + 208.94 + (-147.56)
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{FSPL} \approx 107.4 \text{ dB}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** A 107 dB path loss means **beamforming gain (~30 dB)** and **high-gain directional antennas** are non-negotiable for mmWave V2X. This is why **FR1 sub-6 GHz** is preferred for **non-line-of-sight V2X** at intersections.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — 5G Automotive Use Case ↔ QCI / 5QI Mapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **5QI (5G QoS Identifier)** replaces 4G's QCI in 5G. KTU questions often test this mapping.

| Use Case | 5QI | Resource Type | Default Priority | PDB (ms) | PER |
|----------|-----|----------------|------------------|----------|-----|
| V2X message (safety) | **79** | Non-GBR | 5 | **10 ms** | $10^{-4}$ |
| Cooperative automation | **82** | Delay-Critical GBR | 2 | **5 ms** | $10^{-5}$ |
| V2X message (relaxed) | **80** | Non-GBR | 10 | 100 ms | $10^{-4}$ |
| HD map streaming (eMBB) | **9** | Non-GBR | 9 | 300 ms | $10^{-6}$ |
| OTA software update | **3** | Non-GBR | 8 | 300 ms | $10^{-6}$ |

*PDB = Packet Delay Budget; PER = Packet Error Rate*

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — Conceptual Definition
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

> *"Define C-V2X and explain how 5G NR-V2X improves upon LTE C-V2X for autonomous driving applications."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**C-V2X (Cellular Vehicle-to-Everything)** is a 3GPP-standardized communication technology that enables vehicles to communicate with other vehicles (V2V), infrastructure (V2I), networks (V2N), and pedestrians (V2P) using cellular radio interfaces (Uu and PC5).

**Improvements of 5G NR-V2X over LTE C-V2X:**
- **Latency Reduction:** LTE C-V2X achieves ~20 ms latency; **5G NR-V2X achieves < 3 ms** via flexible numerology (μ=2, 60 kHz SCS).
- **Reliability:** 5G offers **99.999% reliability** vs. LTE's 99.9% — critical for collision avoidance.
- **Higher Bandwidth:** Up to **100 MHz (FR1) / 400 MHz (FR2)** vs. LTE's 20 MHz.
- **Network Slicing:** 5G introduces **dedicated URLLC slices** for safety, ensuring guaranteed QoS.
- **MEC Integration:** Sub-10 ms cloud processing via Multi-access Edge Computing.
- **Beamforming & MIMO:** Enables reliable mmWave V2X for high-throughput infotainment.

*(Examiner: Award 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for differentiation.)*

---

### Question A2 — Direct Recall
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**

> *"List any three 5G service classes and state one automotive application for each."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Service Class | Full Form | Automotive Application |
|---------------|-----------|------------------------|
| **URLLC** | Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communication | **Collision avoidance braking** (latency < 5 ms) |
| **eMBB** | enhanced Mobile BroadBand | **HD map streaming for navigation** |
| **mMTC** | massive Machine-Type Communication | **Fleet telematics with 10⁶ sensors/km²** |

*(Examiner: Award 1 mark per correct pair.)*
