<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define a Head-Up Display. List any two advantages of using a HUD over a conventional instrument cluster.

**Model Answer (10–12 lines, 3 marks):**

A **Head-Up Display (HUD)** is a transparent electronic display system that projects critical driving information directly into the driver's forward line of sight, typically onto the windshield or a dedicated combiner glass, allowing the driver to maintain visual focus on the road.

**Advantages:**

1. **Reduced Eye-Accommodation Time:** The virtual image is placed at 2–3 m (conventional) or 7–15 m (AR), matching the focal distance of the road, so the eyes do not need to refocus.

2. **Improved Safety:** The driver saves approximately **0.3 – 0.5 seconds** per glance, translating to **10–15 meters** of reaction distance at highway speeds.

3. **Better ADAS Integration:** Visual warnings (collision, lane-departure) are delivered within the driver's natural gaze, reducing alert-to-action latency.

*(Any two advantages — 1.5 marks each. Definition — 1 mark.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** What is the difference between a Combiner HUD and a Windshield HUD?

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Combiner HUD | Windshield HUD |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Reflecting Surface** | Small, fold-up transparent plastic panel (combiner) | The vehicle windshield itself |
| **Image Size** | Smaller (~5° FOV) | Larger (~10°–13° FOV) |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher (requires wedge-shaped glass) |
| **Installation** | Easy, retrofit-friendly | Complex, must match windshield curvature |
| **Visibility** | Adequate for basic data | Better for rich AR overlays |

A **Combiner HUD** uses a small separate transparent panel that pops up from the dashboard. A **Windshield HUD** projects directly onto the specially coated windshield, producing a larger and more integrated image.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a Head-Up Display system in a modern automobile. List all major components and their functions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the virtual image distance (VID) of a HUD given a display-to-mirror distance of 80 mm and an aspheric mirror focal length of 160 mm. Comment on whether the image is real or virtual. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a)

**Working Principle:**

A HUD projects driving data as a **virtual image** that the driver perceives to be floating on the road ahead. The light from a small internal display (TFT-LCD or DLP) is magnified by an **aspheric collimating mirror** and reflected off the windshield (or combiner) into the driver's eyes.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CAN Bus Signals] --> B[HUD ECU]
    B --> C[Picture Generation Unit]
    D[Backlight LED] --> C
    C --> E[Aspheric Mirror]
    E --> F[Windshield / Combiner]
    F --> G[Driver Eye]
    H[Light Sensor] --> B
```

**Component Functions:**

| Component | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| HUD ECU | Receives data from CAN/Ethernet; renders graphics |
| PGU (Picture Generation Unit) | Generates the actual image on TFT-LCD or DLP |
| Backlight Unit (BLU) | Provides illumination (>10,000 cd/m²) |
| Aspheric Mirror | Magnifies and collimates the image to form a virtual image |
| Windshield/Combiner | Partially reflects the image toward the driver |
| Ambient Light Sensor | Auto-adjusts brightness for day/night |
| Driver Head Tracker (optional) | Adjusts eye-box position dynamically |

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- Working principle explanation: 2 marks
- Block diagram: 2 marks
- Component listing & functions: 3 marks

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**Given:**
- Display-to-mirror distance, $u = +80 \; mm$ (real object in front of mirror)
- Focal length of aspheric mirror, $f = +160 \; mm$ (concave)

**Step 1 — Apply the mirror equation:**

$$
\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{v} + \frac{1}{u}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values:**

$$
\frac{1}{160} = \frac{1}{v} + \frac{1}{80}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange for $1/v$:**

$$
\frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{160} - \frac{1}{80} = \frac{1}{160} - \frac{2}{160} = -\frac{1}{160}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $v$:**

$$
v = -160 \; mm
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result:**

The negative sign indicates that the image is formed **behind the mirror**, on the same side as the object — this is a **virtual image**. The driver perceives this virtual image at a distance of 160 mm behind the aspheric mirror, but after the secondary reflection by the windshield, the effective perceived VID becomes approximately **2.0 – 2.5 m** ahead of the driver's eye.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The image is **virtual, erect, and magnified** — the ideal configuration for a Head-Up Display.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- Formula statement: 1 mark
- Substitution: 2 marks
- Algebraic manipulation: 2 marks
- Final answer + interpretation: 2 marks

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of an **Augmented Reality Head-Up Display (AR-HUD)**. How does it differ from a conventional HUD? Mention any two automotive applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle travels at 90 km/h. The driver uses a HUD and saves 0.4 seconds of reaction time per glance compared to a conventional cluster. Calculate the equivalent distance saved per glance. If the driver glances 25 times in one hour of driving, what is the total distance gained? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a)

**Augmented Reality HUD (AR-HUD) — Definition:**

An **Augmented Reality Head-Up Display** is an advanced projection system that overlays computer-generated graphics **directly onto the real-world road view**, with the virtual image positioned at a **long distance (7–15 m)** to align with the driver's actual focal point on the road. The graphics appear to "interact" with real objects.

**Differences from Conventional HUD:**

| Feature | Conventional HUD | AR-HUD |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Virtual Image Distance | 2 – 3 m | 7 – 15 m |
| FOV | 5° – 10° | Up to 20°+ (and beyond) |
| Display Source | TFT-LCD | DLP / Laser / Holographic |
| AR Anchoring | None | GPS + Camera + Radar fusion |
| Cost | Moderate | Very High |

**Automotive Applications:**

1. **Navigation Arrow Overlay:** Turn-by-turn arrows are projected *onto* the actual road, showing the precise lane to take.
2. **ADAS Visualization:** Pedestrians, vehicles, and lane boundaries are highlighted in real-time, improving hazard perception.

**Mark Distribution:** Concept 2 marks + Comparison 3 marks + Applications 2 marks = **7 marks**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b)

**Given:**
- Vehicle speed $v = 90 \; km/h$
- Time saved per glance $\Delta t = 0.4 \; s$
- Number of glances $n = 25$ per hour

**Step 1 — Convert speed to m/s:**

$$
v = \frac{90 \times 1000}{3600} = 25 \; m/s
$$

**Step 2 — Distance saved per glance:**

$$
d_{per\ glance} = v \times \Delta t = 25 \times 0.4 = 10 \; m
$$

**Step 3 — Total distance saved in 1 hour:**

$$
d_{total} = d_{per\ glance} \times n = 10 \times 25 = 250 \; m
$$

**Conclusion:** The HUD saves the driver an effective **250 meters** of blind travel per hour of driving, a significant safety enhancement.

**Mark Distribution:** Conversion 2 marks + Per glance 2 marks + Total 2 marks + Conclusion 1 mark = **7 marks**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Confusion:** Many students drop the negative sign in $v = -160$ mm, losing 1 mark. Always state explicitly that **negative v = virtual image**.
> 2. **Unit Conversion Errors:** In distance/speed problems, forgetting to convert km/h to m/s is the most common error. Always show the conversion step.
> 3. **Vague "HUD is good" Answers:** Examiners expect **specific metrics** (VID, FOV, cd/m²) — generic statements like "HUD improves safety" without quantification lose 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Confusing AR-HUD with simple HUD:** AR-HUD is a distinct technology using **DLP/Laser + sensor fusion**, not just a larger conventional HUD.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Head-Up Displays**

- ✅ **HUD = transparent display** projecting data into driver's line of sight.
- ✅ **Virtual Image Distance (VID):** 2–3 m conventional; 7–15 m AR-HUD.
- ✅ **Core Components:** PGU, Backlight, Aspheric Mirror, Combiner/Windshield, HUD ECU, Light Sensor.
- ✅ **Three Types:** Combiner HUD, Windshield HUD, AR-HUD.
- ✅ **Mirror Equation:** $\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{v} + \frac{1}{u}$ — negative $v$ ⇒ virtual image.
- ✅ **FOV Formula:** $FOV = 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\frac{S_{display}}{2 f_{mirror}}\right)$.
- ✅ **Luminance:** Must exceed **10,000 cd/m²** for daylight.
- ✅ **Eye-box:** ~130 mm × 50 mm lateral head-movement tolerance.
- ✅ **Time saved per glance:** 0.3 – 0.5 s ⇒ 10–15 m at highway speeds.
- ✅ **Display Tech:** TFT-LCD (entry) → DLP (mid) → Laser/MEMS (AR).
- ✅ **Wedge-shaped windshield** is required for Windshield HUD to avoid double images.
- ✅ **AR-HUD requires sensor fusion** (GPS + Camera + Radar + Lidar) for accurate overlay.
- ✅ **Network:** Uses CAN FD or Automotive Ethernet 100BASE-T1.
- ✅ **Safety Standard:** Euro NCAP awards points for HUD-based ADAS visualization.
- ✅ **Distinguish:** HUD is a DIS (Driver Information System), part of HMI (Human-Machine Interface).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Navigation Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET414 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Navigation Systems ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition An **Automotive Navigation System** is an integrated electronic assembly that performs real-time vehicle localization, route planning, and turn-by-turn guidance by fusing data from Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS), inertial/dead-reckoning sensors, and digital map databases, presenting the result through a Human-Machine Interface (HMI). In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, it is classified under the broader umbrella of **Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS)** as a *Comfort & Information* subsystem, distinct from safety-critical functions like Adaptive Cruise Control or Lane Keeping. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEIET414 – Module 4):** > The module targets the cognitive understanding of (i) Satellite-based positioning principles, (ii) Sensor fusion between GNSS and INS, (iii) Map matching, and (iv) Route guidance algorithms. Questions are typically pitched at the *Apply* and *Analyze* levels of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy. ### 1.2 Constituent Sub-Systems (Taxonomy) A complete in-vehicle navigation stack consists of four tightly-coupled layers: | Layer | Function | Primary Hardware | |---|---|---| | **Positioning Layer** | Absolute & relative coordinate estimation | GNSS receiver, IMU, wheel speed sensors | | **Map Database Layer** | Geographic referencing, road topology | SD card / HDD with NDS / KIWI format | | **Computation Layer** | Map matching, route search, guidance | Automotive-grade MCU / SoC | | **HMI Layer** | Driver information display | Touchscreen, HUD, voice prompt | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Homing Pigeon with a Map" Imagine you are blindfolded and dropped in an unknown city. You have: - A **radio that hears satellites** shouting "I am at coordinates (X, Y, Z) at time T" — that is your **GNSS receiver**. - A **pedometer and compass** strapped to your body telling how far and in which direction you have walked since you last knew your location — that is your **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)**. - A **paper map** of the city showing every road — that is your **Digital Map Database**. - A **friend** who keeps aligning your reported position to the nearest road on the map, removing silly jumps into buildings — that is the **Map Matching algorithm**. The navigation system is simply this blindfolded traveler, but rebuilt inside a dashboard. > [!NOTE] > **Key Standard Metric:** A modern automotive-grade GNSS receiver achieves positional accuracy of **2.5 m CEP (Circular Error Probable)** under open sky, degrading to **15–50 m** in urban canyons due to multipath and signal blockage. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 GNSS — Operational Principle The Global Navigation Satellite System uses **one-way time-of-flight (ToF) trilateration**. Each satellite continuously broadcasts a *navigation message* containing its ephemeris, almanac, and a precisely time-stamped ranging code. For a 1D conceptual case: - Satellite A is at distance $d_A$ from the vehicle. - Satellite B is at distance $d_B$ from the vehicle. - The locus of possible positions for each is a **sphere** of radius $d$ centered on the satellite. - Intersection of two spheres → a **circle** in 3D. - Intersection of three spheres → **two points** (one is in space, rejected). - A fourth satellite is required to resolve the **receiver clock bias** $\delta t_r$. ### 2.2 Pseudorange Equation The measured range, called *pseudorange* $\rho$, is contaminated by receiver clock error: $$\rho_i = R_i + c \cdot (\delta t_r - \delta t_{s,i}) + I_i + T_i + \epsilon_{mp,i} + \epsilon_{n,i}$$ Where: - $R_i$ = true geometric range to satellite $i$ - $c$ = speed of light $\approx \mathbf{3 \times 10^8 \; m/s}$ - $\delta t_r$ = receiver clock bias - $\delta t_{s,i}$ = satellite clock bias (broadcast in nav message) - $I_i$ = ionospheric delay - $T_i$ = tropospheric delay - $\epsilon_{mp,i}$ = multipath error - $\epsilon_{n,i}$ = receiver noise ### 2.3 Position Fix (Linearized Least Squares) Linearizing around an approximate position $(x_0, y_0, z_0, \delta t_{r,0})$, the observation matrix is: $$H = \begin{bmatrix} -\hat{u}_{x,1} & -\hat{u}_{y,1} & -\hat{u}_{z,1} & 1 \\ -\hat{u}_{x,2} & -\hat{u}_{y,2} & -\hat{u}_{z,2} & 1 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots \\ -\hat{u}_{x,n} & -\hat{u}_{y,n} & -\hat{u}_{z,n} & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ The position correction $\Delta x$ is solved by: $$\Delta x = (H^T H)^{-1} H^T \Delta \rho$$ The minimum required is $n \geq 4$ satellites for a 3D fix with time. ### 2.4 Dilution of Precision (DOP) DOP is a **geometric quality indicator** — it amplifies or attenuates the ranging error into positional error: $$\text{Position Error} = \text{UERE} \times \text{DOP}$$ Where UERE = User Equivalent Range Error. Sub-DOPs are: | Metric | Computes | Good Value | |---|---|---| | **GDOP** | Geometric (3D + time) | < 3 | | **PDOP** | 3D Position | < 6 | | **HDOP** | Horizontal only | < 2 | | **VDOP** | Vertical only | < 3 | | **TDOP** | Time only | < 2 | $$\text{GDOP} = \sqrt{\text{trace}((H^T H)^{-1})}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why it matters in vehicles:** A high DOP in urban canyons (satellites clustered in one hemisphere) is the #1 cause of perceived "GPS jump" complaints — the geometry is poor even when the satellites are technically "visible." ### 2.5 Inertial / Dead Reckoning — Theory When GNSS is denied (tunnels, urban canyons, parking garages), the system falls back to **Dead Reckoning (DR)** using an Inertial Navigation System (INS) and wheel-tick sensors: $$x_k = x_{k-1} + v_{k-1} \cos(\psi_{k-1}) \cdot \Delta t$$ $$y_k = y_{k-1} + v_{k-1} \sin(\psi_{k-1}) \cdot \Delta t$$ $$\psi_k = \psi_{k-1} + \omega_{k-1} \cdot \Delta t$$ Where: - $v$ = vehicle speed from wheel-speed sensors - $\psi$ = heading from gyroscope / magnetometer - $\omega$ = yaw rate - $\Delta t$ = sampling interval > [!WARNING] > **Pure INS error grows as $t^2$ to $t^3$** (Schuler oscillation unbounded for low-cost MEMS). This is the precise reason sensor fusion is mandatory, not optional. ### 2.6 Sensor Fusion — The Kalman Filter The Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) is the de-facto industry choice to fuse GNSS (low frequency, absolute, noisy) and INS (high frequency, relative, drifting). State vector: $$\mathbf{x}_k = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \psi \\ v \\ b_a \\ b_g \end{bmatrix}$$ **Prediction step** (using INS): $$\hat{x}_{k|k-1} = f(\hat{x}_{k-1|k-1}, u_{k-1})$$ **Update step** (using GNSS pseudoranges): $$K_k = P_{k|k-1} H_k^T (H_k P_{k|k-1} H_k^T + R_k)^{-1}$$ $$\hat{x}_{k|k} = \hat{x}_{k|k-1} + K_k (z_k - h(\hat{x}_{k|k-1}))$$ This produces a tightly-coupled, drift-corrected position estimate at **50–100 Hz** while keeping GNSS at **1–10 Hz**. ### 2.7 Map Matching Map matching is the *geometric-topological* snapping of a raw GPS point to the *most probable road segment*. The two dominant families are: 1. **Geometric / Point-to-Curve:** Snap to nearest road polyline. Fast, but fails at intersections and parallel roads. 2. **Topological / Probabilistic (e.g., HMM-based):** Uses road connectivity, heading, and speed to pick the *correct* segment even if geometrically ambiguous. ### 2.8 Route Planning — Dijkstra vs. A* The shortest-path search over a road graph $G(V,E)$ with non-negative weights $w(u,v)$: - **Dijkstra's Algorithm:** $O((V+E) \log V)$ — exhaustive, used for short, dense graphs. - **A* Search:** Adds a heuristic $h(v)$ to guide the search: $$f(v) = g(v) + h(v)$$ Heuristic must be **admissible** (never overestimates) — typical choice is the **Haversine distance** to the goal. ### 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\rho = c \cdot \Delta t$ | Pseudorange from ToF | m | | 2 | $n \geq 4$ | Minimum satellites for 3D + time fix | — | | 3 | $\sigma_p = \text{UERE} \times \text{DOP}$ | Positional accuracy | m | | 4 | $\text{GDOP} = \sqrt{\text{trace}((H^T H)^{-1})}$ | Geometric dilution | dimensionless | | 5 | $R_e = 6371 \; \text{km}$ | WGS-84 mean Earth radius | km | | 6 | $e = 0.0818$ | WGS-84 eccentricity | dimensionless | | 7 | $c = 3 \times 10^8 \; \text{m/s}$ | Speed of light | m/s | | 8 | $f = 1575.42 \; \text{MHz}$ | GPS L1 carrier frequency | MHz | | 9 | $d_{Haversine} = 2R \arcsin(\sqrt{\sin^2(\Delta\phi/2) + \cos\phi_1 \cos\phi_2 \sin^2(\Delta\lambda/2)})$ | Great-circle distance | m | | 10 | $f(v) = g(v) + h(v)$ | A* cost function | — | | 11 | $\text{DR error} \propto t^n, \; n=2 \text{ to } 3$ | INS drift order | — | ### 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility - **ADAS Layer 1:** Lane-level positioning feeds Lane Centering, Predictive ACC. - **Fleet Telematics:** Real-time asset tracking, ETA computation, geofencing. - **V2X / C-ITS:** GNSS provides absolute coordinates for Cooperative Awareness Messages (CAM). - **Insurance Telematics:** Trip reconstruction for usage-based insurance (UBI). - **Autonomous Driving (L3+):** HD map localization requires cm-level accuracy — this is where **PPP / RTK GNSS** comes in, lowering UERE to **2–5 cm**. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — Pseudorange from Time-of-Flight **Step 1:** The satellite $S$ transmits a ranging code at transmission time $t_t$. The receiver $R$ records the time of arrival $t_a$. **Step 2:** The measured signal travel time is: $$\Delta t = t_a - t_t$$ **Step 3:** Multiplying by the speed of light gives the geometric distance, but the receiver and satellite clocks are *not* perfectly synchronized, so: $$\rho_{measured} = c \cdot \Delta t = c(t_a - t_t) + c \cdot \delta t_r - c \cdot \delta t_s$$ **Step 4:** Recognizing that the *true* geometric range is: $$R = \sqrt{(x_s - x_r)^2 + (y_s - y_r)^2 + (z_s - z_r)^2}$$ **Step 5:** The complete pseudorange expression becomes: $$\rho = \sqrt{(x_s - x_r)^2 + (y_s - y_r)^2 + (z_s - z_r)^2} + c \cdot \delta t_r - c \cdot \delta t_s + I + T + \epsilon$$ This is the **fundamental observable** of every GNSS system in the world — GPS, GLONASS, Galileo, BeiDou, IRNSS, and QZSS. --- ### 3.2 Derivation — Dilution of Precision (DOP) from Covariance **Step 1:** Let the measurement noise covariance be $R_{meas} = \sigma^2 I$ (assuming identical UERE for simplicity). **Step 2:** The least-squares covariance of the state estimate is: $$P_x = (H^T H)^{-1} \sigma^2$$ **Step 3:** Define the geometry matrix $Q = (H^T H)^{-1}$. It is a $4 \times 4$ matrix: $$Q = \begin{bmatrix} q_{xx} & q_{xy} & q_{xz} & q_{xt} \\ q_{yx} & q_{yy} & q_{yz} & q_{yt} \\ q_{zx} & q_{zy} & q_{zz} & q_{zt} \\ q_{tx} & q_{ty} & q_{tz} & q_{tt} \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 4:** The GDOP is the ratio of the *position-time error volume* to the *ranging error volume*: $$\text{GDOP} = \frac{\sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2 + \sigma_t^2}}{\sigma} = \sqrt{\text{trace}(Q)}$$ **Step 5:** Sub-DOPs decompose this: $$\text{PDOP} = \sqrt{q_{xx} + q_{yy} + q_{zz}}$$ $$\text{HDOP} = \sqrt{q_{xx} + q_{yy}}$$ $$\text{VDOP} = \sqrt{q_{zz}}$$ $$\text{TDOP} = \sqrt{q_{tt}}$$ --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — 2D Position Fix **Problem:** Two satellites and a known altitude. Find the 2D position. Given: - Satellite 1: $(x_1, y_1, z_1) = (15600, 7540, 20140) \; \text{km}$, $\rho_1 = 22605 \; \text{km}$ - Satellite 2: $(x_2, y_2, z_2) = (18760, 2750, 18640) \; \text{km}$, $\rho_2 = 23905 \; \text{km}$ - True altitude: $u = 200 \; \text{km}$ (above Earth model for simplicity) **Step 1:** Define geometric range: $$R_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x_r)^2 + (y_i - y_r)^2 + (z_i - z_r)^2}$$ **Step 2:** Linearize around an initial guess $(x_0, y_0) = (0, 0)$: $$\frac{\partial R_1}{\partial x} = \frac{x_0 - x_1}{R_1} = \frac{0 - 15600}{22605} = -0.6901$$ $$\frac{\partial R_1}{\partial y} = \frac{y_0 - y_1}{R_1} = \frac{0 - 7540}{22605} = -0.3335$$ $$\frac{\partial R_2}{\partial x} = \frac{0 - 18760}{23905} = -0.7848$$ $$\frac{\partial R_2}{\partial y} = \frac{0 - 2750}{23905} = -0.1150$$ **Step 3:** Form the linear system $\Delta \rho = H \Delta x$: $$\begin{bmatrix} \Delta \rho_1 \\ \Delta \rho_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.6901 & -0.3335 \\ -0.7848 & -0.1150 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta x \\ \Delta y \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 4:** Solve for $\Delta x$ by inverting the $2 \times 2$ matrix: $$H^{-1} = \frac{1}{(-0.6901)(-0.1150) - (-0.3335)(-0.7848)} \begin{bmatrix} -0.1150 & 0.3335 \\ 0.7848 & -0.6901 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 5:** Compute the determinant: $$\det(H) = 0.07936 - 0.2617 = -0.1824$$ **Step 6:** Update the position iteratively. This is the iterative least-squares method; convergence typically in 4–6 iterations to sub-meter accuracy with clean measurements. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — GNSS vs. INS Trade-off | Parameter | GNSS | INS / DR | |---|---|---| | **Error behaviour** | Bounded (≈ constant) | Unbounded (grows with $t^2$–$t^3$) | | **Update rate** | 1–10 Hz | 50–1000 Hz | | **Signal dependency** | External (sky view) | Fully self-contained | | **Susceptibility** | Jamming, multipath, spoofing | Bias drift, scale-factor errors | | **Cost (consumer)** | ₹200–₹800 module | ₹1500–₹6000 MEMS IMU | | **Output** | Absolute lat/lon | Relative displacement | | **Failure mode** | Catastrophic (signal loss) | Graceful degradation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion of Matrix:** Neither system alone is sufficient. The Kalman-filtered fusion of the two is what makes a *practical* automotive navigation system robust. --- ### 3.5 Derivation — Haversine Distance **Why needed:** A* search needs a heuristic that estimates the great-circle (curved-earth) distance to the goal. **Step 1:** Convert latitudes and longitudes to radians: $$\phi_1 = \text{lat}_1 \cdot \frac{\pi}{180}, \quad \phi_2 = \text{lat}_2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{180}, \quad \Delta\phi = \phi_2 - \phi_1, \quad \Delta\lambda = \lambda_2 - \lambda_1$$ **Step 2:** Apply the Haversine of the central angle $\Delta\sigma$: $$a = \sin^2\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) + \cos(\phi_1) \cos(\phi_2) \sin^2\left(\frac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right)$$ **Step 3:** Central angle: $$\Delta\sigma = 2 \arcsin(\sqrt{a})$$ **Step 4:** Arc length on a sphere: $$d = R_e \cdot \Delta\sigma$$ This is admissible (always underestimates for any two points) and is computationally cheap — ideal as an A* heuristic. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Vehicle Navigation System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[GNSS Antenna] --> B[GNSS Receiver Module] B --> C{Sensor Fusion EKF} D[Wheel Speed Sensors] --> C E[MEMS Gyroscope] --> C F[MEMS Accelerometer] --> C G[Magnetometer] --> C C --> H[Position Velocity Time PVT Engine] H --> I[Map Matching Module] J[Map Database NDS KIWI] --> I I --> K[Route Planner Dijkstra or A Star] K --> L[Guidance Engine Turn by Turn] L --> M[HMI Display Touchscreen] L --> N[Voice Prompt TTS] L --> O[HUD Head Up Display] style C fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style I fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style K fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ### 4.2 GNSS Trilateration — 3D Geometric Concept ```mermaid flowchart TB Sat1[SATELLITE 1
Pseudorange rho 1] -->|Sphere radius rho 1| P1[Intersection Region] Sat2[SATELLITE 2
Pseudorange rho 2] -->|Sphere radius rho 2| P1 Sat3[SATELLITE 3
Pseudorange rho 3] -->|Sphere radius rho 3| P2[Intersection Point] P1 -->|Reduces to two points| P2 Sat4[SATELLITE 4
Resolves clock bias] --> P2 P2 --> Fix[VEHICLE POSITION FIX
3D Coordinates] style Fix fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px ``` --- ### 4.3 Kalman Filter Sensor Fusion Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB In1[INS Prediction
50 to 100 Hz] --> Pred[Predicted State x hat k given k minus 1] In2[GNSS Measurement
1 to 10 Hz] --> Meas[Measurement z k] Pred --> K[Kalman Gain K k] Meas --> K K --> Upd[Updated State x hat k given k] Upd --> Out[Corrected Position Heading Speed] Upd -->|Feedback| Pred style K fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style Upd fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ### 4.4 Map Matching Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB Start[Raw GPS Point] --> Nearest[Find candidate road segments within radius R] Nearest --> Score[Compute score using
distance heading speed and topology] Score --> HMM[Hidden Markov Model
Emission and Transition Probabilities] HMM --> Viterbi[Viterbi Decode
Most likely road sequence] Viterbi --> Out[Snapped Position
on Correct Road] style HMM fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style Viterbi fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ### 4.5 GNSS Error Budget — Automotive View ```mermaid flowchart LR Total[Total UERE
approx 3 to 5 m] --> A[Ionospheric Delay
approx 5 m] Total --> B[Tropospheric Delay
approx 0.5 m] Total --> C[Ephemeris Error
approx 2.5 m] Total --> D[Satellite Clock
approx 2 m] Total --> E[Multipath
approx 1 m urban] Total --> F[Receiver Noise
approx 0.5 m] style Total fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]** *Define the term "Dilution of Precision" (DOP) in GNSS. List any three sub-types of DOP and their physical meaning.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** DOP is a dimensionless scalar that quantifies how the **geometric arrangement of satellites** amplifies ranging errors into positional errors. $$\sigma_{position} = \text{UERE} \times \text{DOP}$$ Three sub-types: 1. **GDOP (Geometric DOP):** 3D position + time accuracy factor. Acceptable: GDOP < 4. 2. **PDOP (Position DOP):** 3D spatial position only. Acceptable: PDOP < 6. 3. **HDOP (Horizontal DOP):** 2D horizontal plane accuracy. Acceptable: HDOP < 2. > **Marking Key:** 1 mark for definition + formula, ½ mark each for 3 sub-types, ½ mark for threshold values. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]** *What is Dead Reckoning in the context of vehicle navigation? Why is it necessary even when GNSS is available?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Dead Reckoning (DR)** is the process of estimating the *current* position of a vehicle by adding a *measured displacement* (speed × heading × time) to a previously known position. $$x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \cos(\psi_k) \cdot \Delta t$$ $$y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k \sin(\psi_k) \cdot \Delta t$$ **Necessity:** 1. **Continuity of position** when GNSS is denied (tunnels, urban canyons, parking structures). 2. **Higher update rate** (50–100 Hz INS vs. 1–10 Hz GNSS) — required for continuous map display. 3. **Redundancy and safety** for ADAS functions that need uninterrupted position. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]* **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the working principle of the Global Positioning System (GPS). With a neat diagram, describe the concept of trilateration and derive the pseudorange equation. **(b) [7 marks]** With a block diagram, explain the architecture of a typical automotive navigation system. Discuss the role of sensor fusion using a Kalman filter in such systems. --- #### Model Solution for Q.A (a): **Working Principle of GPS (3 marks):** GPS consists of three segments: - **Space Segment:** 24+ MEO satellites at ~20,200 km altitude, 6 orbital planes, period ≈ 12 hours. - **Control Segment:** Master Control Station (Colorado) + monitor stations + ground antennas. Uploads navigation messages. - **User Segment:** The receiver that computes position, velocity, time (PVT). Each satellite continuously transmits a **navigation message** on L1 (1575.42 MHz) and L2 (1227.60 MHz) containing: - Ephemeris (precise satellite orbit) - Almanac (coarse orbit of all satellites) - Clock correction parameters - Ionospheric model coefficients **Trilateration (2 marks):** Refer to diagram in Section 4.2. With one satellite, the user lies on a sphere of radius $\rho_1$. Two satellites → circle. Three satellites → two points. The **fourth satellite** resolves the receiver clock bias, yielding a unique 3D position. **Pseudorange Equation Derivation (2 marks):** The measured travel time is contaminated by clock errors: $$\Delta t_{measured} = (t_a + \delta t_r) - (t_t + \delta t_s)$$ Multiplying by $c$: $$\rho = c \cdot \Delta t = R + c(\delta t_r - \delta t_s) + I + T + \epsilon_{mp} + \epsilon_n$$ Where $R = \sqrt{(x_s - x_r)^2 + (y_s - y_r)^2 + (z_s - z_r)^2}$. This is the fundamental observation equation. With $n \geq 4$ satellites, the four unknowns $(x_r, y_r, z_r, \delta t_r)$ are solved by linearized least squares. --- #### Model Solution for Q.A (b): **System Architecture (3 marks):** Refer to diagram in Section 4.1. The key blocks are: 1. **GNSS receiver + antenna** — primary absolute position source. 2. **Inertial sensors (IMU)** — gyroscope, accelerometer, magnetometer. 3. **Wheel-speed sensors (WSS)** — from ABS/ESC bus (CAN). 4. **Sensor Fusion EKF** — combines all sources. 5. **Map Database** — pre-loaded road network. 6. **Map Matching + Route Planner** — software modules. 7. **HMI** — display, voice, HUD. **Kalman Filter Sensor Fusion (4 marks):** The Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) maintains a state vector $\mathbf{x} = [x, y, \psi, v, b_a, b_g]^T$ and runs in two stages: **Prediction (using INS / DR):** $$\hat{x}_{k|k-1} = f(\hat{x}_{k-1|k-1}, u_{k-1})$$ $$P_{k|k-1} = F_k P_{k-1|k-1} F_k^T + Q_k$$ **Update (using GNSS pseudoranges):** $$K_k = P_{k|k-1} H^T (H P_{k|k-1} H^T + R_k)^{-1}$$ $$\hat{x}_{k|k} = \hat{x}_{k|k-1} + K_k (z_k - h(\hat{x}_{k|k-1}))$$ $$P_{k|k} = (I - K_k H) P_{k|k-1}$$ **Why it's necessary:** - GNSS provides absolute, bounded-error fixes at 1–10 Hz but is unavailable indoors. - INS provides continuous, high-rate (100+ Hz) but *drifting* estimates. - EKF optimally blends both: bounded final error **and** high output rate. This is called a **tightly-coupled integration** and is the de-facto industry standard for OEM navigation units. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Expected Pattern]* **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of Map Matching in automotive navigation. Compare geometric and topological map-matching approaches. **(b) [7 marks]** Describe the route-planning algorithms used in in-vehicle navigation. With a flowchart, explain the A* search algorithm and discuss the role of an admissible heuristic. --- #### Model Solution for Q.B (a): **Map Matching — Concept (2 marks):** Map matching is the process of *aligning* a sequence of raw GNSS positions to the *digital road network* in the map database. It corrects for typical GNSS noise (5–15 m) and snaps the vehicle to the correct road segment, even at intersections, parallel roads, or overpasses. **Geometric Approach (2.5 marks):** - **Point-to-curve:** Each raw point is independently projected onto the *nearest* polyline. - **Curve-to-curve:** Match the *shape* of the trajectory to the nearest road. - **Pros:** Simple, fast (constant time per point). - **Cons:** Ignores road connectivity, fails at junctions, fails on parallel roads. **Topological / Probabilistic Approach (2.5 marks):** - Uses **Hidden Markov Model (HMM)** where: - *Hidden states* = candidate road segments. - *Observations* = noisy GNSS points. - *Emission probability* = distance from GPS point to candidate segment. - *Transition probability* = based on road connectivity, heading consistency, speed. - **Viterbi algorithm** decodes the most likely sequence of road segments. - **Pros:** Robust at junctions, handles parallel roads, uses prior trajectory. - **Cons:** Higher computational cost, requires good map topology. **Modern OEM systems** use HMM-based matching for accuracy. --- #### Model Solution for Q.B (b): **Route Planning — Overview (1 mark):** The road network is modeled as a **directed weighted graph** $G = (V, E)$: - $V$ = intersections / nodes. - $E$ = road segments. - $w(u, v)$ = cost (distance, time, fuel, toll). **Dijkstra's Algorithm (2 marks):** - Finds the shortest path from source to all nodes. - Time complexity: $O((V + E) \log V)$ with a priority queue. - Used when no goal-directed heuristic is available, or for dense city graphs. **A* Algorithm (3 marks):** A* is Dijkstra + a heuristic $h(v)$ that estimates the cost from $v$ to the goal: $$f(v) = g(v) + h(v)$$ Where: - $g(v)$ = actual cost from start to $v$ (known). - $h(v)$ = estimated cost from $v$ to goal (heuristic). - $f(v)$ = estimated total cost through $v$. **Procedure:** 1. Open set = {start}, closed set = ∅. 2. Pop node $n$ with lowest $f(n)$. 3. If $n$ = goal, reconstruct path. 4. For each neighbour $m$, compute tentative $g(m)$. 5. If better, update $g(m)$, $f(m)$, parent. 6. Push into open set; loop. **Admissible Heuristic (1 mark):** A heuristic is **admissible** if it *never overestimates* the true cost: $$h(v) \leq \text{true\_cost}(v, \text{goal})$$ A typical admissible choice is the **Haversine great-circle distance** (computed earlier in Section 3.5). This guarantees that A* returns the optimal path. > A* is significantly faster than Dijkstra because $h(v)$ prunes branches moving away from the goal, reducing explored nodes by 80–95% in practical road graphs. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps (PEIET414 — Module 4):** > 1. **Confusing pseudorange with true range** — students lose 1–2 marks for writing $\rho = R$ without the clock-bias terms. > 2. **Forgetting the fourth satellite** — the clock-bias term $\delta t_r$ is *unknown*; without 4 satellites, the system is underdetermined. Students often write "3 satellites are enough," losing 2 marks. > 3. **Mixing up DOP types** — GDOP includes time, PDOP does not. Often confused. > 4. **Calling A\* a variant of Dijkstra but forgetting the heuristic** — A* = Dijkstra + admissible heuristic; the heuristic is what makes it goal-directed. > 5. **Omitting units in the formula sheet** — KTU 2024 Scheme deducts ½ mark for missing units in numerical answers. > 6. **Writing $t^3$ for MEMS error growth** — that's high-grade INS. Consumer MEMS grows at $t^2$ over short horizons. State the assumption clearly. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Navigation Systems** - [ ] **GNSS** is one-way ToF ranging; minimum **4 satellites** for 3D + time fix. - [ ] **Pseudorange** $\rho = R + c(\delta t_r - \delta t_s) + I + T + \epsilon$; it is *not* the true range. - [ ] **Speed of light** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \; m/s$; **GPS L1** carrier = 1575.42 MHz. - [ ] **DOP** amplifies UERE into positional error: $\sigma_p = \text{UERE} \times \text{DOP}$; GDOP < 4 is good. - [ ] **Dead Reckoning** uses speed, heading, and time to update position; fails over long horizons due to drift. - [ ] **Kalman Filter (EKF)** fuses GNSS (low-rate, absolute) and INS (high-rate, relative) optimally. - [ ] **Tightly-coupled** integration: uses raw pseudoranges; **Loosely-coupled**: uses GNSS position output. - [ ] **Map Matching:** Geometric (fast, naive) vs. HMM-based (robust, modern). - [ ] **Dijkstra:** $O((V+E) \log V)$, exhaustive; **A*:** adds heuristic $h(v)$, goal-directed. - [ ] **Admissible heuristic:** $h(v) \leq$ true cost — guarantees optimality. - [ ] **Haversine distance** is the standard admissible heuristic for road-graph A*. - [ ] **WGS-84** is the reference ellipsoid: $R_e = 6371 \; \text{km}$, eccentricity $e = 0.0818$. - [ ] **Automotive-grade GNSS accuracy:** 2.5 m CEP (open sky); 15–50 m in urban canyons. - [ ] **Sensor drift order:** MEMS INS error grows as $t^2$ to $t^3$ — fusion is mandatory. - [ ] **Typical update rates:** GNSS = 1–10 Hz; INS = 50–1000 Hz; Display refresh = 30–60 Hz. - [ ] **OEM navigation stack layers:** Positioning → Map DB → Computation (matching + planning) → HMI. - [ ] **RTK / PPP** bring cm-level accuracy for L3+ autonomous driving (not in basic syllabus, but worth knowing). - [ ] **WGS-84 vs. local frames:** Always use ECEF or ENU for calculations; lat/lon is *not* a Cartesian system.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET414 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Automatic Climate Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — AUTOMATIC CLIMATE CONTROL

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Automatic Climate Control (ACC)**, also referred to as **Automatic HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning)** or **Climate Control System (CCS)**, is a closed-loop, multi-sensor electronic regulation system that automatically maintains a pre-selected cabin air temperature, humidity, and airflow distribution by continuously sensing cabin and ambient conditions and dynamically actuating the compressor, blower, and air-mixing doors, without continuous driver intervention.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEIET414 — Modern Automotive Technologies), ACC is positioned as the **convergence of mechatronics, thermodynamics, and human-factors engineering**, forming an integral part of modern vehicle comfort, safety (defogging/demisting), and energy management (especially in EVs).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Smart Thermostat in Your House vs. Your Car"**
>
> Imagine your home split-AC with a remote. You set **24 °C** and forget it. The AC's tiny computer measures the room temperature with a sensor, compares it to your set-point (24 °C), and decides — *should I cool harder? softer? turn off?* That same logic, multiplied by **15+ sensors, 5+ motorized flaps, a refrigeration compressor, and a CAN-bus-linked ECU**, lives inside a modern car. The difference: a car cabin is **100× smaller**, leaks heat 10× faster (glass + metal), has **50 % transparent glass** flooding it with solar radiation, and is occupied by sweating, breathing humans. Hence, ACC is essentially a *supercharged, ultra-fast house thermostat* operating in a hostile thermal environment.

### 1.3 Why It Matters in Modern Vehicles

| Driver | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|
| Passenger thermal comfort | ↑ Brand value, ↓ driver fatigue |
| EV range | HVAC draws **3–6 kW** — biggest auxiliary load after traction |
| ADAS / Autonomous readiness | Cabin must be preconditioned before occupant enters |
| Demist / Defog | Critical for forward visibility, DOT regulation |
| Cabin air quality | HEPA + PM2.5 + CO₂ sensors integrated into ACC ECU |

### 1.4 Core System Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- Cabin target temperature band: **20 °C – 26 °C**
- Typical blower flow rate: **0.05 – 0.30 kg/s** (200 – 450 CFM)
- Refrigerant: **R-134a** (legacy) / **R-1234yf** (current EU / India BS-VI Phase 2) / **R-744 (CO₂)** (future)
- Compressor speed range: **800 – 6 000 rpm** (variable displacement)
- Sensor sampling rate: **10 – 100 Hz**
- Control loop time: **< 100 ms**
- ISO 7730 PMV comfort band: **−0.5 < PMV < +0.5**
- Solar load on greenhouse cabin: **up to 1 000 W/m²** at noon

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of an ACC System

An Automatic Climate Control system is, at its heart, a **multi-input, multi-output (MIMO) closed-loop control system**. It is conventionally split into four functional blocks:

### 2.1.1 Input Sub-System — Sensors
| Sensor | Physical Quantity | Typical Location | Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **In-Car Temperature Sensor (ICT)** | Air temperature | Behind dash, sun-shielded | −40 to +85 °C |
| **Ambient Temperature Sensor (AMB)** | Outside air | Front grille / mirror | −40 to +85 °C |
| **Sun-Load Sensor (Solar Radiation)** | Illuminance on horizontal plane | Top of dash, near windshield | 0 – 1 200 W/m² |
| **Humidity Sensor** | Relative humidity | Behind dash / mirror | 0 – 100 % RH |
| **Evaporator Outlet Temperature (EOT)** | Refrigerant leaving evaporator | In HVAC housing | −10 to +20 °C |
| **Coolant Temperature (for heater core)** | Engine coolant | Heater core inlet | 0 – 120 °C |
| **CO₂ / Air-Quality Sensor** | Cabin CO₂ / VOC | Behind dash | 400 – 5 000 ppm |
| **Blower Feedback** | Airflow / fan speed | Blower housing | 0 – 100 % PWM |

### 2.1.2 Control Sub-System — HVAC ECU
A **dedicated microcontroller** (often a 32-bit Infineon Aurix or NXP S32K) running a **PID / State-Flow / Model-Predictive controller** at 50–100 Hz.

### 2.1.3 Output Sub-System — Actuators
| Actuator | Function | Drive |
|---|---|---|
| **Blower Motor (BLDC)** | Air volume | PWM / CAN |
| **Compressor (Variable Displacement or Electric)** | Refrigerant flow | PWM / CAN |
| **Blend Door Actuator (HVAC's heart)** | Mixes hot/cold air | DC motor / stepper / servo |
| **Mode Door Actuator** | Defrost / face / feet | DC motor / stepper |
| **Recirculation Door** | Fresh vs. recirculated | DC motor |
| **Expansion Valve (TXV or Electronic EXV)** | Refrigerant metering | Mechanical / PWM |

### 2.1.4 Refrigeration Sub-System (Heat Pump / Vapour-Compression Loop)
**Compressor → Condenser → Expansion Valve → Evaporator → Compressor** — the classic Rankine-like reverse loop.

## 2.2 Why & How — Step-Wise Operational Logic

1. **Driver sets a target temperature T_set** (e.g., 22 °C) on the HMI.
2. ECU samples ICT, AMB, sun, humidity, EOT sensors every 10 ms.
3. **Error signal** is computed: $e(t) = T_{set} - T_{cabin}(t)$.
4. **PID controller** (or a fuzzy / MPC variant) generates a control output $u(t)$.
5. The ECU commands three actuators simultaneously:
   - **Blower speed** $\rightarrow$ sets airflow $\dot{m}_{air}$
   - **Blend door position** $\rightarrow$ sets air-mix ratio (cold ↔ hot)
   - **Compressor displacement** $\rightarrow$ sets cooling capacity $\dot{Q}_{L}$
6. The cabin air temperature is re-sensed, and the loop continues.
7. **Steady-state** is reached when $T_{cabin} = T_{set} \pm 0.5\,°C$ for > 30 s.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **KTU-Exam-Hot** and frequently tested. Memorize the boxed entries.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sensible heat of cabin air | $\dot{Q}_{sensible} = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot c_{p,air} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$ | $\dot{m}$ kg/s, $c_p$ = **1.005 kJ/kg·K**, $T$ K or °C | Dominant term for ACC |
| 2 | Latent heat (humidity) | $\dot{Q}_{latent} = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot (w_{in} - w_{out}) \cdot h_{fg}$ | $w$ = humidity ratio, $h_{fg} \approx 2 450$ kJ/kg | Used in tropical climates (Kerala) |
| 3 | Total cabin heat load | $\dot{Q}_{load} = \dot{Q}_{sensible} + \dot{Q}_{latent} + \dot{Q}_{solar} + \dot{Q}_{infiltration} + \dot{Q}_{engine}$ | All in W or kW | Steady-state energy balance |
| 4 | Solar heat gain through glass | $\dot{Q}_{solar} = A_{glass} \cdot I \cdot \tau \cdot \alpha$ | $A$ m², $I$ W/m², $\tau, \alpha$ transmission, absorption | 50–70 % of cabin load at noon |
| 5 | Conduction through walls | $\dot{Q}_{cond} = \dfrac{(T_{out} - T_{in}) \cdot A \cdot k}{L}$ | $k$ W/m·K, $L$ m | Thin glass dominates |
| 6 | Infiltration | $\dot{Q}_{inf} = \dot{V}_{leak} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$ | $\dot{V}_{leak}$ m³/s | AC duct leakage |
| 7 | COP of refrigeration | $\text{COP}_{cooling} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{L}}{\dot{W}_{comp}}$ | dimensionless | Typical **2.0 – 3.5** for R-134a |
| 8 | COP of heat pump | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{H}}{\dot{W}_{comp}} = 1 + \text{COP}_{cooling}$ | dimensionless | Used in EVs |
| 9 | Carnot COP (ideal) | $\text{COP}_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_{L}}{T_{H} - T_{L}}$ | $T$ in K | Upper bound; ~ 8 for 35 °C / 5 °C |
| 10 | PID control law | $u(t) = K_{p} e(t) + K_{i} \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) d\tau + K_{d} \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | $u$ = control signal | **⭐ KTU favourite** |
| 11 | Cabin thermal RC model | $C_{cab} \dfrac{dT_{cab}}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{comp} - \dfrac{(T_{cab} - T_{amb})}{R_{cab}}$ | $C$ J/K, $R$ K/W | First-order LTI |
| 12 | Time constant | $\tau_{cab} = R_{cab} \cdot C_{cab}$ | seconds | Typical 60–180 s |
| 13 | PMV (Fanger) — comfort | $\text{PMV} = f(M, W, I_{cl}, T_a, \bar{T}_{r}, v_{ar}, p_a)$ | 7 inputs | ISO 7730 |
| 14 | Energy ratio of EV | $\eta_{HVAC} = \dfrac{E_{HVAC}}{E_{battery}} \cdot 100\,\%$ | % | Up to **30 %** in winter EVs |
| 15 | Reynolds number for duct | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D_{h}}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Duct flow regime check |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **EVs (Tata Nexon EV, MG ZS EV)**: Heat-pump ACC cuts winter range loss from ~40 % to ~20 %.
- **Luxury segment (BMW, Mercedes)**: 4-zone ACC with PMV-based adaptive comfort.
- **Commercial vehicles (buses/trucks)**: Driver-fatigue mitigation via zone cooling.
- **Autonomous robotaxis**: Pre-conditioning of cabin 5 min before passenger entry, via OTA-scheduled ACC.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cabin Thermal Energy Balance (Lumped-Capacity Model)

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption**: Cabin air is a *lumped mass* at a single uniform temperature $T_{cab}(t)$. Walls and glass are lumped into a single thermal capacitance $C_{cab}$ and resistance $R_{cab}$. This is the *standard KTU-level model* used in every modern ACC paper.

### Step 1: Define the thermal capacitance of the cabin

The cabin air + interior trim + glass + seats act as a single lumped mass.

$$C_{cab} = m_{air}\,c_{p,air} + m_{trim}\,c_{p,trim} + m_{glass}\,c_{p,glass}$$

Substituting typical values for a mid-size sedan:
- $m_{air} \approx 2.5$ kg, $c_{p,air} = 1 005$ J/kg·K
- $m_{trim} \approx 30$ kg, $c_{p,trim} = 1 500$ J/kg·K
- $m_{glass} \approx 20$ kg, $c_{p,glass} = 750$ J/kg·K

$$C_{cab} = (2.5)(1 005) + (30)(1 500) + (20)(750) = 2 512 + 45 000 + 15 000 = 62 512 \;\text{J/K}$$

> 📌 Engineering round-off: $C_{cab} \approx 6.25 \times 10^{4}$ J/K ≈ **62.5 kJ/K**.

### Step 2: Define the thermal resistance of the cabin envelope

The cabin loses heat by conduction through walls, glass, and infiltration.

$$\frac{1}{R_{cab}} = \frac{1}{R_{walls}} + \frac{1}{R_{glass}} + \frac{1}{R_{infiltration}} + \frac{1}{R_{ventilation}}$$

For a representative sedan: $R_{cab} \approx 0.01$ K/W (i.e., 100 W of heat ingress per °C of $\Delta T$).

### Step 3: Energy balance — the heart of the model

The rate of change of internal energy equals net heat in:

$$C_{cab}\,\frac{dT_{cab}}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{comp} + \dot{Q}_{heater} + \dot{Q}_{solar} - \frac{T_{cab} - T_{amb}}{R_{cab}}$$

This is a **first-order linear ordinary differential equation (ODE)** — *the single most important equation in ACC design*.

### Step 4: Rearrange into standard state-space form

$$\frac{dT_{cab}}{dt} + \frac{1}{R_{cab}\,C_{cab}}\,T_{cab} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{comp} + \dot{Q}_{heater} + \dot{Q}_{solar}}{C_{cab}} + \frac{T_{amb}}{R_{cab}\,C_{cab}}$$

Define the time constant:

$$\tau_{cab} = R_{cab} \cdot C_{cab} = (0.01)(62 500) = 625\;\text{s} \approx 10.4\;\text{min}$$

### Step 5: Solve the ODE (homogeneous + particular)

**Homogeneous solution:**

$$T_{h}(t) = A\,e^{-t/\tau_{cab}}$$

**Particular solution (steady-state, $\dot{T}_{cab} = 0$):**

$$T_{ss} = T_{amb} + R_{cab}\left(\dot{Q}_{comp} + \dot{Q}_{heater} + \dot{Q}_{solar}\right)$$

**General solution:**

$$T_{cab}(t) = T_{ss} + \left[T_{cab}(0) - T_{ss}\right]e^{-t/\tau_{cab}}$$

### Step 6: Numerical Worked Example

A car parked at noon: $T_{amb} = 38$ °C, $\dot{Q}_{solar} = 800$ W, $\dot{Q}_{infiltration} = 200$ W. ACC delivers $\dot{Q}_{comp} = -3 000$ W (cooling). Initial cabin temp $T_{cab}(0) = 60$ °C.

$$T_{ss} = 38 + 0.01 \times (-3 000 + 800 + 200) = 38 + 0.01 \times (-2 000) = 38 - 20 = 18\;°C$$

$$T_{cab}(t) = 18 + (60 - 18)e^{-t/625} = 18 + 42\,e^{-t/625}\;\;°C$$

At $t = 5$ min (300 s):

$$T_{cab}(300) = 18 + 42\,e^{-0.48} = 18 + 42(0.619) = 18 + 26.0 = 44.0\;°C$$

At $t = 15$ min (900 s):

$$T_{cab}(900) = 18 + 42\,e^{-1.44} = 18 + 42(0.237) = 18 + 9.95 \approx 27.9\;°C$$

✅ **Comfortable 28 °C** achieved in **15 minutes** — realistic for a hot-soak Indian cabin.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — PID Controller for the ACC Loop

> [!NOTE]
> The HVAC-ECU's *control algorithm* is most often a **PID controller** (Proportional–Integral–Derivative) because it handles the slow thermal dynamics of the cabin with negligible overshoot and zero steady-state error.

### Step 1: Define the error

$$e(t) = T_{set} - T_{cab}(t)$$

### Step 2: Construct the PID law

$$u(t) = K_{p}\,e(t) + K_{i}\int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_{d}\,\frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

The three terms have distinct physical meanings:

| Term | Action | Physical Role in ACC |
|---|---|---|
| $K_{p}\,e(t)$ | Proportional | Immediate correction — *turn the compressor on harder* |
| $K_{i}\int e\,d\tau$ | Integral | Eliminates steady-state offset (cabin never quite hits set-point) |
| $K_{d}\,\dot{e}(t)$ | Derivative | Anticipates future error — *pre-cool when door is opened* |

### Step 3: Ziegler–Nichols Tuning (KTU-friendly)

For a first-order plant with dead-time (our cabin model fits this exactly):

$$K_{p} = 0.9\,\frac{\tau_{cab}}{K_{proc}\,\theta}, \quad T_{i} = 3\,\theta, \quad T_{d} = 0.5\,\theta$$

where $K_{proc}$ is the static gain (°C per % actuator) and $\theta$ is the dead time.

### Step 4: Closed-Loop Transfer Function (Laplace Domain)

Take the Laplace transform of the PID + plant:

$$\frac{T_{cab}(s)}{T_{set}(s)} = \frac{\left(K_{p} + K_{i}/s + K_{d}s\right)\cdot G_{plant}(s)}{1 + \left(K_{p} + K_{i}/s + K_{d}s\right)\cdot G_{plant}(s)}$$

For the cabin plant (first-order + dead-time):

$$G_{plant}(s) = \frac{K_{proc}\,e^{-\theta s}}{(\tau_{cab}\,s + 1)}$$

✅ This expression is the **basis of every ACC ECU tuning exercise** and is a frequent 14-mark KTU question.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — COP of the Vapour-Compression Refrigeration Loop

The refrigeration cycle (compressor → condenser → TXV → evaporator) provides the cooling capacity $\dot{Q}_{L}$.

### Step 1: Apply the 1st Law to the cycle as a control volume

$$\dot{W}_{comp,in} + \dot{Q}_{H,in} = \dot{Q}_{L,out}$$

For a steady cyclic device:

$$\dot{W}_{comp} = \dot{Q}_{H} - \dot{Q}_{L}$$

### Step 2: Define the COP (Coefficient of Performance)

$$\text{COP} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{L}}{\dot{W}_{comp}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{L}}{\dot{Q}_{H} - \dot{Q}_{L}}$$

### Step 3: Carnot upper bound

$$\text{COP}_{Carnot} = \frac{T_{L}}{T_{H} - T_{L}}$$

For $T_{L} = 278$ K (5 °C evaporator) and $T_{H} = 318$ K (45 °C condenser):

$$\text{COP}_{Carnot} = \frac{278}{318 - 278} = \frac{278}{40} = 6.95$$

A real R-134a automotive system achieves $\text{COP}_{real} \approx 2.0$ – $3.0$, giving a **second-law efficiency** of $28\% - 43\%$.

### Step 4: Heat-pump COP (used in EVs for heating)

$$\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{H}}{\dot{W}_{comp}} = 1 + \text{COP}_{cooling} = 1 + 2.5 = 3.5$$

✅ A heat-pump delivers **3.5 kW of heat for 1 kW of electrical input** — making it **3.5× more efficient** than resistive PTC heaters, and a major reason modern EVs (e.g., Hyundai Kona EV, MG Windsor EV) use heat-pump ACC.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Compressor Technologies

| Feature | Fixed-Displacement (Swash-Plate) | Variable-Displacement (VDM) | Electric Scroll (EV) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Capacity control | Clutch on/off | Inline stroke change | Speed control |
| Part-load efficiency | **Poor** | **Good** | **Excellent** |
| Mechanical link to engine | **Yes (belt)** | Yes | **No (HV-driven)** |
| Use in HEV | Limited | Yes | ✅ Preferred |
| Use in BEV | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ Mandatory |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High |
| Noise | High cycling | Low | Lowest |

## 4.2 Refrigeration / Heat-Pump Loop Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    comp["ELECTRIC OR VDM COMPRESSOR - High Pressure Vapor Out"]
    cond["CONDENSER - Front of Radiator - Rejects Qh to Ambient"]
    recv["RECEIVER DRIER - Removes Moisture and Debris"]
    tXV["EXPANSION VALVE - TXV or EXV - Pressure Drop"]
    evap["EVAPORATOR - Inside HVAC Box - Absorbs QL from Air"]
    accu["ACCUMULATOR - Protects Compressor - Liquid to Vapor"]

    comp --> cond
    cond --> recv
    recv --> tXV
    tXV --> evap
    evap --> accu
    accu --> comp
```

## 4.4 Control Loop Functional Flow (Closed-Loop ACC)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    stepA["STEP 1 - Read Tset from HMI and Driver Profile"]
    stepB["STEP 2 - Sample All Sensors at 10 ms"]
    stepC["STEP 3 - Compute Error e of t = Tset minus Tcab"]
    stepD["STEP 4 - Run PID or MPC Algorithm"]
    stepE["STEP 5 - Generate Three Outputs - Blower Blend Compressor"]
    stepF["STEP 6 - Drive Actuators via CAN or LIN"]
    stepG["STEP 7 - Measure New Tcab after dt = 100 ms"]
    stepH["STEP 8 - Re enter Loop at Step 3"]

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> stepC
```
