Unlike a battery which stores energy, a fuel cell *generates* electricity on demand from external fuel. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metrics for Hydrogen Fuel Cells:** > - Theoretical Open Circuit Voltage: **E° = 1.229 V** (at Standard Temperature & Pressure, 25°C, 1 atm) > - Practical Operating Voltage (single cell): **0.6 V – 0.8 V** under load > - Automotive Stack Voltage: **300 V – 750 V** (achieved by stacking ~400 cells in series) > - Operating Temperature: **60°C – 1000°C** depending on electrolyte type > - Faraday's Constant: **F = 96,485 C/mol** ### 1.3 Basic Electrochemistry of a Hydrogen Fuel Cell The hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell operates on the following half-cell reactions: **At the Anode (Oxidation — fuel electrode):** $$H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^- \quad (E°_{anode} = 0\,V \text{ vs. SHE})$$ **At the Cathode (Reduction — oxidant electrode):** $$\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O_{(l)} \quad (E°_{cathode} = 1.229\,V \text{ vs. SHE})$$ **Overall Cell Reaction:** $$H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O_{(l)} \quad (E°_{cell} = 1.229\,V)$$ The **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)** serves as the universal zero-reference, and the standard cell potential is calculated as: $$E°_{cell} = E°_{cathode} - E°_{anode} = 1.229 - 0 = 1.229\,V$$ > [!NOTE] > For **PEMFC (Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cells)** — the dominant automotive type used in cars like the Toyota Mirai and Hyundai NEXO — the operating temperature is typically **60–80°C**, with a membrane that conducts only **H⁺ protons** and uses **Platinum** as a catalyst. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Polarization Concept — A Structured Logic Breakdown When a fuel cell is operated, the actual terminal voltage drops below the theoretical reversible voltage. This drop is called **Polarization** or **Overpotential (η)**. The total polarization is the sum of three major categories: #### **Step 1: Activation Polarization (η_act) — The "Starter Friction"** - **Why it happens:** To initiate the electrochemical reaction, energy must overcome an **activation energy barrier** at the electrode surface. Sluggish reaction kinetics (especially the Oxygen Reduction Reaction — ORR at the cathode) require this extra "push" of voltage. - **How it dominates:** Most prominent at **low current densities** (the initial steep drop in the V–I curve). - **Governing equation (Tafel Equation):** $$\eta_{act} = \frac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right)$$ Where: - $\alpha$ = Charge transfer coefficient (typically 0.5 for ORR) - $n$ = Number of electrons transferred (2 for H₂/O₂) - $i$ = Current density (A/cm²) - $i_0$ = Exchange current density (A/cm²) — depends on catalyst, temperature, pressure #### **Step 2: Ohmic Polarization (η_ohmic) — The "Wire Resistance"** - **Why it happens:** Resistance to the flow of **ions** through the electrolyte/membrane and **electrons** through electrodes, bipolar plates, and interconnects. This behaves exactly like a standard resistor obeying Ohm's Law. - **How it dominates:** Creates the **linear middle region** of the V–I curve. - **Governing equation (Ohm's Law):** $$\eta_{ohmic} = i \cdot R_{internal}$$ Where $R_{internal}$ is the area-specific resistance (ASR) in Ω·cm², including membrane, electrode, contact, and plate resistances. #### **Step 3: Concentration Polarization (η_conc) — The "Traffic Jam"** - **Why it happens:** At high current densities, reactant gases (H₂, O₂) are consumed faster than they can be supplied to the electrode surface, and product water cannot be removed quickly enough. This creates concentration gradients. - **How it dominates:** Causes the **sharp cliff-like drop** at high current densities (the curve "falls off a cliff"). - **Governing equation:** $$\eta_{conc} = \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(\frac{i_L}{i_L - i}\right)$$ Where $i_L$ is the **Limiting Current Density** — the maximum rate at which reactants can be supplied by mass transport. #### **Step 4: The Net Operational Voltage** $$V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$$ This is the **single most important equation** for Module 4 numerical problems. ### 2.2 The Nernst Equation — Theoretical Voltage Under Any Condition The reversible cell potential changes with **temperature, pressure, and reactant concentration**. The Nernst equation captures this: $$E = E° + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\left(\frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$$ **At Standard Conditions (25°C, 1 atm):** $E = E° = 1.229$ V (liquid product water) **At 80°C (typical PEMFC operating temperature):** $E° \approx 1.185$ V (slightly lower due to temperature effect on Gibbs free energy) **Simplified Pressure Correction (for automotive use, where air is supplied, not pure O₂):** $$E = E° + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\left(\frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}}{P_{H_2O}}\right) - \frac{RT}{4F} \ln\left(\frac{1}{P_{O_2,air}}\right) \cdot (correction\, for\, air\, vs.\, pure\, O_2)$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A common mistake students make is using the Nernst equation with pure oxygen partial pressure (1 atm) when the cathode actually receives **air** (O₂ partial pressure = 0.21 atm). This introduces a voltage penalty of about **~30–50 mV**. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Standard Cell Potential** | $E° = 1.229\,V$ | At 25°C, 1 atm, liquid H₂O | Theoretical max voltage for H₂/O₂ cell | | 2 | **Nernst Equation** | $E = E° + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\left(\frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$ | R = 8.314 J/(mol·K), T in K, F = 96,485 C/mol, P in atm | Voltage under non-standard T, P, conc. | | 3 | **Activation Loss (Tafel)** | $\eta_{act} = \frac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right)$ | $\alpha$ ≈ 0.5, $i_0$ in A/cm² | Slow electrode kinetics penalty | | 4 | **Ohmic Loss (Ohm's Law)** | $\eta_{ohmic} = i \cdot R_{internal}$ | $i$ in A/cm², $R$ in Ω·cm² | Ionic + electronic resistance penalty | | 5 | **Concentration Loss** | $\eta_{conc} = \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(\frac{i_L}{i_L - i}\right)$ | $i_L$ = limiting current density (A/cm²) | Mass transport starvation penalty | | 6 | **Operational Cell Voltage** | $V_{cell} = E - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$ | All in Volts | Actual deliverable voltage | | 7 | **Stack Voltage** | $V_{stack} = N_{cells} \cdot V_{cell}$ | N = number of cells in series | Total automotive stack voltage | | 8 | **Faraday's Efficiency** | $\eta_F = \frac{I_{actual}}{I_{theoretical}}$ | Dimensionless | Fuel utilization efficiency | | 9 | **Power Density** | $P_{density} = V_{cell} \cdot i$ | W/cm² | Power per unit active area | | 10 | **Energy Conversion** | $\Delta G° = -nFE°$ | ΔG in J/mol | Gibbs free energy ↔ reversible voltage | > **Constants Table (Memorize for KTU exams):** > - R = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** > - F = **96,485 C/mol** (also written as 96,500) > - Standard T = **298.15 K (25°C)** > - PEMFC operating T = **353 K (80°C)** > - RT/F at 25°C ≈ **0.02569 V** > - RT/2F at 25°C ≈ **0.01285 V** ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In automotive applications: - **Toyota Mirai (Gen 2)**: 128 kW stack, 650 V nominal, ~330 cells - **Hyundai NEXO**: 95 kW, 600 V, range ~666 km - **Honda Clarity**: 103 kW, 365 cells, 370 V The operational voltage governs **vehicle power electronics design** — specifically the DC-DC boost converter that raises the ~300V fuel cell stack voltage to the ~650V traction bus required by motor inverters. A 10% drop in cell voltage translates to roughly a **10–15% drop in stack power output** for a fixed current, directly impacting vehicle acceleration and range. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: The Nernst Equation from Gibbs Free Energy The Nernst equation can be derived from fundamental thermodynamic principles linking Gibbs free energy to electrical work. **Starting Point — First Law applied to a reversible electrochemical cell:** The maximum electrical work obtainable from a chemical reaction equals the negative change in Gibbs free energy: $$W_{max} = -\Delta G$$ The electrical work per mole of electrons transferred is: $$W_{electrical} = nFE$$ Equating the two: $$-\Delta G = nFE$$ $$\boxed{E = \frac{-\Delta G}{nF}}$$ For the standard state: $$E° = \frac{-\Delta G°}{nF}$$ **Derivation of the Nernst Equation from Chemical Potential:** For a general cell reaction: $aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$ The change in Gibbs free energy at non-standard conditions: $$\Delta G = \Delta G° + RT \ln\left(\frac{a_C^c \cdot a_D^d}{a_A^a \cdot a_B^b}\right)$$ Substituting into $E = -\Delta G / nF$: $$E = \frac{-\Delta G°}{nF} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(\frac{a_C^c \cdot a_D^d}{a_A^a \cdot a_B^b}\right)$$ $$\boxed{E = E° - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(\frac{a_C^c \cdot a_D^d}{a_A^a \cdot a_B^b}\right)}$$ **Application to the Hydrogen–Oxygen Fuel Cell:** Reaction: $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O_{(l)}$ - Reactants: $H_2$ (a = 1), $O_2$ (a = 0.5) - Product: $H_2O$ (a = 1) $$E = E° - \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\left(\frac{a_{H_2O}}{a_{H_2} \cdot a_{O_2}^{0.5}}\right)$$ For gas-phase activities approximated by partial pressures (in atm), with liquid water having activity = 1: $$\boxed{E = E° + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\left(\frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)}$$ > **Q.E.D. — The Nernst Equation is thermodynamically derived.** ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Nernst Voltage Under Real Conditions **Problem:** A PEM fuel cell operates at 80°C (353 K) with hydrogen at 1.5 atm, air (O₂ partial pressure = 0.21 × 3 atm = 0.63 atm) at the cathode, and saturated water vapor. Calculate the reversible cell voltage. **Given:** - T = 353 K - $P_{H_2}$ = 1.5 atm - $P_{O_2}$ = 0.63 atm - $P_{H_2O}$ = 0.467 atm (saturated vapor pressure at 80°C) **Solution:** Step 1 — Use $E°$ at 80°C: For PEMFC at 80°C, the corrected standard potential is $E° \approx 1.185$ V (slightly reduced from 1.229 V at 25°C due to entropy change of reaction). Step 2 — Apply the Nernst equation: $$E = E° + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\left(\frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$$ Step 3 — Calculate the RT/2F term: $$\frac{RT}{2F} = \frac{8.314 \times 353}{2 \times 96,485}$$ $$\frac{RT}{2F} = \frac{2934.84}{192,970}$$ $$\frac{RT}{2F} = 0.01521\,V$$ Step 4 — Evaluate the logarithm argument: $$\frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}}{P_{H_2O}} = \frac{1.5 \times (0.63)^{0.5}}{0.467}$$ $$(0.63)^{0.5} = \sqrt{0.63} = 0.7937$$ $$\frac{1.5 \times 0.7937}{0.467} = \frac{1.1906}{0.467} = 2.5497$$ Step 5 — Compute the natural logarithm: $$\ln(2.5497) = 0.9359$$ Step 6 — Final calculation: $$E = 1.185 + (0.01521)(0.9359)$$ $$E = 1.185 + 0.01424$$ $$\boxed{E \approx 1.199\,V}$$ This is the **open-circuit (reversible) voltage** before any current is drawn. ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Full Operational Voltage Calculation **Problem:** For the same fuel cell above, at an operating current density of $i = 0.5\,A/cm^2$, calculate the terminal voltage. Given: - Exchange current density: $i_0 = 10^{-3}\,A/cm^2$ (Pt catalyst, 80°C) - Charge transfer coefficient: $\alpha = 0.5$ - Internal resistance (ASR): $R_{internal} = 0.15\,\Omega \cdot cm^2$ - Limiting current density: $i_L = 1.5\,A/cm^2$ **Solution — Step by Step:** **Step 1: Activation Loss (Tafel)** $$\eta_{act} = \frac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right)$$ Calculate the Tafel slope factor: $$\frac{RT}{\alpha n F} = \frac{8.314 \times 353}{0.5 \times 2 \times 96,485} = \frac{2934.84}{96,485} = 0.03042\,V$$ Compute the log ratio: $$\frac{i}{i_0} = \frac{0.5}{0.001} = 500$$ $$\ln(500) = 6.2146$$ Therefore: $$\eta_{act} = (0.03042)(6.2146)$$ $$\eta_{act} = 0.1890\,V$$ **Step 2: Ohmic Loss (Ohm's Law)** $$\eta_{ohmic} = i \cdot R_{internal} = 0.5 \times 0.15$$ $$\eta_{ohmic} = 0.075\,V$$ **Step 3: Concentration Loss** $$\eta_{conc} = \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(\frac{i_L}{i_L - i}\right)$$ Compute the concentration ratio: $$\frac{i_L}{i_L - i} = \frac{1.5}{1.5 - 0.5} = \frac{1.5}{1.0} = 1.5$$ $$\ln(1.5) = 0.4055$$ Calculate the prefactor: $$\frac{RT}{nF} = \frac{2934.84}{2 \times 96,485} = \frac{2934.84}{192,970} = 0.01521\,V$$ Therefore: $$\eta_{conc} = (0.01521)(0.4055)$$ $$\eta_{conc} = 0.00617\,V$$ **Step 4: Total Cell Voltage** $$V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$$ $$V_{cell} = 1.199 - 0.1890 - 0.075 - 0.00617$$ $$\boxed{V_{cell} \approx 0.929\,V}$$ **Interpretation:** The cell delivers ~0.93 V at 0.5 A/cm² — a drop of 270 mV from the reversible voltage, with **activation loss dominating (~70% of total losses)** — a hallmark of PEM fuel cell behavior at moderate current density. **Step 5: Power Density** $$P_{density} = V_{cell} \times i = 0.929 \times 0.5$$ $$P_{density} = 0.4645\,W/cm^2$$ **Step 6: Stack Power (300-cell automotive stack, 500 cm² active area)** $$P_{stack} = 0.4645 \times 500 \times 300 = 69,675\,W \approx 69.7\,kW$$ This represents a mid-sized automotive fuel cell stack power output. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Loss Region Identification | Region | Current Density Range | Dominant Loss | V–I Curve Shape | Mitigation Strategy | |---|---|---|---|---| | **I. Activation** | 0 – 0.1 A/cm² | $\eta_{act}$ | Exponential drop | Better catalysts (Pt alloys), higher T | | **II. Ohmic** | 0.1 – 1.0 A/cm² | $\eta_{ohmic}$ | Linear decline | Thinner membrane, better conductors | | **III. Concentration** | > 0.8 A/cm² | $\eta_{conc}$ | Sharp cliff fall | Higher flow rates, better GDL design | --- ## 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Define a fuel cell. State the standard open-circuit voltage of a hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell at 25°C and 1 atm pressure.** **Model Answer:** A fuel cell is an electrochemical device that directly converts the chemical energy of a fuel (such as hydrogen) and an oxidant (such as oxygen) into electrical energy through an electrochemical reaction, with water and heat as by-products. The overall reaction is: $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O_{(l)}$ The **standard open-circuit voltage (E°)** of a hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell at 25°C and 1 atm is: $$\boxed{E° = 1.229\,V}$$ This value is derived from the standard Gibbs free energy of formation of liquid water (–237.13 kJ/mol) using $E° = -\Delta G°/(nF)$. --- #### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: List the three major polarization losses in a fuel cell and state the region of the V–I curve where each dominates.** **Model Answer:** The three major polarization losses in a fuel cell are: | # | Loss Type | Physical Origin | Dominant Region | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Activation Polarization (η_act)** | Slow electrode kinetics, especially the Oxygen Reduction Reaction (ORR) at the cathode | **Low current density** (0 – 0.1 A/cm²) | | 2 | **Ohmic Polarization (η_ohmic)** | Ionic resistance of electrolyte/membrane + electronic resistance of electrodes/plates | **Medium current density** (0.1 – 1.0 A/cm²) — linear region | | 3 | **Concentration Polarization (η_conc)** | Mass transport limitations — reactants cannot reach electrode surface fast enough | **High current density** (> 0.8 A/cm²) — sharp drop region | --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice --- #### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: (a)** Derive the Nernst equation for a hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell starting from the Gibbs free energy relationship. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A PEM fuel cell operates at 70°C with the following conditions: $P_{H_2} = 2.0$ atm, $P_{O_2} = 0.5$ atm (cathode partial pressure of oxygen in air at 2.4 atm total), and $P_{H_2O} = 0.31$ atm. The exchange current density is $i_0 = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ A/cm², the charge transfer coefficient is $\alpha = 0.5$, the internal area-specific resistance is 0.18 Ω·cm², and the limiting current density is 1.2 A/cm². Calculate the operational cell voltage at $i = 0.4$ A/cm². Use $E° = 1.185$ V at 70°C. **(7 marks)** --- ##### **Model Solution — Part (a): Derivation of Nernst Equation** **Step 1:** The maximum non-PV (pressure-volume) work obtainable from a chemical reaction equals the negative change in Gibbs free energy: $$W_{max} = -\Delta G$$ **Step 2:** For an electrochemical cell, the electrical work per mole of reaction is: $$W_{elect} = nFE$$ **Step 3:** Equating the two (reversible cell operation): $$-\Delta G = nFE \implies E = \frac{-\Delta G}{nF}$$ **Step 4:** For non-standard conditions, the change in Gibbs free energy is: $$\Delta G = \Delta G° + RT \ln Q$$ where $Q$ is the reaction quotient. **Step 5:** Substituting: $$E = \frac{-\Delta G°}{nF} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q = E° - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$ **Step 6:** For the H₂/O₂ reaction $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$: $$Q = \frac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}}$$ **Step 7:** Therefore: $$\boxed{E = E° + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\left(\frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)}$$ This is the Nernst equation for a hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell. --- ##### **Model Solution — Part (b): Operational Voltage Calculation** **Given:** - T = 343 K (70°C) - $P_{H_2} = 2.0$ atm, $P_{O_2} = 0.5$ atm, $P_{H_2O} = 0.31$ atm - $i_0 = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ A/cm², $\alpha = 0.5$, $R = 0.18$ Ω·cm² - $i_L = 1.2$ A/cm², $i = 0.4$ A/cm² - $E° = 1.185$ V **Step 1 — Nernst Voltage:** $$E = 1.185 + \frac{8.314 \times 343}{2 \times 96,485} \ln\left(\frac{2.0 \times (0.5)^{0.5}}{0.31}\right)$$ $$\frac{RT}{2F} = \frac{2851.7}{192,970} = 0.01478\,V$$ $$\frac{2.0 \times 0.7071}{0.31} = \frac{1.4142}{0.31} = 4.5614$$ $$\ln(4.5614) = 1.5179$$ $$E = 1.185 + (0.01478)(1.5179) = 1.185 + 0.02243 = 1.2074\,V$$ **Step 2 — Activation Loss:** $$\eta_{act} = \frac{0.01478}{0.5} \ln\left(\frac{0.4}{0.0005}\right) = 0.02956 \times \ln(800)$$ $$\ln(800) = 6.6846$$ $$\eta_{act} = 0.02956 \times 6.6846 = 0.1976\,V$$ **Step 3 — Ohmic Loss:** $$\eta_{ohmic} = 0.4 \times 0.18 = 0.072\,V$$ **Step 4 — Concentration Loss:** $$\eta_{conc} = 0.01478 \times \ln\left(\frac{1.2}{1.2 - 0.4}\right) = 0.01478 \times \ln(1.5)$$ $$\ln(1.5) = 0.4055$$ $$\eta_{conc} = 0.01478 \times 0.4055 = 0.00599\,V$$ **Step 5 — Operational Voltage:** $$V_{cell} = 1.2074 - 0.1976 - 0.072 - 0.00599$$ $$\boxed{V_{cell} \approx 0.9318\,V}$$ --- #### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: (a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working principle of a Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC). List its advantages and disadvantages for automotive applications. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell stack consists of 250 cells in series. Each cell has an internal area-specific resistance of 0.2 Ω·cm² and an active area of 400 cm². At a stack current of 200 A, the activation loss per cell is 0.15 V and the concentration loss is 0.03 V. If the Nernst voltage per cell is 1.18 V, calculate: (i) the ohmic voltage drop per cell, (ii) the operational voltage per cell, and (iii) the total stack power output. **(7 marks)** --- ##### **Model Solution — Part (a): PEMFC Working Principle, Advantages & Disadvantages** **Working Principle:** A PEMFC uses a **proton-conducting polymer membrane** (typically Nafion™) as the electrolyte. The membrane is sandwiched between two platinum-catalyzed electrodes. **At the Anode:** Hydrogen gas is fed to the anode side, where the platinum catalyst dissociates H₂ into protons and electrons: $$H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$$ The protons migrate through the membrane toward the cathode, while the electrons are forced through an external circuit (producing useful electric current) because the membrane is impermeable to electrons. **At the Cathode:** Oxygen (from air) is fed to the cathode, where it combines with the arriving protons and electrons: $$\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O$$ The product water is removed (often as vapor) from the cathode side, and unreacted air helps sweep out the water. **Advantages of PEMFC for Automobiles:** 1. **Low operating temperature** (60–80°C) → fast cold-start, suitable for cars 2. **High power density** (~1 W/cm²) → compact stack 3. **Solid electrolyte** → no leakage, no corrosion 4. **Quick response** to load changes 5. **Zero tailpipe emissions** — only water vapor **Disadvantages of PEMFC for Automobiles:** 1. **Expensive platinum catalyst** (~$30/g, ~30–50 g per vehicle) 2. **Sensitivity to CO poisoning** (< 10 ppm tolerable) 3. **Membrane dehydration** at high temperatures 4. **Hydrogen storage challenges** — high-pressure tanks (700 bar) or cryogenic liquid 5. **Durability** issues under automotive vibration and freeze-thaw cycles --- ##### **Model Solution — Part (b): Stack Power Calculation** **Given:** - $N_{cells} = 250$ - $R = 0.2$ Ω·cm² per cell - $A = 400$ cm² per cell - $I_{stack} = 200$ A - $\eta_{act} = 0.15$ V/cell - $\eta_{conc} = 0.03$ V/cell - $E = 1.18$ V/cell **Step 1 — Current Density:** $$i = \frac{I_{stack}}{A} = \frac{200}{400} = 0.5\,A/cm^2$$ **Step 2 (i) — Ohmic Loss Per Cell:** $$\eta_{ohmic} = i \cdot R = 0.5 \times 0.2 = 0.1\,V$$ **Step 3 (ii) — Operational Voltage Per Cell:** $$V_{cell} = E - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$$ $$V_{cell} = 1.18 - 0.15 - 0.1 - 0.03$$ $$\boxed{V_{cell} = 0.90\,V}$$ **Step 4 (iii) — Total Stack Voltage and Power:** $$V_{stack} = N_{cells} \times V_{cell} = 250 \times 0.90 = 225\,V$$ $$P_{stack} = V_{stack} \times I_{stack} = 225 \times 200$$ $$\boxed{P_{stack} = 45,000\,W = 45\,kW}$$ This represents a typical mid-size automotive fuel cell stack power rating. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Examinations:** > > 1. **Wrong sign in Nernst equation** — Students often flip the log ratio (writing $P_{H_2O}$ in the numerator instead of denominator). The Nernst equation **adds** the log of the reactant/product ratio to E°, not subtracts. > > 2. **Using pure O₂ partial pressure (1 atm) when air is used** — Air contains only 21% O₂, so $P_{O_2} = 0.21 \times P_{air,total}$. This error typically gives ~30 mV of error. > > 3. **Confusing activation loss's Tafel slope** — The factor is $\frac{RT}{\alpha n F}$, NOT $\frac{RT}{n F}$. The $\alpha$ (charge transfer coefficient) must appear in the denominator. > > 4. **Forgetting to subtract ALL THREE losses** — Students sometimes compute activation loss correctly but forget the other two. The full equation $V = E - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$ must always be used. > > 5. **Using 25°C values (298 K) for PEMFC problems** — PEMFCs operate at 60–80°C (333–353 K). Using room temperature gives wrong Nernst voltage. > > 6. **Units mismatch** — Current density in A/cm² must be paired with resistance in Ω·cm². Mixing SI units (Ω·m²) with cgs current density will cause 10⁴ errors. > > 7. **Skipping derivation steps** — In 7-mark derivation questions, the KTU examiner expects at least 5–6 explicit steps. Skipping from $\Delta G$ directly to the final Nernst form costs 3–4 marks. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fuel Cell Operational Voltages** - [ ] **Standard reversible voltage** for H₂/O₂ cell at 25°C = **1.229 V**; at 80°C ≈ **1.185 V** - [ ] **Nernst equation** is derived from $-\Delta G = nFE$ and modifies E° with a logarithmic pressure term - [ ] **Three polarization losses** in order of dominance at low current: Activation → Ohmic → Concentration - [ ] **Activation loss** uses the **Tafel equation**: $\eta_{act} = \frac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln(i/i_0)$; dominant at low current - [ ] **Ohmic loss** follows **Ohm's Law** linearly: $\eta_{ohmic} = iR$; dominant in the middle region - [ ] **Concentration loss** has a logarithmic form with limiting current $i_L$; dominant at high current - [ ] **Operational voltage equation**: $V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$ - [ ] **PEMFC** operates at 60–80°C with Nafion membrane; suitable for automobiles - [ ] **Stack voltage** = N × V_cell; typical automotive stacks have 300–400 cells delivering 300–750 V - [ ] **Air vs. pure O₂**: Use $P_{O_2} = 0.21 \times P_{air}$ when air is the oxidant - [ ] **Constants to memorize**: R = 8.314 J/(mol·K), F = 96,485 C/mol, RT/F at 25°C ≈ 0.0257 V - [ ] **Power output** is limited by V_cell × I, so reducing polarization losses directly increases vehicle range and performance - [ ] **The polarization curve** is the single most important graphical tool — it must be drawn qualitatively with all three regions labeled for full credit on diagram questions ---

topics.title: Types (Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cells)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET414 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types (Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cells)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: TYPES OF FUEL CELLS — PROTON EXCHANGE MEMBRANE FUEL CELLS (PEMFC)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> A **Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC)**, also called a Polymer Electrolyte Membrane Fuel Cell, is an electrochemical energy conversion device that generates electricity directly from hydrogen and oxygen (or air) using a solid, water-based, perfluorosulfonic acid polymer membrane (e.g., Nafion™) as the proton-conducting electrolyte, with platinum-based catalysts at both electrodes, operating at low temperatures of **60 °C – 100 °C**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Sandwich That Makes Electricity"

Imagine a **chocolate-stuffed biscuit (Oreo-style sandwich)**:
- The **two outer chocolate wafers** = the **bipolar plates** (current collectors and gas distributors).
- The **inner cream filling** = the **Membrane Electrode Assembly (MEA)**.
- You blow hydrogen gas on one side of the sandwich, oxygen on the other, and a tiny electrical current flows out of the biscuit — just like a battery that **never runs flat as long as you keep feeding it gas**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight — Why PEMFC matters in Automotive Technology:**
> PEMFCs are the **primary fuel cell technology** used in modern hydrogen Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEVs) such as the *Toyota Mirai*, *Hyundai Nexo*, and *Honda Clarity*, because of their low operating temperature, quick start-up, and high power density (~0.5 – 1.0 W/cm²).

### Key Standard Metrics (KTU 2024 Expectation)

| Metric | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Operating Temperature | **60 °C – 100 °C** |
| Operating Pressure | 1 – 3 bar (atmospheric to mild pressurization) |
| Theoretical Open Circuit Voltage (OCV) | **1.229 V** |
| Practical Cell Voltage | 0.6 – 0.75 V under load |
| Power Density | **0.5 – 1.0 W/cm²** |
| Energy Efficiency | 50 – 60 % (LHV basis) |
| Common Membrane | Nafion™ 115, 117, 212 |
| Catalyst Loading | 0.1 – 0.4 mg Pt/cm² |

---

## 2. Position of PEMFC in the Fuel Cell Family (KTU Module Context)

Within Module 4 — *Types of Fuel Cells* — PEMFC belongs to the **low-temperature, solid-electrolyte** class.

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison Anchor for Exam:** Students must be able to contrast PEMFC with **Alkaline (AFC)**, **Phosphoric Acid (PAFC)**, **Molten Carbonate (MCFC)**, and **Solid Oxide (SOFC)**. The major differentiator is the **state of the electrolyte and operating temperature**.

| Fuel Cell Type | Electrolyte | Operating Temp | Charge Carrier | Mobile Ion | Key Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PEMFC** | Polymer membrane (Nafion) | 60 – 100 °C | H⁺ | H⁺ | **Automotive (FCEV)** |
| AFC | KOH solution | 60 – 90 °C | OH⁻ | OH⁻ | Space, military |
| PAFC | H₃PO₄ | 150 – 220 °C | H⁺ | H⁺ | Stationary power |
| MCFC | Molten Li₂CO₃/K₂CO₃ | 600 – 700 °C | CO₃²⁻ | CO₃²⁻ | Large stationary |
| SOFC | YSZ (Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia) | 600 – 1000 °C | O²⁻ | O²⁻ | Stationary, APU |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Anatomy of a PEMFC

A single PEMFC cell is built as a **layered stack**. From outside inwards, the repeating unit (called a **cell**) consists of:

- **Bipolar Plate (BPP)** — graphite or metal, with flow channels for H₂ and air; collects current and separates cells in a stack.
- **Gas Diffusion Layer (GDL)** — porous carbon paper/cloth; distributes reactant gases uniformly and removes product water.
- **Anode Catalyst Layer (ACL)** — Pt nanoparticles (≈ 2–4 nm) on carbon support; site of **Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction (HOR)**.
- **Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM)** — Nafion™ or equivalent perfluorosulfonic acid film (25 – 175 µm thick); conducts **H⁺ ions** but is impermeable to electrons and gases.
- **Cathode Catalyst Layer (CCL)** — Pt nanoparticles; site of **Oxygen Reduction Reaction (ORR)**.
- **Gas Diffusion Layer (GDL)** — symmetric to anode side; supplies air/O₂ and expels product water.

> The **ACL + PEM + CCL** is collectively termed the **Membrane Electrode Assembly (MEA)** — the heart of the PEMFC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:**
> The membrane must remain **fully hydrated** to conduct protons efficiently. Two failure modes frequently tested: **membrane drying** (low ionic conductivity) and **flooding** (blocks gas access at cathode). Vehicle systems use **humidifiers and water management loops** to keep the MEA in the optimal hydration window.

## 2.2 Electrochemistry — Half-Cell & Full-Cell Reactions

### At the Anode (Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction, HOR)

$$H_{2(g)} \;\rightarrow\; 2H^{+}_{(aq)} \;+\; 2e^{-}$$

The electrons are forced through the **external circuit** (producing useful work) because the membrane is electronically insulating.

### At the Cathode (Oxygen Reduction Reaction, ORR)

$$\tfrac{1}{2}O_{2(g)} \;+\; 2H^{+}_{(aq)} \;+\; 2e^{-} \;\rightarrow\; H_{2}O_{(l)}$$

### Overall Cell Reaction

$$H_{2(g)} \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}O_{2(g)} \;\rightarrow\; H_{2}O_{(l)} \;+\; \text{Electrical Energy} \;+\; \text{Heat}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The only by-product is water** — which is why PEMFCs are termed *zero-emission* powertrains. In an automotive stack, this water must be actively drained to prevent cathode flooding.

## 2.3 Why Each Step Happens — The "Why" Behind the "How"

- **Why is Pt used as catalyst?**
  Pt has an optimal binding energy for both H (anode) and O (cathode), enabling fast kinetics at low temperatures (< 100 °C). No other affordable metal matches this balance.

- **Why is the membrane acidic?**
  The mobile ion is H⁺. The fixed SO₃⁻ groups on the Nafion backbone attract water clusters, forming proton-conducting channels — analogous to a *hydrated highway* for H⁺.

- **Why are GDLs hydrophobic-treated?**
  They use PTFE (Teflon) coating to repel liquid water, pushing it out of the pores so that O₂ gas can still reach the catalyst sites.

- **Why is pressurization beneficial?**
  Higher pressure increases gas solubility at the catalyst surface, raising the **Nernst voltage** and improving the **mass-transport limited current density**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta G = -nFE$ | Gibbs free energy ↔ Cell potential | J/mol, V |
| 2 | $E_{thermo} = -\dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF}$ | Thermodynamic reversible cell voltage | V |
| 3 | $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ | **Nernst Equation** (general) | V |
| 4 | $E = 1.229 - 0.000845\,(T - 298.15) + \dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left[\,P_{H_2}\,\cdot\,P_{O_2}^{\,1/2}\,\right]$ | PEMFC-specific Nernst | V |
| 5 | $\Delta H = \Delta G + T\Delta S$ | Enthalpy split (Heat vs Work) | J/mol |
| 6 | $\eta_{thermo} = \dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{\Delta H^\circ} = \dfrac{237.2}{285.8} = 0.83$ | Thermodynamic (LHV-based) efficiency | — |
| 7 | $\eta_{voltage} = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{1.229}$ | Voltage efficiency of operating cell | — |
| 8 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{thermo} \times \eta_{voltage} \times \eta_{faradaic}$ | Stack efficiency | — |
| 9 | $P_{stack} = N_{cells} \times V_{cell} \times I$ | Stack power | W |
| 10 | $I_{lim} = \dfrac{nFD_{O_2}\,C_{O_2}}{\delta}$ | Mass-transport limiting current (Fick's 1st law) | A |
| 11 | $V_{stack} = N_{cells} \times V_{cell}$ | Stack voltage | V |
| 12 | $P_{density} = \dfrac{V_{cell} \times j}{A_{active}}$ | Volumetric/gravimetric power density | W/cm² |

**Standard Constants to memorize (board exam favorites):**

$$F = 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}, \quad R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}, \quad T_{std} = 298.15\ \text{K}$$

$$\Delta G^\circ_{H_2O(l)} = -237.2\ \text{kJ/mol}, \quad \Delta H^\circ_{H_2O(l)} = -285.8\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Formula 4 is the basis of every PEMFC **polarization curve** used in automotive BMS/fuel cell control units. Formula 12 dictates *packaging* — i.e., how compactly a 100 kW stack can be fitted into a passenger car chassis (e.g., Toyota Mirai stack: ~114 kW in 37 L volume).

## 2.5 Operating Pressure & Temperature — The KTU "Sweet Spot"

- **Low Temperature (60–100 °C):** Platinum catalysts are active; quick start-up (< 30 s cold start achievable with proper MEA design).
- **Pressure 1–3 bar:** Mild pressurization (using a small compressor) raises O₂ partial pressure at cathode, improving both Nernst voltage and current density.
- **Humidity 50 – 100 % RH:** Membrane ionic conductivity scales linearly with water content.

> Trade-off: Higher temperature speeds up ORR kinetics (good) but accelerates membrane dehydration and CO poisoning (bad). This is why automotive systems cap at ~ 80 °C for *steady-state* operation.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Reversible Cell Voltage of a H₂/O₂ Cell

**Goal:** Show that the standard reversible potential of a PEMFC equals **1.229 V**.

**Step 1 — Write the overall cell reaction:**

$$H_{2(g)} + \tfrac{1}{2}O_{2(g)} \;\longrightarrow\; H_{2}O_{(l)}$$

**Step 2 — Identify n (number of electrons transferred per mole of H₂).**
Hydrogen is oxidized from 0 to +1 state, and oxygen is reduced from 0 to –2 state. Therefore:

$$n = 2\ \text{electrons per mole of } H_2$$

**Step 3 — Use the standard Gibbs free energy of formation of liquid water:**

$$\Delta G^\circ = -237.2\ \text{kJ/mol} = -237{,}200\ \text{J/mol}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the fundamental electrochemical relation:**

$$\Delta G = -nFE$$

**Step 5 — Solve for E° (rearranging the equation):**

$$E^\circ = -\dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$E^\circ = -\dfrac{(-237{,}200)}{(2)(96{,}485)}$$

$$E^\circ = \dfrac{237{,}200}{192{,}970}$$

$$\boxed{E^\circ_{cell} = 1.229\ \text{V}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result interpretation:** Each cell in a PEMFC stack can, at most, deliver 1.229 V when supplied with pure H₂ and O₂ at standard conditions, drawing negligible current. A 100 kW stack at 0.7 V/cell therefore needs ≈ 143 kW of chemical input — explaining the dominance of *cell-count* in stack design.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Thermodynamic Efficiency (LHV basis)

**Step 1 — Recall the standard enthalpy of combustion of H₂:**

$$\Delta H^\circ_{H_2} = -285.8\ \text{kJ/mol (LHV, product = liquid water)}$$

**Step 2 — Define the thermodynamic (ideal) efficiency:**

$$\eta_{thermo} = \dfrac{\text{Useful electrical work}}{\text{Chemical energy in fuel}} = \dfrac{|\Delta G^\circ|}{|\Delta H^\circ|}$$

**Step 3 — Plug in the values:**

$$\eta_{thermo} = \dfrac{237.2}{285.8}$$

**Step 4 — Compute:**

$$\boxed{\eta_{thermo} = 0.8296 \;\approx\; 83\,\%}$$

**Step 5 — Account for the lost fraction (TΔS term — heat):**

$$\dfrac{T\Delta S}{\Delta H} = 1 - \eta_{thermo} \approx 17\,\%$$

This 17 % is *unavoidable entropy-generated heat* — it sets the theoretical upper bound for a fuel cell (an internal combustion engine tops out near 50 % Carnot efficiency for the same temperature limit).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Nernst Equation for a PEMFC under Non-Standard Conditions

**Step 1 — Start from the general Nernst expression:**

$$E = E^\circ - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$

**Step 2 — For the cell reaction H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O, the reaction quotient is:**

$$Q = \dfrac{a_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2}\,\cdot\,P_{O_2}^{\,1/2}}$$

**Step 3 — For liquid water, $a_{H_2O} = 1$. Therefore:**

$$E = E^\circ + \dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left[\,P_{H_2}\,\cdot\,P_{O_2}^{\,1/2}\,\right]$$

**Step 4 — Combine with the empirical temperature-correction term for PEMFC (commonly quoted in literature):**

$$E = 1.229 - 0.000845\,(T - 298.15) + \dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left[\,P_{H_2}\,P_{O_2}^{\,1/2}\,\right]$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example (commonly tested in KTU):**
Take $T = 353\ \text{K} (80\,°C)$, $P_{H_2} = 1.5\ \text{atm}$, $P_{O_2} = 0.21 \times 3 = 0.63\ \text{atm}$ (pressurized air at 3 bar).

- Temperature correction:

$$-0.000845 \times (353 - 298.15) = -0.0464\ \text{V}$$

- Logarithmic correction:

$$\dfrac{RT}{2F} = \dfrac{(8.314)(353)}{(2)(96{,}485)} = 0.01521\ \text{V}$$

- Argument of ln:

$$P_{H_2}\,P_{O_2}^{\,1/2} = 1.5 \times (0.63)^{0.5} = 1.5 \times 0.7937 = 1.1906$$

$$\ln(1.1906) = 0.1745$$

- Log correction term:

$$0.01521 \times 0.1745 = 0.00265\ \text{V}$$

- Final Nernst voltage:

$$E = 1.229 - 0.0464 + 0.00265 = 1.185\ \text{V}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:** Even before current draw, the cell sits at **1.185 V** (not 1.229 V) due to elevated temperature and gas composition. As current increases, activation, ohmic, and concentration losses further drop operating voltage to ~0.65 V under full load.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Loss Mechanisms in a PEMFC (Polarization Curve Decomposition)

| Loss Region | Dominant Cause | Mathematical Form | Mitigation Strategy in Vehicles |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Activation Loss (low current)** | Slow ORR kinetics at cathode | $\Delta V_{act} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{j}{j_0}\right)$ | Higher Pt loading, Pt-alloy catalysts, Pt-M (M=Co, Ni) |
| **Ohmic Loss (mid current)** | Membrane ionic resistance + contact resistance | $\Delta V_{ohmic} = jR_{ASR}$ | Thinner membranes (Nafion 211), better BPP coatings |
| **Concentration Loss (high current)** | Mass transport limit of O₂ to cathode | $\Delta V_{conc} = -\dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(1 - \dfrac{j}{j_L}\right)$ | Higher back-pressure, optimized GDL porosity |
| **Crossover Loss** | H₂ diffusing through membrane | Appears as a *mixed-potential* drop at OCV | Thinner yet denser membranes, reinforced PFSA |
| **Thermal Loss** | Heat from TΔS + irreversibilities | $Q = I\,(E_{thermo} - V_{cell})$ | Active coolant loop in stack (Toyota Mirai uses dedicated radiator) |

**Total cell voltage (operating point):**

$$V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$$

This is the **canonical polarization equation** for a PEMFC and forms the spine of almost every KTU numerical on this topic.

## 3.5 Worked Example — Stack Sizing for a Compact Car

**Problem (KTU-style):** A FCEV requires **100 kW** net output. Each cell operates at **0.7 V** under load and the stack current is **300 A**. How many cells are needed? What is the stack voltage?

**Solution:**

$$V_{stack} = \dfrac{P}{I} = \dfrac{100{,}000}{300} = 333.3\ \text{V}$$

$$N_{cells} = \dfrac{V_{stack}}{V_{cell}} = \dfrac{333.3}{0.7} \approx 476\ \text{cells}$$

**Interpretation:** A 100 kW automotive stack needs ≈ 480 cells. The Toyota Mirai 2nd-gen stack uses 330 cells with higher current density — a useful comparison point for an exam answer.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Single PEMFC Cell Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hydrogen Tank - H2 at 1 to 3 bar] -->|Humidified H2| B(Anode GDL)
    B --> C(Anode Catalyst Layer - Pt)
    C -->|H splits to 2H plus 2e| D(Proton Exchange Membrane - Nafion)
    D -->|H plus ions migrate| E(Cathode Catalyst Layer - Pt)
    E --> F(Cathode GDL)
    F -->|Pressurized Air 0.21 O2| G[Ambient Air Inlet via Compressor]
    E -.->|ORR produces H2O| H[Product Water Drain]
    C -->|Electrons blocked by membrane| I(External Circuit - Electric Load)
    I -->|e returns to cathode| E
    J[Coolant Loop at 80 C] -.->|Thermal management| C
    J -.->|Thermal management| E
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows = species flow; dashed arrows = thermal coupling. The membrane permits **only H⁺** to pass, forcing electrons around the external circuit where they do useful work in the traction motor.

## 4.2 PEMFC Stack Assembly — Modular Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph stack1 [Cell 1]
        A1[Bipolar Plate] --> B1[Anode GDL]
        B1 --> C1[Anode Catalyst]
        C1 --> D1[MEA Core]
        D1 --> E1[Cathode Catalyst]
        E1 --> F1[Cathode GDL]
        F1 --> G1[Bipolar Plate]
    end
    subgraph stack2 [Cell 2]
        A2[Bipolar Plate] --> B2[Anode GDL]
        B2 --> C2[Anode Catalyst]
        C2 --> D2[MEA Core]
        D2 --> E2[Cathode Catalyst]
        E2 --> F2[Cathode GDL]
        F2 --> G2[Bipolar Plate]
    end
    subgraph stackN [Cell N - 100s of cells]
        AN[Bipolar Plate] --> BN[Anode GDL]
        BN --> CN[Anode Catalyst]
        CN --> DN[MEA Core]
        DN --> EN[Cathode Catalyst]
        EN --> FN[Cathode GDL]
        FN --> GN[Bipolar Plate]
    end
    stack1 -->|Series electrical connection| stack2
    stack2 -->|Series electrical connection| stackN
    H[H2 Manifold] -.->|Parallel gas feed| B1
    H -.-> B2
    H -.-> BN
    K[Air Manifold] -.->|Parallel gas feed| F1
    K -.-> F2
    K -.-> FN
    stackN --> L[DC Bus to Vehicle Inverter]
```

> This **series-electrical / parallel-fluid** topology is the universal architecture of every FCEV stack: voltage scales with cell count, current scales with active area.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Vehicle-Integrated PEMFC System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[High Pressure H2 Tank - 70 MPa] -->|Regulator| P2[Pressure Reducer to 1 to 3 bar]
    P2 --> P3[Humidifier]
    P3 --> P4[PEMFC Stack]
    A1[Ambient Air] --> A2[Air Compressor]
    A2 --> A3[Air Filter and Humidifier]
    A3 --> P4
    P4 -->|H2O liquid| W1[Water Separator]
    W1 -->|Recycled| P3
    W1 -->|Excess| W2[Drain]
    P4 -->|DC 300 to 650 V| INV[DC to DC Converter and Inverter]
    INV --> M[Traction Motor]
    M --> WHL[Drive Wheels]
    P4 -->|Heat| CL[Coolant Radiator Loop]
    CL --> P4
    BAT[Li ion Battery Pack] <-->|Bidirectional buffer| INV
    ECU[Vehicle Control Unit] -.->|Manages power split| INV
    ECU -.->|Manages H2 flow| P2
    ECU -.->|Manages air flow| A2
    ECU -.->|Thermal setpoint 80 C| CL
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a battery buffer?** The fuel cell cannot respond instantly to acceleration transients. The Li-ion pack supplies peak power; the stack handles steady cruise — the same strategy used in *Toyota Mirai*, *Hyundai Nexo*, and *Honda Clarity*.

## 4.4 Polarization Curve — Visual Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Open Circuit Voltage 1.23 V] -->|Activation losses dominate low j| B[Knee Region around 0.85 V]
    B -->|Ohmic losses linear region| C[Mid Current Region 0.6 to 0.75 V]
    C -->|Concentration losses rise sharply| D[Limiting Current where jL reached]
    style A fill:#90ee90
    style B fill:#ffe680
    style C fill:#87cefa
    style D fill:#ff7f7f
```

> Three losses stack additively, producing the characteristic **S-shaped polarization curve**. The **linear ohmic region** is the design sweet spot for FCEVs — most operation lies in the 0.6 – 0.75 V/cell band.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC). List any four advantages that make it suitable for automotive applications.**

**Model Answer (Key Points):**
A PEMFC is an electrochemical device that converts the chemical energy of hydrogen and oxygen directly into electrical energy using a **solid polymer membrane (e.g., Nafion)** as the electrolyte, with platinum-based catalysts, operating at **60 – 100 °C**.

**Four advantages for automotive use:**
1. **Low operating temperature** → quick cold start (suitable for passenger cars).
2. **High power density (0.5–1.0 W/cm²)** → compact stack packaging.
3. **Solid electrolyte** → no leakage, robust to vibration.
4. **Zero tail-pipe emissions** — only water is exhausted.
5. (Bonus) Modular — voltage scales by adding cells.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sample Paper]**
**Write the half-cell and overall reactions of a PEMFC. State the standard reversible cell voltage.**

**Model Answer (Key Points):**

- Anode (HOR): $H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$
- Cathode (ORR): $\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O$
- Overall: $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O_{(l)}$
- **Standard reversible cell voltage = 1.229 V** (derived from $\Delta G^\circ = -237.2\ \text{kJ/mol}$ with $n=2$).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat schematic, explain the construction and working of a Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell. State the materials used for membrane and catalyst.

**Model Solution Structure:**

1. **Construction (3 marks):**
   - Draw a labelled schematic showing: bipolar plates (anode + cathode), anode GDL, anode catalyst layer, PEM, cathode catalyst layer, cathode GDL.
   - H₂ inlet on anode side, air inlet on cathode side, external circuit for current, water exhaust.

2. **Materials used (2 marks):**
   - **Membrane:** Perfluorinated sulfonic acid polymer, e.g., **Nafion™ (DuPont)** — thickness 25 – 175 µm.
   - **Catalyst:** Platinum nanoparticles (2 – 4 nm) dispersed on high-surface-area carbon black (e.g., Vulcan XC-72).
   - **GDL:** Carbon paper/cloth, often PTFE-treated for hydrophobicity.
   - **Bipolar plates:** Graphite (early designs) or metal (Ti, SS) with protective coatings.

3. **Working (2 marks):**
   - At anode: H₂ dissociates on Pt, releasing H⁺ and e⁻.
   - H⁺ migrates through hydrated membrane; e⁻ flows via external load.
   - At cathode: O₂ + H⁺ + e⁻ → H₂O (l).
   - Continuously supplied gases → continuous DC current.

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the Nernst equation for a PEMFC. Compute the cell voltage at 80 °C when the anode is supplied with H₂ at 1.5 atm and the cathode with air at 3 bar (21 % O₂).

**Model Solution (full derivation as in §3.3):**

Using $E = 1.229 - 0.000845\,(T - 298.15) + \dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln(P_{H_2} P_{O_2}^{1/2})$:

- $T = 353$ K, $P_{H_2} = 1.5$ atm, $P_{O_2} = 0.21 \times 3 = 0.63$ atm.
- Temperature correction: $-0.000845 \times 54.85 = -0.0464$ V.
- $\dfrac{RT}{2F} = 0.01521$ V.
- Argument: $1.5 \times 0.7937 = 1.1906$; $\ln(1.1906) = 0.1745$.
- Log term: $0.01521 \times 0.1745 = 0.00265$ V.
- **E = 1.229 − 0.0464 + 0.00265 = 1.185 V.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare PEMFC with other major types of fuel cells (AFC, PAFC, MCFC, SOFC) under the heads: electrolyte, operating temperature, mobile ion, and typical application.

**Model Solution Table (must be drawn in the answer sheet):**

| Type | Electrolyte | Operating Temp | Mobile Ion | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PEMFC** | Polymer membrane (Nafion) | 60 – 100 °C | H⁺ | **Automotive (FCEV)** |
| AFC | KOH (liquid) | 60 – 90 °C | OH⁻ | Space, defence |
| PAFC | H₃PO₄ (liquid) | 150 – 220 °C | H⁺ | Stationary CHP |
| MCFC | Molten carbonate (Li/K) | 600 – 700 °C | CO₃²⁻ | Large stationary power |
| SOFC | YSZ (solid ceramic) | 600 – 1000 °C | O²⁻ | Stationary, APU |

**Justification (1 mark):** PEMFC uniquely combines low temperature + solid electrolyte + high power density — making it the only practical choice for road vehicles.

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the three major loss mechanisms in a PEMFC using the polarization curve. Why does the cell voltage drop from 1.229 V (theoretical) to about 0.65 V in practice?

**Model Solution Outline:**

1. **Activation loss** (low current region): Stemming from sluggish ORR kinetics at cathode. Voltage drop: $\eta_{act} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F}\ln(j/j_0)$. Largest contributor in the 0 – 0.1 A/cm² region.

2. **Ohmic loss** (linear region): From ionic resistance of membrane + electronic resistance of plates and contacts. $\eta_{ohmic} = j \times R_{ASR}$. Linear with current.

3. **Concentration loss** (high current region): Mass-transport limitation of O₂ to the cathode catalyst. $\eta_{conc} = -\dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln(1 - j/j_L)$. Causes the steep "tail" of the polarization curve.

4. **Total operating voltage:**

$$V_{cell} = 1.229 - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$$

5. **Why drop to 0.65 V?** All three losses compound. At a typical automotive current density (~ 1 A/cm²) the individual drops are roughly 0.4 V (activation) + 0.1 V (ohmic) + 0.05 V (concentration) = ~0.55 V, leaving ~0.65 V from the 1.229 V starting point.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students lose marks in PEMFC questions:**
> 1. **Mixing up HOR and ORR directions** — always write the anode reaction as oxidation (loss of e⁻).
> 2. **Forgetting that water is the cathode product, not the anode product** — in a PEMFC (acidic membrane), H⁺ moves from anode to cathode, hence H₂O forms at the **cathode**.
> 3. **Using ΔH instead of ΔG in the efficiency formula** — efficiency is $|\Delta G|/|\Delta H|$, NOT 1. The Carnot-like upper limit is 83 % (LHV), not 100 %.
> 4. **Ignoring the "n = 2" factor in the Nernst equation** — for H₂/O₂ cells, always use n = 2.
> 5. **Confusing PEMFC with SOFC** — examiner often asks "which fuel cell uses a *solid* electrolyte?" Both do, but **PEMFC uses a polymer**, SOFC uses a *ceramic*. Conflating these is a guaranteed half-mark loss.
> 6. **Not stating operating conditions** — always mention **T = 60–100 °C** and **n = 2** explicitly when writing equations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **PEMFC = Polymer Electrolyte Membrane Fuel Cell**, also called Proton Exchange Membrane FC.
- [x] Operating temperature: **60 – 100 °C** (low-temp FC).
- [x] Electrolyte: **Perfluorinated sulfonic acid polymer (Nafion)**.
- [x] Mobile ion: **H⁺ (proton)** — moves from **anode to cathode** through the membrane.
- [x] Catalyst: **Platinum (Pt)** on both anode and cathode.
- [x] Anode reaction: $H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$ (HOR).
- [x] Cathode reaction: $\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O$ (ORR).
- [x] Overall: $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$ + Electricity + Heat.
- [x] Standard reversible voltage: **1.229 V** per cell.
- [x] Practical operating voltage: **0.6 – 0.75 V** per cell.
- [x] Thermodynamic efficiency: **83 %** (LHV basis) — 17 % is unavoidable TΔS loss.
- [x] Three loss mechanisms: **Activation + Ohmic + Concentration** = $V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$.
- [x] Membrane must stay **hydrated** — dehydration = loss of proton conductivity.
- [x] Cathode is the **rate-limiting electrode** (ORR is ~ 6× slower than HOR).
- [x] **Automotive relevance:** PEMFC is the *only* fuel cell type used in current FCEVs (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo, Honda Clarity).
- [x] Nernst equation for PEMFC: $E = 1.229 - 0.000845\,(T-298.15) + \dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln(P_{H_2} P_{O_2}^{1/2})$.
- [x] CO poisoning is a key challenge — even **10 ppm CO** can degrade Pt performance; requires air bleed or Pt-Ru anode catalyst.
- [x] Bipolar plates must conduct current AND distribute gases — typically graphite or coated metal.
- [x] MEA = Anode catalyst + PEM + Cathode catalyst (the *active* heart of the cell).
- [x] GDL = hydrophobic-treated porous carbon — balances water removal and gas diffusion.
- [x] Stack architecture: **Series electrical, parallel fluid** — voltage scales with cell count, current with active area.
- [x] Typical automotive stack: 300 – 500 cells, ~ 100 kW, DC bus 300 – 650 V.
- [x] Battery hybrid is mandatory — fuel cell cannot follow transient load alone.
- [x] **KTU one-liner:** *"PEMFC is the automotive fuel cell because it is the only low-temperature FC with a solid, leak-free electrolyte and high power density."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Alkaline Fuel Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET414 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Alkaline Fuel Cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Alkaline Fuel Cell (AFC)** is a low-temperature electrochemical energy conversion device that generates electricity by directly combining hydrogen fuel and oxygen oxidant across a **liquid alkaline electrolyte** (typically concentrated aqueous **Potassium Hydroxide, KOH**) separated by a porous matrix or membrane. It belongs to the family of Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) family predecessors, having been the **first fuel cell technology practically deployed** (NASA Apollo missions, 1960s).

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context for Modern Automotive Technologies, AFCs are studied as the **baseline/reference hydrogen fuel cell technology** from which modern automotive PEMFCs and SOFCs evolved. They are characterized by:

- **Electrolyte:** Aqueous KOH (concentrations of **30–45 wt%** historically; modern designs use **6–8 mol/L** circulating loops).
- **Operating Temperature Range:** **60 °C – 90 °C** (modern designs) up to **~250 °C** (legacy Apollo-era Bacon cell).
- **Charge Carrier in Electrolyte:** Hydroxyl ion (**OH⁻**), which migrates from cathode to anode.
- **Primary Automotive Relevance:** Benchmark for understanding fuel cell stack architecture, water management, and bipolar plate design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** AFCs are *not* the primary choice for current road-going electric vehicles (which use PEMFCs). However, AFCs remain the **reference case** for understanding the chemistry, water balance, and thermal management of any automotive fuel cell powertrain.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Two-City Trade"

Imagine a city split into two districts (the anode and the cathode) separated by a **river of alkaline water (the KOH electrolyte)**. People in the **Anode District** (hydrogen town) need to send messages (electrons) to the **Cathode District** (oxygen town).

- The messengers (**electrons, e⁻**) cannot swim across the river directly, so they are forced to take a long detour through an **external electric wire** (the load) — this detour powers streetlights and factories along the way.
- Meanwhile, **ferry boats carrying hydroxide ions (OH⁻)** constantly cross the river from cathode to anode, completing the circuit internally.
- The trip ends with the formation of pure **water (H₂O)** at the anode side, which must be removed so the ferry boats do not get bogged down.

This is exactly how an AFC works: hydrogen gives up electrons at the anode, those electrons do useful electrical work through the load, and the OH⁻ ions ferry the charge through the electrolyte internally.

### 1.3 Key Physical & Engineering Constants

| Parameter | Value | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| Standard Cell Potential ($E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$) | **1.229 V** | Thermodynamic ceiling per cell |
| Practical Operating Voltage | **0.7 V – 0.85 V** | Real loading (losses reduce this) |
| Practical Current Density | **100 – 400 mA/cm²** | At nominal load |
| Stack Power Density | **0.5 – 1.0 kW/kg** | Mass-to-power benchmark |
| Faraday Constant ($F$) | **96,485 C/mol** | Charge per mole of electrons |
| Universal Gas Constant ($R$) | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | Used in Nernst equation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Viva:** The Nernst theoretical voltage of **1.229 V** is the *same* for all hydrogen–oxygen fuel cells regardless of electrolyte. The electrolyte only changes the **reaction pathway**, not the underlying thermodynamic limit.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The AFC operates on the principle of **reverse electrolysis of water**. The hydrogen–oxygen reaction is split into two spatially separated half-reactions across the alkaline electrolyte:

**Step 1 — Fuel Delivery (Anode Side):**
- Gaseous H₂ is humidified and fed into the anode flow channels.
- H₂ diffuses through a porous **nickel-based anode** (typically Ni or Ni–C with Pt catalyst historically, modern designs use Ag/Ni or Raney Ni).
- A catalyst on the anode surface dissociates H₂ into H atoms: $\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}$.

**Step 2 — Anode Electrochemical Reaction:**
- Each H atom donates an electron to the anode: $\text{H} \rightarrow \text{H}^+ + e^-$.
- However, in *alkaline* media there are no free H⁺ protons. Instead, the H reacts with **OH⁻ ions** (supplied from the electrolyte) to form water:
$$2\text{H}_2 + 4\text{OH}^- \rightarrow 4\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^-$$

**Step 3 — External Electron Flow:**
- The released **electrons (e⁻) cannot pass through the electrolyte**.
- They are forced to travel through the **external circuit (the automotive traction motor, battery, accessories)**, doing electrical work.
- This is the **useful electrical output** of the fuel cell vehicle.

**Step 4 — Cathode Electrochemical Reaction:**
- O₂ is fed into the cathode side, diffusing through a porous **silver or platinum-doped nickel cathode**.
- O₂ combines with the **electrons returning from the load** and with **water from the electrolyte** to regenerate OH⁻ ions:
$$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$$
- The newly formed OH⁻ ions migrate back to the anode through the electrolyte, closing the internal ionic loop.

**Step 5 — Product Water Management:**
- Net water is produced **at the anode** side.
- In an automotive AFC stack, this water must be **continuously removed** (via circulating electrolyte loop) to prevent **flooding of the anode catalyst layer**, which would block H₂ access.
- The recovered water is highly pure and can be used for vehicle humidity control or drinking water (Apollo program used this feature).

**Step 6 — Overall Net Reaction:**
$$2\text{H}_2(g) + \text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}$$

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why an alkaline electrolyte?** It enables the use of **non-precious metal catalysts** (Ni, Ag) instead of platinum, drastically reducing automotive stack cost.
- **Why does OH⁻ move from cathode to anode?** In an electrolytic solution, anions (negative ions) move toward the **anode** in a galvanic cell, completing the internal circuit.
- **Why is water produced at the anode?** Mass balance of the half-reactions shows 4 H₂O produced at anode and 2 H₂O consumed at cathode, giving a net of 2 H₂O on the anode side.
- **Why remove water?** Accumulated water dilutes KOH, lowering ionic conductivity, and floods the porous gas diffusion electrodes (GDEs), starving the catalyst of reactant gas.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Equation | Application in AFC |
|---|---|---|
| Nernst Equation (General) | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ | Determines actual cell voltage under non-standard T, P |
| Nernst for H₂/O₂ | $E = 1.229 - \dfrac{0.000198(T-298.15)}{2} + \dfrac{RT}{4F}\ln\left[P_{\text{H}_2}^2 \cdot P_{\text{O}_2}\right]$ | Open-circuit voltage at given T and partial pressures |
| Gibbs Free Energy | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$ | Links thermodynamics to electrical work |
| Enthalpy of Reaction | $\Delta H^{\circ} = -285.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$ (HHV) | For thermodynamic (Carnot-limited) efficiency |
| Thermodynamic Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{\Delta H^{\circ}} = \dfrac{237.2}{285.8} \approx 83\%$ | Upper limit if all $\Delta G$ is converted |
| Voltage Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{v}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{operating}}}{E^{\circ}}$ | Ratio of real to ideal voltage |
| Current Efficiency (Faradaic) | $\eta_{\text{F}} = \dfrac{I_{\text{actual}}}{I_{\text{theoretical}}}$ | Accounts for parasitic losses/crossover |
| Total Cell Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{cell}} = \eta_{\text{th}} \times \eta_{\text{v}} \times \eta_{\text{F}}$ | Overall stack efficiency for automotive rating |
| Electrical Efficiency Range (AFC) | **50 % – 60 %** | Practical HHV-based electrical efficiency |
| Combined Heat & Power (CHP) | **up to 85 %** | With thermal recovery for cabin heating |
| Power Output | $P = n \cdot V_{\text{cell}} \cdot I$ | Stack rating for EV traction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on `|x|` Notation:** In all KTU formula tables, the symbol `|x|` is rendered as `\vert x \vert` (e.g., $\vert E^{\circ} \vert$) to avoid breaking the markdown table pipe syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Automotive Utility

Although pure AFCs are not the standard for road-going fuel cell electric vehicles (FCEVs) like the Toyota Mirai (which uses a **PEMFC**), AFC technology still influences modern automotive engineering in:

- **Stationary range-extender units** for heavy-duty trucks (where higher operating temperature and cheaper catalysts offset CO₂-sensitivity).
- **Auxiliary Power Units (APUs)** for military and off-road vehicles (originally developed for U.S. Army).
- **Reference design** for studying water and heat management in any hydrogen fuel cell stack.
- **Cost-down studies** — AFCs use **no platinum** in modern alkaline membrane variants (AEMFCs), which are an active automotive R&D direction.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Context:** Module 4 typically covers *electric and hybrid powertrains including fuel cell technology*. AFCs are the **introductory fuel cell** taught before PEMFC and SOFC.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Nernst Equation Applied to AFC Operating Voltage

We start from the fundamental thermodynamic relation linking Gibbs free energy to cell potential:

$$\Delta G = -nFE_{\text{cell}}$$

For a non-standard state (real automotive conditions: temperature $T$, partial pressures $P_{\text{H}_2}$, $P_{\text{O}_2}$):

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

Equating and solving for $E_{\text{cell}}$:

$$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

**Applying to the AFC reaction:**
$2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$
$n = 4$ electrons transferred per mole of O₂.
$Q = \dfrac{a_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}^2}{(P_{\text{H}_2})^2 \cdot P_{\text{O}_2}}$, where $a_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \approx 1$ for pure liquid.

Substituting $n=4$:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{4F} \ln\left[\frac{1}{(P_{\text{H}_2})^2 \cdot P_{\text{O}_2}}\right]$$

$$E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{4F} \ln\left[(P_{\text{H}_2})^2 \cdot P_{\text{O}_2}\right]$$

**Numerical evaluation at $T = 353 \text{ K}$ (80 °C, a typical AFC operating point), with $P_{\text{H}_2} = 1.2 \text{ atm}$, $P_{\text{O}_2} = 1.2 \text{ atm}$:**

$$E = 1.229 + \frac{(8.314)(353)}{4(96485)} \ln\left[(1.2)^2 \cdot (1.2)\right]$$

$$E = 1.229 + \frac{2934.84}{385940} \ln(1.728)$$

$$E = 1.229 + (0.007603)(0.5471)$$

$$E = 1.229 + 0.00416 \approx 1.233 \text{ V}$$

This is the **open-circuit voltage (OCV)** at the given operating point. Under load, voltage drops due to activation, ohmic, and concentration overpotentials.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Theoretical and Practical Efficiency of an AFC

**Step 1 — Thermodynamic (Carnot-Limited) Efficiency:**

By definition, for the hydrogen oxidation reaction:
$$\eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{\Delta H^{\circ}}$$

**Step 2 — Numerical Substitution (Higher Heating Value basis):**

$$\eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{237.2 \text{ kJ/mol}}{285.8 \text{ kJ/mol}} = 0.8299 \approx 83.0\%$$

**Step 3 — Voltage Efficiency:**

A typical AFC operates at $V_{\text{op}} = 0.80 \text{ V}$:
$$\eta_{\text{v}} = \frac{0.80}{1.229} = 0.651 \approx 65.1\%$$

**Step 4 — Current (Faradaic) Efficiency:**

Assuming $\eta_{\text{F}} = 0.97$ (3% loss to side reactions, gas crossover):
$$\eta_{\text{F}} = 0.97$$

**Step 5 — Combined Cell Efficiency (HHV basis):**

$$\eta_{\text{cell}} = \eta_{\text{th}} \times \eta_{\text{v}} \times \eta_{\text{F}}$$

$$\eta_{\text{cell}} = 0.830 \times 0.651 \times 0.97$$

$$\eta_{\text{cell}} = 0.5241 \approx 52.4\%$$

This is consistent with the **50–60% practical electrical efficiency** of AFCs and explains why an AFC stack delivering 100 kW of electrical output also produces **~90 kW of waste heat** that must be rejected by the vehicle cooling system.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Electrode Materials, Conditions, and Polarization Losses

| Component | AFC Material Choice | Function in Stack | Polarization Loss Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Anode Catalyst | Porous Ni or Pt-doped Ni | Dissociates H₂, releases e⁻ | **Activation loss** ($\eta_{\text{act,a}}$) |
| Cathode Catalyst | Ag, Ag/Hg amalgam, or Pt-doped Ni | Reduces O₂ | **Activation loss** ($\eta_{\text{act,c}}$) — dominant |
| Electrolyte | 6–8 M aqueous KOH | Conducts OH⁻ ions | **Ohmic loss** ($\eta_{\text{ohm}}$) |
| Matrix / Separator | Asbestos (legacy) / PVA / ZrO₂ | Holds electrolyte, blocks gas crossover | Mixed |
| Bipolar Plates | Nickel-plated steel or graphite | Distribute gas, conduct current | Contact resistance |
| Operating Temp | 60 – 90 °C (modern) | Reaction kinetics | Reduces $\eta_{\text{act}}$ |
| Operating Pressure | 1 – 4 atm | Mass transport | Reduces $\eta_{\text{conc}}$ |
| Fuel Purity | H₂ < 50 ppm CO₂ | Prevents electrolyte carbonation | Critical lifetime factor |

The **total cell voltage** under load is:

$$V_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cell}} - \eta_{\text{act,a}} - \eta_{\text{act,c}} - \eta_{\text{ohm}} - \eta_{\text{conc}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** In AFCs, the **cathode activation loss** is the largest contributor (≈ 60–70% of total loss at low current densities) because O₂ reduction kinetics in alkaline media are still sluggish, although faster than in acid media.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 AFC Stack — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the functional flow of an automotive AFC system, from hydrogen storage to traction motor, including the auxiliary subsystems (BOP — Balance of Plant) needed for real vehicle operation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H2tank[H2 Storage Tank 350 bar] --> H2reg[H2 Pressure Regulator]
    H2reg --> H2humid[H2 Humidifier]
    H2humid --> anodeFC[AFC Anode Porous Ni]
    O2tank[O2 Supply or Air Blower] --> O2filter[O2 CO2 Scrubber]
    O2filter --> cathodeFC[AFC Cathode Ag or Pt-Ni]
    KOHloop[KOH Electrolyte Circulating Loop 6-8 M] --> anodeFC
    KOHloop --> cathodeFC
    anodeFC --> elecOut[Electron Flow External Circuit]
    elecOut --> dcdc[DC-DC Converter]
    dcdc --> traction[EV Traction Motor]
    elecOut --> aux12V[12V Auxiliary Bus]
    cathodeFC --> ohms[OH- Ion Migration to Anode]
    anodeFC --> waterOut[Anode Product Water Outlet]
    waterOut --> watertank[Vehicle Water Tank]
    waterOut --> radiator[Coolant Radiator]
    radiator --> heatloop[Thermal Management Loop]
    KOHloop --> heatloop
    traction --> wheels[Drive Wheels]
```

### 4.2 Half-Cell Reaction Flow Diagram

This second diagram focuses on the electrochemical half-reactions at each electrode, useful for board-exam visual answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Anode_Block["ANODE SIDE  H2 Oxidation"]
        H2in[H2 Gas In] --> H2ads[H2 Adsorbed on Ni Catalyst]
        H2ads --> H2split[2H  Dissociation]
        H2split --> react1[2H + 2OH- yields 2H2O + 2e-]
        react1 --> eout[e- released to external circuit]
        react1 --> h2oprod[2H2O produced at anode]
    end

    subgraph Cathode_Block["CATHODE SIDE  O2 Reduction"]
        O2in[O2 Gas In] --> O2ads[O2 Adsorbed on Ag Catalyst]
        O2ads --> react2[O2 + 2H2O + 4e- yields 4OH-]
        react2 --> ohmig[4OH- migrate to anode through KOH]
        react2 --> ein[e- return from external load]
    end

    ohmig --> react1
    ein --> react2
    eout --> extload[External Load  EV Motor and Auxiliaries]
    extload --> ein
```

### 4.3 AFC Stack — Bipolar Plate Cross-Section (Functional Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph cell1["Single AFC Cell Architecture"]
        BP1[Bipolar Plate  Anode Side] --> ch1[H2 Flow Channel]
        ch1 --> GDEA[Gas Diffusion Electrode Anode Ni]
        GDEA --> catA[Catalyst Layer Anode]
        catA --> mat[KOH-soaked Matrix]
        mat --> catC[Catalyst Layer Cathode Ag]
        catC --> GDEC[Gas Diffusion Electrode Cathode]
        GDEC --> ch2[O2 Flow Channel]
        ch2 --> BP2[Bipolar Plate  Cathode Side]
    end
```

### 4.4 Polarization Curve — Conceptual Block

The voltage-current behavior of an AFC stack can be summarized as three loss regions:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[OCV Region  E approx 1.23 V  No Load] --> B[Activation Loss Region  Steep drop at low current]
    B --> C[Ohmic Loss Region  Linear drop with current]
    C --> D[Concentration Loss Region  Sharp drop at high current]
    D --> E[Limiting Current Density  Fuel starvation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip:** At open circuit, voltage is highest (1.23 V). As current draw increases (vehicle accelerates), voltage falls progressively. The **operating point** for a fuel cell vehicle is typically chosen in the **ohmic-loss region** to balance power output and efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*State the half-cell and overall reactions occurring in an Alkaline Fuel Cell. Why is the electrolyte restricted to a narrow KOH concentration range?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Anode (oxidation):** $2\text{H}_2 + 4\text{OH}^- \rightarrow 4\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^-$
- **Cathode (reduction):** $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$
- **Overall reaction:** $2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$

The KOH concentration is restricted (typically **6–8 mol/L**) because:
1. Too low a concentration → reduced ionic conductivity and lower cell voltage.
2. Too high a concentration → water activity drops, slowing the cathode reaction, and the electrolyte may crystallize at low temperatures.
3. Carbonate formation from any CO₂ ingress is more severe at high KOH.

*(1 mark for each reaction, 1 mark for concentration reasoning — split appropriately.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List any three advantages and three disadvantages of Alkaline Fuel Cells in automotive applications.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Advantages:**
1. **High electrical efficiency** (50–60%, up to 85% with CHP), higher than PEMFC at low loads.
2. **Use of non-precious metal catalysts** (Ni, Ag) — significantly lower stack cost than PEMFC.
3. **Fast oxygen reduction kinetics** in alkaline media, giving higher current densities.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **CO₂ sensitivity** — atmospheric CO₂ reacts with KOH to form K₂CO₃, poisoning the electrolyte.
2. **Water management complexity** — water produced at anode must be continuously removed.
3. **Electrolyte leakage / corrosion** — liquid KOH is corrosive to vehicle components and must be carefully sealed.

*(1 mark per correctly classified advantage/disadvantage.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]*

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat schematic, explain the construction and working principle of an Alkaline Fuel Cell. Clearly identify the electrolyte, electrodes, fuel, oxidant, and the direction of ion and electron flow.

**(b) [7 marks]** An AFC operates at 80 °C with hydrogen and oxygen partial pressures of 1.2 atm each. Calculate the open-circuit voltage using the Nernst equation. Given that the operating voltage at rated load is 0.78 V, determine the voltage efficiency and overall cell efficiency (assume Faradaic efficiency = 97%, use HHV basis).

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- The AFC consists of two porous electrodes (**anode: Ni-based**; **cathode: Ag or Pt-doped Ni**) separated by a **matrix saturated with aqueous KOH electrolyte (6–8 M)**.
- **Bipolar plates** (Ni-plated steel) separate adjacent cells in a stack, distributing H₂ (anode side) and O₂ (cathode side) via flow channels.
- External manifolds supply humidified H₂ and O₂ (or scrubbed air).

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
- At the **anode**, hydrogen is oxidized: $2\text{H}_2 + 4\text{OH}^- \rightarrow 4\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^-$.
- The released **electrons flow through the external circuit** (vehicle traction motor, battery, accessories) providing electrical power.
- At the **cathode**, oxygen is reduced: $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$.
- The **OH⁻ ions migrate through the KOH electrolyte from cathode to anode**, completing the internal ionic circuit.
- Net product: pure water, generated at the anode.

**Direction of Flow (1 mark):**
- Electrons: anode → external circuit → cathode.
- OH⁻ ions: cathode → anode (inside electrolyte).
- Water and heat: removed via circulating electrolyte loop and radiator.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Nernst Equation Setup (2 marks):**

$$E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{4F} \ln\left[(P_{\text{H}_2})^2 \cdot P_{\text{O}_2}\right]$$

Given: $E^{\circ} = 1.229 \text{ V}$, $T = 80 + 273.15 = 353.15 \text{ K}$, $P_{\text{H}_2} = P_{\text{O}_2} = 1.2 \text{ atm}$.

**Step 2 — Numerical Evaluation of the Log Term (2 marks):**

$$(P_{\text{H}_2})^2 \cdot P_{\text{O}_2} = (1.2)^2 \cdot (1.2) = 1.728$$

$$\ln(1.728) = 0.5471$$

**Step 3 — Calculate E (1 mark):**

$$E = 1.229 + \frac{(8.314)(353.15)}{4(96485)} \cdot 0.5471$$

$$E = 1.229 + (0.007604)(0.5471)$$

$$E = 1.229 + 0.00416 \approx 1.233 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Voltage Efficiency (1 mark):**

$$\eta_{\text{v}} = \frac{V_{\text{op}}}{E^{\circ}} = \frac{0.78}{1.229} = 0.6346 \approx 63.5\%$$

**Step 5 — Overall Cell Efficiency (1 mark):**

$$\eta_{\text{cell}} = \eta_{\text{th}} \times \eta_{\text{v}} \times \eta_{\text{F}} = 0.830 \times 0.6346 \times 0.97$$

$$\eta_{\text{cell}} = 0.511 \approx 51.1\%$$

**Final Answer (boxed in answer script):** $E_{\text{OCV}} \approx 1.233 \text{ V}$; $\eta_{\text{v}} = 63.5\%$; $\eta_{\text{cell}} = 51.1\%$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]*

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare Alkaline Fuel Cells (AFC) and Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cells (PEMFC) on the basis of electrolyte, charge carrier, operating temperature, catalyst cost, CO₂ sensitivity, and automotive suitability. Comment on why PEMFC is preferred over AFC for current road-going FCEVs.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss in detail the water management and thermal management challenges in an AFC stack. How are these addressed in a typical automotive AFC system?

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

| Comparison Parameter | AFC | PEMFC |
|---|---|---|
| Electrolyte | Liquid aqueous KOH (6–8 M) | Solid polymer membrane (Nafion) |
| Charge Carrier | OH⁻ (hydroxyl ion) | H⁺ (proton) |
| Operating Temperature | 60 – 90 °C (modern) | 65 – 85 °C |
| Catalyst | Ni, Ag (non-precious) | Pt (precious metal) |
| CO₂ Sensitivity | **High** (forms K₂CO₃) | Low (membrane tolerant) |
| Cost | **Lower** | Higher (Pt cost) |
| Power Density | Moderate | **High** |
| Automotive Suitability | Stationary / APU use | **Road-going FCEVs** (preferred) |

**Conclusion (2 marks):** PEMFC is preferred for road-going FCEVs because:
1. It tolerates **air-borne CO₂** directly (no need for CO₂ scrubber), simplifying the Balance of Plant.
2. The **solid electrolyte** eliminates leakage and corrosion problems of liquid KOH.
3. **Higher power density** and **faster start-up** suit variable-load automotive duty cycles.
4. Mature supply chain and OEM adoption (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO, Honda Clarity).

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Water Management Challenge (3 marks):**
- In AFCs, water is **produced at the anode**, not the cathode.
- Excess water dilutes the KOH electrolyte, reducing ionic conductivity.
- Water can also flood the porous anode, blocking H₂ access to the catalyst.
- **Solution:** Use a **circulating KOH loop** that continuously removes product water via a separator; control electrolyte concentration to maintain 6–8 M KOH.

**Thermal Management Challenge (2 marks):**
- AFCs generate significant waste heat (≈ 45–50% of input energy at 50% electrical efficiency).
- Temperature must be held in the 60–90 °C window: too cold → slow kinetics, too hot → electrolyte evaporation and membrane damage.
- **Solution:** Integrate a **vehicle cooling system** (radiator + coolant pump) sized for stack thermal load. Coolant flows through bipolar plate cooling channels embedded in the stack.

**System-Level BOP (Balance of Plant) (2 marks):**
- A complete automotive AFC system includes: H₂ supply, CO₂ scrubber (if air is used as oxidant), humidifier, electrolyte circulation pump, water separator, radiator, and DC-DC converter for the traction bus.
- The complexity of this BOP is one reason PEMFC has displaced AFC for road vehicles.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (AFC Questions):**
> 1. **Direction of OH⁻ migration:** Many students incorrectly write that OH⁻ moves from anode to cathode. In a galvanic cell, **anions move toward the anode**, i.e., OH⁻ moves **from cathode to anode**. Losing 1 mark here is very common.
> 2. **Water at the anode, not the cathode:** PEMFC textbooks emphasize cathode water production, so students often transfer this incorrectly to AFCs. Memorize the half-reactions explicitly.
> 3. **Nernst equation n-value:** Use **n = 4** (electrons per mole of O₂) for hydrogen–oxygen cells, not n = 2. Half-mark deductions are common for this.
> 4. **Using $\Delta H$ (HHV) for efficiency:** For automotive fuel cells, KTU expects **HHV-based efficiency (285.8 kJ/mol)** unless LHV is explicitly specified.
> 5. **Confusing AFC with PEMFC terminology:** Saying "proton" or "H⁺" in an AFC question is an instant 0.5–1 mark deduction. Always use "hydroxyl ion / OH⁻".

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Full form:** Alkaline Fuel Cell (AFC); first deployed by **Francis Bacon (1932)**, used in **NASA Apollo (1960s)**.
- **Electrolyte:** Aqueous **KOH (6–8 mol/L)** — liquid alkaline, not solid.
- **Charge carrier:** **OH⁻ ions**, migrating from cathode to anode.
- **Anode reaction:** $2\text{H}_2 + 4\text{OH}^- \rightarrow 4\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^-$
- **Cathode reaction:** $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$
- **Overall reaction:** $2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- **Open-circuit voltage:** **1.229 V** (Nernst theoretical); up to **1.233 V** at 80 °C with elevated pressures.
- **Operating voltage range:** **0.7 – 0.85 V** per cell under load.
- **Operating temperature:** **60 – 90 °C** (modern); legacy up to 250 °C.
- **Electrical efficiency:** **50 – 60%** (HHV); up to **85%** with CHP.
- **Catalysts:** **Non-precious metals** (Ni anode, Ag/Ni cathode) — key cost advantage.
- **Major drawback:** **CO₂ sensitivity** — KOH reacts to form K₂CO₃, poisoning the electrolyte.
- **Water management:** Water produced at **anode**, must be removed via circulating loop.
- **Thermal management:** ~45–50% of input energy is waste heat, removed by vehicle cooling system.
