- **Automotive relevance:** Not used in current road FCEVs (PEMFC preferred), but important for **stationary APUs, military vehicles, and cost-down research (AEMFC)**.
- **Polarization losses:** Cathode activation loss is dominant; ohmic and concentration losses scale with current.
- **Faraday constant:** $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$ — use this in Nernst equation evaluations.
- **Remember:** $\eta_{\text{cell}} = \eta_{\text{th}} \times \eta_{\text{v}} \times \eta_{\text{F}}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** When asked to "explain AFC working principle", always include: (1) construction diagram, (2) both half-reactions, (3) direction of electrons and OH⁻ ions, (4) net reaction, (5) water/heat management, and (6) one practical limitation. This structure guarantees full marks on a 7-mark sub-question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Medium and High Temperature Fuel Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET414 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Medium and High Temperature Fuel Cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Medium and High Temperature Fuel Cells

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> Medium and High Temperature Fuel Cells are electrochemical energy conversion devices that operate at elevated temperatures (typically between **150 °C and 1000 °C**), using a variety of liquid, molten, or solid electrolytes to directly convert the chemical energy of a fuel (commonly hydrogen, natural gas, methanol, or hydrocarbons) into electrical energy with high efficiency and low emissions.

The classification of fuel cells by operating temperature is a critical parameter because it determines:
- The type of electrolyte used.
- The catalyst required (or absence of precious metal catalysts).
- The fuel flexibility (reforming capability).
- The application domain (stationary power, automotive, auxiliary power units).

### 1.2 Operational Temperature Classification

| Class | Temperature Range | Representative Cell |
|-------|------------------|---------------------|
| Low Temperature | 50 °C – 100 °C | Alkaline (AFC), PEMFC |
| Medium Temperature | 150 °C – 250 °C | Phosphoric Acid (PAFC) |
| Medium-High Temperature | 600 °C – 700 °C | Molten Carbonate (MCFC) |
| High Temperature | 600 °C – 1000 °C | Solid Oxide (SOFC) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Engine That Eats Heat to Make Electricity"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine a regular car engine that must first burn fuel to create hot, expanding gases, and only then converts that motion into rotation. A **fuel cell** skips the fire-and-piston routine entirely. Think of it as a **"silent sandwich"**: two slices of bread (the electrodes) with a special sauce (the electrolyte) in between. You drip hydrogen on one side and oxygen on the other, and the sauce helps them exchange a tiny "electron currency" through an external wire — that flow of electrons is electricity.

> For **medium and high temperature** fuel cells, picture heating this sandwich to a few hundred degrees. This makes the sauce so reactive that you no longer need expensive platinum as a catalyst — cheaper nickel and ceramics work fine. The heat also lets the cell "chew up" raw natural gas, gasoline, or diesel directly without needing a separate reformer — making these cells highly attractive for heavy-duty vehicles, trucks, buses, and range-extender applications in modern automotive platforms.

## 1.4 Significance in Automotive Engineering

- **PAFC** is widely deployed in **stationary combined heat and power (CHP)** for fleet garages and bus depots.
- **MCFC** is used in **large-scale stationary power plants** and proposed for **ship propulsion**.
- **SOFC** is the **flagship technology** for heavy-duty trucks (e.g., **diesel reformer–SOFC Auxiliary Power Units (APUs)** for long-haul trucks to power hotel loads like refrigeration, lighting, and HVAC without idling the main diesel engine).
- They are **tolerant to CO** and, in the case of SOFCs and MCFCs, can internally reform hydrocarbon fuels.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A direct question on **"Compare MCFC and SOFC"** is a high-probability 7 or 14 mark question. Memorize the electrolyte, mobile ion, operating temperature, anode/cathode materials, and applications in tabular form.

## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Faraday's Constant:** $F = 96485 \text{ C/mol}$
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
- **Standard Reversible Voltage (H₂/O₂ cell at 25 °C):** $E^0 = 1.229 \text{ V}$
- **Steam Reforming Endothermicity:** $\Delta H_{298} = +206 \text{ kJ/mol}$ for CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3H₂
- **Water-Gas Shift Exothermicity:** $\Delta H_{298} = -41 \text{ kJ/mol}$ for CO + H₂O → CO₂ + H₂
- **Carbon Deposition (Boudouard) Limit:** Reaction becomes thermodynamically favorable below **~700 °C** at standard pressure — hence SOFCs (>800 °C) avoid carbon deposition from CH₄.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cell (PAFC) — The "First-Generation Commercial Cell"

### 2.1.1 Cell Architecture

- **Electrolyte:** Concentrated **phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄, ~100 %)** held in a **silicon carbide (SiC) matrix**.
- **Operating Temperature:** **150 °C – 220 °C** (typically **200 °C**).
- **Anode:** Porous carbon (graphitized) with **Pt catalyst** (loading ~0.1–0.3 mg/cm²).
- **Cathode:** Porous carbon with **Pt catalyst** (loading ~0.5–1.0 mg/cm²).
- **Charge Carrier:** **H⁺ (proton)**.
- **Fuel:** Pure or reformed **H₂** (with low CO tolerance, ~1–2 %).

### 2.1.2 Electrode Reactions

The half-cell reactions at the platinum electrodes are:

$$ \text{Anode (H}_2 \text{ side):} \quad H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^- $$

$$ \text{Cathode (O}_2 \text{ side):} \quad \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O $$

$$ \text{Overall:} \quad H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O \quad (E^0 = 1.229 \text{ V}) $$

### 2.1.3 Why "Medium" Temperature?

At 200 °C, phosphoric acid is a stable, ionically-conductive liquid. The higher temperature compared to PEMFC (~80 °C) gives:
- Better **CO tolerance** (catalyst poisoning reduced).
- Faster **electro-kinetics** (lower activation loss).
- Higher-grade **waste heat** usable for CHP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cathode Reaction Insight:** The cathode reaction in PAFC is the **slowest (rate-limiting) step** because the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) has a high activation energy even at 200 °C. This is why cathode Pt loading is **3–5× higher** than anode.

---

## 2.2 Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell (MCFC) — The "Melted-Salt Powerhouse"

### 2.2.1 Cell Architecture

- **Electrolyte:** Mixture of **lithium carbonate (Li₂CO₃) and potassium carbonate (K₂CO₃)** or **Li₂CO₃/Na₂CO₃**, held in a **LiAlO₂ (lithium aluminate) ceramic matrix**.
- **State:** **Molten (liquid)** at operating temperature.
- **Operating Temperature:** **600 °C – 700 °C** (typically **650 °C**).
- **Anode:** **Porous Ni–Cr or Ni–Al alloy** (Cr/Al prevents sintering and creep).
- **Cathode:** **Porous lithiated NiO** (lithium doped into NiO during operation by Li⁺ from electrolyte).
- **Charge Carrier:** **CO₃²⁻ (carbonate ion)**.
- **Fuel:** H₂, CO (directly used!), and reformed hydrocarbons.

### 2.2.2 Electrode Reactions

$$ \text{Anode (Fuel side):} \quad H_2 + CO_3^{2-} \rightarrow H_2O + CO_2 + 2e^- $$

$$ \text{Cathode (Oxidant side):} \quad \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + CO_2 + 2e^- \rightarrow CO_3^{2-} $$

$$ \text{Overall:} \quad H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + CO_2(\text{cathode}) \rightarrow H_2O + CO_2(\text{anode}) $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Net CO₂ Transfer:** CO₂ is consumed at the cathode and produced at the anode. The **net** reaction is identical to hydrogen combustion. For system operation, **CO₂ must be recycled** from anode exhaust back to the cathode inlet.

### 2.2.3 CO Tolerance — The Killer Feature

At 650 °C, the **CO is a fuel, not a poison**. It is electrochemically oxidized at the anode:

$$ CO + CO_3^{2-} \rightarrow 2CO_2 + 2e^- $$

This is why MCFCs can directly use **coal-derived syngas** and **reformed natural gas** without complex CO cleanup.

---

## 2.3 Solid Oxide Fuel Cell (SOFC) — The "Ceramic Engine"

### 2.3.1 Cell Architecture

- **Electrolyte:** **Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia (YSZ)** — ZrO₂ doped with 8 mol% Y₂O₃.
- **State:** **Solid ceramic** (no liquid phase, no leakage).
- **Operating Temperature:** **600 °C – 1000 °C** (traditional: 800–1000 °C; intermediate-temperature SOFC or IT-SOFC: 600–800 °C).
- **Anode:** **Ni-YSZ cermet** (Ni metal + YSZ ceramic composite; porous, ~30–40 % porosity).
- **Cathode:** **Lanthanum Strontium Manganite (LSM, La₁₋ₓSrₓMnO₃)** — for high-temp; or **LSCF (La₁₋ₓSrₓCo₁₋ᵧFeᵧO₃)** for IT-SOFC.
- **Interconnect:** **Lanthanum Chromite (LaCrO₃)** for high-temp; **Ferritic stainless steel (Crofer 22 APU)** for IT-SOFC.
- **Charge Carrier:** **O²⁻ (oxide ion)**.
- **Fuel:** H₂, CO, CH₄ (with internal reforming), syngas.

### 2.3.2 Electrode Reactions

$$ \text{Anode (Fuel side):} \quad H_2 + O^{2-} \rightarrow H_2O + 2e^- $$

$$ \text{Cathode (Oxidant side):} \quad \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2e^- \rightarrow O^{2-} $$

$$ \text{Overall:} \quad H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O $$

For **direct methane** (internal reforming in SOFC):

$$ CH_4 + 4O^{2-} \rightarrow 2H_2O + CO_2 + 8e^- $$

$$ CO + O^{2-} \rightarrow CO_2 + 2e^- $$

### 2.3.3 Internal Reforming — The Automotive USP

Because of the **high temperature** and **steam** byproduct, SOFCs can perform **endothermic steam reforming** internally on the Ni-YSZ anode:

$$ CH_4 + H_2O \rightarrow 3H_2 + CO \quad (\Delta H > 0, \text{ endothermic}) $$

This endothermic reaction provides **in-cell cooling** — a massive thermal-management advantage for vehicle integration.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates all derivable, examinable equations for this topic. Memorize these for KTU ESE 2024.

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Use in KTU Exam |
|---|---------------------|----------------|------------------|
| 1 | $\Delta G = -nFE$ | Gibbs free energy, moles of e⁻, Faraday constant, reversible voltage | Open-circuit voltage derivation |
| 2 | $E = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\left(\dfrac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$ | Nernst equation for H₂/O₂ cell | Temperature & pressure effects |
| 3 | $\eta_{act} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha nF}\ln\left(\dfrac{i}{i_0}\right)$ | Activation overpotential (Butler-Volmer simplified/Tafel) | Polarization curve analysis |
| 4 | $\eta_{ohm} = iR_{ASR}$ | Ohmic loss, with area-specific resistance (Ω·cm²) | ASR-based cell design |
| 5 | $\eta_{conc} = \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\left(1 - \dfrac{i}{i_L}\right)$ | Concentration overpotential, with limiting current density $i_L$ | Mass-transport loss |
| 6 | $V_{cell} = E - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc}$ | Full cell voltage equation | Polarization curve |
| 7 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} = 1 - \dfrac{T\Delta S}{\Delta H}$ | Thermodynamic efficiency (Carnot limit for H₂: 83% at 25 °C) | Efficiency comparison |
| 8 | $P_{max} = i \cdot V_{cell}(i)$ | Maximum power point | System design |
| 9 | $\sigma_{ion} = \sigma_0 \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{k_BT}\right)$ | Ionic conductivity (Arrhenius) — governs YSZ/MC electrolyte choice | High-temp advantage |
| 10 | $E^0(T) = 1.253 - 0.000245(T-298)$ in V (with T in K) | Empirical Nernst shift for H₂/O₂ (Kirkhoff-type) | Open-circuit voltage vs T |

> [!WARNING]
> **Formula Pitfall:** In the Nernst equation row, do not write `P_H2 * P_O2^(1/2)` in a markdown table cell using the bare pipe symbol `|` — it breaks the table. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` or rephrase without vertical bars.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PAFC**: ~200 kW CHP units by **Doosan Fuel Cell** and **UTC Power** deployed in hospitals, hotels, and bus depots. Acts as a **range extender for electric buses**.
- **MCFC**: **1.4 MW – 60 MW** stationary plants by **FuelCell Energy (USA)** and **POSCO Energy (Korea)**; suited for **marine propulsion** because exhaust heat drives absorption refrigeration.
- **SOFC**: **Bloom Energy (USA)** produces 200 kW – 1 MW SOFC servers for **data centers, retail chains, and Walmart stores**. **Delphi / BMW / Nissan** have prototyped **SOFC APUs for trucks** running on diesel reformate. **Cummins** is commercializing SOFC–gas turbine hybrid systems reaching **~60 % LHV efficiency** (record-breaking).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Reversible Open-Circuit Voltage from Thermodynamics

The reversible cell voltage (also called **electromotive force, EMF**, or **open-circuit voltage, OCV**) is the maximum possible voltage a fuel cell can deliver at zero current. It comes from the **change in Gibbs free energy** of the electrochemical reaction.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — The First Law Connection

For any reversible electrochemical reaction at constant T and P, the **maximum non-PV (electrical) work** equals the **decrease in Gibbs free energy**:

$$ W_{elec,max} = -\Delta G $$

The electrical work delivered per mole of electrons transferred is:

$$ W_{elec,max} = nFE $$

Equating the two:

$$ -\Delta G = nFE \quad \Rightarrow \quad E = -\dfrac{\Delta G}{nF} $$

### 3.1.2 Hydrogen–Oxygen Cell — Step-by-Step

For the overall reaction $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$:

At standard conditions (298 K, 1 atm):

$$ \Delta G^0_{H_2O(l)} = -237.2 \text{ kJ/mol} $$

$$ n = 2 \text{ (electrons per mole of H}_2 \text{)} $$

$$ F = 96485 \text{ C/mol} $$

$$ E^0 = -\dfrac{-237200 \text{ J/mol}}{2 \times 96485 \text{ C/mol}} = \dfrac{237200}{192970} \text{ V} $$

$$ E^0 = 1.229 \text{ V} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** A single H₂/O₂ fuel cell **theoretically** delivers 1.229 V. Real automotive stacks connect many cells in series (typically 300–400 cells) to achieve 300–400 V for traction inverters.

### 3.1.3 Temperature Dependence of E (The Kirkhoff / Nernst Form)

Starting from the **Gibbs–Helmholtz equation**:

$$ \left(\dfrac{\partial(\Delta G/T)}{\partial T}\right)_P = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{T^2} $$

Integrating with $\Delta H \approx \text{const}$ (small T range):

$$ \dfrac{\Delta G(T)}{T} = \dfrac{\Delta G(T_0)}{T_0} - \Delta H \left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right) $$

Dividing through by $-nF$ and using $E = -\Delta G / nF$:

$$ E(T) = E(T_0) + \dfrac{\Delta H}{nF}\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right) $$

This **decreases** with temperature because $\Delta H$ is negative for the exothermic H₂ oxidation. At 1000 K (~727 °C, SOFC regime):

$$ E(1000) \approx 1.229 + \dfrac{-286000}{2 \times 96485}\left(\dfrac{1}{1000} - \dfrac{1}{298}\right) $$

$$ E(1000) \approx 1.229 - 1.482 \times (-2.69 \times 10^{-3}) \approx 1.229 - (-0.00399) $$

$$ E(1000) \approx 0.917 \text{ V} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Higher temperature → lower OCV** for H₂/O₂. BUT — the gain in faster kinetics and lower ohmic loss far outweighs the OCV drop, so the **net power density at peak power increases** with temperature. This is why SOFCs are competitive despite lower OCV.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Nernst Equation for a Generic Fuel Cell

For the generalized reaction: $aA + bB \rightarrow cC + dD$, the Gibbs free energy change under non-standard partial pressures is:

$$ \Delta G = \Delta G^0 + RT\ln\left(\dfrac{P_C^c P_D^d}{P_A^a P_B^b}\right) $$

Dividing by $-nF$:

$$ E = E^0 - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\left(\dfrac{P_C^c P_D^d}{P_A^a P_B^b}\right) $$

For the H₂/O₂ cell with product H₂O:

$$ E = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\left(\dfrac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right) $$

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Note for Automotive Reformate SOFC:** When using diesel reformate, $P_{H_2}$ is replaced by the equivalent hydrogen partial pressure, and CO partial pressure enters separately (since CO is also a fuel). Real SOFC systems use empirical Nernst expressions fitted to multi-component gas mixtures.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: The Activation Overpotential (Tafel Slope)

The **Butler–Volmer equation** governs electrode kinetics. For a single-electron transfer at sufficiently high overpotential (Tafel approximation):

$$ \eta_{act,a} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha_c nF}\ln\left(\dfrac{i}{i_0}\right) \quad \text{(cathodic)} $$

$$ \eta_{act,an} = \dfrac{RT}{(1-\alpha_a) nF}\ln\left(\dfrac{i}{i_0}\right) \quad \text{(anodic)} $$

For the **oxygen reduction reaction (ORR)** in PAFC at 200 °C with $\alpha_c \approx 0.5$, $n=1$ (rate-determining step), $i_0 \approx 10^{-9}$ A/cm²:

$$ \eta_{act} = \dfrac{(8.314)(473)}{(0.5)(1)(96485)}\ln\left(\dfrac{i}{10^{-9}}\right) $$

$$ \eta_{act} = 0.0815 \ln\left(\dfrac{i}{10^{-9}}\right) \text{ V} $$

At $i = 0.5$ A/cm²:

$$ \eta_{act} = 0.0815 \times \ln(5 \times 10^{8}) = 0.0815 \times 20.03 = 1.63 \text{ V} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Excessive loss!** This is precisely why PAFC is "medium" temperature — it suffers from huge cathode activation loss. Raising T to 650 °C (MCFC) or 800 °C (SOFC) boosts $i_0$ by **6–8 orders of magnitude**, making ORR nearly activation-loss-free.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Area-Specific Resistance (ASR) and Ohmic Loss

The voltage drop from ionic and electronic resistance in the cell is:

$$ \eta_{ohm} = i \cdot R_{ASR} $$

where $R_{ASR}$ has units of $\Omega \cdot cm^2$. For a **SOFC** with YSZ electrolyte of thickness $L$ and ionic conductivity $\sigma$:

$$ R_{ASR,elec} = \dfrac{L}{\sigma} $$

For YSZ at 800 °C: $\sigma \approx 0.01$ S/cm. With $L = 50 \text{ μm} = 0.005$ cm:

$$ R_{ASR,elec} = \dfrac{0.005}{0.01} = 0.5 \text{ }\Omega \cdot cm^2 $$

At $i = 0.5$ A/cm²:

$$ \eta_{ohm} = 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.25 \text{ V} $$

For a 10 μm thin-film YSZ IT-SOFC at 700 °C ($\sigma \approx 0.005$ S/cm):

$$ R_{ASR,elec} = \dfrac{0.001}{0.005} = 0.2 \text{ }\Omega \cdot cm^2 $$

This **55 % reduction** in ohmic loss justifies the push toward IT-SOFC.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Cell Efficiency vs. Temperature (Carnot Comparison)

The **thermodynamic (reversible) efficiency** of a fuel cell is:

$$ \eta_{rev} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} $$

For H₂ at 25 °C (liquid water product):

$$ \eta_{rev} = \dfrac{237.2}{286.0} = 0.829 \text{ or } 82.9 \text{ %} $$

For H₂ at 800 °C (SOFC, gaseous water product):

$$ \eta_{rev} = \dfrac{\Delta G(1073 K)}{\Delta H(1073 K)} $$

Using $\Delta G \approx 177$ kJ/mol and $\Delta H \approx -243$ kJ/mol (steam product, standard tables):

$$ \eta_{rev} = \dfrac{177}{243} = 0.728 \text{ or } 72.8 \text{ %} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Surprising Insight:** The **thermodynamic** efficiency of an SOFC at 800 °C is **lower** than a PEMFC at 80 °C. But SOFC systems can **co-generate** high-grade heat (CHP) and reach 85–90 % **total** efficiency. Combined with internal reforming, SOFC APUs achieve **net diesel-equivalent mileage savings of 6–8 %** for long-haul trucks.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — PAFC vs MCFC vs SOFC

| Parameter | PAFC | MCFC | SOFC |
|-----------|------|------|------|
| Electrolyte | H₃PO₄ (liquid, in SiC matrix) | Li₂CO₃/K₂CO₃ (molten, in LiAlO₂) | YSZ (solid ceramic) |
| Operating T | 150–220 °C | 600–700 °C | 600–1000 °C |
| Mobile Ion | H⁺ | CO₃²⁻ | O²⁻ |
| Anode Material | Pt/C | Ni–Cr / Ni–Al | Ni–YSZ cermet |
| Cathode Material | Pt/C | Lithiated NiO | LSM (LaSrMnO₃) |
| Catalyst Needed? | Yes (Pt) | No (Ni suffices) | No (Ni suffices) |
| CO Tolerance | ~1–2 % | High (CO is fuel) | Very high (CO is fuel) |
| Internal Reforming | No | Partial | Yes (full) |
| Power Density | ~0.2–0.3 W/cm² | ~0.1–0.2 W/cm² | ~0.3–1.0 W/cm² |
| Electrical Efficiency | 40–45 % | 50–55 % | 50–60 % |
| Cogeneration Eff. | 70–85 % | 70–85 % | 75–90 % |
| Start-up Time | Hours | Hours | Hours (high-T) / faster (IT-SOFC) |
| Major Application | Stationary CHP, bus depots | Large stationary power, marine | Truck APUs, data centers, hybrid cycles |
| Main Challenge | Pt cost, CO poisoning | Electrolyte corrosion, CO₂ recycle | Thermal cycling, sealants, ceramic brittleness |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PAFC Functional Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FuelInlet["Fuel Inlet (H2-rich)"]
        H2["Reformed H2 Gas"]
    end
    subgraph AnodeHalf["Anode Half-Cell"]
        Anode["Porous Carbon + Pt Catalyst"]
        ElecAcid["H3PO4 Electrolyte in SiC Matrix"]
    end
    subgraph CathodeHalf["Cathode Half-Cell"]
        Cathode["Porous Carbon + Pt Catalyst"]
    end
    subgraph OxidantInlet["Oxidant Inlet"]
        Air["Ambient Air O2"]
    end
    subgraph ExternalCircuit["External Load"]
        Load["DC Bus / Battery / Inverter"]
    end
    H2 --> Anode
    Anode -->|"H+ ions migrate"| ElecAcid
    ElecAcid -->|"H+ arrives"| Cathode
    Air --> Cathode
    Anode -->|"e- through wire"| Load
    Load -->|"e- returns"| Cathode
    Anode -.->|"H2O byproduct vented"| ExhaustA["Anode Exhaust: H2O + unreacted H2"]
    Cathode -.->|"Unreacted N2, O2, H2O"| ExhaustC["Cathode Exhaust: Air + H2O"]
```

    ElectrolyteMC --> CathodeMC

    ExtLoadMC -->|"e- to cathode"| CathodeMC

> **Examination Tip:** When drawing a polarization curve in the exam, label: (1) the OCV intercept, (2) the activation-dominated region, (3) the linear ohmic region, (4) the concentration-limited region, and (5) the **maximum power point** (where $d(IV)/di = 0$).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q: List any three advantages of Solid Oxide Fuel Cells (SOFCs) over Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cells (PAFCs).**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per advantage):**

1. **No precious metal catalyst required:** SOFCs operate at 800–1000 °C and use cheap **nickel-YSZ cermet** anodes, whereas PAFCs require expensive **platinum** catalysts at both electrodes.

2. **Higher electrical efficiency and fuel flexibility:** SOFCs achieve **50–60 %** electrical efficiency and can directly use **CO, CH₄, and diesel reformate** as fuel because of internal reforming on the Ni-YSZ anode. PAFCs are limited to nearly pure H₂ and suffer CO poisoning above ~2 %.

3. **Solid-state electrolyte — no liquid management:** SOFCs use a **solid YSZ ceramic** electrolyte that eliminates problems of electrolyte leakage, flooding, and pump-out that plague PAFCs and MCFCs. This also permits planar and tubular geometries that simplify stack design.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: Why must CO₂ be recycled from the anode exhaust to the cathode inlet in an MCFC system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The mobile charge carrier in a Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell is the **carbonate ion CO₃²⁻**. Looking at the cathode half-reaction:

$$ \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + CO_2 + 2e^- \rightarrow CO_3^{2-} $$

The **CO₂ is consumed at the cathode** as a reactant. At the anode, CO₂ is produced:

$$ H_2 + CO_3^{2-} \rightarrow H_2O + CO_2 + 2e^- $$

If the CO₂ produced at the anode is **not recycled** to the cathode, the cathode will starve of CO₂ within minutes, the electrolyte will lose its carbonate ion population, the cell voltage will collapse, and the stack will fail. Practically, a **CO₂ separator/recirculator** (often a condenser + ejector) is mandatory in every MCFC system, adding parasitic load and complexity. **(3 marks — 1 mark each for: charge carrier identification, cathode consumption, anode production, and recycling necessity.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Module 4 Choice]`
**Q: (a) With neat sketches, explain the construction and working principle of a Solid Oxide Fuel Cell (SOFC). Discuss the half-cell reactions at both electrodes.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

#### Construction (3 marks)

- **Electrolyte:** A dense, gas-tight layer of **Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia (YSZ)**, typically 8 mol% Y₂O₃ in ZrO₂, of thickness 10–50 μm for IT-SOFC, 50–200 μm for traditional SOFC.
- **Anode (Fuel side):** Porous **Nickel-YSZ cermet** (~40 % porosity). The Ni provides electronic conductivity and catalytic activity; the YSZ provides ionic pathways and prevents Ni coarsening.
- **Cathode (Air side):** Porous **Lanthanum Strontium Manganite (LSM, La₁₋ₓSrₓMnO₃)** for high-temperature operation; **LSCF** for IT-SOFC.
- **Interconnect:** Connects cells in series. For high-T: **LaCrO₃** ceramic; for IT-SOFC: **ferritic stainless steel (Crofer 22 APU)**.
- **Sealants:** Glass or mica-based gaskets to prevent gas crossover.
- **Geometry:** Either **planar** (high power density, fragile) or **tubular** (robust, lower power density; used by Siemens/Westinghouse).

#### Working Principle and Half-Cell Reactions (4 marks)

At the **cathode** (air side), molecular oxygen is reduced and ionized by electrons arriving from the external circuit:

$$ \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2e^- \rightarrow O^{2-} $$

The oxide ion **O²⁻** migrates through the YSZ electrolyte (driven by the chemical potential gradient and the applied load) to the anode side.

At the **anode** (fuel side), hydrogen (or reformed hydrocarbon) reacts with the incoming oxide ion:

$$ H_2 + O^{2-} \rightarrow H_2O + 2e^- $$

The released electrons travel through the external circuit, performing useful electrical work, before returning to the cathode. The **net** reaction is:

$$ H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O + \text{Electrical Work} + \text{Heat} $$

The high operating temperature (800 °C) ensures high ionic conductivity of YSZ, fast electrode kinetics without precious metals, and **direct internal reforming** of CH₄ + H₂O → 3H₂ + CO on the Ni-YSZ surface.

---

**(b) Compare the performance, materials, and applications of MCFCs and SOFCs in a tabular format. Which is more suitable as a truck Auxiliary Power Unit (APU) and why?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

| Parameter | MCFC | SOFC |
|-----------|------|------|
| Electrolyte | Molten Li₂CO₃/K₂CO₃ in LiAlO₂ | Solid YSZ |
| Mobile ion | CO₃²⁻ | O²⁻ |
| Operating T | 600–700 °C | 600–1000 °C |
| Catalyst | Not needed (Ni sufficient) | Not needed (Ni sufficient) |
| CO tolerance | Excellent | Excellent |
| Internal reforming | Partial (slow) | Yes (fast) |
| Electrical efficiency | 50–55 % | 50–60 % |
| Stack start-up | Hours | Hours (high-T) |
| CO₂ recycling | Required | Not required |
| Primary application | Large stationary power, marine | Truck APUs, data centers, hybrid cycles |
| Critical issues | Electrolyte corrosion, CO₂ recycle loop | Thermal cycling, ceramic brittleness, sealants |

**More suitable for truck APU: SOFC** (3 marks)

1. **No CO₂ recycle loop required** — drastically simplifies the auxiliary system, reducing parasitic load, weight, and volume in a space-constrained truck cab/chassis.

2. **Solid-state electrolyte** — immune to vibration-induced liquid sloshing, leakage, and pump-out failures that occur over rough road conditions in MCFCs.

3. **Higher power density and efficiency** with direct internal reforming of diesel — eliminates an external reformer and integrates with existing diesel fuel infrastructure.

4. **High-grade waste heat** (700–800 °C) can drive a **bottoming gas turbine** (SOFC–GT hybrid) reaching 60–65 % LHV efficiency, making it ideal for long-haul fleet economics.

5. **Direct hydrocarbon use:** the SOFC anode can use methane, propane, gasoline, or diesel reformate, whereas MCFCs require **sulfur-free** fuel (Ni anode is sulfur-sensitive).

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Module 4 Choice]`
**Q: (a) Explain the construction, working, and electrode reactions of a Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cell (PAFC). Why is its operating temperature restricted to around 200 °C?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

#### Construction (2 marks)

- **Electrolyte:** Concentrated **100 % phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄)** immobilized in a porous **silicon carbide (SiC)** matrix to prevent flooding and acid migration.
- **Electrodes:** Both anode and cathode are **porous graphitized carbon paper** (or carbon black) with a **platinum catalyst** dispersed on carbon support. Cathode Pt loading (0.5–1.0 mg/cm²) is higher than anode (0.1–0.3 mg/cm²).
- **Bipolar plates:** Graphite or carbon-composite plates with flow channels for fuel and oxidant.
- **Operating temperature:** 150–220 °C, typically 200 °C, with **water-cooling** of the stack to remove waste heat and produce hot water for CHP.

#### Working and Electrode Reactions (3 marks)

Hydrogen-rich fuel (from reformer or pure H₂) is fed to the anode. At the anode, hydrogen is oxidized:

$$ H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^- $$

The protons **H⁺** migrate through the phosphoric acid electrolyte to the cathode. Air (or O₂) is fed to the cathode, where the **oxygen reduction reaction (ORR)** occurs:

$$ \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O $$

Liquid water is produced at the cathode and removed by evaporation/flow. The electrons flow through the external circuit to deliver DC power.

#### Why ~200 °C? (2 marks)

1. **Phosphoric acid dehydration:** Above ~210 °C, H₃PO₄ begins to **dehydrate to pyrophosphoric acid (H₄P₂O₇)** and polyphosphoric acids, which are poor ionic conductors. The acid becomes viscous, losing conductivity.

2. **Electrolyte stability:** The SiC matrix and carbon components are stable up to ~220 °C. Beyond this, carbon oxidation (C + O₂ → CO₂) becomes rapid, destroying electrodes.

3. **Pt catalyst CO tolerance:** At ~200 °C, PAFC tolerates ~1–2 % CO (versus ~10 ppm for PEMFC at 80 °C). Below 150 °C, the CO tolerance disappears; above 220 °C, the acid decomposes. Hence **200 °C is the sweet spot**.

---

**(b) Discuss the four major voltage losses in a fuel cell polarization curve. Illustrate each region on a V–i plot. Calculate the activation loss at 200 °C for a PAFC cathode with $i_0 = 10^{-9}$ A/cm², $i = 0.5$ A/cm², and transfer coefficient $\alpha = 0.5$.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

#### Four Major Voltage Losses (4 marks)

The polarization curve plots **cell voltage V** (y-axis) against **current density i** (x-axis). Starting from the open-circuit voltage $E^0$, four losses bring V down as i rises:

1. **Activation loss ($\eta_{act}$):** Energy required to initiate the electrochemical reactions. Dominant at low current densities. Governed by **Butler–Volmer** kinetics, simplified by the **Tafel equation** at high $\eta$:

$$ \eta_{act} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F}\ln\left(\dfrac{i}{i_0}\right) $$

2. **Ohmic loss ($\eta_{ohm}$):** Resistive drop from ionic conduction in the electrolyte and electronic conduction in electrodes, interconnects, and contact resistances. Linear in i:

$$ \eta_{ohm} = i \cdot R_{ASR} $$

3. **Concentration loss ($\eta_{conc}$):** Mass-transport limitation when reactants cannot reach the electrode fast enough (or products cannot leave). Dominant at high current densities, approaching the **limiting current $i_L$**:

$$ \eta_{conc} = \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\left(1 - \dfrac{i}{i_L}\right) $$

4. **Fuel crossover / internal currents** (sometimes considered a 4th or grouped with activation loss): parasitic losses from fuel diffusing through the electrolyte and short-circuit currents. Small but non-zero.

The full cell voltage equation:

$$ V_{cell} = E - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc} $$

#### V–i Plot Illustration (refer to Section 4.4 diagram) (1 mark)

- Region 1 (low i): **Steep non-linear drop** from $E^0$ — activation loss dominated.
- Region 2 (mid i): **Linear drop** — ohmic loss dominated.
- Region 3 (high i): **Sharp non-linear drop** — concentration loss dominates, V → 0 as $i \rightarrow i_L$.

#### Numerical Calculation: Activation Loss (2 marks)

Given:
- $T = 200 \text{ °C} = 473 \text{ K}$
- $i_0 = 10^{-9} \text{ A/cm}^2$
- $i = 0.5 \text{ A/cm}^2$
- $\alpha = 0.5$, $n = 1$ (rate-determining electron transfer in ORR)

Using the Tafel equation:

$$ \eta_{act} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F}\ln\left(\dfrac{i}{i_0}\right) $$

$$ \eta_{act} = \dfrac{(8.314)(473)}{(0.5)(1)(96485)} \times \ln\left(\dfrac{0.5}{10^{-9}}\right) $$

$$ \eta_{act} = \dfrac{3932.5}{48242.5} \times \ln(5 \times 10^{8}) $$

$$ \eta_{act} = 0.0815 \times \ln(5 \times 10^{8}) $$

$$ \eta_{act} = 0.0815 \times 20.03 $$

$$ \boxed{\eta_{act} \approx 1.63 \text{ V}} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Critical for Marks):**
> - **Unit error:** Forgetting to convert 200 °C to **473 K** — instant 0.5 mark deduction.
> - **n selection:** Using n = 2 (total electrons per H₂) instead of n = 1 (rate-determining step) — incorrect by a factor of 2. For ORR, the standard practice is to use n = 1 unless the problem explicitly states otherwise.
> - **Sign convention:** Writing $\eta_{act}$ as positive when it should subtract from E. Always report it as a **loss** (positive number) in the polarization equation $V = E - \eta$.
> - **Forgetting natural log vs. log₁₀:** The constant $\dfrac{RT}{\alpha nF}$ in the Tafel equation uses **natural log (ln)**, not log₁₀. Using log₁₀ gives a result that is **2.303 times too small**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Module 4: Medium and High Temperature Fuel Cells**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ PAFC uses liquid H₃PO₄ at 200 °C with Pt catalyst, mobile ion **H⁺**, ideal for stationary CHP.
- ☐ MCFC uses molten Li₂CO₃/K₂CO₃ at 650 °C, mobile ion **CO₃²⁻**, requires **CO₂ recycling**, no Pt needed.
- ☐ SOFC uses solid YSZ at 600–1000 °C, mobile ion **O²⁻**, no Pt, can directly reform hydrocarbons.
- ☐ Higher temperature = faster kinetics = lower activation loss = can use non-precious catalysts.
- ☐ Higher temperature = **lower OCV** (Nernst), but **higher peak power** due to reduced losses.
- ☐ CO is a **poison** for PAFC, but a **fuel** for MCFC and SOFC.

### Key Equations to Memorize
- ☐ $E = -\Delta G / nF$ (Reversible voltage)
- ☐ $E = E^0 + (RT/nF) \ln(P_{H_2} P_{O_2}^{1/2} / P_{H_2O})$ (Nernst)
- ☐ $\eta_{act} = (RT / \alpha n F) \ln(i / i_0)$ (Tafel)
- ☐ $\eta_{ohm} = i R_{ASR}$ (Ohmic)
- ☐ $\eta_{conc} = (RT / nF) \ln(1 - i / i_L)$ (Concentration)
- ☐ $V_{cell} = E - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc}$
- ☐ $\eta_{rev} = \Delta G / \Delta H$ (Thermodynamic efficiency)

### Numerical Values
- ☐ $E^0$ for H₂/O₂ at 25 °C = **1.229 V**
- ☐ Faraday's constant $F$ = **96485 C/mol**
- ☐ PAFC Pt loading — anode: 0.1–0.3, cathode: 0.5–1.0 mg/cm²
- ☐ SOFC anode: Ni-YSZ cermet; Cathode: LSM; Electrolyte: 8 mol% Y₂O₃-ZrO₂

### Automotive Application Mapping
- ☐ PAFC → **Stationary bus depot CHP**
- ☐ MCFC → **Large MW-scale grid, marine propulsion**
- ☐ SOFC → **Truck APU, data centers, SOFC–GT hybrids**
- ☐ SOFC + bottoming gas turbine = **60–65 % LHV efficiency** (record)

### Common Mistakes to Avoid
- ☐ Don't confuse **PAFC mobile ion (H⁺)** with **PEMFC mobile ion (also H⁺)** — they differ in electrolyte and temperature.
- ☐ Don't write the MCFC cathode reaction without the **CO₂** term.
- ☐ Don't claim SOFC is "100 % efficient" — the **thermodynamic** reversible limit at 800 °C is only **~73 %**.
- ☐ Don't forget to specify **temperature in Kelvin** in Nernst and Tafel equations.
- ☐ Don't use the vertical pipe `|` symbol inside markdown table cells — it breaks the table.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Sentence Summary for Quick Recall:**
> *"At low temperature we use PEMFC with Pt and H⁺; at medium (200 °C) we use PAFC with Pt and H⁺; at medium-high (650 °C) we use MCFC with Ni and CO₃²⁻ needing CO₂ recycle; at high (800–1000 °C) we use SOFC with Ni-YSZ and O²⁻ enabling direct hydrocarbon reforming."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Choices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET414 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fuel Choices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
In modern automotive engineering, **Fuel Choices** refer to the complete spectrum of energy carriers — both conventional (derived from crude petroleum) and alternative (renewable, gaseous, synthetic, or electrical) — that can be used to power internal combustion engines (ICE) and electric/hybrid powertrains. The selection of a fuel is governed by its **thermochemical properties**, **availability**, **cost**, **emission profile**, **engine compatibility**, and **energy density**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module demands a comparative study of fuels based on calorific value, knock resistance, volatility, and their impact on engine design, efficiency, and tailpipe emissions. Selection of the "right fuel" is the central design decision in any modern vehicle.

### 1.2 Classification of Automotive Fuels (KTU Taxonomy)

| **Class** | **Examples** | **State at NTP** |
|---|---|---|
| Petroleum-derived | Petrol (Gasoline), Diesel, Kerosene | Liquid |
| Gaseous fossil fuels | CNG, LNG, LPG | Gas |
| Alcohol fuels | Methanol, Ethanol, Butanol | Liquid |
| Bio-fuels | Biodiesel (FAME), Biogas, Bio-ethanol | Liquid / Gas |
| Synthetic fuels | Synthetic diesel, e-fuels, P-series | Liquid / Gas |
| Hydrogen-based | H₂ (gaseous), Liquid H₂ | Gas / Liquid |
| Electrical | Battery electricity, Fuel cell electricity | Stored energy |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Energy" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of fuels like the energy choices in a modern kitchen.**
> - **Petrol** is like a microwave — quick, convenient, but expensive and slightly polluting.
> - **Diesel** is like a pressure cooker — slow to heat but extremely energy-efficient for heavy tasks (long-haul trucks).
> - **CNG/LPG** is like piped gas — cheap, clean, but needs a tank and special pipeline.
> - **Biodiesel/Ethanol** is like biogas from a backyard plant — renewable, eco-friendly, but needs proper handling.
> - **Hydrogen** is like solar power — the cleanest, but expensive infrastructure (like solar panels).
> - **Battery electricity** is like induction cooking — efficient, no combustion, but needs a "charged battery" to work.

Just as you choose a fuel based on the dish, budget, and infrastructure, an automotive engineer chooses a fuel based on the **vehicle duty cycle, emission norms, cost, and regional availability**.

### 1.4 Why Fuel Choice Matters in Modern Automobiles

- **Emission Compliance:** BS-VI / Euro 6 / Euro 7 norms have pushed OEMs toward cleaner fuels.
- **Energy Security:** Reducing dependency on crude oil imports.
- **Climate Targets:** COP26, NEP 2020 and India's **Panchamrit** pledge (Net-Zero by 2070) demand low-carbon fuels.
- **Performance Demands:** Higher compression ratios demand fuels with specific knock resistance.
- **Cost Sensitivity:** Fuel cost = ~40–50% of vehicle operating cost in commercial vehicles.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Standard Reference Metrics (commit to memory):**
> - Calorific Value of Petrol ≈ **44 MJ/kg**
> - Calorific Value of Diesel ≈ **45 MJ/kg**
> - Calorific Value of CNG ≈ **50 MJ/kg**
> - Calorific Value of Hydrogen (LHV) ≈ **120 MJ/kg**
> - Stoichiometric A/F ratio (Petrol) = **14.7 : 1**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Governing Properties for Fuel Selection

A fuel must satisfy **seven canonical criteria** before it can be adopted in any modern powertrain. Each criterion is explained in plain language along with the underlying physics:

1. **Calorific Value (CV)** — Energy released per unit mass (MJ/kg) or per unit volume (MJ/L). Higher CV = more mileage per tank.
2. **Volatility** — Tendency to vaporize. Governed by the **Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP)**. Too volatile → vapour lock; too low → cold-start difficulty.
3. **Octane Number (ON)** — Resistance to **knocking** in Spark-Ignition (SI) engines. Higher ON allows higher compression ratio.
4. **Cetane Number (CN)** — Ease of **auto-ignition** in Compression-Ignition (CI) engines. Higher CN = smoother cold-start.
5. **Flash Point & Fire Point** — Safety metrics. Diesel has higher flash point → safer to handle than petrol.
6. **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** — The minimum temperature at which fuel ignites without a spark. Critical for storage and transport safety.
7. **Air-Fuel (A/F) Ratio** — Mass ratio of air to fuel for complete (stoichiometric) combustion.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Fuel Selection

- **Step 1:** Identify the engine type — SI or CI.
- **Step 2:** Match the ignition characteristic — high ON for SI, high CN for CI.
- **Step 3:** Check calorific value to estimate vehicle range.
- **Step 4:** Evaluate volatility for the regional climate (cold start vs. vapour lock).
- **Step 5:** Compute emissions footprint (CO₂, NOₓ, PM, unburnt HC).
- **Step 6:** Check storage safety (flash point, AIT).
- **Step 7:** Verify supply infrastructure and cost.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every formula, definition, and metric that has appeared in past KTU university examinations on Fuel Choices. Memorize the column headers and key numerical values.

| **Quantity** | **Formula / Expression** | **Unit** | **Typical Value (Petrol)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Calorific Value (CV) | $CV = \dfrac{Q}{m}$ | kJ/kg or MJ/kg | $\approx 44{,}000$ kJ/kg |
| Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | $bsfc = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b}$ | kg/kWh | $0.25 - 0.40$ |
| Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{P_b \times 3600}{\dot{m}_f \times CV}$ | dimensionless | $25\% - 35\%$ |
| Stoichiometric A/F (generic $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z$) | $A/F = \dfrac{4.76(2x + y/2 - z) \times 29}{12x + y + 16z}$ | kg air / kg fuel | $\approx 14.7$ |
| Air-Fuel Ratio (lambda) | $\lambda = \dfrac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$ | dimensionless | $\lambda = 1$ = stoichiometric |
| Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{a,actual}}{\rho_{a} \times V_d \times N}$ | dimensionless | $0.80 - 0.95$ |
| Mean Effective Pressure | $P_m = \dfrac{W_{net}}{V_d}$ | kPa or bar | $8 - 12$ bar (SI) |
| Energy per litre of fuel | $E_V = CV \times \rho_f$ | MJ/L | $\approx 32$ MJ/L |

> [!WARNING]
> When copying formulas in the exam, **never** write the vertical pipe symbol $\vert$ inside the table. Use $\mid$ or \vert to keep the markdown intact.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Engine Calibration Engineers** use the stoichiometric A/F formula to map fuel injectors.
- **Emission Testing Labs** rely on calorific value and A/F ratio to compute specific emissions (g/kWh).
- **Fuel Refineries** blend components (iso-octane, toluene, ethanol) to meet MON/RON targets of 91, 95, or 98 octane grades.
- **EV vs. ICE comparison** uses *well-to-wheel (WTW)* efficiency: Battery EV ≈ 70–80 %, Petrol ICE ≈ 20–30 %.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "lambda" is the heart of fuel choice:**
> Once you fix the fuel, the stoichiometric A/F ratio is fixed. Modern ECUs continuously measure lambda using a **lambda sensor** (oxygen sensor) and trim the injector pulse width to keep $\lambda = 1$ for a three-way catalytic converter to operate efficiently.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric A/F Ratio for Iso-octane $\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18}$

**Combustion equation (balanced):**

$$\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + 12.5\left(\text{O}_2 + 3.76\,\text{N}_2\right) \longrightarrow 8\,\text{CO}_2 + 9\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 47\,\text{N}_2$$

**Step 1 — Oxygen demand:**
Each C atom needs 1 O₂ (becomes CO₂); each H₂ pair needs ½ O₂ (becomes H₂O).

$$\text{O}_2\ \text{required} = \dfrac{2 \times 8 + 18/2}{2} = 12.5 \ \text{mol per mol of fuel}$$

**Step 2 — Air supplied (moles):**

$$\text{Air moles} = 12.5 \times (1 + 3.76) = 12.5 \times 4.76 = 59.5 \ \text{mol air per mol fuel}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of air:**

$$m_{air} = 59.5 \times 29 = 1725.5 \ \text{kg air per kmol fuel}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of iso-octane:**

$$m_{fuel} = (8 \times 12) + (18 \times 1) = 96 + 18 = 114 \ \text{kg per kmol fuel}$$

**Step 5 — Stoichiometric A/F ratio:**

$$\left(\dfrac{A}{F}\right)_{stoich} = \dfrac{1725.5}{114} = 15.14$$

$$\boxed{(A/F)_{stoich,\ \text{iso-octane}} = 15.14 : 1}$$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Stoichiometric A/F Ratio for Methane (CNG, $\text{CH}_4$)

**Balanced reaction:**

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\left(\text{O}_2 + 3.76\,\text{N}_2\right) \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 7.52\,\text{N}_2$$

**Step 1 — Mass of air per kg of methane:**

$$m_{air} = 2 \times 4.76 \times 29 = 276.08 \ \text{kg air per kmol CH}_4$$

**Step 2 — Mass of methane per kmol:**

$$m_{CH_4} = 12 + 4 = 16 \ \text{kg per kmol}$$

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric A/F:**

$$\left(\dfrac{A}{F}\right)_{stoich} = \dfrac{276.08}{16} = 17.26$$

$$\boxed{(A/F)_{stoich,\ \text{methane}} \approx 17.26 : 1}$$

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Stoichiometric A/F Ratio for Hydrogen

**Balanced reaction:**

$$\text{H}_2 + 0.5\left(\text{O}_2 + 3.76\,\text{N}_2\right) \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + 1.88\,\text{N}_2$$

**Step 1 — Mass of air per kmol H₂:**

$$m_{air} = 0.5 \times 4.76 \times 29 = 69.02 \ \text{kg air per kmol H}_2$$

**Step 2 — Mass of hydrogen per kmol:**

$$m_{H_2} = 2 \ \text{kg per kmol}$$

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric A/F:**

$$\left(\dfrac{A}{F}\right)_{stoich} = \dfrac{69.02}{2} = 34.51$$

$$\boxed{(A/F)_{stoich,\ \text{hydrogen}} = 34.51 : 1}$$

### 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation 4 — Energy Equivalence per kg of Fuel

To compare fuels on a "km per kg" basis, multiply CV by overall powertrain efficiency $\eta_{th}$.

**Effective work output per kg:**

$$W_{out} = \eta_{th} \times CV$$

**For petrol ($\eta_{th}$ = 0.30, CV = 44 MJ/kg):**

$$W_{out} = 0.30 \times 44 = 13.2 \ \text{MJ per kg}$$

**For hydrogen ($\eta_{th}$ = 0.40 in a fuel cell, CV = 120 MJ/kg):**

$$W_{out} = 0.40 \times 120 = 48 \ \text{MJ per kg}$$

**Conclusion (KTU numerical expected):** Hydrogen yields **~3.6× more useful work per kg** than petrol — but its low volumetric density demands high-pressure (700 bar) tanks.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Fuel Property Chart

| **Property** | **Petrol** | **Diesel** | **CNG** | **LPG** | **Ethanol** | **Hydrogen** | **Biodiesel** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Chemical formula | $\text{C}_{4}-\text{C}_{12}$ | $\text{C}_{12}-\text{C}_{25}$ | $\text{CH}_4$ | $\text{C}_3\text{H}_8/\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}$ | $\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$ | $\text{H}_2$ | FAME |
| Calorific value (MJ/kg) | $\approx 44$ | $\approx 45$ | $\approx 50$ | $\approx 46$ | $\approx 27$ | $\approx 120$ | $\approx 38$ |
| Octane number | $90-98$ | $15-25$ | $120+$ | $95-110$ | $100-110$ | $130+$ | $50-65$ |
| Cetane number | $5-20$ | $45-55$ | $-$ | $4-8$ | $5-8$ | low | $45-65$ |
| Stoichiometric A/F | $14.7$ | $14.5$ | $17.2$ | $15.6$ | $9.0$ | $34.5$ | $\approx 13.8$ |
| Flash point ($\degree$C) | $-43$ | $55-65$ | $-188$ | $-104$ | $13$ | flammable gas | $110-170$ |
| Auto-ignition ($\degree$C) | $246-280$ | $210$ | $540$ | $450$ | $365$ | $500$ | $\approx 350$ |
| CO₂ (g/MJ) | $73$ | $74$ | $56$ | $65$ | $70$ | $0$ (tailpipe) | $50-70$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** *Define the term "calorific value" of a fuel. Why is the calorific value of hydrogen considered the highest among conventional automotive fuels?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **calorific value (CV)** of a fuel is the amount of heat energy released per unit mass (kJ/kg) or per unit volume (kJ/L) when the fuel undergoes complete combustion with oxygen under standard conditions. It is measured using a **bomb calorimeter** for solid/liquid fuels and a **Junkers calorimeter** for gaseous fuels.

Hydrogen has the highest CV (~**120 MJ/kg**) because:
- Its combustion product is only **water (H₂O)**, with no carbon to form CO or CO₂.
- The H–H bond releases more energy per unit mass than C–C or C–H bonds.
- However, its volumetric density is extremely low, requiring **700 bar** high-pressure tanks or **cryogenic** liquid storage in vehicles.

*(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for hydrogen CV value, 1 mark for the reasoning + storage limitation.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between Octane Number and Cetane Number. Which one is relevant for SI engines and why?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Octane Number (ON)** | **Cetane Number (CN)** |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Resistance to **knocking** (auto-ignition) | Ease of **auto-ignition** |
| Relevant engine | **Spark-Ignition (SI)** | **Compression-Ignition (CI)** |
| Reference fuel | Iso-octane (ON=100) and n-heptane (ON=0) | n-cetane (CN=100) and α-methyl naphthalene (CN=0) |
| Higher value means | Better knock resistance | Easier cold start |
| Typical fuel | Petrol (~91-98), CNG (120+) | Diesel (~45-55) |

For **SI engines**, the **Octane Number** is critical because combustion is initiated by a spark. If the air-fuel mixture auto-ignites before the spark due to compression heating, **engine knock** occurs — damaging pistons, rings, and reducing efficiency. Therefore, SI engines demand fuels with **high ON**, typically 91 RON for regular and 95/98 RON for premium variants.

*(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for relevance to SI engines, 1 mark for the knocking explanation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern

> **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> *(a)* Explain the major **alternative fuels** used in modern automobiles. Discuss any four alternatives in detail with their **advantages, disadvantages, and engine compatibility**. (7 marks)
> *(b)* With neat sketches/flowcharts, describe the **fuel supply system** of a **multi-point petrol injection (MPFI)** engine and compare it with a **CRDi diesel system**. (7 marks)

**Model Answer for Question A:**

**(a) Major Alternative Fuels (7 marks):**

**1. Compressed Natural Gas (CNG):**
- *Advantages:* High octane (~120), lower CO₂ emissions (~25% less than petrol), cheaper per km, lower fire hazard due to high flash point.
- *Disadvantages:* Low volumetric energy density → large cylinders, limited range; high-pressure storage (200-250 bar); reduced boot space.
- *Engine Compatibility:* Modified SI engines (CNG kit) or dedicated OEM-CNG vehicles.

**2. Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG):**
- *Advantages:* Higher energy density than CNG, clean burning, easy availability.
- *Disadvantages:* Heavier than air → hazardous in closed garages; vapour lock risk in hot climates.
- *Engine Compatibility:* Aftermarket LPG kits (vaporizer + mixer) or factory-fitted.

**3. Biodiesel (FAME — Fatty Acid Methyl Ester):**
- *Advantages:* Renewable, ~50% lower particulate emissions, biodegradable, high cetane (~55).
- *Disadvantages:* Higher viscosity → cold-flow issues, attacks rubber hoses (needs FKM seals), food-vs-fuel debate.
- *Engine Compatibility:* B20 blend (20% biodiesel + 80% diesel) in unmodified CI engines.

**4. Ethanol (E85 / E100):**
- *Advantages:* High octane (~110), renewable from sugarcane/corn, lower CO₂ lifecycle emissions.
- *Disadvantages:* Lower CV (~27 MJ/kg) → ~30% lower mileage; hygroscopic (absorbs water); corrodes aluminium and rubber components.
- *Engine Compatibility:* Flex-fuel vehicles (FFVs) with alcohol-resistant fuel system components.

**5. Hydrogen (H₂ ICE / FCEV):**
- *Advantages:* Zero CO₂ tailpipe emissions, highest CV (~120 MJ/kg), abundant source.
- *Disadvantages:* High production cost (mostly from natural gas reforming), bulky 700-bar tanks, NOₓ formation at high loads.
- *Engine Compatibility:* Hydrogen ICE (modified SI) or Proton-Exchange-Membrane Fuel Cells (PEMFC) for FCEVs.

---

**(b) Fuel Supply System — MPFI vs. CRDi (7 marks):**

**MPFI (Multi-Point Fuel Injection) — Petrol Engine:**
- Fuel is injected **individually** into each cylinder's intake port (one injector per cylinder).
- Uses an **electrical fuel pump** delivering fuel at 3-4 bar to a **fuel rail**.
- The **ECU** controls injector pulse width based on MAP, MAF, crank position, and lambda sensor signals.
- **Injector** sprays atomized fuel onto the back of the intake valve.
- A **fuel pressure regulator** maintains rail pressure relative to manifold vacuum.

**Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDi) — Diesel Engine:**
- Fuel is injected **directly into the combustion chamber** at very high pressures (**1600-2000 bar**).
- A **rotary or inline high-pressure pump** pressurizes fuel, supplied by a transfer pump from the tank.
- Fuel is stored in a **common rail** that feeds all injectors simultaneously.
- **Piezo-electric solenoid injectors** (Bosch) perform **multiple injections per cycle**: pilot, main, and after-injections to reduce NOₓ and PM.
- Controlled electronically by the **EDC (Electronic Diesel Control) unit** via the CAN bus.

**Comparison Table:**

| **Feature** | **MPFI** | **CRDi** |
|---|---|---|
| Injection point | Intake port | Direct into cylinder |
| Injection pressure | 3 – 4 bar | 1600 – 2000 bar |
| Number of injections per cycle | 1 (continuous) | Up to 9 (pilot + main + after) |
| Atomization | Excellent (multi-hole) | Excellent (7-9 hole nozzles) |
| Typical fuel | Petrol | Diesel |
| Emission control | Three-way catalyst | EGR + DPF + SCR |

---

### 5.3 Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> *(a)* Derive the **stoichiometric air-fuel ratio** for **methane (CH₄)** and **iso-octane (C₈H₁₈)**. Comment on the **significance** of these values in modern engine calibration. (7 marks)
> *(b)* Explain the **Well-to-Wheel (WTW) efficiency** concept. Compare the WTW efficiency of **petrol ICE**, **hybrid electric vehicle (HEV)**, and **battery electric vehicle (BEV)** with neat block diagrams. (7 marks)

**Model Answer for Question B:**

**(a) Stoichiometric A/F Derivation (7 marks):**

For **Methane (CH₄)**:
The balanced reaction is:

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\left(\text{O}_2 + 3.76\,\text{N}_2\right) \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 7.52\,\text{N}_2$$

- Moles of air required = $2 \times (1 + 3.76) = 9.52$ kmol per kmol fuel.
- Mass of air = $9.52 \times 29 = 276.08$ kg.
- Mass of methane = $12 + 4 = 16$ kg.

$$\left(\dfrac{A}{F}\right)_{stoich} = \dfrac{276.08}{16} = 17.26$$

For **Iso-octane (C₈H₁₈):**
The balanced reaction is:

$$\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + 12.5\left(\text{O}_2 + 3.76\,\text{N}_2\right) \longrightarrow 8\,\text{CO}_2 + 9\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 47\,\text{N}_2$$

- Moles of air required = $12.5 \times 4.76 = 59.5$ kmol per kmol fuel.
- Mass of air = $59.5 \times 29 = 1725.5$ kg.
- Mass of iso-octane = $(8 \times 12) + 18 = 114$ kg.

$$\left(\dfrac{A}{F}\right)_{stoich} = \dfrac{1725.5}{114} = 15.14$$

**Significance in Engine Calibration:**
1. The stoichiometric A/F ratio is the **reference value** for the ECU's closed-loop lambda control ($\lambda = 1$).
2. CNG requires **~17% more air** than petrol to burn stoichiometrically — hence CNG vehicles use **larger injector orifices** or **longer injection pulses** to deliver the required mass of fuel.
3. The lambda sensor (oxygen sensor) output oscillates around the stoichiometric point, and the ECU uses this signal for **closed-loop trim**, ensuring the **three-way catalytic converter** (TWC) reduces CO, HC, and NOₓ simultaneously with > 98% efficiency.
4. Lean-burn engines (e.g., some CNG and HCCI engines) operate at $\lambda > 1$ for better fuel economy, but require **NOₓ trap catalysts** because the TWC is inefficient in lean conditions.

---

**(b) Well-to-Wheel (WTW) Efficiency (7 marks):**

**Definition:** Well-to-Wheel (WTW) efficiency is the **overall energy efficiency** of a fuel/vehicle system, accounting for both **upstream (well-to-tank, WTT)** energy losses in fuel extraction, refining, and distribution, AND **downstream (tank-to-wheel, TTW)** losses in the vehicle's powertrain.

$$ \eta_{WTW} = \eta_{WTT} \times \eta_{TTW} $$

**Block Diagram — Energy Flow Stages:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeWT1[Oil Well] --> nodeWT2[Refinery]
    nodeWT2 --> nodeWT3[Fuel Pump Station]
    nodeWT3 --> nodeWT4[Fuel Tank]
    nodeWT4 --> nodeWT5[Engine or Motor]
    nodeWT5 --> nodeWT6[Wheels]
```

For **Petrol ICE:**
- WTT (extraction + refining + transport) ≈ **80–85%**
- TTW (engine + drivetrain) ≈ **20–25%**
- **WTW efficiency ≈ 16–21%**

For **Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV):**
- WTT (same petrol pipeline) ≈ **80–85%**
- TTW (engine + electric motor + regenerative braking) ≈ **35–40%**
- **WTW efficiency ≈ 28–34%**

For **Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV):**
- WTT (coal/renewable electricity generation + grid losses + battery charging) ≈ **40–60%**
- TTW (electric motor + battery + drivetrain) ≈ **85–90%**
- **WTW efficiency ≈ 35–55%**

**Conclusion:**
BEVs are most efficient when powered by a **renewable-heavy grid** (solar/wind/hydro), reaching WTW efficiency > 60%. HEVs offer a **bridge technology** with up to ~40% improvement over conventional ICE without charging infrastructure.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 3.76 factor:** When computing moles of air, students often use only O₂ moles instead of (O₂ + 3.76 N₂). Always use $4.76 \times n_{O_2}$ to get the total air moles.
> 2. **Confusing Octane and Cetane:** Octane = knock resistance for SI; Cetane = ignition quality for CI. Mixing these up costs at least 1-2 marks.
> 3. **Not stating assumptions in derivations:** Always write *"Assuming complete combustion with stoichiometric air at 21% O₂ and 79% N₂ by volume"*.
> 4. **Skipping units:** Every numerical answer MUST carry the correct unit (kg/kg, MJ/kg, bar, °C). KTU evaluators deduct 0.5 marks for missing units consistently.
> 5. **Vague fuel comparisons:** Writing *"CNG is better than petrol"* without quantitative values (CV, ON, A/F, CO₂) fetches zero marks. Always back claims with numbers.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **The 60-second Recall Sheet for "Fuel Choices":**

- **Stoichiometric A/F (must memorize):** Petrol 14.7 | Diesel 14.5 | CNG 17.2 | LPG 15.6 | Ethanol 9.0 | Hydrogen 34.5 | Biodiesel 13.8.
- **Calorific Values (must memorize):** Petrol 44 | Diesel 45 | CNG 50 | Hydrogen 120 | Ethanol 27 MJ/kg.
- **Octane Number** = knock resistance for SI; **Cetane Number** = ignition quality for CI.
- **Petrol** is rated by **RON (Research Octane Number)**; reference: iso-octane (100), n-heptane (0).
- **Diesel** is rated by **Cetane Number**; reference: n-cetyl (100), α-methyl naphthalene (0).
- **CNG** is stored at **200–250 bar**; **LPG** is stored as a liquid under moderate pressure; **Hydrogen** is stored at **700 bar** or as liquid H₂ at **−253°C**.
- **Alternative Fuels** are mandated under India's **SATAT scheme** (Sustainable Alternative Towards Affordable Transportation) for compressed biogas.
- **India's ethanol blending target**: **E20 (20% ethanol)** is the policy goal by 2025 (already achieved in many states).
- **Well-to-Wheel efficiency ranking (best to worst, renewable-grid scenario):** BEV (up to 70%) > FCEV (~50%) > HEV (~40%) > Diesel ICE (~30%) > Petrol ICE (~20%).
- **Lambda (λ)**: $\lambda = (A/F)_{actual} / (A/F)_{stoich}$ — used by ECU for closed-loop fuel trim.
- **Three-Way Catalyst (TWC)** operates efficiently ONLY at $\lambda = 1 \pm 0.01$.
- **BS-VI norms** (India) and **Euro 6 / Euro 7** (EU) drive the modern shift toward electrified and hydrogen powertrains.
- **Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDi)** operates at **1600–2000 bar** with multi-pulse injection (pilot + main + after).
- **Hybrid Powertrains** are classified as: Micro, Mild, Full, Plug-in Hybrid (PHEV), and Range-Extended EV (REEV).
- **Hydrogen ICE** is NOT a fuel cell — it burns H₂ in a modified SI engine; FCEV uses a PEMFC stack with electric drive.
- **Safety Metric**: Diesel > Biodiesel > LPG > CNG > Petrol (in terms of flash point; higher = safer to handle).
- **CO₂ Emission Index (g/MJ)**: Hydrogen = 0 (tailpipe) | CNG = 56 | Ethanol = 70 | Petrol = 73 | Diesel = 74.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET414 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Fuel Processing # ⚙️ MODULE 4: FUEL PROCESSING FOR AUTOMOTIVE APPLICATIONS ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fuel Processing** is defined as the integrated set of thermochemical and catalytic unit operations that convert a primary hydrocarbon or oxygenated fuel (gasoline, diesel, methanol, ethanol, natural gas, LPG, or biogas) into a hydrogen-rich gas mixture (reformate or syngas) of controlled purity, pressure, and temperature, suitable for downstream energy conversion devices such as **Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM)** or **Solid Oxide Fuel Cells (SOFC)**, and in some cases, for advanced internal combustion strategies. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, fuel processing forms the **upstream chemical engineering bridge** between the vehicle's fuel tank and the electrochemical/hybrid power plant. It is mandatory for fuel-cell electric vehicles (FCEV) and is gaining importance in dual-fuel HCCI/RCCI engines where reformed gas (RG) is used as a low-reactivity pilot fuel. ### 1.2 The Three Functional Blocks of an Automotive Fuel Processor | Block | Function | Typical Temperature | |---|---|---| | **Reformer** | Converts hydrocarbons → H₂ + CO + CO₂ | 600 °C – 900 °C | | **Water-Gas Shift (WGS) Reactor** | Converts CO → CO₂ + additional H₂ | 200 °C – 500 °C (two-stage) | | **Preferential Oxidation (PROX) / CO Cleanup** | Reduces CO to < 10–50 ppm | 80 °C – 200 °C | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The "Kitchen Blender" View:** Imagine your fuel tank is a *whole fruit basket* (diesel, gasoline, ethanol). Your fuel cell is a *finicky blender* that only accepts smooth, sweet mango pulp (pure hydrogen). **Fuel processing is the kitchen assistant** who picks up the basket, peels the fruit, slices it, cooks it, strains the seeds, and sweetens the juice — finally handing a uniform mango pulp to the blender. Without the assistant, the blender either refuses to run or chokes up. The *peeler* is the **desulfurizer**, the *cooker* is the **reformer**, the *strainer* is the **WGS reactor**, and the *final polish* is the **PROX unit**. ### 1.4 Why On-Board Fuel Processing is Critical - **Hydrogen Storage Bottleneck:** On-board H₂ storage at 350 bar or 700 bar requires bulky composite tanks (≈ 4–7 kg H₂ for 500 km range). Storing the parent fuel (e.g., gasoline) gives **~5–7× higher volumetric energy density**. - **Infrastructure Compatibility:** Existing petrol/diesel pumps can be reused. - **Reformer Efficiency:** Modern catalytic partial-oxidation units reach **> 85 % thermal efficiency** with sub-second start-up. - **Emission Synergy:** Coupled with PEM fuel cell, the tailpipe emits only **H₂O** (zero CO₂ if bio-feedstock is used). ### 1.5 Key Performance Metrics in Fuel Processing > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Reformer Thermal Efficiency (η_th):** Ratio of LHV of H₂ produced to LHV of fuel input. Typical value: **70 % – 90 %**. > - **H₂ Yield:** Moles of H₂ produced per mole of fuel (CH₄ → 3 mol H₂ via SR; gasoline C₈H₁₈ → ≈ 17 mol H₂ theoretical). > - **CO Tolerance of PEMFC:** **< 10 ppm** (Pt anode poisoned at > 50 ppm). > - **Cold-Start Time:** Industry benchmark for automotive reformer = **< 60 s** to 50 % rated H₂ output. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Principal Reforming Chemistries #### A. Steam Reforming (SR) — Endothermic The most mature, highest H₂-yield pathway. Operates at **S/C (Steam-to-Carbon) ratio = 2–3**. **General reaction:** $$C_nH_mO_p + (n-p)H_2O \longrightarrow nCO + \left(\frac{m}{2} + n - p\right)H_2$$ **For methane (simplest case):** $$CH_4 + H_2O \longrightarrow CO + 3H_2 \quad \Delta H_{298} = +206\ \text{kJ/mol}$$ **Why endothermic?** The C–H bond in CH₄ (≈ 413 kJ/mol) must be broken; the O–H bonds reformed are weaker, demanding net heat input. The H₂-to-CO ratio is **3:1**, which is the highest of all reforming routes — ideal when maximum H₂ is desired. **Catalyst:** Nickel on α-Al₂O₃ or promoted with Rh, Pt, or Ru for higher activity. Sulfur poisons Ni instantly; tolerance < **0.1 ppm S**. #### B. Partial Oxidation (POX) — Exothermic Uses a sub-stoichiometric oxygen supply (O/C ≈ 0.5–0.7). **General reaction:** $$C_nH_mO_p + \frac{(n-p)}{2}O_2 \longrightarrow nCO + \frac{m}{2}H_2$$ **For methane:** $$CH_4 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \longrightarrow CO + 2H_2 \quad \Delta H_{298} = -36\ \text{kJ/mol}$$ **Why exothermic?** Partial combustion releases more energy than is needed to break the C–H bonds. H₂-to-CO ratio is **2:1** (lower than SR), but the response time is **< 1 s** — perfect for dynamic automotive transients. **Catalyst:** Rh, Pt, or Pd on ceria-zirconia; capable of auto-thermal operation. #### C. Autothermal Reforming (ATR) — Thermally Neutral A clever hybrid: combines endothermic SR with exothermic POX in one reactor, **balancing ΔH ≈ 0**. **Net reaction (CH₄):** $$CH_4 + x\,H_2O + y\,O_2 \longrightarrow CO + 2H_2 \text{ (excess)} \quad \text{with } y \approx 0.4x \text{ for } \Delta H \approx 0$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** ATR is the **preferred choice for automotive applications** because it eliminates the need for external heat exchangers — the heat from POX drives the SR reaction in the same catalyst bed. Cold-start is faster than SR; H₂ yield is higher than POX. ### 2.2 Water-Gas Shift (WGS) Reactor After the reformer, the gas contains 8–12 mol % CO. PEM fuel cells cannot tolerate this. The WGS reaction converts CO to additional H₂: $$CO + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO_2 + H_2 \quad \Delta H_{298} = -41\ \text{kJ/mol}$$ **Le Chatelier consideration:** The reaction is mildly exothermic and equilibrium-limited at high temperature. Therefore, **two-stage WGS** is universally used: | Stage | Catalyst | Temperature | CO Outlet | |---|---|---|---| | **High-Temperature Shift (HTS)** | Fe₃O₄/Cr₂O₃ | 310 °C – 450 °C | 3 – 5 % | | **Low-Temperature Shift (LTS)** | Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ | 190 °C – 250 °C | 0.1 – 1 % | ### 2.3 Preferential Oxidation (PROX) The final cleanup step. Controlled O₂ injection oxidizes residual CO **without** consuming too much H₂: $$CO + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 \quad \Delta H = -283\ \text{kJ/mol}$$ The competing (undesired) reaction: $$H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \longrightarrow H_2O \quad \Delta H = -242\ \text{kJ/mol}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Selectivity (S)** is the key PROX metric. Modern Pt–CeO₂ or Au/Fe₂O₃ catalysts achieve **S = 40 – 60 %** at 80 °C–150 °C, with O₂/CO = 1–2, dropping CO from ~1 % to **< 10 ppm**. ### 2.4 Desulfurization — The Critical Front-End Sulfur compounds (thiophenes, benzothiophenes, H₂S in natural gas) **permanently poison** reformer (Ni) and PROX (Pt/Au) catalysts. Removal is mandatory: - **Activated Carbon Adsorption:** Effective for H₂S; removes to < 50 ppb. - **Hydrodesulfurization (HDS):** Co–Mo or Ni–Mo catalysts at 300–400 °C convert organic S → H₂S, then ZnO trap (ZnO + H₂S → ZnS + H₂O) at 350 °C. - **Sulfur Tolerance Target:** **< 0.05 ppm** for Ni reforming catalyst; < **0.5 ppm** for Rh-based ATR. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Reformer Thermal Efficiency** | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{H_2}\cdot LHV_{H_2}}{\dot{m}_{fuel}\cdot LHV_{fuel}}$ | Dimensionless (0–1); 70 % – 90 % typical | | 2 | **Steam-to-Carbon Ratio (S/C)** | $S/C = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{H_2O}}{\dot{n}_{C_{in fuel}}}$ | Molar ratio; optimum 2.5–3.0 for SR | | 3 | **Oxygen-to-Carbon Ratio (O/C)** | $O/C = \dfrac{2\dot{n}_{O_2}}{\dot{n}_{C_{in fuel}}}$ | 0.5 for POX; 0.6–0.7 for ATR | | 4 | **Hydrogen Yield (SR of CH₄)** | $Y_{H_2} = 3\ \text{mol}\ H_2/\text{mol}\ CH_4$ | Maximum by stoichiometry | | 5 | **WGS Equilibrium Constant** | $K_{eq} = \dfrac{[CO_2][H_2]}{[CO][H_2O]}$ | Decreases with T (exothermic) | | 6 | **PROX Selectivity** | $S = \dfrac{0.5 \cdot \Delta[CO]}{\Delta[O_2]}$ | 40 % – 60 % | | 7 | **PEM CO Tolerance** | $[CO]_{anode} < 10\ \text{ppm}$ | Air bleed ≈ 1 – 2 % raises limit to 50 ppm | | 8 | **Reformer Start-up Energy** | $E_{su} = m_{cat} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Target < 1 MJ for 60 s cold-start | | 9 | **Volumetric H₂ Density of Reformer** | $\rho_{v,H_2} \approx 1.5\text{–}2.5\ \text{kWh/L}$ | 5–7× > 700 bar H₂ tank | | 10 | **LHV Constants** | H₂ = 120 MJ/kg; CH₄ = 50 MJ/kg; C₈H₁₈ = 44 MJ/kg | Use LHV (water vapor) for engine calcs | ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Toyota Mirai (2014–):** Uses **on-board H₂ storage at 70 MPa**; however, research-grade FCEVs from GM and Nissan have demonstrated **on-board gasoline reformers** (e.g., GM "HydroGen3" with Autothermal Reformer). - **Auxiliary Power Units (APU):** Trucks and military vehicles use diesel reformers + SOFC stacks for **1–10 kW** hotel-load power. - **HCCI/RCCI Engines:** Reformed gas (RG) injected as a low-reactivity fuel to control auto-ignition, enabling **premixed low-temperature combustion** and **> 60 % BTE**. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — Theoretical H₂ Yield from a Generic Fuel CₙHₘOₚ via Steam Reforming **Starting equation (general SR):** $$C_nH_mO_p + (n-p)H_2O \longrightarrow nCO + \left(\frac{m}{2} + n - p\right)H_2$$ **Step 1 — Express the H₂ yield per mole of fuel:** $$Y_{H_2} = \frac{m}{2} + n - p \quad [\text{mol}\ H_2/\text{mol fuel}]$$ **Step 2 — Follow-up WGS in same train** converts each mole of CO into one more mole of H₂: $$CO + H_2O \longrightarrow CO_2 + H_2$$ So the **overall H₂ yield including WGS** becomes: $$Y_{H_2}^{total} = \left(\frac{m}{2} + n - p\right) + n = \frac{m}{2} + 2n - p$$ **Step 3 — Apply to methane (n=1, m=4, p=0):** $$\begin{aligned} Y_{H_2}^{SR} &= \frac{4}{2} + 1 - 0 = 3\ \text{mol}\ H_2 \\ Y_{H_2}^{total} &= \frac{4}{2} + 2(1) - 0 = 4\ \text{mol}\ H_2 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Apply to gasoline surrogate iso-octane C₈H₁₈ (n=8, m=18, p=0):** $$\begin{aligned} Y_{H_2}^{SR} &= \frac{18}{2} + 8 - 0 = 17\ \text{mol}\ H_2 \\ Y_{H_2}^{total} &= \frac{18}{2} + 16 - 0 = 25\ \text{mol}\ H_2 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Mass-based yield and reformer efficiency:** $$\eta_{th} = \frac{\dot{n}_{H_2} \cdot LHV_{H_2}}{\dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot LHV_{fuel}}$$ For 1 kg/h of C₈H₁₈ with 25 mol H₂ produced: $$\begin{aligned} \dot{n}_{fuel} &= \frac{1000}{114.23} = 8.755\ \text{mol/h} \\ \dot{n}_{H_2} &= 8.755 \times 25 = 218.9\ \text{mol/h} = 0.441\ \text{kg/h} \\ \eta_{th} &= \frac{0.441 \times 120}{1 \times 44.3} = 1.194\ (\text{= 119.4 %}) \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Apparent paradox explained:** Efficiency > 100 % is normal here because the *water steam* contributes mass-energy via the WGS reaction — this is the **"Hydrogen Boost"** that makes SR superior to POX for maximum H₂ recovery. ### 3.2 Derivation — Reformer Cold-Start Energy Budget **Problem:** A catalyst bed of mass **m_cat = 2 kg** with specific heat **c_p = 1000 J/(kg·K)** must be heated from **T_amb = 25 °C** to operating temperature **T_op = 750 °C** in **Δt = 60 s**. **Step 1 — Sensible heat required:** $$Q_{sens} = m_{cat} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{op} - T_{amb})$$ **Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:** $$Q_{sens} = 2 \times 1000 \times (750 - 25) = 1.45 \times 10^6\ \text{J} = 1.45\ \text{MJ}$$ **Step 3 — Required heating power:** $$P_{req} = \frac{Q_{sens}}{\Delta t} = \frac{1.45 \times 10^6}{60} \approx 24.2\ \text{kW}$$ **Step 4 — Implication for vehicle electrical architecture:** A **24 V bus** would draw **> 1000 A** — impossible. Therefore, automotive reformers must use **stored chemical energy** (start-up burner) or **rapid exothermic POX light-off**, not electrical heaters. This is why **ATR** dominates auto applications. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing a Reforming Route | Criterion | Steam Reforming (SR) | Partial Oxidation (POX) | Autothermal (ATR) | |---|---|---|---| | **Heat of reaction** | Endothermic (+206 kJ/mol CH₄) | Exothermic (−36 kJ/mol) | Near-zero ΔH | | **H₂/CO ratio** | **3 : 1** | 2 : 1 | 2 : 2.5 (tunable) | | **Cold-start time** | Slow (2–5 min) | **Fast (< 1 s)** | Medium (30–60 s) | | **External heat required?** | **Yes** | No | No | | **H₂ yield** | **Highest** | Lowest | Medium-High | | **Sulfur tolerance** | Very poor | Poor | Improved with Rh | | **Catalyst cost** | Low (Ni) | High (Rh, Pt) | High (Rh-Pt) | | **Best automotive use** | Stationary APU | Quick light-off | **On-board vehicle reformer** | ### 3.4 Derivation — CO Removal by PROX **Initial CO** from LTS WGS = **1 mol %** = 10,000 ppm. **Target** = 10 ppm. **Step 1 — Required conversion:** $$X_{CO} = \frac{10000 - 10}{10000} = 0.999 \equiv 99.9\ \%$$ **Step 2 — With selectivity S = 50 %, oxygen stoichiometry:** $$O_2\text{ needed} = \frac{1}{S} = 2\ \text{mol}\ O_2 / \text{mol}\ CO\text{ converted}$$ **Step 3 — H₂ loss (parasitic):** $$H_2\text{ loss} = \frac{(1/S) - 0.5}{0.5} = 3\ \text{mol}\ H_2\ \text{per mol}\ CO$$ **Step 4 — Practical impact:** If reformer produces 200 kmol/h H₂, PROX loses ≈ 0.5 kmol/h — small but non-zero. Alternative: **membrane separator (Pd-Ag)** or **Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA)** for > 99.99 % purity, used in stationary plants only. --- ## 4.
