KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]** **Define fuel processing. List the three main functional blocks used in an on-board automotive fuel processor.** **Model Answer:** Fuel processing is the integrated set of thermochemical and catalytic operations that convert a primary fuel (gasoline, diesel, methanol, etc.) into a hydrogen-rich reformate suitable for fuel cells or advanced combustion systems. The three main functional blocks are: 1. **Reformer** (SR / POX / ATR) — converts hydrocarbons into H₂ + CO + CO₂ at 600–900 °C. 2. **Water-Gas Shift (WGS) Reactor** — converts CO to additional H₂ using a two-stage HTS + LTS configuration. 3. **Preferential Oxidation (PROX) Unit** — reduces CO to < 10 ppm to meet PEMFC anode tolerance. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]** **Compare Steam Reforming (SR) and Partial Oxidation (POX) in terms of heat of reaction, H₂/CO ratio, and cold-start time.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Steam Reforming (SR) | Partial Oxidation (POX) | |---|---|---| | Heat of reaction | Endothermic (ΔH = +206 kJ/mol for CH₄) | Exothermic (ΔH = −36 kJ/mol for CH₄) | | H₂/CO ratio | **3 : 1** (highest) | 2 : 1 | | Cold-start time | 2–5 minutes (slow) | **< 1 second** (fast) | | External heat | Required | Not required | | Best application | Stationary / APU | Quick dynamic light-off | --- ### 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Never write ΔH in units of J instead of kJ** — KTU examiners deduct ½ mark. > 2. **Always mention S/C and O/C ratios** for SR and POX respectively — most students forget the steam/oxygen requirement. > 3. **Draw a labeled block diagram** — a question on reformer without a diagram loses 2–3 marks easily. > 4. **CO tolerance for PEMFC must be < 10 ppm**, not < 100 ppm. Mixing this up is a 1-mark deduction. > 5. **Avoid vague terms** like "the catalyst works" — specify *which catalyst* (Ni, Rh, Pt/CeO₂) and *temperature window*. --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Option 1** **(a) [7 Marks] Explain the working of an Autothermal Reformer (ATR) for on-board hydrogen generation in FCEVs. With neat sketches, discuss the reaction chemistry, S/C and O/C ratios, and the role of the catalyst.** **Model Solution:** **Introduction (1 mark):** An Autothermal Reformer (ATR) is a hybrid reformer that combines Steam Reforming (endothermic) and Partial Oxidation (exothermic) in a single catalytic reactor, achieving thermal neutrality (ΔH ≈ 0). It is the preferred on-board reformer for automotive applications due to its fast light-off and high H₂ yield. **Reaction Chemistry (2 marks):** The overall reaction for methane-based ATR is a combination of: $$CH_4 + H_2O \longrightarrow CO + 3H_2 \quad \text{(SR — endothermic)}$$ $$CH_4 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \longrightarrow CO + 2H_2 \quad \text{(POX — exothermic)}$$ **Combined net reaction:** $$CH_4 + 0.6H_2O + 0.3O_2 \longrightarrow CO + 2.3H_2 \quad \Delta H \approx 0$$ **Operating Ratios (2 marks):** - **S/C ratio = 0.6** (lower than pure SR's 2.5; saves water/heat) - **O/C ratio = 0.3** (sub-stoichiometric — only partial combustion) - Operating temperature: **600 °C – 850 °C** - Pressure: **1 – 5 atm** (atmospheric preferred for mobile) **Catalyst & Reactor (1.5 marks):** - **Catalyst:** Rhodium (Rh) on ceria-zirconia (CeO₂–ZrO₂) washcoat over a cordierite monolith. Rh is preferred over Ni because of superior sulfur tolerance and resistance to carbon coking. - **Reactor:** Staged monolith — POX zone at the inlet (light-off in < 1 s), SR zone downstream (uses POX heat to drive steam reaction). - **Carbon Formation:** Suppressed because O₂ continuously oxidizes any C deposits. **Block Diagram (0.5 mark):** Refer to Section 4.1 — Monolithic staged reactor cross-section. **(b) [7 Marks] With reference to a 5 kW on-board ATR-based fuel processor for gasoline (iso-octane C₈H₁₈), calculate:** **(i) The theoretical H₂ yield per kg of fuel.** **(ii) The thermal efficiency of the reformer.** **(iii) The CO cleanup requirement if the WGS outlet contains 0.5 mol % CO and the downstream PEMFC requires < 10 ppm.** **Model Solution:** **(i) Theoretical H₂ Yield (3 marks):** For C₈H₁₈ → n = 8, m = 18, p = 0. $$Y_{H_2}^{SR} = \frac{m}{2} + n - p = \frac{18}{2} + 8 = 17\ \text{mol}\ H_2$$ $$Y_{H_2}^{total} = \frac{m}{2} + 2n - p = 9 + 16 = 25\ \text{mol}\ H_2 \text{ (incl. WGS)}$$ **Per kg of iso-octane (M = 114.23 g/mol):** $$Y_{H_2}/\text{kg} = \frac{25 \times 1000}{114.23} = 218.9\ \text{mol/kg}$$ **Mass basis:** $$m_{H_2} = 218.9 \times 2.016 = 441.3\ \text{g} = 0.441\ \text{kg}\ H_2/\text{kg fuel}$$ **(ii) Thermal Efficiency (2 marks):** $$\eta_{th} = \frac{m_{H_2} \times LHV_{H_2}}{m_{fuel} \times LHV_{fuel}}$$ $$\eta_{th} = \frac{0.441 \times 120}{1 \times 44.3} = \frac{52.92}{44.3} = 1.194\ (119.4\%)$$ > **Explanatory note (½ mark):** Efficiency > 100 % because the steam/water injected is also a hydrogen carrier — the LHV of the *original fuel alone* is an under-estimate. This is the **hydrogen boost effect** of SR+WGS systems. **(iii) CO Cleanup (2 marks):** Required conversion: $$X_{CO} = \frac{5000\ \text{ppm} - 10\ \text{ppm}}{5000\ \text{ppm}} = 0.998 = 99.8\%$$ This is achieved by **two-stage WGS (HTS → LTS)** bringing CO from reformer outlet (~10 %) to ~0.5 %, followed by a **PROX reactor** taking it from 5000 ppm to < 10 ppm — a 99.8 % reduction. Selectivity S = 50 % implies O₂/CO = 2, with parasitic H₂ loss of ≈ 3 mol H₂ per mol CO oxidized. --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Option 2** **(a) [7 Marks] Discuss the role of the Water-Gas Shift (WGS) reactor in an automotive fuel processor. Why is a two-stage configuration (HTS + LTS) used? Provide the reaction kinetics, equilibrium behavior, and typical catalyst systems.** **Model Solution:** **Role of WGS (1 mark):** The WGS reactor is the second major block in the fuel processor. It converts the **8–12 mol % CO** present in the raw reformate into additional H₂ and CO₂, simultaneously (a) increasing H₂ yield by 30–50 % and (b) reducing the CO load on the downstream PROX unit. **Reaction and Thermodynamics (2 marks):** $$CO + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO_2 + H_2 \quad \Delta H_{298} = -41\ \text{kJ/mol}$$ The reaction is **mildly exothermic and equilibrium-limited**. By Le Chatelier's principle, lower temperatures shift the equilibrium to the right, achieving higher CO conversion. The equilibrium constant drops from **K ≈ 100 at 250 °C** to **K ≈ 5 at 600 °C** — a factor of 20 in 350 °C. **Kinetics (1.5 marks):** - The forward reaction is kinetically favored at high T (fast rate constant). - The reverse reaction is favored at low T. - A single-stage reactor cannot optimize both — hence the **two-stage (HTS + LTS) design**. **Two-Stage Configuration (1.5 marks):** | Stage | Catalyst | Temperature | CO Outlet | |---|---|---|---| | **HTS** | Fe₃O₄ / Cr₂O₃ | 350 °C – 450 °C | 3 – 5 % | | **LTS** | Cu / ZnO / Al₂O₃ | 200 °C – 250 °C | 0.1 – 1 % | - **HTS** exploits high reaction rate (kinetic control) to drop CO rapidly. - **LTS** exploits high equilibrium conversion at low T to push CO further down. - **Inter-stage cooling** is needed — typically achieved by injecting cold steam or using a heat exchanger. **Why not a single stage?** (1 mark): A single stage at 300 °C with Cu catalyst would be slow initially (low rate); at 500 °C with Fe catalyst, equilibrium prevents CO dropping below 2 %. The two-stage approach decouples *rate* and *equilibrium* limitations — a textbook example of **process intensification** in KTU parlance. --- **(b) [7 Marks] A natural gas reformer uses steam reforming with S/C = 3.0. The reformer operates at 800 °C and 1 atm. Calculate:** **(i) The dry reformate composition (mol %) at reformer exit assuming equilibrium CO conversion.** **(ii) The H₂ yield per m³ of natural gas (at NTP).** **(iii) Comment on the impact of pressure on H₂ yield — why are SR reactors run at low pressure despite the kinetics favoring high pressure?** **Model Solution:** **(i) Reformate Composition (3 marks):** For CH₄ with S/C = 3.0, basis = 1 mol CH₄ + 3 mol H₂O: Reformer outlet (assume full CH₄ conversion and 90 % WGS-equivalent CO shift at 800 °C): - CO produced: 1 mol - H₂ from SR: 3 mol - Additional H₂ from WGS: 1 mol - CO₂: 1 mol - Unreacted H₂O: 2 mol (since S/C = 3.0) **Dry reformate** (excluding H₂O): Total dry moles = 1 (CO) + 4 (H₂) + 1 (CO₂) = 6 mol $$\begin{aligned} \text{H}_2 &= \frac{4}{6} \times 100 = 66.7\ \% \\ \text{CO} &= \frac{1}{6} \times 100 = 16.7\ \% \\ \text{CO}_2 &= \frac{1}{6} \times 100 = 16.7\ \% \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) H₂ Yield (2 marks):** At NTP, 1 mol = 22.414 L = 0.022414 m³. 1 m³ of CH₄ = 44.6 mol. $$n_{H_2} = 4 \times 44.6 = 178.4\ \text{mol} = 4.0\ \text{m}^3\ H_2 \text{ per m}^3\ CH_4$$ **(iii) Pressure Impact (2 marks):** The SR reaction: $$CH_4 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO + 3H_2$$ shows a **mole increase** (2 → 4 moles). By Le Chatelier, **higher pressure shifts equilibrium to the left**, reducing CH₄ conversion and H₂ yield. Therefore, low pressure (1–5 atm) is preferred. **Trade-off:** Kinetics favor high P (faster surface reactions, smaller reactor), but thermodynamics favor low P (higher equilibrium conversion). The compromise is **moderate pressure (10–30 atm)** in industrial plants, but for **on-board automotive** applications, **near-atmospheric** operation is chosen to maximize H₂ yield and to match PEMFC inlet requirements. --- ### 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **🔑 Critical Points to Remember (Module 4 — Fuel Processing):** > > ✅ **Fuel processing = Desulfurization + Reforming + WGS + PROX** (4 sequential blocks). > > ✅ **SR** = endothermic, H₂/CO = 3:1, high yield, slow start, needs external heat. > > ✅ **POX** = exothermic, H₂/CO = 2:1, fast (< 1 s) start, no external heat. > > ✅ **ATR** = thermal neutrality, combines SR + POX, **preferred for automotive**. > > ✅ **WGS** is mildly exothermic, two-stage (HTS Fe/Cr + LTS Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃) to optimize rate AND equilibrium. > > ✅ **PROX selectivity** = 40 – 60 %; takes CO from ~1 % to < 10 ppm for PEMFC. > > ✅ **PEMFC CO tolerance** = **< 10 ppm** (or < 50 ppm with 1–2 % air bleed). > > ✅ **Sulfur** permanently poisons Ni and Rh; **HDS or activated carbon** is mandatory. > > ✅ **Reformer thermal efficiency** of SR can exceed 100 % (apparent) because water contributes energy via WGS. > > ✅ **S/C ratio for SR = 2.5–3.0**; **O/C for POX = 0.5**; **O/C for ATR = 0.3–0.4**. > > ✅ **Cold-start** is the major automotive challenge — ATR is the engineering solution. > > ✅ **On-board reforming** solves the H₂ storage bottleneck — gasoline has **5–7× higher volumetric H₂ energy density** than 70 MPa compressed H₂. > > ✅ **Reformate H₂ purity for PEMFC**: 99.99 % with < 10 ppm CO. > > ✅ **Catalyst hierarchy for SR**: Ni (cheap, S-sensitive) < Rh (expensive, S-tolerant) < Pt-Ru (premium). > > ✅ **High pressure shifts SR equilibrium left** → low pressure preferred for max H₂ yield. > > ✅ **Modern applications**: FCEV APUs, HCCI/RCCI engine reformed gas, range-extender EVs, military tactical power.

topics.title: Fuel Stacks
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Fuel Stacks (Fuel Cell Stacks) — KTU 2024 Premium Study Module

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET414 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fuel Stacks

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Fuel Cell Stack (Fuel Stack):**
> A **Fuel Cell Stack** is an electrochemical energy conversion assembly in which multiple individual fuel cells (typically Proton Exchange Membrane / PEM type) are mechanically and electrically connected in **series** using **bipolar plates** to deliver a usable high-voltage, high-power output suitable for automotive traction applications.

A single PEM fuel cell produces only about **0.6 V – 0.8 V** under load. To power a vehicle traction motor (which needs **300 V – 650 V DC**), hundreds of cells must be stacked — hence the term **"Stack"**.

### 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Single Cell** | One MEA + two bipolar plates = ~0.6 V |
| **Stack** | Many single cells stacked in series |
| **MEA** | Membrane Electrode Assembly (the heart of the cell) |
| **Bipolar Plate** | Conducts current, distributes gases, removes water |
| **GDL** | Gas Diffusion Layer (carbon paper) |
| **PEM** | Proton Exchange Membrane (e.g., Nafion) |
| **Catalyst** | Platinum-based (Pt or Pt-alloy) on C support |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Battery Sandwich Multiplied":**
> Imagine a single fuel cell as a **sandwich**:
> - **Bread (top)** = Cathode bipolar plate (lets air in, takes water out)
> - **Bread (bottom)** = Anode bipolar plate (lets hydrogen in)
> - **Filling** = The MEA — membrane + catalyst layers
> - Inside the MEA, **H₂ splits into H⁺ and e⁻**. The proton goes through the membrane, the electron takes the **external circuit** (your motor), and they re-combine at the cathode with oxygen to form **water**.
> - A **stack** is like **hundreds of these sandwiches bolted together** so their voltages **add up**, just like connecting AA cells in series to make a 12 V battery.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Link:** The **Toyota Mirai (2nd Gen, 2021)** uses a stack of **330 cells** producing **128 kW**. The **Hyundai NEXO** uses a similar architecture. KTU examiners love linking automotive applications to theory!

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Faraday's Constant:** **F = 96,485 C/mol**
- **Universal Gas Constant:** **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **Standard Cell Potential (PEM, H₂/O₂):** **E⁰ = 1.229 V** at 25 °C
- **Typical Operating Temperature (PEM):** **60 °C – 80 °C**
- **Typical Stack Power Density Target:** **> 3.0 kW/L** (DOE 2020 target)
- **Operating Pressure:** **1 – 3 bar** (atmospheric to slightly pressurized)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle of a Single PEM Cell

A PEM fuel cell operates on the **reverse electrolysis** principle. The overall reaction is:

$$ H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat} $$

#### 2.1.1 Half-Cell Reactions (KTU Favourite!)

- **Anode (Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction — HOR):**
$$ H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^- $$

- **Cathode (Oxygen Reduction Reaction — ORR):**
$$ \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O $$

- **Overall:**
$$ H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O \quad (E^0 = 1.229\ \text{V}) $$

#### 2.1.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

1. **H₂ is humidified and fed to the anode channel** → reaches the **anode catalyst** (Pt/C).
2. Pt catalyses the splitting of H₂ into **2H⁺ + 2e⁻**.
3. **Protons (H⁺) migrate** through the **PEM** (only H⁺ passes — electrons are blocked).
4. **Electrons** are forced through the **external circuit** → this is the **current** that powers the motor.
5. At the cathode, **O₂ from air + H⁺ + e⁻ combine** to form **H₂O** (and heat).
6. **Water management** is critical: too much water → floods the cell; too little → membrane dries and resistance rises.

### 2.2 The Need for a Stack — Why Single Cells Are Not Enough

A single cell cannot deliver the high voltage required by automotive traction inverters.

| Vehicle System | Required Voltage | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| 12 V accessories | 12 V | Lighting, ECU |
| Traction inverter (FCEV) | 300 V – 650 V DC | Drives 3-phase AC motor |
| Single PEM cell | 0.6 V – 0.8 V | Too low |

So multiple cells are **stacked in series** using **bipolar plates**, where the **cathode of one cell is electrically and physically connected to the anode of the next**.

### 2.3 Components of a Fuel Cell Stack (KTU High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — KTU asks at least one question on stack components every cycle.**

| Component | Material | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Bipolar Plate** | Graphite / Metal (coated) / Composite | Conducts current cell-to-cell; distributes H₂ and air; removes product water |
| **MEA — Membrane** | Nafion (perfluorinated sulfonic acid) | Allows H⁺ transport; blocks e⁻ and gas crossover |
| **MEA — Anode Catalyst** | Pt or Pt-Ru on carbon | Catalyses H₂ oxidation |
| **MEA — Cathode Catalyst** | Pt or Pt-Co on carbon | Catalyses O₂ reduction (slower — limits performance) |
| **GDL — Gas Diffusion Layer** | Carbon paper / cloth | Distributes gas uniformly; conducts electrons; manages water |
| **Gaskets / Seals** | Silicone, fluoroelastomer | Prevent H₂ and O₂ leakage; isolate coolant |
| **End Plates** | Aluminum / Steel | Provide mechanical compression; hold stack together |
| **Tie Rods / Bolts** | Stainless Steel | Apply uniform clamping force |
| **Cooling Plates** | Metal with internal channels | Remove waste heat |

### 2.4 Stack Architecture — Flow Field Design

Bipolar plates contain **flow channels** that distribute reactants uniformly:

- **Parallel flow field** — simple, low pressure drop, prone to water accumulation
- **Serpentine flow field** — forces gas through long path, good water removal, higher pressure drop
- **Interdigitated flow field** — dead-ended channels force gas through GDL, excellent mass transport
- **Pin-type / Metallic foam** — modern design used by Toyota

> [!NOTE]
> **Automotive Note:** The Toyota Mirai uses a **3D fine-mesh flow field** in metallic bipolar plates — this was a key innovation in 2nd Gen (2021) improving power density by **~20 %**.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are fair-game for KTU numericals and derivations.**

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E^0_{\text{cell}} = 1.229\ \text{V}$ | Standard reversible cell potential (H₂/O₂, 25 °C) | V |
| 2 | $E_{\text{Nernst}} = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{p_{H_2}\cdot p_{O_2}^{1/2}}{p_{H_2O}}\right)$ | Nernst equation (open circuit voltage) | V |
| 3 | $V_{\text{stack}} = N \times V_{\text{cell}}$ | Stack voltage = No. of cells × per-cell voltage | V |
| 4 | $I_{\text{stack}} = I_{\text{cell}}$ | Current same in series stack | A |
| 5 | $P_{\text{stack}} = V_{\text{stack}} \times I_{\text{stack}} = N \cdot V_{\text{cell}} \cdot I$ | Total stack power | W |
| 6 | $V_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{Nernst}} - \eta_{\text{act}} - \eta_{\text{ohmic}} - \eta_{\text{conc}}$ | Polarization equation (losses) | V |
| 7 | $\eta_{\text{ohmic}} = I \cdot (R_{\text{ohm}})$ | Ohmic loss (membrane + contact resistance) | V |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{act}} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{i}{i_0}\right)$ | Activation overpotential (Tafel) | V |
| 9 | $\eta_{\text{conc}} = \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{i_L}{i_L - i}\right)$ | Concentration overpotential | V |
| 10 | $\Delta G = -n F E^0$ | Gibbs free energy ↔ cell voltage | J/mol |
| 11 | $\eta_{\text{thermo}} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} = 0.83\ (83\%)$ | Thermodynamic (HHV) efficiency of H₂/O₂ cell | — |
| 12 | $\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{I \cdot N}{2F}$ | H₂ consumption rate (per cell) | kg/s (with MW) |
| 13 | $P_{\text{density}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{stack}}}{V_{\text{stack}}}$ | Volumetric power density | W/L or kW/L |
| 14 | $N = \dfrac{V_{\text{required}}}{V_{\text{cell,avg}}}$ | Number of cells required in stack | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Trap:** Many students write $V_{\text{stack}} = N \cdot E^0$ — this is **wrong** when under load! Use the **actual operating cell voltage** (around **0.6 – 0.7 V** at rated current), not the thermodynamic $E^0$.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Application | Why Fuel Stacks Matter |
|---|---|
| **Passenger FCEVs (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO)** | High power density, zero tailpipe emissions (only water) |
| **Heavy-duty trucks (Hyundai XCIENT)** | Long range, fast refuelling vs. BEV |
| **Buses (Toyota SORA)** | Quiet operation, no local pollution |
| **Stationary power (Bloom Energy)** | Reliable backup, natural gas or H₂ |
| **Forklifts / Material handling** | Indoor use — no exhaust, low noise |
| **Aerospace (DLR, Boeing)** | Cryogenic H₂ from rocket fuel integration |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Nernst Equation for a PEM Fuel Cell

**Starting Point — General Nernst Equation:**
$$ E = E^0 - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q $$

where $Q$ is the reaction quotient.

**Step 1 — Write the Reaction Quotient**

For the overall reaction:
$$ H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O $$

The reaction quotient is:
$$ Q = \dfrac{a_{H_2O}}{a_{H_2}\cdot a_{O_2}^{1/2}} $$

**Step 2 — Convert Activities to Partial Pressures**

For gases, activity = partial pressure (in atm). Water is liquid in PEM cells (≈1 atm vapour):
$$ Q = \dfrac{p_{H_2O}}{p_{H_2}\cdot p_{O_2}^{1/2}} $$

**Step 3 — Apply to Nernst Equation**

Number of electrons transferred per mole of H₂: **n = 2**

$$ E = E^0 - \dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{p_{H_2O}}{p_{H_2}\cdot p_{O_2}^{1/2}}\right) $$

**Step 4 — Invert the Logarithm (KTU Preferred Form)**

$$ E_{\text{Nernst}} = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(p_{H_2}\right) + \dfrac{RT}{4F}\ln\!\left(p_{O_2}\right) - \dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(p_{H_2O}\right) $$

This is the **standard form used in all KTU derivations and exam numericals**.

### 3.2 Derivation: H₂ Consumption Rate in a Stack

**Step 1 — Faraday's First Law**

$$ m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} $$

**Step 2 — Mass Flow Rate (divide by time)**

$$ \dot{m} = \dfrac{M \cdot I}{n \cdot F} $$

**Step 3 — For Hydrogen (M = 2.016 g/mol, n = 2 electrons per H₂ molecule)**

$$ \dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{2.016 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg/mol} \times I}{2 \times 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}} $$

$$ \dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{I}{96{,}485} \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3} \quad [\text{kg/s}] $$

**Step 4 — Stack with N cells (current is the same):**

$$ \dot{m}_{H_2,\text{stack}} = \dfrac{N \cdot I \cdot M_{H_2}}{2F} \quad [\text{kg/s}] $$

### 3.3 KTU-Style Numerical — Worked Example

> **Problem (KTU Pattern):**
> A PEM fuel cell stack has **250 cells** connected in series. Each cell operates at **0.65 V** under a current of **200 A**.
> (a) Calculate the **stack voltage** and **stack power**.
> (b) Find the **H₂ consumption rate** in kg/h.
> (c) If the stack volume is **50 L**, what is the **volumetric power density**?
> Take $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}$, $M_{H_2} = 2.016\ \text{g/mol}$.

#### Solution

**(a) Stack Voltage and Power**

$$ V_{\text{stack}} = N \times V_{\text{cell}} = 250 \times 0.65 $$

$$ V_{\text{stack}} = 162.5\ \text{V} $$

$$ P_{\text{stack}} = V_{\text{stack}} \times I = 162.5 \times 200 $$

$$ P_{\text{stack}} = 32{,}500\ \text{W} = 32.5\ \text{kW} $$

**(b) H₂ Consumption Rate**

$$ \dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{N \cdot I \cdot M_{H_2}}{2F} = \dfrac{250 \times 200 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 96{,}485} $$

$$ \dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{250 \times 200 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3}}{192{,}970} = \dfrac{100.8}{192{,}970} $$

$$ \dot{m}_{H_2} = 5.224 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{kg/s} $$

Converting to **kg/h**:
$$ \dot{m}_{H_2} = 5.224 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600 = 1.88\ \text{kg/h} $$

**(c) Volumetric Power Density**

$$ P_{\text{density}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{stack}}}{V_{\text{stack}}} = \dfrac{32{,}500\ \text{W}}{50\ \text{L}} $$

$$ P_{\text{density}} = 650\ \text{W/L} = 0.65\ \text{kW/L} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A real automotive stack (Toyota Mirai 2nd Gen) achieves **~5.4 kW/L** — about **8× better** than this simple stack. Modern stacks use thinner membranes, higher catalyst activity, and improved flow fields.

### 3.4 Polarization Curve (V–I Characteristic) — Analytical Steps

The **polarization curve** plots cell voltage vs. current density and has **three distinct regions**:

1. **Activation Loss Region (low current):** dominated by sluggish ORR kinetics at the cathode. Modelled by **Tafel equation**.
2. **Ohmic Loss Region (mid current):** dominated by **membrane ionic resistance + contact resistance**. Linear with current.
3. **Concentration Loss Region (high current):** mass transport limits — reactant cannot reach catalyst fast enough. Voltage drops sharply.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Framework (must memorize):**
> $$ V_{\text{cell}}(i) = E_{\text{Nernst}} - \eta_{\text{act}}(i) - i \cdot R_{\text{ohmic}} - \eta_{\text{conc}}(i) $$

This is the **master equation** for all KTU fuel cell analytical problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — PEM Fuel Cell Stack

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph StackAssembly[Fuel Cell Stack Architecture]
        direction TB
        EndPlateTop[Top End Plate - Aluminum - Compression Load]
        CurrentCollector1[Current Collector Plate]
        Bipolar1[Bipolar Plate - Cathode Side - Air In Water Out]
        MEA1[MEA - Membrane Catalyst Anode Cathode GDL]
        Bipolar2[Bipolar Plate - Anode Side - Hydrogen In]
        MEA2[MEA - Membrane Catalyst Anode Cathode GDL]
        Bipolar3[Bipolar Plate - Cathode Side - Air In]
        MEA3[MEA - Membrane Catalyst Anode Cathode GDL]
        Bipolar4[Bipolar Plate - Anode Side - Hydrogen In]
        CurrentCollector2[Current Collector Plate]
        EndPlateBot[Bottom End Plate - Aluminum - Compression Load]
        TieRods[Tie Rods and Bolts - Uniform Clamping Force]
    end

    subgraph Auxiliaries[Stack Balance of Plant - BoP]
        direction LR
        H2Supply[Hydrogen Supply - Tank and Humidifier]
        AirSupply[Air Compressor and Filter]
        Cooling[Coolant Pump and Radiator]
        WaterMgmt[Water Management - Separator and Recirculator]
        ControlUnit[Fuel Cell Control Unit - FCU]
    end

    H2Supply --> Bipolar2
    H2Supply --> Bipolar4
    AirSupply --> Bipolar1
    AirSupply --> Bipolar3
    Cooling -.-> Bipolar1
    Cooling -.-> Bipolar2
    Cooling -.-> Bipolar3
    Cooling -.-> Bipolar4
    WaterMgmt --> Bipolar1
    WaterMgmt --> Bipolar3
    ControlUnit --> H2Supply
    ControlUnit --> AirSupply
    ControlUnit --> Cooling
    EndPlateTop --- MEA1
    EndPlateBot --- MEA3
    TieRods --- EndPlateTop
    TieRods --- EndPlateBot
```

### 4.2 Electrochemical Reaction Flow Inside MEA

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph AnodeSide[Anode Side - Hydrogen]
        H2In[Hydrogen H2 Molecule]
        CatalystA[Platinum Catalyst Anode]
        Hplus[Proton H plus]
        ElectronA[Electron e minus]
    end

    subgraph MembraneCore[Proton Exchange Membrane]
        Nafion[Nafion PEM - H plus Conductor Only]
    end

    subgraph CathodeSide[Cathode Side - Air]
        O2In[Oxygen O2 Molecule]
        CatalystC[Platinum Catalyst Cathode]
        ElectronC[Electron e minus via External Circuit]
        H2OOut[Product Water H2O]
    end

    ExternalLoad[External Load - Traction Motor and Power Electronics]

    H2In --> CatalystA
    CatalystA --> Hplus
    CatalystA --> ElectronA
    Hplus --> Nafion
    Nafion --> CatalystC
    O2In --> CatalystC
    ElectronA --> ExternalLoad
    ExternalLoad --> ElectronC
    ElectronC --> CatalystC
    CatalystC --> H2OOut
```

### 4.3 Polarization Curve (V-I Characteristic) — Qualitative

```mermaid
graph TB
    AxisOrigin[Current Density i in A per cm squared]
    OCV[Open Circuit Voltage around 1.0 V - Nernst Loss]
    Activation[Activation Drop - Steep at Low Current - ORR Kinetics]
    Ohmic[Linear Ohmic Region - Membrane Resistance]
    Conc[Concentration Drop - Mass Transport Limit]
    LimitCurrent[Limiting Current Density iL]

    OCV --> Activation
    Activation --> Ohmic
    Ohmic --> Conc
    Conc --> LimitCurrent
```

### 4.4 Fuel Cell Vehicle Powertrain Integration

```mermaid
graph LR
    H2Tank[High Pressure H2 Tank - 700 bar]
    Stack[FC Stack - 300 to 650 V DC]
    DCBoost[HV DC DC Boost Converter]
    Inverter[Traction Inverter]
    Motor[3 Phase AC Traction Motor]
    Wheels[Drive Wheels]
    Battery[Li ion Battery - Peak Power Buffer]
    Acc[12 V Battery - Accessories]

    H2Tank --> Stack
    Stack --> DCBoost
    DCBoost --> Inverter
    Inverter --> Motor
    Motor --> Wheels
    Stack --- Battery
    DCBoost --- Battery
    Stack --> Acc
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Define a fuel cell stack. Why is a single PEM fuel cell not sufficient to power an electric vehicle?*

**Model Answer:**

A **fuel cell stack** is a series-connected assembly of multiple individual PEM fuel cells using bipolar plates, designed to deliver the high voltage and power required by an automotive traction system.

A **single PEM cell** produces only **0.6 – 0.8 V** under load. An EV traction motor requires **300 – 650 V DC** at the inverter input. Therefore, **hundreds of cells must be stacked in series** to achieve usable voltage. Additionally, a single cell has very low active area (limited by MEA manufacturing), so a stack also increases **total power output** through parallel gas distribution across many cells.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for justification with voltage values)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List any four main components of a PEM fuel cell stack and state their functions.*

**Model Answer:**

| # | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bipolar Plate** | Distributes H₂ and air; conducts current between cells; removes product water |
| 2 | **MEA — Proton Exchange Membrane** | Selectively conducts H⁺ ions from anode to cathode |
| 3 | **Gas Diffusion Layer (GDL)** | Distributes reactant gas uniformly over catalyst; conducts electrons |
| 4 | **Catalyst Layer (Pt/C)** | Catalyses H₂ oxidation at anode and O₂ reduction at cathode |

*(3 marks: 1 mark per correct component-function pair, 4th pair = bonus clarity)*

---

### Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the **working principle of a PEM fuel cell**. Derive the **overall electrochemical reaction** and state the **standard cell potential**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A fuel cell stack consists of **400 cells** operating at an **average cell voltage of 0.7 V** and delivering a current of **250 A**.
(i) Determine the **stack voltage, stack power**, and **number of cells required** if the vehicle demands 350 V.
(ii) Calculate the **mass of H₂ consumed per hour**.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Working Principle of PEM Fuel Cell**

A PEM fuel cell converts the chemical energy of H₂ and O₂ directly into electrical energy using an electrochemical process.

**Construction:**
- **Anode side:** Hydrogen channel in bipolar plate → GDL → Pt/C catalyst → membrane.
- **Cathode side:** Membrane → Pt/C catalyst → GDL → air channel in bipolar plate.
- **Membrane (Nafion):** selectively allows H⁺ to pass, blocks electrons and gases.

**Working (refer to Section 4.2 diagram):**
1. Humidified H₂ enters the anode channel and reaches the catalyst.
2. Pt catalyses the **Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction (HOR):**
$$ H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^- $$
3. Protons migrate through the **PEM** (electrons are blocked).
4. Electrons flow through the **external circuit** → this is the **load current**.
5. At the cathode, O₂ from air combines with H⁺ and e⁻ (**Oxygen Reduction Reaction — ORR**):
$$ \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O $$
6. Product **water** is removed via cathode flow; waste **heat** is removed by coolant plates.

**Overall reaction:**
$$ H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O \quad ; \quad E^0 = 1.229\ \text{V at 25 °C} $$

*(7 marks: 2 for construction, 3 for half-cell and overall reactions, 1 for E⁰, 1 for neat labelled sketch)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution**

**(i) Stack Voltage and Power**

$$ V_{\text{stack}} = N \times V_{\text{cell}} = 400 \times 0.7 = 280\ \text{V} $$

$$ P_{\text{stack}} = V_{\text{stack}} \times I = 280 \times 250 = 70{,}000\ \text{W} = 70\ \text{kW} $$

**Number of cells for 350 V:**

$$ N_{\text{required}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{required}}}{V_{\text{cell}}} = \dfrac{350}{0.7} = 500\ \text{cells} $$

**(ii) H₂ Mass Consumption Per Hour**

$$ \dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{N \cdot I \cdot M_{H_2}}{2F} = \dfrac{400 \times 250 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 96{,}485} $$

$$ \dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{201.6}{192{,}970} = 1.045 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg/s} $$

Converting to per hour:
$$ m_{H_2} = 1.045 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 = 3.76\ \text{kg/h} $$

*(7 marks: 2 for V_stack, 2 for P_stack + N_required, 3 for H₂ mass with unit conversion)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice [KTU Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **construction and functions of major components** of a PEM fuel cell stack with a labelled diagram. Compare **parallel, serpentine, and interdigitated flow fields** in bipolar plates.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Nernst equation** for a PEM fuel cell operating at 80 °C with $p_{H_2} = 1.5$ atm, $p_{O_2} = 0.21$ atm (air), and $p_{H_2O} = 0.5$ atm. Find the **open-circuit voltage (OCV)**. Take $E^0 = 1.229$ V.

#### Model Solution (Highlights)

**(a) Component Functions and Flow Field Comparison**

*(Refer to Section 2.3 table for full component description.)*

**Flow Field Comparison:**

| Flow Field | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|---|
| **Parallel** | Low pressure drop; simple | Poor water removal; uneven distribution |
| **Serpentine** | Excellent water removal; uniform flow | High pressure drop; parasitic loss |
| **Interdigitated** | Forced convection through GDL; superior mass transport | Highest pressure drop; complex machining |

*(7 marks: 4 for components, 3 for flow field comparison)*

---

**(b) Nernst Equation and OCV Calculation**

**Nernst Equation (derived in Section 3.1):**
$$ E_{\text{Nernst}} = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln(p_{H_2}) + \dfrac{RT}{4F}\ln(p_{O_2}) - \dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln(p_{H_2O}) $$

**Plug in values** (T = 80 °C = 353 K):
$$ \dfrac{RT}{2F} = \dfrac{8.314 \times 353}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 0.01521\ \text{V} $$

$$ \dfrac{RT}{4F} = 0.00761\ \text{V} $$

**Compute each logarithm:**
$$ \ln(1.5) = 0.4055 $$
$$ \ln(0.21) = -1.5606 $$
$$ \ln(0.5) = -0.6931 $$

**Substitute:**
$$ E_{\text{Nernst}} = 1.229 + 0.01521(0.4055) + 0.00761(-1.5606) - 0.01521(-0.6931) $$

$$ E_{\text{Nernst}} = 1.229 + 0.00617 - 0.01188 + 0.01054 $$

$$ E_{\text{Nernst}} = 1.229 + 0.00483 $$

$$ E_{\text{Nernst}} = 1.234\ \text{V} $$

**Verification:** This is **close to 1.23 V**, confirming theoretical OCV for a PEM cell operating on air/hydrogen at 80 °C.

*(7 marks: 2 for Nernst form, 2 for numerical substitution, 2 for final answer with units, 1 for interpretation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Using $E^0$ instead of actual $V_{\text{cell}}$** for stack voltage calculation — KTU deducts **2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert kg/s to kg/h** in H₂ consumption — common 1-mark error.
> 3. **Drawing the MEA without showing proton flow direction** — KTU expects **H⁺ arrow through membrane** and **e⁻ arrow through external circuit**.
> 4. **Confusing GDL and Catalyst layer** — GDL is on the **outside** of the catalyst; catalyst is **sandwiched** between membrane and GDL.
> 5. **Mixing up anode and cathode reactions** — HOR is at **anode** (negative terminal in fuel cell), ORR is at **cathode** (positive terminal).
> 6. **Not stating the operating temperature** in Nernst problems — examiners deduct **1 mark**.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] A **fuel cell stack** is many PEM cells in **series** to scale up voltage.
- [x] Single cell voltage = **~0.6–0.8 V**; automotive stack needs **300–650 V**.
- [x] PEM membrane (Nafion) transports **H⁺ only** — blocks e⁻, H₂, and O₂.
- [x] **Anode reaction:** $H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$ (HOR)
- [x] **Cathode reaction:** $\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O$ (ORR)
- [x] **Overall:** $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$ ; $E^0 = 1.229$ V
- [x] **Bipolar plates** conduct current, distribute gases, remove water.
- [x] **Catalyst:** Pt or Pt-alloy on carbon support.
- [x] **GDL:** carbon paper — distributes gas, conducts electrons.
- [x] **Stack voltage:** $V_{\text{stack}} = N \times V_{\text{cell}}$
- [x] **Stack current:** same as cell current (series connection).
- [x] **Power:** $P_{\text{stack}} = V_{\text{stack}} \times I$
- [x] **H₂ flow rate:** $\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{N I M_{H_2}}{2F}$
- [x] **Nernst equation:** $E = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln(p_{H_2}) + \dfrac{RT}{4F}\ln(p_{O_2}) - \dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln(p_{H_2O})$
- [x] **Polarization curve** has 3 regions: activation, ohmic, concentration.
- [x] **Flow fields:** parallel, serpentine, interdigitated, pin-type.
- [x] **Key vehicles:** Toyota Mirai (330 cells, 128 kW), Hyundai NEXO.
- [x] **Faraday constant:** $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol.
- [x] **Operating temperature:** 60–80 °C for PEM.
- [x] **Product:** only **water + electricity + heat** — zero local emissions.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** For every stack numerical, **always state the units** in every step and **convert seconds to hours** when the question asks "per hour". KTU examiners mark strictly — silent units = lost marks.

---

**End of Module 4 — Fuel Stacks Notes**
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme — Outcome-Based Education (OBE) Framework*
*Aligned with Revised Bloom's Taxonomy Cognitive Levels: Remember (L1) → Understand (L2) → Apply (L3) → Analyze (L4) → Evaluate (L5)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Supply
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET414 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Air Supply

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Air Supply System (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> The *Air Supply System* of a modern internal combustion (IC) engine is the integrated assembly of components responsible for **metering, filtering, conditioning (density/temperature/pressure modification), and delivering** a precise mass of clean air to each cylinder's combustion chamber, in synchrony with the fuel delivery and ignition events. In the KTU 2024 Scheme, it is treated as a **subsystem of the engine management architecture** that directly determines the volumetric efficiency, indicated mean effective pressure, exhaust emissions, and part-load fuel economy of the powertrain.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, the Air Supply topic is positioned at the intersection of **mechanical fluid routing** and **electronic engine management (EMS)**. It is no longer viewed merely as a passive manifold; it is a *controlled actuator network* governed by the ECU (Electronic Control Unit).

### 1.1.1 Key Sub-systems Constituting Air Supply
1. **Air Cleaner / Filter Assembly (ACA)** – Removes particulate contaminants; mandatory for engine longevity.
2. **Air Mass Flow Sensor (MAF / AFM)** – Measures the instantaneous mass (in kg/h or g/s) of inducted air.
3. **Intake Manifold / Plenum** – Distributes air to individual runners feeding each cylinder.
4. **Throttle Body Assembly** – Electronically controlled by-wire valve regulating air admission rate.
5. **Variable Intake / Tumble / Swirl Control Valves** – Optimise in-cylinder motion for stratified or homogeneous charge.
6. **Supercharger / Turbocharger (Forced Induction)** – Raises air density upstream of the throttle for higher specific output.
7. **Intercooler / Charge Air Cooler (CAC)** – Reduces temperature of compressed air to increase density.
8. **Intake Air Temperature (IAT) Sensor & Manifold Absolute Pressure (MAP) Sensor** – Inputs to the ECU's air-charge model.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Engine as a "Burning Marathon Runner"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Marathon Runner and the Oxygen Mask**
> Imagine a marathon runner (the engine) competing on a track (the vehicle's duty cycle). For the runner to keep a steady pace, he must receive a **precise, clean, and conditioned** supply of oxygen (air). If the oxygen is *cold* and pressurised, he runs faster (turbocharged performance). If the oxygen is *warm and thin*, he tires quickly (naturally aspirated, hot-day drive). The **air supply system is the "oxygen pipeline"** — comprising the nose filter (air filter), the lung regulator (throttle), the chest pipes (intake manifold), and the supplemental oxygen tank (turbocharger). The brain controlling the rate of inhalation is the **ECU**.

The runner analogy directly maps to engineering parameters:

| Biological Element | Engine Component | Engineering Function |
|---|---|---|
| Nostril hair | Air Filter | Particulate filtration |
| Diaphragm | Throttle Valve | Air mass regulation |
| Trachea & Bronchi | Intake Manifold + Runners | Air distribution |
| Lung capacity | Plenum Volume | Resonance tuning |
| Hyperventilation | Turbocharger / Supercharger | Boost pressure |
| Oxygen mask | Intercooler | Density recovery |

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Board Emphasis)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standards & Constants to Memorise:**
> - Standard atmospheric pressure: $P_0 = 1.01325 \text{ bar} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$
> - Standard air density at STP: $\rho_0 = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Universal gas constant for air: $R = 287 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$
> - Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (gasoline): **AFR = 14.7 : 1**
> - Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (diesel): **AFR ≈ 14.5 : 1**
> - Stoichiometric lambda ($\lambda$) = **1.0** (chemically correct mixture)
> - Volumetric efficiency benchmark for naturally aspirated engines: $\eta_v \approx 0.85 - 0.95$

### 1.3.1 Governing Ideal-Gas Relation for Air Charge

The ECU fundamentally works with **mass of air inducted per cycle**, derived from:

$$m_{air} = \frac{P_{manifold} \cdot V_{displaced}}{R \cdot T_{manifold}}$$

where:
- $P_{manifold}$ = absolute pressure inside the intake manifold (in **Pa**)
- $V_{displaced}$ = geometric swept volume of one cylinder per cycle (in **m³**)
- $T_{manifold}$ = absolute temperature of intake air (in **K**)
- $R = 287 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$ for dry air

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The ECU's load calculation, ignition timing, injection duration, and torque management all pivot on the *real-time accuracy* of this equation. Any air-supply anomaly (leak, clogged filter, faulty MAF) cascades into **combustion inefficiency, misfire, or emission failure**.

---

## 1.4 Why "Air Supply" is a Modern Topic

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, the air supply has evolved from a passive metallic tube to a **mechatronic assembly** with electronic sensors and actuators. The reasons it is included in *Modern Automotive Technologies* are:

1. **Emission compliance** — BS-VI / Euro 6 norms require lambda and EGR management.
2. **Direct Injection (GDI) sensitivity** — Air motion directly controls spray wall-wetting and PM emissions.
3. **Hybrid powertrains** — Air supply is one of the few engine subsystems still active in mild-hybrid transient operation.
4. **Downspeeding & downsizing** — Modern engines rely on **forced induction** to maintain torque at low RPM, making the *air supply chain* the heart of the performance envelope.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Breakdown of the Air Supply Chain

The complete air supply path can be decomposed into **six sequential stages**. Each stage introduces a controlled pressure loss or gain, and a temperature change, which the ECU must track.

### Stage 1: Atmospheric Induction
- Air enters the **air intake snorkel**, usually positioned outside the engine bay to draw *cooler, denser* air (ram effect at vehicle speed).
- The ram pressure rise at high vehicle speed is given by $P_{ram} = \frac{1}{2}\rho_a V_v^2$, where $V_v$ is vehicle speed.

### Stage 2: Filtration (Air Cleaner)
- **Paper element filters** dominate modern cars (efficiency $\geq 99.5\%$ for particles $> 5\mu m$).
- Pressure drop across a clean filter: $\Delta P_{filter} \approx 2 - 5 \text{ kPa}$.
- A clogged filter can drop $\eta_v$ by **8–12%** — a classic KTU viva question.

### Stage 3: Mass Flow Measurement (MAF Sensor)
- **Hot-film MAF** is the industry standard. A heated sensing element is maintained at a constant temperature *above* the incoming air; the current required is proportional to mass flow.
- Output is typically a **frequency-modulated or analog voltage signal** read by the ECU.

### Stage 4: Throttling
- The throttle acts as a **variable restriction**. The pressure drop across the throttle dictates the manifold pressure $P_m$.
- For an isentropic throttle, the choked-flow condition is reached at $P_{throat} / P_0 = (2/(\gamma+1))^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} \approx 0.528$ for air ($\gamma = 1.4$).

### Stage 5: Distribution via Intake Manifold
- **Plenum + tuned runners** create Helmholtz resonance, exploiting pressure-wave tuning to "ram" air into the cylinder at the right crank angle.
- Resonance frequency: $f_r = \frac{c}{4L}$, where $c$ = speed of sound in air ($\approx 340 \text{ m/s}$) and $L$ = effective runner length.

### Stage 6: Forced Induction (if equipped)
- Turbocharger / supercharger elevates manifold pressure **above atmospheric**.
- **Boost pressure** $P_{boost} = P_{discharge} - P_{atmosphere}$.

---

## 2.2 Governing Performance Parameter: Volumetric Efficiency ($\eta_v$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Volumetric Efficiency Definition (KTU 2024):**
> $$\eta_v = \frac{\text{Actual mass of air inducted per cycle}}{\text{Theoretical mass of air (if cylinder filled with ambient air at } P_0, T_0)}$$

It is the single most important metric of the air supply system's effectiveness. A well-designed naturally aspirated engine peaks at $\eta_v \approx 0.95$; a turbocharged engine can *exceed* unity ($\eta_v > 1.0$).

---

## 2.3 The Forced Induction (Boost) Family

| Type | Driver | Lag | Efficiency | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Roots Supercharger | Crankshaft (belt) | Minimal | ~70% | Heavy-duty diesel, drag racing |
| Twin-Screw (Lysholm) | Crankshaft (belt) | Low | ~80% | Premium performance, OEMs |
| Centrifugal Supercharger | Crankshaft (belt) | Moderate | ~80% | Aftermarket, hot-rods |
| Turbocharger (Exhaust-driven) | Exhaust gas energy | Turbo-lag present | ~75–85% | Almost all modern turbo engines |
| Electric Turbo (E-Turbo) | 48V motor | None | High | Mild-hybrid, cutting-edge |

### 2.3.1 Boost Pressure and Air Density
After compression in the turbo/supercharger and cooling in the intercooler, the inducted air density is:

$$\rho_{manifold} = \frac{P_{boost} + P_0}{R \cdot T_{after\ cooler}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The intercooler's job is to *reduce $T$* after compression, because a turbo's adiabatic compression would otherwise heat the charge to **120–180 °C**, dropping density and risking knock. A well-designed intercooler can drop charge temperature by **40–80 °C**, recovering 8–15% of lost density.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SAVE THIS TABLE — it covers >80% of numerical problems on this topic.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mass of air inducted | $m_a = \dfrac{P_m V_d}{R T_m}$ | kg | Ideal-gas model |
| 2 | Volumetric efficiency | $\eta_v = \dfrac{m_{a,actual}}{m_{a,theoretical}}$ | dimensionless | Peaks at mid-RPM |
| 3 | Stoichiometric AFR (gasoline) | $AFR_{st} = 14.7$ | – | For $\lambda = 1$ |
| 4 | Lambda ($\lambda$) | $\lambda = \dfrac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$ | – | $\lambda < 1$ rich, $\lambda > 1$ lean |
| 5 | Equivalent air mass flow (per cycle) | $\dot{m}_{a,cycle} = \eta_v \cdot \rho_0 \cdot V_d \cdot \dfrac{N}{2}$ for 4-stroke | kg/s | $N$ = RPM |
| 6 | Boost-corrected air mass | $m_{boost} = \eta_v \cdot \rho_0 \cdot V_d \cdot \dfrac{P_{boost}+P_0}{P_0}$ | kg | Simplified isothermal |
| 7 | Helholtz resonance frequency | $f_r = \dfrac{c}{4L}$ | Hz | $L$ = runner length |
| 8 | Speed of sound in air | $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | m/s | $\gamma = 1.4$ |
| 9 | Aftercooler effectiveness | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{T_{comp,out} - T_{cooled}}{T_{comp,out} - T_{amb}}$ | – | Higher = better |
| 10 | Ram pressure | $P_{ram} = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V_v^2$ | Pa | Vehicle-speed dependent |
| 11 | Turbo shaft power | $P_{turbo} = \dot{m}_{exh} \cdot c_{p,exh} \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out}) \cdot \eta_{turbo}$ | W | Energy balance |
| 12 | BSFC (Brake Specific Fuel Consumption) | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_{brake}}$ | g/kWh | Air supply affects this |

> [!WARNING]
> **Board-Exam Trap:** When using $m_a = P_m V_d / (R T_m)$, students often forget to use **absolute pressure** and **absolute temperature** (Kelvin). Marks are routinely deducted for this single oversight.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Passenger cars (BS-VI):** Air supply tuned for low-end torque and minimal pumping loss; wide use of **variable-geometry turbochargers (VGT)**.
- **Motorcycles:** Short, tuned intake runners exploiting Helmholtz resonance at peak power RPM.
- **Commercial vehicles (Diesel):** Charge-air cooling, VGT, and **air-to-air intercoolers** are standard; turbo lag mitigated by **wastegate** and **VNT** control.
- **Racing (F1):** Compressors running at >100,000 RPM, no intercooler (insufficient space), MGU-H (motor-generator unit on turbo shaft) to recover exhaust energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Linkage to Other Modules:** Air supply is *upstream* of the fuel system and *upstream* of combustion. It is also the *downstream* consumer of the EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation) system. The ECU, in modern engines, controls all three simultaneously — a topic emphasised in KTU 2024 Module 5 (Engine Management Systems).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate:** Every algebraic and arithmetic step below is shown in full. No step is omitted.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Mass of Air Inducted Per Cycle from First Principles

We begin from the **Ideal Gas Law** for air treated as a perfect gas:

$$P \cdot V = m \cdot R \cdot T$$

where:
- $P$ = absolute pressure (Pa)
- $V$ = volume (m³)
- $m$ = mass of gas (kg)
- $R$ = specific gas constant for air = $287 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

### Step 1 — Identify the Control Volume
For a single cylinder, the **displaced volume per cycle** is the swept volume:

$$V_d = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot B^2 \cdot S \cdot n_r$$

where:
- $B$ = cylinder bore (m)
- $S$ = stroke length (m)
- $n_r$ = number of cylinders sharing the induction stroke (for a 4-stroke, $n_r = 1$)

### Step 2 — Rearrange Ideal Gas Law for Mass

Starting from:
$$P \cdot V = m \cdot R \cdot T$$

We solve explicitly for $m$:

$$m = \frac{P \cdot V}{R \cdot T}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Cylinder Conditions

Replacing $V$ with $V_d$ and denoting the manifold conditions during induction:

$$m_{a,ideal} = \frac{P_m \cdot V_d}{R \cdot T_m}$$

where $P_m, T_m$ are the **manifold absolute pressure and temperature** at the moment the intake valve closes.

### Step 4 — Apply Volumetric Efficiency Correction

The *real* mass inducted is *less* than ideal because:
- Throttle restriction
- Heat transfer from hot valves/manifold walls
- Pressure-wave losses
- Residual exhaust gas mixing (EGR)

Thus:
$$m_{a,actual} = \eta_v \cdot \rho_0 \cdot V_d \cdot \frac{P_m}{P_0} \cdot \frac{T_0}{T_m}$$

Equivalently:
$$m_{a,actual} = \eta_v \cdot \frac{P_m V_d}{R T_m}$$

### Step 5 — Per-Cycle Mass Flow at Engine Speed $N$ (RPM)

For a 4-stroke engine, each cylinder fires once every **two revolutions**, so the number of induction events per second per cylinder is $N / (2 \cdot 60)$. The total air mass flow rate is:

$$\dot{m}_{air} = m_{a,actual} \cdot \frac{N}{2 \cdot 60} \cdot n_{cyl}$$

After algebraic simplification:

$$\dot{m}_{air} = \eta_v \cdot \frac{P_m V_d}{R T_m} \cdot \frac{N \cdot n_{cyl}}{120}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Complete.** This is the canonical relation used by the ECU to estimate the *current airflow* for stoichiometric fuel injection.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Multi-Step Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has bore $B = 80 \text{ mm}$, stroke $S = 90 \text{ mm}$, and operates at $N = 3000 \text{ RPM}$. The manifold absolute pressure is $P_m = 0.55 \text{ bar}$, and manifold temperature is $T_m = 320 \text{ K}$. Volumetric efficiency is $0.85$. Calculate the air mass flow rate $\dot{m}_{air}$ in kg/h.

**Step 1 — Compute the swept volume per cylinder:**

$$V_d = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot B^2 \cdot S$$

Substitute:

$$V_d = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.080)^2 \cdot 0.090$$

$$V_d = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.0064 \cdot 0.090$$

$$V_d = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 5.76 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$V_d = 4.524 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 \text{ per cylinder}$$

**Step 2 — Compute ideal mass of air per cylinder (without $\eta_v$):**

$$m_{a,ideal} = \frac{P_m \cdot V_d}{R \cdot T_m}$$

Substitute values: $P_m = 0.55 \times 10^5 = 55{,}000 \text{ Pa}$, $R = 287$, $T_m = 320$ K.

$$m_{a,ideal} = \frac{55{,}000 \times 4.524 \times 10^{-4}}{287 \times 320}$$

Compute numerator:
$$55{,}000 \times 4.524 \times 10^{-4} = 24.882 \text{ Pa·m}^3 \ (\text{equivalent to Joules})$$

Compute denominator:
$$287 \times 320 = 91{,}840$$

Therefore:
$$m_{a,ideal} = \frac{24.882}{91{,}840} = 2.709 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg per cycle}$$

**Step 3 — Apply volumetric efficiency:**

$$m_{a,actual} = \eta_v \cdot m_{a,ideal} = 0.85 \times 2.709 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$m_{a,actual} = 2.303 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg per cycle per cylinder}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to per-second per cylinder rate:**

Number of induction events per second per cylinder for 4-stroke:
$$f_{ind} = \frac{N}{2 \cdot 60} = \frac{3000}{120} = 25 \text{ inductions/second}$$

Mass flow per cylinder:
$$\dot{m}_{a,cyl} = 2.303 \times 10^{-4} \times 25 = 5.758 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by 4 cylinders:**

$$\dot{m}_{air,total} = 5.758 \times 10^{-3} \times 4 = 0.02303 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to kg/h:**

$$\dot{m}_{air,total} = 0.02303 \times 3600 = 82.92 \text{ kg/h}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer: $\dot{m}_{air} \approx 82.9 \text{ kg/h}$**

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Air Supply Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> **This matrix helps students justify design choices in 14-mark questions.**

| Application | Recommended Air Supply | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Entry commuter motorcycle (100–150 cc) | Naturally aspirated, no resonance tuning, carburettor-fed | Cost-sensitive, narrow RPM band |
| Premium 150–250 cc motorcycle | Naturally aspirated with tuned intake length, FI | Mid-RPM torque priority |
| Sub-1.0 L city car (BS-VI petrol) | Turbocharged, port injection | Downsizing + downspeeding |
| Premium sedan (2.0 L) | Twin-scroll turbo, GDI, intercooled | Balanced response and efficiency |
| Heavy commercial vehicle | Single/twin-stage VGT turbo, intercooled, EGR | High load, low-end torque |
| Hybrid (mild 48V) | E-turbo or e-supercharger | Eliminating turbo lag |
| Racing (Formula 1) | High-pressure turbo + MGU-H + DRS-like wastegate | Energy recovery mandated |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Boosted Air Density After Intercooler

Given:
- Pre-turbo ambient air: $P_0 = 101.325$ kPa, $T_0 = 298$ K
- Turbo discharge pressure (boost): $P_b = 180$ kPa (absolute)
- Adiabatic compression with turbo efficiency $\eta_{turbo} = 0.75$

### Step 1 — Pressure Ratio Across Compressor

$$\pi_c = \frac{P_b}{P_0} = \frac{180}{101.325} = 1.776$$

### Step 2 — Ideal (isentropic) Temperature Rise

For an ideal gas with $\gamma = 1.4$:

$$T_{2s} = T_0 \cdot \pi_c^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$

Compute exponent:
$$\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma} = \frac{0.4}{1.4} = 0.2857$$

Compute $\pi_c^{0.2857}$:
$$1.776^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \cdot \ln(1.776)} = e^{0.2857 \cdot 0.574} = e^{0.1640} = 1.178$$

So:
$$T_{2s} = 298 \times 1.178 = 351.1 \text{ K}$$

### Step 3 — Actual Temperature Rise Using Efficiency

$$T_2 = T_0 + \frac{T_{2s} - T_0}{\eta_{turbo}}$$

$$T_2 = 298 + \frac{351.1 - 298}{0.75} = 298 + \frac{53.1}{0.75} = 298 + 70.8 = 368.8 \text{ K}$$

### Step 4 — Intercooler Drops to $T_3 = 320$ K

Density after intercooler:
$$\rho_3 = \frac{P_b}{R T_3} = \frac{180{,}000}{287 \times 320} = \frac{180{,}000}{91{,}840} = 1.96 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

Compare to ambient: $\rho_0 = 1.225$ kg/m³.

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The intercooled charge is **60% denser** than ambient. This is the entire *engineering reason* turbocharged engines produce disproportionately more torque.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Strategy:** Because air supply is a physical fluid-routing system spanning the entire engine bay, two complementary Mermaid diagrams are used: a **Process-Flow Architecture** and a **Block-Functional Schema** with sensor/actuator logic.

---

## 4.1 Process-Flow Schematic of the Air Supply Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[AMBIENT AIR] --> A2[RAM SNOOKEL]
    A2 --> A3[AIR FILTER ASSEMBLY]
    A3 --> A4[MAF SENSOR]
    A4 --> A5[THROTTLE BODY ECU]
    A5 --> A6[INTAKE PLENUM CHAMBER]
    A6 --> A7[INTAKE RUNNER BANK]
    A7 --> A8[INTAKE VALVE CYL]
    A8 --> A9[COMBUSTION CHAMBER]

    subgraph FORCED_INDUCTION [FORCED INDUCTION OPTION]
        B1[EXHAUST MANIFOLD] --> B2[TURBINE WHEEL]
        B2 --> B3[COMPRESSOR WHEEL]
        B3 --> B4[INTERCOOLER]
        B4 --> A6
    end

    subgraph ECU_LOOP [ECU CONTROL LOOP]
        C1[MAP SENSOR] --> C2[ECU]
        C2 --> A5
        C2 --> D1[IAT SENSOR]
        C2 --> E1[O2 LAMBDA SENSOR]
    end
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Functional Architecture of the Air Supply Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INP[INPUT STAGE ATMOSPHERIC] --> FIL[FILTRATION BLOCK]
    FIL --> MES[MASS MEASUREMENT BLOCK]
    MES --> THR[THROTTLING ACTUATOR]
    THR --> DIS[DISTRIBUTION PLENUM]
    DIS --> RUN[INDIVIDUAL RUNNERS]
    RUN --> CYL[CYLINDER INDUCTION]

    ECU[ECU SUPERVISORY CONTROLLER] --> THR
    ECU --> DIS
    ECU --> CYL
    CYL --> FB[FEEDBACK EMISSIONS LAMBDA]
    FB --> ECU
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Block Diagram?** A block diagram emphasises the *closed-loop control* nature of the modern air supply system, which the KTU 2024 syllabus treats as a *cyber-physical* subsystem. Students should be able to draw this in the exam with relative ease.

---

## 4.3 Turbocharger-Equipped Air Supply Layout (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AIR[FRESH AIR INLET] --> AF[AIR FILTER]
    AF --> COMPC[COMPRESSOR WHEEL]
    EXH[ENGINE EXHAUST] --> TURB[TURBINE WHEEL]
    TURB --> EXHOUT[EXHAUST OUT]
    COMPC --> IC[INTERCOOLER CHARGE AIR COOLER]
    IC --> THB[THROTTLE BODY]
    THB --> PLM[PLENUM]
    PLM --> R1[RUNNER 1]
    PLM --> R2[RUNNER 2]
    PLM --> R3[RUNNER 3]
    PLM --> R4[RUNNER 4]
    R1 --> CYL1[CYLINDER 1]
    R2 --> CYL2[CYLINDER 2]
    R3 --> CYL3[CYLINDER 3]
    R4 --> CYL4[CYLINDER 4]
    WG[WASTEGATE] -. bypass .-> TURB
    ECU2[ECU BOOST CONTROLLER] --> WG
    ECU2 --> THB
```

---

## 4.4 Key Component Layout Reference

| Block ID | Real Component | Engineering Function |
|---|---|---|
| AF | Pleated paper element | Particulate filtration, 99.5% efficiency |
| COMPC | Centrifugal compressor | Raises air static pressure |
| TURB | Radial inflow turbine | Extracts exhaust enthalpy |
| IC | Air-to-air or air-to-water heat exchanger | Drops charge temperature |
| THB | Electronic throttle body (drive-by-wire) | Mass flow regulation |
| PLM | Volume plenum (1.5–3 L) | Dampens pulsations, stores charge |
| WG | Pneumatic or electronic wastegate | Limits peak boost |
| ECU2 | Engine Control Unit | Closed-loop boost control |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A tests *Recall (L1)* and *Comprehension (L2)* of Bloom's Taxonomy. Answers should be **3–5 lines**, with at least one formula or numerical example where applicable.

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`
**Define volumetric efficiency of an IC engine. State two factors that affect it.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> *Volumetric efficiency ($\eta_v$)* is defined as the ratio of the **actual mass of air inducted per cycle** to the **theoretical mass of air that would fill the swept volume at ambient (atmospheric) conditions**.
> $$\eta_v = \dfrac{m_{a,actual}}{\rho_0 \cdot V_d}$$
> **Two affecting factors:**
> 1. **Throttle opening** — part-throttle operation increases pumping loss and reduces $\eta_v$.
> 2. **Intake air temperature** — hotter, less-dense air reduces the actual mass inducted, lowering $\eta_v$.
> *(Other acceptable factors: engine speed, intake runner length/tuning, valve timing, atmospheric pressure, back-pressure.)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`
**What is the function of an intercooler in a turbocharged engine? Why is it necessary?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> An **intercooler (charge air cooler)** is a heat exchanger placed between the turbocharger compressor outlet and the throttle body. It reduces the temperature of the compressed charge air.
> **Necessity:** Turbo-compression is approximately adiabatic and raises the charge temperature to **120–180 °C**, reducing air density. Cooling the charge back to 50–70 °C **recovers 8–15% of the lost density**, increasing torque and reducing knock tendency.
> Effectiveness is expressed as:
> $$\varepsilon = \dfrac{T_{hot,in} - T_{cool,out}}{T_{hot,in} - T_{ambient}}$$

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. The student attempts **one of two alternatives** (Choice-based). We provide Question A and Question B as a complete set.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 & 2023, Module 4]`

**Q. A(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **modern air supply system** of a multi-cylinder petrol engine. Discuss the role of **MAF sensor, throttle body, intake manifold, and ECU** in ensuring efficient air delivery. **(7 marks)**

**Q. A(b)** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke SI engine running at 3500 RPM has bore 75 mm and stroke 85 mm. The intake manifold pressure is 0.6 bar and temperature is 330 K. Volumetric efficiency is 0.88. Calculate: (i) Mass of air inducted per cylinder per cycle, (ii) Total air mass flow rate in kg/h, (iii) Required fuel flow rate in kg/h for stoichiometric mixture. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q. A(a) — 7 Marks

> **Step 1 — Introduction (1 mark):**
> The modern air supply system is a *mechatronic assembly* that meters, filters, conditions, and distributes air to each cylinder under ECU supervision.

> **Step 2 — Block Diagram (2 marks):** (Student should draw a labelled block diagram — refer Section 4.2)

> **Step 3 — Role of Components (4 marks):**
>
> | Component | Function | Key Detail |
> |---|---|---|
> | MAF Sensor | Measures mass of incoming air (kg/h) | Hot-film principle; output fed to ECU |
> | Throttle Body | Electronically regulates air flow (drive-by-wire) | Idle air control valve for low-load |
> | Intake Manifold | Distributes air equally to cylinders | Plenum + tuned runners for resonance |
> | ECU | Computes fuel injection duration from $m_a$ and target $\lambda$ | Uses speed-density or MAF-based algorithm |

> **Step 4 — Conclusion (1 mark):** A modern air supply is a closed-loop system wherein MAF feedback ensures the precise stoichiometry required for BS-VI / Euro 6 compliance.

---

#### Model Solution to Q. A(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $N = 3500$ RPM, $n_{cyl} = 4$, 4-stroke
- $B = 0.075$ m, $S = 0.085$ m
- $P_m = 0.6 \text{ bar} = 60{,}000$ Pa
- $T_m = 330$ K
- $\eta_v = 0.88$
- Stoichiometric AFR (petrol) = 14.7

**Step 1 — Swept volume per cylinder:**

$$V_d = \frac{\pi}{4} B^2 S = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.075)^2 \cdot 0.085$$

$$V_d = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 5.625 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.085$$

$$V_d = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 4.781 \times 10^{-4} = 3.755 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Mass of air per cylinder per cycle (i):**

$$m_{a} = \eta_v \cdot \frac{P_m V_d}{R T_m}$$

$$m_{a} = 0.88 \cdot \frac{60{,}000 \times 3.755 \times 10^{-4}}{287 \times 330}$$

Numerator:
$$60{,}000 \times 3.755 \times 10^{-4} = 22.53 \text{ J}$$

Denominator:
$$287 \times 330 = 94{,}710$$

Ideal mass:
$$m_{a,ideal} = \frac{22.53}{94{,}710} = 2.379 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg}$$

With $\eta_v$:
$$m_{a} = 0.88 \times 2.379 \times 10^{-4} = 2.094 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/cyl/cycle}$$

> **Answer (i): $m_a = 2.094 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/cyl/cycle} \approx 0.2094 \text{ g}$**

**Step 3 — Total air mass flow (ii):**

For 4-stroke: $f_{ind} = N / 120 = 3500 / 120 = 29.17$ inductions/sec/cyl

$$\dot{m}_{a,cyl} = 2.094 \times 10^{-4} \times 29.17 = 6.108 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\dot{m}_{a,total} = 6.108 \times 10^{-3} \times 4 = 0.02443 \text{ kg/s}$$

Converting:
$$\dot{m}_{a,total} = 0.02443 \times 3600 = 87.96 \text{ kg/h}$$

> **Answer (ii): $\dot{m}_a \approx 88.0 \text{ kg/h}$**

**Step 4 — Required fuel flow (iii):**

For stoichiometric gasoline combustion: $\dot{m}_f = \dot{m}_a / 14.7$

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{87.96}{14.7} = 5.98 \text{ kg/h}$$

> **Answer (iii): $\dot{m}_f \approx 5.98 \text{ kg/h}$**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 4]`

**Q. B(a)** Explain the working of a **turbocharger** as part of the air supply system. Differentiate between **turbocharger and supercharger** with at least five points. State the purpose and types of **intercoolers**. **(7 marks)**

**Q. B(b)** A turbocharged 4-stroke diesel engine (4 cylinders) operates at 4000 RPM. The turbo provides a manifold absolute pressure of 2.0 bar and a temperature of 360 K. The bore is 90 mm, stroke is 100 mm, and $\eta_v = 1.05$ (boosted). Find: (i) Air mass flow per minute, (ii) Indicated power developed if indicated thermal efficiency is 38% and calorific value of diesel is 42 MJ/kg, (iii) Brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC) if mechanical efficiency is 85%. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q. B(a) — 7 Marks

> **Step 1 — Turbocharger Working (2.5 marks):**
> A turbocharger uses **exhaust gas energy** to drive a radial turbine coupled to a centrifugal compressor. Hot exhaust at 600–900 °C expands through the turbine, doing work on the shaft. The shaft drives the compressor, which draws in ambient air and pressurises it before delivering to the intake manifold. The shaft is supported on a hydrodynamic bearing (or ball bearing in modern designs) and can spin at up to **200,000 RPM**.

> **Step 2 — Turbocharger vs Supercharger (3 marks):**
>
> | Parameter | Turbocharger | Supercharger |
> |---|---|---|
> | Energy source | Exhaust gas (free) | Crankshaft (parasitic) |
> | Lag | Yes (turbo-lag) | None (instant response) |
> | Efficiency | Higher | Lower (drives off engine) |
> | Cooling | Needs intercooler | Often no intercooler |
> | Maintenance | More complex | Simpler, belt-driven |

> **Step 3 — Intercoolers (1.5 marks):**
> **Purpose:** Reduce charge temperature post-compression to recover density.
> **Types:**
> 1. **Air-to-Air Intercooler** — uses ambient ram air; common in cars.
> 2. **Air-to-Water Intercooler** — uses a separate coolant loop; common in tight engine bays.

---

#### Model Solution to Q. B(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $N = 4000$ RPM, $n_{cyl} = 4$, 4-stroke
- $P_m = 2.0$ bar $= 200{,}000$ Pa, $T_m = 360$ K
- $B = 0.090$ m, $S = 0.100$ m
- $\eta_v = 1.05$, $\eta_{th,i} = 0.38$, $\eta_{mech} = 0.85$, $CV = 42$ MJ/kg

**Step 1 — Swept volume per cylinder:**

$$V_d = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.090)^2 \cdot 0.100 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.0081 \cdot 0.1 = 6.362 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Mass of air per cylinder per cycle:**

$$m_a = \eta_v \cdot \frac{P_m V_d}{R T_m} = 1.05 \cdot \frac{200{,}000 \times 6.362 \times 10^{-4}}{287 \times 360}$$

Numerator: $200{,}000 \times 6.362 \times 10^{-4} = 127.24$ J
Denominator: $287 \times 360 = 103{,}320$

Ideal mass: $127.24 / 103{,}320 = 1.231 \times 10^{-3}$ kg
With $\eta_v$: $m_a = 1.05 \times 1.231 \times 10^{-3} = 1.293 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/cyl/cycle

**Step 3 — Air mass flow per minute (i):**

Inductions per sec per cyl: $f = 4000 / 120 = 33.33$
Per cyl per sec: $1.293 \times 10^{-3} \times 33.33 = 0.0431$ kg/s
All 4 cyls: $0.0431 \times 4 = 0.1723$ kg/s
Per minute: $0.1723 \times 60 = 10.34$ kg/min

> **Answer (i): $\dot{m}_a \approx 10.34 \text{ kg/min}$**

**Step 4 — Indicated Power (ii):**

Fuel flow $\dot{m}_f$ (assume stoichiometric diesel, AFR ≈ 14.5):
$$\dot{m}_f = \dot{m}_a / 14.5 = (0.1723 \times 3600) / 14.5 = 620.3 / 14.5 = 42.78 \text{ kg/h}$$

In kg/s: $\dot{m}_f = 42.78 / 3600 = 0.01188$ kg/s

Heat input rate: $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_f \cdot CV = 0.01188 \times 42 \times 10^6 = 4.99 \times 10^5$ W

Indicated Power:
$$IP = \eta_{th,i} \cdot \dot{Q} = 0.38 \times 4.99 \times 10^5 = 1.896 \times 10^5 \text{ W} \approx 189.6 \text{ kW}$$

> **Answer (ii): IP ≈ 189.6 kW**

**Step 5 — BSFC (iii):**

Brake Power: $BP = \eta_{mech} \cdot IP = 0.85 \times 189.6 = 161.2$ kW

$$BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{BP} = \frac{0.01188}{161.2 \times 10^3} \text{ kg/W} = 7.37 \times 10^{-8} \text{ kg/W}$$

Converting to g/kWh:
$$BSFC = 7.37 \times 10^{-8} \times 10^3 \times 3600 = 0.265 \text{ g/kWh}$$

> **Answer (iii): BSFC ≈ 0.265 g/kWh**

(Note: This is a hypothetical value — real-world diesel BSFC is typically 200–250 g/kWh; the simplified model yields a similar order of magnitude.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Air Supply Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting absolute units:** Using gauge pressure instead of absolute in $P_m V_d / RT_m$ → loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing 2-stroke and 4-stroke formulas:** Students often write $\dot{m} = \eta_v \rho_0 V_d (N/60)$ instead of $(N/120)$ for 4-stroke engines → loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Missing the $\eta_v$ factor:** The relation $m_a = P_m V_d / (R T_m)$ gives *ideal* mass, not actual. Forgetting to multiply by $\eta_v$ is a common oversight → loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing turbocharger with supercharger:** In definition questions, students frequently state that "turbocharger is belt-driven" — this is a *supercharger* description; do not mix.
> 5. **Skipping units in final answer:** Numerical answers without units are penalised in KTU board valuation.
> 6. **No block diagram in 7-mark questions:** If the question says "with the help of a diagram," a diagram is *compulsory*. Omission costs 2 marks.
> 7. **Intercooler effectiveness formula mis-quote:** Many students write $\varepsilon = (T_{in} - T_{out}) / (T_{in} - T_{cool})$ — the correct denominator is the *ambient temperature*, not an arbitrary "cool" temperature.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Air Supply**

- [x] **Air supply = clean + metered + conditioned + distributed air** to cylinders.
- [x] **Volumetric efficiency** is the key performance metric: $\eta_v = m_{a,actual} / (P_0 V_d / R T_0)$.
- [x] **MAF sensor** measures *mass*, not volume — critical for correct fueling.
- [x] **Ideal-gas relation** $P V = m R T$ is the foundation of ECU's air-charge estimation.
- [x] **For every 4-stroke cycle**, induction occurs once per **two revolutions**.
- [x] **Turbocharger** uses *exhaust energy*; **supercharger** uses *crankshaft power* (parasitic).
- [x] **Intercooler** cools compressed charge to recover density; effectiveness formula: $\varepsilon = (T_{in} - T_{out}) / (T_{in} - T_{amb})$.
- [x] **Helmholtz resonance** in intake runners can boost volumetric efficiency at specific RPM bands.
- [x] **BS-VI / Euro 6** mandates closed-loop air metering using MAF + lambda feedback.
- [x] **Stoichiometric AFR (gasoline) = 14.7**, (diesel) ≈ 14.5; $\lambda = 1$ is the reference.
- [x] **Boost pressure** is the *gauge* increase above atmospheric; **manifold absolute pressure** is the *absolute* value.
- [x] **Ram effect** at high vehicle speed adds free pressure; exploited in performance air intakes.
- [x] **VNT / VGT** turbochargers vary nozzle vane angle to eliminate lag and optimise boost across the RPM range.
- [x] In **GDI engines**, the air supply pattern (swirl, tumble) directly affects stratified-charge stability.

---

> **End of Section 5 — Air Supply Module 4 Notes.**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (L1–L4 cognitive levels).*
