7. **Vehicle-side HV Junction Box** — closes internal contactors, routes power to the battery.
8. **Battery Management System (BMS)** — the master controller. Requests voltage and current from the station.
9. **Thermal Management** — liquid cooling keeps the cells in the 20–40 °C window.

*(Examiner expects a labelled block diagram here — see Section 4.1.)*

**Part (b):**

**(i) Energy drawn from the grid:**

$$E_{\text{stored}} = (0.90 - 0.20) \times 64 = 0.70 \times 64 = 44.8 \text{ kWh}$$

$$E_{\text{grid}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{stored}}}{\eta} = \dfrac{44.8}{0.92} = 48.70 \text{ kWh}$$

**(ii) Charging time (derated power = 110 kW):**

$$t = \dfrac{E_{\text{grid}}}{P_{\text{derated}}} = \dfrac{48.70}{110} = 0.4427 \text{ h} \approx 26.56 \text{ minutes}$$

**(iii) Range added:**

$$\text{Range} = \dfrac{44.8}{16} \times 100 = 280 \text{ km}$$

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper Style]**
> **Q.** (a) Compare **CHAdeMO, CCS, and NACS** DC fast-charging standards in terms of maximum power, connector design, communication protocol, and regional adoption. Why has **CCS emerged as the global standard** in most regions outside China and Japan? **(7 marks)**
>
> (b) A battery cell with internal resistance **$3 \text{ m}\Omega$** and capacity **60 Ah** is charged at **3C** rate for **15 minutes**.
> - (i) Calculate the charging current. **(2 marks)**
> - (ii) Calculate the heat generated in the cell. **(3 marks)**
> - (iii) If the cell mass is **0.40 kg** and specific heat is **1050 J/kg·K**, what is the temperature rise? Assume adiabatic conditions. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a):** Comparison table:

| Feature | CHAdeMO | CCS | NACS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Max Power | 400 kW (v2.0) | 350 kW | 250 kW (V3) |
| Connector | Round, large | Type 1/2 + 2 DC pins | Compact, Tesla design |
| Communication | CAN bus | PLC (ISO 15118) | CAN bus |
| Region | Japan, partly EU | EU, USA, Korea, AU | USA (now global) |
| V2G Support | Yes (native) | Yes (ISO 15118-20) | Yes (via ISO 15118) |

**Why CCS dominates:** CCS uses the **same Type 1/Type 2 AC connector for slow charging** and adds 2 high-current DC pins below it — a single inlet handles both AC and DC. It uses **PLC over the pilot line**, eliminating the dedicated CAN bus wire of CHAdeMO. The **ISO 15118** standard enables "Plug & Charge" and V2G, which CHAdeMO's older protocol lacks natively.

**Part (b):**

**(i) Charging current:**

$$I = 3 \times 60 = 180 \text{ A}$$

**(ii) Heat generated:**

$$Q = I^2 R t = (180)^2 \times 3 \times 10^{-3} \times (15 \times 60)$$

$$= 32{,}400 \times 3 \times 10^{-3} \times 900$$

$$= 32{,}400 \times 2.7 = 87{,}480 \text{ J} = 87.48 \text{ kJ}$$

**(iii) Temperature rise:**

$$\Delta T = \dfrac{87{,}480}{0.40 \times 1050} = \dfrac{87{,}480}{420} = 208.29 \text{ °C}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Students frequently forget to convert **minutes to seconds** in the Joule heating equation $Q = I^2 R t$. This costs **1 mark minimum**.
> 2. **Forgetting the efficiency factor:** In charging-time problems, many students compute $t = E / P$ directly. The correct formula is $t = E_{\text{grid}} / P$ where $E_{\text{grid}} = E_{\text{stored}} / \eta$.
> 3. **Ignoring the C-rate taper:** Examiners sometimes ask *"Why doesn't a 350 kW charger fully charge a 75 kWh pack in 13 minutes?"* The answer is the **CV taper** above 70% SoC — always mention this in long answers.
> 4. **Confusing AC and DC Level terminology:** SAE J1772 Level 3 = **DC** fast charging only. Saying "Level 3 AC" is a mark-losing error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fast-Charging Technologies**

- **Definition:** Fast charging = DC Level 3 charging, **50–350+ kW**, restores 80% SoC in 15–30 min.
- **Core Equation:** $P = V \times I$ — increase V and I to raise power.
- **Why DC, not AC?** Bypasses the on-board charger; off-board rectifier delivers DC directly to the battery.
- **Key Standards:**
  - **CCS2** → Global standard (Europe, US, Korea)
  - **CHAdeMO** → Japan, supports V2G natively
  - **NACS (SAE J3400)** → Tesla, now adopted by Ford, GM, Rivian
  - **GB/T** → China
  - **MCS** → Heavy-duty trucks (1000+ kW)
- **Charging Curve Phases:** Precharge → Constant Power → CV Taper → Trickle.
- **Thermal Rule:** Cells can heat at 3–5 °C/min during 150 kW charging → **liquid cooling is mandatory**.
- **Communication:** ISO 15118 (PLC over pilot line) enables **Plug & Charge** and **V2G**.
- **Efficiency:** Station end-to-end = 85–93%. Loss mechanisms: rectification (~2%), DC-DC conversion (~3–5%), cable I²R (~1–2%).
- **C-rate:** Fast charging = typically >1C; the 2C–4C range is the modern target.
- **Safety Cutoffs:** Cell voltage must stay below **4.2 V (NMC)** / **4.35 V (NCA)**; cell temp must stay below **60 °C**.
- **Charging Time Formula (must memorize):**
$$t_{\text{charge}} = \dfrac{(SoC_{\text{final}} - SoC_{\text{initial}}) \times E_{\text{batt}}}{\eta \cdot P_{\text{charger}}}$$
- **Joule Heating Formula (must memorize):**
$$Q_{\text{heat}} = I^2 R_{\text{cell}} \cdot t$$
- **Bidirectional Charging (V2G):** The future — EVs supply power back to the grid during peak hours, monetizing parked battery capacity.
- **Taper Limitation:** Real-world 80% charge happens ~3× faster than 80→100% — always cite this when asked "why not 100% fast?"

> **One-Line Mantra for the Exam:**
> *"Fast charging is not just high power — it is high power **plus** intelligent BMS control, active thermal management, and PLC-based digital handshake via ISO 15118."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Second-Life Applications of EV Batteries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET414 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Second-Life Applications of EV Batteries ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme Engineering education, **Second-Life Applications of EV Batteries** refer to the **cascaded reuse of automotive lithium-ion battery packs** that have degraded below the threshold required for traction applications (typically **70% to 80% of their original State of Health, SOH**) in less demanding **stationary energy storage applications** before final recycling. According to the **KTU 2024 Module 4 syllabus (PEIET414)**, this topic is classified under *Energy Management and End-of-Life Strategies for EV Powertrains*, bridging the gap between electrochemical engineering, sustainable manufacturing, and power systems. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEIET414 / Module 4):** *Second-life applications of EV batteries – repurposing, grid storage, backup power, microgrid integration, and challenges in reuse.* Mathematically, the **End-of-First-Life (EOF-L)** criterion is defined as: $$SOH_{\text{EOF-L}} = \frac{C_{\text{actual}}}{C_{\text{nominal}}} \times 100\% \leq 80\%$$ Where: - $C_{\text{actual}}$ = Current measurable capacity of the battery (in Ah) - $C_{\text{nominal}}$ = Original rated capacity when the battery was new (in Ah) - **80%** is the universally accepted automotive cut-off threshold (per **USABC / EUCAR** standards) ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of an **EV battery like a human's professional career**: - **First Life (Automotive Use):** A 40-year-old athlete runs marathons — demanding, high-performance, requiring peak power and rapid bursts of energy. This is the **"prime career"** of the battery. - **Retirement (End of First Life):** At 50, the athlete can no longer run marathons at peak speed, but is still strong enough to **walk, teach, or manage**. - **Second Life (Stationary Storage):** The same person is now employed as a **trainer, coach, or security guard** — they still have value, but the demands are different. - **Recycling (End of Second Life):** Eventually, when the battery degrades to **60–70% SOH**, even stationary applications become unviable, and the materials (lithium, cobalt, nickel) are recovered to make new batteries. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for KTU Students:** The battery is *not dead* at the end of its automotive life. It still retains **70% to 90% residual capacity** and can serve 5–10 more productive years in stationary applications. ### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 - **Sustainable Engineering Mandate (NEP 2020):** Aligns with SDG 7 (Affordable and Clean Energy) and SDG 12 (Responsible Consumption and Production). - **Circular Economy Principle:** Reduces mining pressure on critical raw materials (Lithium, Cobalt, Nickel). - **Economic Viability:** A new EV battery costs **₹12,000–₹18,000 per kWh** (2024 market), while a second-life battery costs **₹3,000–₹6,000 per kWh** — a **60–70% cost reduction**. ### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants | Parameter | Standard Value | Significance | |-----------|---------------|--------------| | **First-Life Cut-off SOH** | **80%** | Automotive application end-of-life | | **Second-Life Cut-off SOH** | **60% to 70%** | Stationary application end-of-life | | **Residual Capacity Range** | **70% to 90%** | Usable energy for second-life | | **Repurposing Efficiency** | **85% to 95%** | Energy retained after re-packaging | | **Second-Life Duration** | **5 to 10 years** | Calendar life in stationary use | | **Round-Trip Efficiency** | **85% to 92%** | AC-AC for LFP-based packs | > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** Always quote the **80% SOH threshold** in short-answer questions. It is the most frequently tested figure in KTU ESE for this topic. --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The "Why" Behind Second-Life Applications The fundamental driver behind second-life battery applications is rooted in three engineering realities: 1. **Capacity Fade is Gradual, Not Catastrophic** - EV batteries do not fail abruptly. They undergo slow, predictable electrochemical degradation. - Typical automotive warranty guarantees **70% SOH for 8 years or 1,60,000 km** (as per AIS-156 / IEC 62660 standards). - Beyond this warranty, the battery is *replaced in the vehicle* but is *perfectly usable* elsewhere. 2. **The "Energy vs. Power" Demand Mismatch** - **Automotive applications** require *high power density* (rapid acceleration, regenerative braking) and tolerate a narrow SOC window (typically 20%–80%). - **Stationary applications** require *high energy density* (long-duration discharge) and tolerate a wider SOC window (10%–90%). - A battery retired from automotive use still has **excellent energy capability** but reduced power capability — making it ideal for stationary use. 3. **Economic and Environmental Imperative** - Manufacturing a new battery incurs significant **embedded carbon** (~150 kg CO₂ per kWh). - Reusing an existing battery **avoids 60% to 75% of manufacturing emissions**. ### 2.2 Operational Concept: The Cascaded Use Pipeline The second-life ecosystem operates through a structured **5-stage pipeline**: - **Stage 1 — First-Life Operation in EV** - Battery operates in traction application. - Subjected to high C-rates (1C to 3C continuous, 5C peak). - Thermal stress and cyclic stress cause capacity fade. - When SOH drops to **≤ 80%**, the battery is retired from automotive service. - **Stage 2 — Collection and Transportation** - Batteries are collected from dealerships, scrap yards, or end-of-life vehicles. - Transported to a **Battery Repurposing Facility (BRF)** under controlled conditions (per **UN 38.3** transport regulations). - The battery's provenance, usage history, and BMS logs are retrieved. - **Stage 3 — Testing and Sorting (Characterization)** - Each module is subjected to **Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS)**, capacity testing, and internal resistance measurement. - Cells are sorted into **homogeneous groups** based on capacity, impedance, and SOH. - **Stage 4 — Reconfiguration and Re-packaging** - Modules are disassembled, tested at cell level, and reassembled into new packs. - A new or refurbished **Battery Management System (BMS)** is installed. - The pack is rated for its new application (e.g., 100 kWh grid storage unit). - **Stage 5 — Second-Life Deployment** - The repurposed pack is installed in a stationary storage system. - It operates at lower C-rates (0.2C to 0.5C) and gentler cycling. - Monitored remotely until it reaches the second-life cut-off (typically **60% to 70% SOH**). ### 2.3 Primary Second-Life Application Categories | Application | Power Rating | Energy Capacity | Key Requirement | |-------------|-------------|-----------------|-----------------| | **Grid-Scale Energy Storage** | 1 MW to 100 MW | 1 MWh to 100 MWh | High energy density, long duration | | **Renewable Integration (Solar/Wind)** | 100 kW to 10 MW | 500 kWh to 50 MWh | Cyclic stability, deep DOD | | **Behind-the-Meter (BTM) Residential** | 5 kW to 10 kW | 10 kWh to 50 kWh | Safety, compact form factor | | **Commercial / Industrial UPS** | 50 kW to 500 kW | 200 kWh to 2 MWh | High reliability, fast response | | **EV Charging Buffer (V2G)** | 50 kW to 350 kW | 100 kWh to 1 MWh | High power capability | | **Off-Grid / Microgrid** | 10 kW to 500 kW | 50 kWh to 5 MWh | Long cycle life, low maintenance | | **Telecom Tower Backup** | 3 kW to 10 kW | 20 kWh to 100 kWh | Reliable float operation | ### 2.4 The "How": Detailed Engineering Logic #### 2.4.1 Capacity Fade Mechanism Recap Capacity fade in lithium-ion batteries arises from three primary mechanisms: - **Loss of Active Material (LAM):** Due to particle cracking, SEI growth, and structural collapse. - **Loss of Lithium Inventory (LLI):** Due to parasitic side reactions consuming cyclable Li⁺ ions. - **Kinetic Degradation (Increased Impedance):** Due to electrolyte depletion and contact loss. These mechanisms are **less critical in stationary applications** because: - Stationary use involves **lower C-rates** (less thermal stress). - Stationary use involves **slower cycling** (fewer cycles per year, typically 200–300 vs. 1000+ in automotive). - The **anode overhang** and **excess electrolyte** in EV cells can be more fully utilized in stationary applications. #### 2.4.2 The Repurposing Decision Matrix A battery module is suitable for second-life use **only if** the following criteria are met: $$SOH_{\text{module}} \geq 70\%$$ $$R_{\text{internal, module}} \leq 1.5 \times R_{\text{internal, new}}$$ $$\sigma_{\text{capacity, pack}} \leq 5\%$$ $$\text{Leakage current} \leq 1 \text{ mA per cell}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Heterogeneity Problem:** EV battery packs consist of cells with **varying SOH** (some at 85%, some at 65%). This "**cell-to-cell variability**" is the **single biggest technical challenge** in repurposing. It requires **careful sorting and rebalancing** before second-life deployment. ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Expression | Description | Typical Units | |---|----------------------|-------------|---------------| | 1 | $SOH = \frac{C_{\text{actual}}}{C_{\text{nominal}}} \times 100\%$ | State of Health definition | % | | 2 | $E_{\text{remaining}} = C_{\text{actual}} \times V_{\text{nominal}} \times \eta_{\text{DOD}}$ | Remaining usable energy | kWh | | 3 | $C_{\text{rate}} = \frac{I_{\text{discharge}}}{C_{\text{nominal}}}$ | C-rate definition | Dimensionless | | 4 | $R_{\text{internal, max}} = R_{\text{internal, new}} \times (1 + 0.5 \times N_{\text{cycles}} / 1000)$ | Impedance growth estimate | mΩ | | 5 | $\text{Repurposing Cost} = C_{\text{transport}} + C_{\text{testing}} + C_{\text{reassembly}}$ | Total repurposing cost | ₹ per kWh | | 6 | $\text{LCOS} = \frac{C_{\text{capital}} + C_{\text{O\&M}}}{E_{\text{discharged, lifetime}}}$ | Levelized Cost of Storage | ₹ per kWh | | 7 | $\text{Carbon Saved} = E_{\text{reused}} \times 150 \, \text{kg CO}_2/\text{kWh}$ | Embedded carbon avoided | kg CO₂ | | 8 | $\text{Cycle Life}_{\text{2nd-life}} \approx 2000 \text{ to } 4000 \text{ cycles}$ | Expected cycles in stationary use | cycles | | 9 | $P_{\text{max,continuous}} = V_{\text{oc}} \times I_{\text{max,continuous}}$ | Maximum power output | kW | | 10 | $\eta_{\text{RT}} = \frac{E_{\text{discharged}}}{E_{\text{charged}}} \times 100\%$ | Round-trip efficiency | % | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Valuation Trap:** Do **NOT** confuse **SOH (State of Health)** with **SOC (State of Charge)**. SOH is a *lifetime degradation metric*; SOC is an *instantaneous charge level*. Examiners frequently test this distinction. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Nissan-Sumitomo JV (4R Energy, Japan):** Repurposed Leaf batteries for streetlight and grid storage. The Yokohama Plant uses **600 repurposed Leaf battery packs** for a 2.7 MWh grid storage system. - **BMW i3 → Bosch / EVgo (USA):** BMW partnered with Bosch to deploy 100 second-life i3 battery units in 5 US cities for fast-charging buffers. - **Tesla Powerwall 2 / Megapack (USA):** Tesla uses both new and repurposed cells in stationary storage. The Nevada Gigafactory has a dedicated repurposing line. - **Renault Re-Factory (France):** Renault's end-of-life facility repurposes Zoe batteries into 1.2 MWh mobile storage units for construction sites. - **Ather Energy (India):** The Indian EV OEM has piloted second-life battery deployment for telecom tower backup in Karnataka and Tamil Nadu. ### 2.7 Challenges in Second-Life Applications | Challenge | Description | Mitigation Strategy | |-----------|-------------|---------------------| | **Cell Heterogeneity** | Variability in SOH across cells | Sorting, screening, and rebalancing | | **Unknown Usage History** | Lack of BMS data from first life | Mandatory BMS log retrieval, OBD-II diagnostics | | **Safety Risks** | Higher thermal runaway probability | Advanced BMS, fire suppression, derated operation | | **Economic Viability** | Repurposing cost may exceed new battery cost at high SOH | Optimize at **70%–80% SOH** sweet spot | | **Regulatory Uncertainty** | No universal certification for second-life packs | Develop **IEC 62933** and **AIS-156** compliant testing | | **Logistics** | Collection, transport of heavy packs | Develop regional collection networks | --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Useful Residual Energy After First-Life Retirement This derivation is a **KTU high-yield problem** and frequently appears in Part B (14-mark) questions. **Problem:** A 60 kWh EV battery pack (NMC chemistry) has completed 1500 full charge-discharge cycles at 80% Depth of Discharge (DOD). The nominal voltage is 360 V, and the nominal capacity is 167 Ah. The capacity fade per 1000 cycles is empirically determined to be **8% of nominal capacity**. Calculate: (a) The State of Health (SOH) after first-life use. (b) The remaining usable energy if the battery is repurposed for a stationary application with 90% DOD. (c) The equivalent number of cycles possible in second-life operation if the cycle life in stationary use is 3500 cycles. **Given:** - $C_{\text{nominal}} = 167$ Ah - $V_{\text{nominal}} = 360$ V - $E_{\text{nominal}} = 60$ kWh - Number of cycles completed: $N_{\text{cycles}} = 1500$ - Capacity fade rate: $\Delta C = 8\%$ per 1000 cycles - $\text{DOD}_{\text{stationary}} = 90\%$ **Step 1: Calculate the SOH** The capacity fade per 1000 cycles is 8%. Over 1500 cycles, the total capacity loss is: $$\Delta C_{\text{total}} = \frac{1500}{1000} \times 8\% = 12\%$$ The current capacity is: $$C_{\text{actual}} = C_{\text{nominal}} \times \left(1 - \frac{\Delta C_{\text{total}}}{100}\right)$$ $$C_{\text{actual}} = 167 \times (1 - 0.12) = 167 \times 0.88$$ $$C_{\text{actual}} = 146.96 \text{ Ah}$$ The State of Health is: $$SOH = \frac{C_{\text{actual}}}{C_{\text{nominal}}} \times 100\%$$ $$SOH = \frac{146.96}{167} \times 100\%$$ $$\boxed{SOH = 88.0\%}$$ **Step 2: Calculate the remaining usable energy for stationary use** The nominal energy equation is: $$E_{\text{nominal}} = V_{\text{nominal}} \times C_{\text{nominal}}$$ The remaining energy at the current SOH is: $$E_{\text{remaining}} = V_{\text{nominal}} \times C_{\text{actual}} \times \eta_{\text{DOD}}$$ Where $\eta_{\text{DOD}} = 0.90$ (for stationary application). Substituting values: $$E_{\text{remaining}} = 360 \times 146.96 \times 0.90$$ $$E_{\text{remaining}} = 47,615.04 \text{ Wh}$$ $$E_{\text{remaining}} = 47.62 \text{ kWh}$$ $$\boxed{E_{\text{remaining, stationary}} \approx 47.6 \text{ kWh}}$$ **Step 3: Equivalent number of cycles possible in second-life operation** For stationary applications, the effective cycle life at 80% SOH (start of second life) versus 60% SOH (end of second life) is approximately: $$\Delta SOH_{\text{2nd-life}} = 88\% - 60\% = 28\%$$ Given a typical second-life cycle capacity fade of **7% per 1000 cycles** in stationary applications: $$N_{\text{cycles, 2nd-life}} = \frac{28}{7} \times 1000$$ $$N_{\text{cycles, 2nd-life}} = 4000 \text{ cycles}$$ $$\boxed{N_{\text{cycles, 2nd-life}} \approx 4000 \text{ cycles}}$$ **Verification (Sanity Check):** - The SOH of 88% is *above* the 80% first-life cut-off, but the problem assumes it has been retired. In real practice, the user may opt for early retirement. - The remaining 47.6 kWh represents **~79% of the original 60 kWh** capacity. - 4000 cycles at 1 cycle/day gives **~11 years** of stationary use — consistent with the typical 5–10 year second-life duration. > [!NOTE] > **This exact numerical problem type has appeared in KTU 2022 and 2023 university exams.** Practice the substitution of values into the formula. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of First-Life vs. Second-Life Operating Regimes | Parameter | First-Life (Automotive) | Second-Life (Stationary) | |-----------|------------------------|--------------------------| | **Typical Application** | EV traction | Grid / residential storage | | **C-Rate Range** | 1C to 3C (continuous), 5C (peak) | 0.2C to 0.5C | | **Depth of Discharge (DOD)** | 70% to 80% | 80% to 90% | | **Cycles per Year** | 800 to 1500 | 200 to 350 | | **Calendar Life** | 8 to 10 years | 5 to 10 years (additional) | | **Thermal Stress** | High (active cooling required) | Low (natural convection adequate) | | **Vibration / Shock** | High (road conditions) | Minimal | | **Power Density Required** | High (1000 to 3000 W/kg) | Moderate (200 to 500 W/kg) | | **Energy Density Required** | Moderate (150 to 250 Wh/kg) | High (volume not constrained) | | **BMS Complexity** | Very high | Moderate (relaxed safety margins) | | **End-of-Life SOH** | 70% to 80% | 60% to 70% | | **Cost per kWh** | ₹12,000 to ₹18,000 (new) | ₹3,000 to ₹6,000 (repurposed) | ### 3.3 Step-by-Step: Economic Viability Calculation **Problem:** A repurposed 50 kWh battery pack costs ₹2,50,000 to procure, test, and repackage. It is to be deployed in a commercial UPS application. The expected cycle life is 3000 cycles. The annual energy throughput is 12,000 kWh. The annual O&M cost is ₹15,000. The discount rate is 8%. Calculate the **Levelized Cost of Storage (LCOS)** over its second life. **Step 1: Total capital cost** $$C_{\text{capital}} = \text{₹} 2{,}50{,}000$$ **Step 2: Total energy discharged over lifetime** $$E_{\text{lifetime}} = 3000 \times 50 \text{ kWh} = 1{,}50{,}000 \text{ kWh}$$ **Step 3: Total O&M cost (undiscounted)** $$C_{\text{O\&M,total}} = ₹15{,}000 \times 10 = ₹1{,}50{,}000 \text{ (for 10 years)}$$ **Step 4: Total cost (undiscounted)** $$C_{\text{total}} = ₹2{,}50{,}000 + ₹1{,}50{,}000 = ₹4{,}00{,}000$$ **Step 5: LCOS calculation** $$LCOS = \frac{C_{\text{total}}}{E_{\text{lifetime}}}$$ $$LCOS = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{1{,}50{,}000}$$ $$LCOS = ₹2.67 \text{ per kWh}$$ $$\boxed{LCOS \approx ₹2.67/\text{kWh}}$$ > [!TIP] > **Interpretation for KTU Answers:** This LCOS is **30% to 50% lower** than a new battery-based UPS system (typically ₹4–₹6 per kWh), confirming the **economic viability** of second-life applications. ### 3.4 Mathematical Relationship: SOH vs. Cycle Count The capacity fade in lithium-ion batteries can be approximated by a **square-root model**: $$Q_{\text{loss}} = k \times \sqrt{N_{\text{cycles}}}$$ Where: - $Q_{\text{loss}}$ = Fractional capacity loss (dimensionless) - $k$ = Empirical fade constant (typically 0.001 to 0.005) - $N_{\text{cycles}}$ = Number of cycles Rearranging for SOH: $$SOH = 1 - k \times \sqrt{N_{\text{cycles}}}$$ **Example:** If $k = 0.0025$ and $N = 1000$: $$SOH = 1 - 0.0025 \times \sqrt{1000} = 1 - 0.0025 \times 31.62 = 1 - 0.0791 = 0.921$$ $$SOH = 92.1\%$$ This aligns with industry observations that capacity loss is **fastest in the first 200 cycles** and **decelerates** over time. --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Second-Life Battery Value Chain The following Mermaid diagram maps the **end-to-end second-life ecosystem** from automotive retirement to final recycling, with all key stakeholders, processes, and decision gates. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Stage 1: First-Life EV Use
SOH 100% to 80%
80 to 1500 cycles/year] --> B[End of First-Life Trigger
SOH Less Than or Equal To 80%
OR Vehicle Scrappage] B --> C[Stage 2: Collection and Logistics
Dealer Take-Back
Transport to BRF per UN 38.3] C --> D[Stage 3: Battery Repurposing Facility
BRF Entry Inspection
OBD Data Retrieval] D --> E[Module-Level Testing
Capacity Test
EIS Impedance
Visual Inspection] E --> F[Cell-Level Sorting
Sort by SOH and Impedance
Reject Cells Below 60% SOH] F --> G{Reconfiguration
Decision Gate} G -->|SOH Above 70%| H[Stage 4: Re-Pack into New Pack
Install New BMS
Certification IEC 62660] G -->|SOH 60% to 70%| I[Direct Recycling Path
Hydrometallurgical Recovery] H --> J[Stage 5: Second-Life Deployment
Grid Storage OR BTM OR Telecom] J --> K[Operate at 0.2C to 0.5C
5 to 10 Years Duration

Final Recycling
Material Recovery Li Co Ni] A --> A1[OEM Warranty Support
AIS 156 Standard
8 Years or 160000 km] J --> J1[Remote Monitoring
Cloud BMS
Predictive Maintenance] L --> L1[Circular Economy Loop
Closed Loop Material Flow] style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style B fill:#dc2626,stroke:#dc2626,color:#ffffff style C fill:#ea580c,stroke:#ea580c,color:#ffffff style D fill:#ca8a04,stroke:#ca8a04,color:#ffffff style E fill:#16a34a,stroke:#16a34a,color:#ffffff style F fill:#16a34a,stroke:#16a34a,color:#ffffff style G fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff style H fill:#0891b2,stroke:#0891b2,color:#ffffff style I fill:#be185d,stroke:#be185d,color:#ffffff style J fill:#0891b2,stroke:#0891b2,color:#ffffff style K fill:#0891b2,stroke:#0891b2,color:#ffffff style L fill:#be185d,stroke:#be185d,color:#ffffff style A1 fill:#475569,stroke:#475569,color:#ffffff style J1 fill:#475569,stroke:#475569,color:#ffffff style L1 fill:#475569,stroke:#475569,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Functional Block Architecture of a Second-Life Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) The following diagram illustrates the **internal architecture** of a stationary second-life BESS, including the battery modules, BMS, power conversion system, and grid interface. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Second-Life Battery Pack

TMS

PCS Bidirectional Inverter

PCC Point of Common Coupling] A --> A1[Cell 1 SOH 88%] A --> A2[Cell 2 SOH 82%] A --> A3[Cell 3 SOH 76%] A --> A4[Cell 4 SOH 71%] B --> B1[State of Health Estimator

Stationary Application] CheckSOH -->|SOH 60% to 70%| Reuse2[Proceed to Light Duty Reuse
Telecom OR Low Power UPS] CheckSOH -->|SOH Below 60%| Recycle[Direct to Recycling
Material Recovery] Reuse --> ReTest[Module Re-Test] ReTest --> Reuse3[Re-Configure into New Pack] Reuse2 --> ReTest2[Light Re-Packaging] ReTest2 --> Reuse4[Deploy as Telecom OR UPS] Recycle --> Recovery[Hydrometallurgical Recovery
Li Co Ni Mn] style Start fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style Test fill:#0891b2,stroke:#0891b2,color:#ffffff style CheckSOH fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff style Refuse fill:#dc2626,stroke:#dc2626,color:#ffffff style Reuse fill:#16a34a,stroke:#16a34a,color:#ffffff style Reuse2 fill:#16a34a,stroke:#16a34a,color:#ffffff style Recycle fill:#be185d,stroke:#be185d,color:#ffffff style ReTest fill:#0891b2,stroke:#0891b2,color:#ffffff style Reuse3 fill:#16a34a,stroke:#16a34a,color:#ffffff style ReTest2 fill:#0891b2,stroke:#0891b2,color:#ffffff style Reuse4 fill:#16a34a,stroke:#16a34a,color:#ffffff style Recovery fill:#be185d,stroke:#be185d,color:#ffffff ``` --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper]` **What is meant by "Second-Life Application" of an EV battery? State the typical State of Health (SOH) threshold at which an EV battery is considered for second-life use.** **Model Answer:** Second-life application refers to the **reuse of EV battery packs in non-automotive stationary applications** (such as grid storage, backup power, or residential energy storage) after they have degraded below the **80% State of Health (SOH)** threshold required for traction use. The typical SOH thresholds are: - **End of First Life (Automotive):** SOH ≤ **80%** - **End of Second Life (Stationary):** SOH ≤ **60% to 70%** This approach extends the useful life of the battery by **5 to 10 additional years**, contributing to the **circular economy** and reducing embedded carbon emissions. --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 / Sample Paper]` **List any four major second-life applications of EV batteries and briefly explain why EV batteries are suitable for stationary storage applications.** **Model Answer:** **Four major second-life applications:** 1. **Grid-Scale Energy Storage:** For load leveling, peak shaving, and frequency regulation in utility-scale systems. 2. **Behind-the-Meter (BTM) Residential Storage:** For solar self-consumption and backup power in homes. 3. **Renewable Energy Integration:** Buffering intermittency of solar PV and wind power plants. 4. **Telecom Tower Backup Power:** Replacing diesel generators with cleaner battery-based systems. **Why EV batteries are suitable for stationary use:** - They retain **70% to 90% residual capacity** even after automotive retirement. - Stationary applications require **lower C-rates** (0.2C to 0.5C), which the batteries can comfortably deliver. - The **energy density** requirement is more important than power density in stationary use — exactly what retired EV cells offer. - The wider **SOC operating window** (10% to 90%) in stationary use fully utilizes the battery's residual capacity. --- ### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, students must answer **ONE full question** from a choice of two. Each 14-mark question is typically divided into two 7-mark sub-parts. --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** Explain the **capacity fade mechanism** in lithium-ion EV batteries. Discuss the three primary degradation modes and how they affect the suitability of a battery for second-life applications. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A **40 kWh Nissan Leaf battery pack** (LFP chemistry) has completed **2000 cycles** at 80% Depth of Discharge (DOD). The nominal voltage is **360 V** and the nominal capacity is **110 Ah**. The capacity fade rate is **5% per 1000 cycles**. Determine: 1. The current State of Health (SOH) of the battery. 2. The remaining usable energy in second-life stationary application with 90% DOD. 3. The expected additional cycles in second-life if the second-life cycle fade rate is **6% per 1000 cycles** and the second-life end SOH is **60%**. **(7 Marks)** --- ##### **Model Solution to Question A:** **Part (a) — Capacity Fade Mechanisms:** Lithium-ion battery degradation occurs through three primary mechanisms: **1. Loss of Lithium Inventory (LLI):** - Continuous parasitic side reactions at the **anode-electrolyte interface** consume cyclable lithium ions. - The **Solid Electrolyte Interphase (SEI) layer** grows with time and temperature. - Effect: Reduces the number of lithium ions available for charge transfer, directly reducing capacity. - Impact on second-life: LLI is largely **irreversible** — the lost lithium cannot be recovered, so the second-life capacity is permanently reduced. **2. Loss of Active Material (LAM):** - **Cathode:** Particle cracking, transition metal dissolution, and structural disordering. - **Anode:** Graphite exfoliation, lithium plating, and contact loss with the current collector. - Effect: Reduces the number of sites available for lithium intercalation. - Impact on second-life: LAM is **partially reversible** if the failure mode is contact loss. However, structural collapse is irreversible. **3. Kinetic Degradation (Impedance Growth):** - Increase in **charge-transfer resistance** at both electrodes. - Electrolyte depletion and viscosity increase. - Contact resistance growth at the current collector interface. - Effect: Reduces peak power capability, increases heat generation, and reduces efficiency. - Impact on second-life: The most critical degradation for **second-life use** because stationary applications are more sensitive to impedance-related energy losses. Cells with high impedance must be **sorted and matched** carefully. **Summary Table:** | Mechanism | Effect | Reversibility | Impact on Second Life | |-----------|--------|---------------|----------------------| | LLI | Reduced cyclable Li⁺ | Irreversible | Permanent capacity loss | | LAM | Reduced active sites | Partially reversible | Capacity and power loss | | Kinetic | Increased impedance | Mostly irreversible | Reduced efficiency and power | **(7 Marks) — Award 2 marks for LLI, 2 marks for LAM, 2 marks for kinetic, 1 mark for summary table.** --- **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **Given:** - $E_{\text{nominal}} = 40$ kWh - $V_{\text{nominal}} = 360$ V - $C_{\text{nominal}} = 110$ Ah - $N_{\text{cycles}} = 2000$ - $\Delta C = 5\%$ per 1000 cycles - $\text{DOD}_{\text{2nd-life}} = 90\%$ - $\Delta C_{\text{2nd-life}} = 6\%$ per 1000 cycles - $SOH_{\text{end, 2nd-life}} = 60\%$ **Step 1: Current SOH** $$\Delta C_{\text{total}} = \frac{2000}{1000} \times 5\% = 10\%$$ $$C_{\text{actual}} = 110 \times (1 - 0.10) = 99 \text{ Ah}$$ $$SOH = \frac{99}{110} \times 100\% = 90\%$$ $$\boxed{SOH = 90\%}$$ **Step 2: Remaining usable energy** $$E_{\text{remaining}} = V_{\text{nominal}} \times C_{\text{actual}} \times \eta_{\text{DOD}}$$ $$E_{\text{remaining}} = 360 \times 99 \times 0.90$$ $$E_{\text{remaining}} = 32{,}076 \text{ Wh} = 32.08 \text{ kWh}$$ $$\boxed{E_{\text{remaining, 2nd-life}} \approx 32.1 \text{ kWh}}$$ **Step 3: Additional cycles in second life** The current SOH is 90%, and the end-of-second-life SOH is 60%: $$\Delta SOH = 90\% - 60\% = 30\%$$ With a fade rate of 6% per 1000 cycles: $$N_{\text{2nd-life}} = \frac{30}{6} \times 1000 = 5000 \text{ cycles}$$ $$\boxed{N_{\text{2nd-life}} \approx 5000 \text{ cycles}}$$ **Interpretation:** At 1 cycle/day, this yields **~13.7 years** of stationary use, exceeding the typical 5–10 year expectation. This is because the fade rate in stationary use is lower than in automotive use. **(7 Marks) — Award 2 marks for SOH, 2 marks for energy calculation, 2 marks for cycle calculation, 1 mark for interpretation.** --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 - Alternative]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **second-life battery value chain** from EV retirement to final recycling. Identify the key stakeholders at each stage. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss in detail the **major technical, economic, and regulatory challenges** in deploying second-life EV batteries for grid-scale energy storage. Suggest suitable mitigation strategies for each challenge. **(7 Marks)** --- ##### **Model Solution to Question B:** **Part (a) — Second-Life Battery Value Chain:** The second-life value chain consists of **five distinct stages** with different stakeholders: **Stage 1: First-Life EV Use** - **Stakeholder:** EV OEM (e.g., Tata Motors, Ather, Hyundai) - **Activity:** Battery operated in vehicle, BMS logs maintained. - **Output:** Battery with SOH = 70% to 80% at end of life. **Stage 2: Collection and Transport** - **Stakeholders:** Dealership network, scrap dealers, logistics partners. - **Activity:** Battery removed from vehicle, transported to BRF per **UN 38.3** transport regulations. - **Output:** Battery at Battery Repurposing Facility (BRF). **Stage 3: Testing and Sorting** - **Stakeholder:** BRF Operator (e.g., Lohum, Attero, Tata Chemicals). - **Activity:** Capacity test, EIS, cell sorting. - **Output:** Homogeneous cell groups classified by SOH. **Stage 4: Re-Packaging and Certification** - **Stakeholders:** BRF, System Integrator, Certification Body (BIS, ARAI). - **Activity:** Reassemble modules, install new BMS, certify per **AIS-156** and **IEC 62660**. - **Output:** Repurposed battery pack with new warranty. **Stage 5: Second-Life Deployment** - **Stakeholders:** End user (utility, commercial, residential), EPC contractor. - **Activity:** Install in stationary storage system, connect to grid. - **Output:** Functional BESS operating for 5 to 10 years. **Stage 6: End of Second Life — Recycling** - **Stakeholder:** Material recovery company (e.g., Lohum, Attero, Li-Cycle). - **Activity:** Hydrometallurgical or pyrometallurgical recovery of Li, Co, Ni, Mn. - **Output:** Battery-grade raw materials re-entering the supply chain. **Block Diagram (Schematic):** ``` EV OEM → Collection Network → BRF (Test + Sort + Re-Pack) ↓ ↓ First-Life Use Certified Second-Life Pack ↓ ↓ End-of-Vehicle End-User (Grid/BTM/Telecom) ↓ ↓ Battery Removal End of 2nd Life ↓ Material Recovery ↓ Raw Material to OEM (Closed Loop) ``` **(7 Marks) — Award 2 marks for stages, 2 marks for stakeholders, 2 marks for diagram, 1 mark for closed-loop explanation.** --- **Part (b) — Challenges and Mitigation:** **Technical Challenges:** 1. **Cell-to-Cell Heterogeneity:** Cells from the same pack can have SOH varying from **65% to 90%**. - *Mitigation:* Mandatory **cell-level screening** using EIS, sorting into homogeneous groups, and derated operation of packs with high SOH variance. 2. **Thermal Runaway Risk:** Aged cells are more susceptible to thermal runaway due to SEI growth and lithium plating. - *Mitigation:* **Advanced BMS** with multi-parameter monitoring, **liquid cooling**, fire suppression systems, and physical isolation between modules. 3. **BMS Incompatibility:** Original BMS is designed for automotive duty cycles. - *Mitigation:* Install a **new second-life-specific BMS** with relaxed power limits and enhanced safety interlocks. 4. **Unknown Usage History:** Without complete BMS logs, degradation state is uncertain. - *Mitigation:* **Mandatory OBD data retrieval** at point of collection, non-destructive testing (EIS, ultrasound). **Economic Challenges:** 1. **High Repurposing Cost:** Testing, transport, and reassembly can cost **₹1,500 to ₹3,000 per kWh**. - *Mitigation:* **Economies of scale** through centralized BRFs, automation of testing, and standardized pack designs. 2. **Competition with New Batteries:** Falling prices of new LFP cells (₹6,000/kWh in 2024) may undercut second-life economics. - *Mitigation:* Position second-life batteries in **long-duration, low-power applications** where the cost advantage is greatest. 3. **Logistics:** Heavy, hazardous battery packs require specialized transport. - *Mitigation:* Develop **regional BRFs** near major EV deployment zones. **Regulatory Challenges:** 1. **Lack of Standards:** No universal certification standard for second-life packs. - *Mitigation:* Adopt **IEC 62933-2-1** and **IEC 62660** for stationary storage; lobby for **AIS-156** amendments to include second-life provisions. 2. **Warranty and Liability:** Unclear who is liable for a second-life battery failure. - *Mitigation:* **Tiered warranty** model: OEM (first life) → BRF (second life) → System integrator (operational). 3. **Transport Regulations:** Lithium batteries classified as Class 9 dangerous goods. - *Mitigation:* Compliance with **UN 38.3** testing for transport, dedicated transport corridors. **Summary Table for Examiner:** | Category | Top Challenge | Mitigation | |----------|--------------|------------| | Technical | Cell heterogeneity | EIS screening and sorting | | Technical | Thermal runaway | Advanced BMS and liquid cooling | | Economic | High repurposing cost | Automation and scale | | Regulatory | Lack of standards | Adoption of IEC 62933 | **(7 Marks) — Award 2 marks for technical, 2 marks for economic, 2 marks for regulatory, 1 mark for summary table.** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing SOH with SOC:** SOH is a *lifetime health metric*; SOC is *instantaneous charge level*. Examiners award 0 marks if these are interchanged in numerical problems. > > 2. **Forgetting to Apply DOD in Energy Calculations:** The remaining energy must be multiplied by the **DOD factor (η_DOD)**. Students who calculate $E = V \times C_{\text{actual}}$ without considering DOD lose 2 marks. > > 3. **Wrong Threshold for First-Life Cut-off:** The correct value is **80% SOH**, not 70% or 90%. This is a 1-mark penalty in Part A questions. > > 4. **Not Identifying Heterogeneity as the Biggest Challenge:** When asked to identify the *primary* challenge in second-life applications, the answer is **cell-to-cell variability (heterogeneity)**, not "cost" or "safety." > > 5. **Skipping the Closed-Loop Material Flow:** Examiners specifically look for mention of the **circular economy concept** — the material recovered at the end of second life returns to the OEM supply chain. Missing this loses 1 mark. > > 6. **Numerical Substitution Errors:** When calculating SOH, students often write $SOH = 1 - \Delta C$ instead of $SOH = (1 - \Delta C) \times 100\%$. The unit is **always in percent**. > > 7. **Ignoring C-Rate Differences:** A common mistake is to say that second-life batteries can deliver the same power as new batteries. They cannot — the **power capability is reduced by 30% to 50%** due to increased internal resistance. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before KTU ESE** - **Definition:** Second-life applications are the **cascaded reuse of EV batteries** in stationary applications after they fall below **80% SOH**. - **First-Life Cut-off SOH:** **80%** (universally accepted standard). - **Second-Life Cut-off SOH:** **60% to 70%**. - **Typical Second-Life Duration:** **5 to 10 years** additional calendar life. - **Cost Advantage:** Second-life batteries cost **60% to 70% less** than new batteries (₹3,000–₹6,000/kWh vs. ₹12,000–₹18,000/kWh). - **Key Applications:** Grid storage, BTM residential, renewable integration, telecom backup, EV charging buffer, microgrids. - **C-Rate Range:** Second-life: **0.2C to 0.5C** (vs. 1C to 3C in automotive). - **Depth of Discharge:** Second-life: **80% to 90%** (vs. 70% to 80% in automotive). - **Biggest Technical Challenge:** **Cell-to-cell heterogeneity (variability in SOH)**. - **Biggest Economic Challenge:** **High repurposing cost** (testing + transport + reassembly). - **Key Standards:** **AIS-156** (India), **IEC 62660**, **UN 38.3** (transport), **IEC 62933** (stationary storage). - **Capacity Fade Formula:** $SOH = (1 - k \times \sqrt{N_{\text{cycles}}}) \times 100\%$. - **Capacity Fade Rate (NMC):** ~**8% per 1000 cycles** in automotive use. - **Capacity Fade Rate (LFP, Stationary):** ~**5% to 7% per 1000 cycles**. - **Key OEMs Active in Second-Life:** **Nissan (4R Energy), BMW, Tesla, Renault, Ather Energy**. - **Key Indian Players:** **Lohum, Attero, Tata Chemicals, Exide Industries**. - **Environmental Benefit:** Reuse avoids **~150 kg CO₂ per kWh** of embedded carbon. - **Repurposing Pipeline (5 stages):** Collection → Testing → Sorting → Re-packaging → Deployment. - **Repurposing Efficiency:** **85% to 95%** of original energy is retained in the repack. - **Safety Mitigation:** Advanced BMS, liquid cooling, fire suppression, physical isolation. - **Economic Metric:** **LCOS (Levelized Cost of Storage)** is typically **₹2.5 to ₹3.5 per kWh** for second-life systems. - **Closed-Loop Concept:** End of second life → material recovery → raw material to OEM → new battery (circular economy). - **3 Degradation Mechanisms:** LLI (Loss of Lithium Inventory), LAM (Loss of Active Material), Kinetic (Impedance growth). - **Decision Gate:** If SOH < 60%, go directly to recycling. If SOH = 60% to 80%, repurpose for stationary use. - **Key Regulatory Gap:** No universal certification for second-life packs in India (as of 2024). - **Power Density Reduction:** Second-life batteries deliver **30% to 50% less peak power** than new batteries. - **Cycle Life in Second Life:** **2000 to 5000 cycles** depending on chemistry and DOD. - **Round-Trip Efficiency:** **85% to 92%** for LFP-based second-life systems. - **Use Case Sweet Spot:** **Long-duration, low-power applications** (4+ hours discharge duration). - **Avoid in Second Life:** High-power fast charging buffers (unless cells are LFP and well-sorted). ---

topics.title: Sustainable Automotive Materials: Use of Lightweight Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET414 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sustainable Automotive Materials: Use of Lightweight Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — SUSTAINABLE AUTOMOTIVE MATERIALS: USE OF LIGHTWEIGHT MATERIALS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Lightweight Automotive Materials (LAMs)** are defined by KTU 2024 (PEIET414) as engineered metallic, polymeric, ceramic, and composite substances possessing **significantly lower density (ρ)** than conventional mild steel (ρ ≈ 7850 kg/m³), while simultaneously delivering the required **specific strength (σ/ρ)**, **specific stiffness (E/ρ)**, **crashworthiness**, **corrosion resistance**, **formability**, and **recyclability** demanded by the structural, semi-structural, and non-structural sub-systems of a modern road vehicle.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the goal of lightweighting is quantified by the **Mass Reduction Factor (MRF)**:

$$
\text{MRF (\%)} = \left(1 - \frac{\rho_{\text{substitute}}}{\rho_{\text{baseline}}}\right) \times 100
$$

where the baseline is **low-carbon mild steel (mild steel, MS – ASTM A1008)**, the reference material traditionally used in a car body-in-white (BIW).

### 1.2 Classification under the KTU 2024 Scheme

The syllabus explicitly groups lightweight materials into the following five families:

1. **Advanced High-Strength Steels (AHSS)** — including Dual-Phase (DP), Transformation-Induced Plasticity (TRIP), Complex-Phase (CP), and Martensitic (MS) grades.
2. **Aluminium (Al) alloys** — cast, wrought, and heat-treatable grades (5xxx, 6xxx, 7xxx series).
3. **Magnesium (Mg) alloys** — principally AZ91, AM60, and AM50 for die-castings.
4. **Polymer-Matrix Composites (PMCs)** — Glass-Fibre Reinforced Polymers (GFRP) and **Carbon-Fibre Reinforced Polymers (CFRP)**.
5. **Bio-composites and natural-fibre reinforcements** — flax, hemp, jute, sisal, kenaf, and natural-fibre-reinforced polypropylene (PP).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Bicycle vs Motorcycle" Analogy**
>
> Imagine two delivery bikes built for the same courier workload. Bike A uses a heavy steel frame; Bike B uses a **carbon-fibre reinforced polymer (CFRP) frame** that is 60 % lighter for the *same* stiffness. The lighter frame:
>
> - Accelerates faster (less inertia).
> - Stops in a shorter distance (lower kinetic energy, $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$).
> - Consumes less fuel/electricity (smaller traction torque needed).
> - Carries more payload before hitting the Gross Vehicle Weight (GVW) limit.
>
> A modern car faces the *exact* same trade-off. Every kilogram shaved off the *unsprung* and *sprung* masses multiplies the gains in **fuel economy, CO₂ reduction, and dynamic performance**.

### 1.4 Why Lightweighting Matters in 2024–2030

| **Driver** | **Quantitative Target** | **Relevant KTU CO Mapping** |
|---|---|---|
| CAFE / BS-VII CO₂ norms | ≤ 95 g CO₂/km by 2025 (India) | CO1 – Identify material drivers |
| NEP-2020 sustainability pillar | 30 % bio-based content in trims by 2030 | CO2 – Sustainable design |
| EV range extension | +6–8 % range per 100 kg saved | CO3 – EV integration |
| Crash safety (Bharat NCAP 5★) | Maintain or improve intrusion index | CO4 – Performance under crash |
| End-of-Life Vehicle (ELV) Directive | ≥ 85 % recyclability by mass | CO5 – Circular economy |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant for Reference:** Density of water at 4 °C = **1000 kg/m³** (used as a benchmark when comparing lightweight materials, all of which — except steels — float on water).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Governing Physics of Lightweighting

The fundamental principle is captured by the **specific properties** family of metrics. The lower the density, the more you can use the material before the part mass increases. Two derived metrics dominate KTU problems:

### 2.1.1 Specific Strength

$$
\text{Specific Strength} \; S_{sp} = \frac{\sigma_{uts}}{\rho} \quad \left[\frac{\text{MPa}}{\text{kg/m}^3}\right]
$$

$\sigma_{uts}$ = ultimate tensile strength. Higher $S_{sp}$ ⇒ more load-bearing capacity per unit mass.

### 2.1.2 Specific Stiffness (Specific Modulus)

$$
\text{Specific Stiffness} \; E_{sp} = \frac{E}{\rho} \quad \left[\frac{\text{GPa}}{\text{kg/m}^3}\right]
$$

$E$ = Young's modulus. Higher $E_{sp}$ ⇒ less deflection per unit mass, critical for **bodies-in-white (BIW) bending and torsional rigidity**.

### 2.1.3 Mass Reduction for Equivalent Stiffness

If a component is redesigned for *equal bending stiffness* ($EI$) using a lower-density material of equal section modulus, the achievable mass saving is:

$$
\Delta m \;(\%) = 1 - \frac{\rho_{\text{new}}}{\rho_{\text{steel}}} \times \left(\frac{E_{\text{steel}}}{E_{\text{new}}}\right)^{1/3}
$$

The cubic root appears because bending stiffness scales as $EI \sim E \cdot b h^3$, so geometry may be adjusted to compensate for the lower $E$.

### 2.1.4 Fuel-Economy Linkage

A widely-quoted KTU reference relation (U.S. EPA validated) is:

$$
\text{Fuel-Economy Gain} \approx 0.5 - 0.7 \,\% \;\; \text{per 1 \% mass reduction (ICE vehicle)}
$$

For Battery EVs (BEVs), the gain is even higher (≈ 1 % per 1 % mass reduction) because regenerative braking and battery mass both scale with the vehicle's inertia.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Unit** | **Significance** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Density | $\rho$ | $m/V$ | kg/m³ | Baseline light-weighting metric |
| Mass Reduction Factor | $MRF$ | $(1 - \rho_{new}/\rho_{steel}) \times 100$ | % | % weight saved vs. steel |
| Specific Strength | $S_{sp}$ | $\sigma_{uts} / \rho$ | MPa·m³/kg | Strength per unit mass |
| Specific Stiffness | $E_{sp}$ | $E / \rho$ | GPa·m³/kg | Rigidity per unit mass |
| Equiv. Stiffness Mass Save | $\Delta m$ | $1 - (\rho_{new}/\rho_{steel})(E_{steel}/E_{new})^{1/3}$ | – | Redesign allowance |
| Fuel Economy Gain | $\Delta FE$ | $0.5 - 0.7 \times \Delta m_{ICE}$ or $1.0 \times \Delta m_{BEV}$ | % | Driving force for lightweighting |
| Kinetic Energy at impact | $E_k$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | J | Crash-energy scaling |
| CO₂ per kg material | $C_{CO_2}$ | Process-life-cycle data | kgCO₂e/kg | Sustainability metric |
| Recycled Content | $R_c$ | $m_{recycled}/m_{total} \times 100$ | % | Circular-economy metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **all KTU numerical answers** involving the pipe symbol for absolute value, write it as `\vert x \vert` inside math, never as a raw `|x|` inside a markdown table cell — this preserves table formatting.

## 2.3 Material-Family Deep-Dive (Engineering Logic)

### 2.3.1 Advanced High-Strength Steels (AHSS)

- **Microstructure:** Multi-phase (ferrite + martensite ± bainite ± retained austenite).
- **Why used:** Retains steel's $E$ (~210 GPa) and existing manufacturing infrastructure, but offers 2–3× higher $\sigma_{uts}$ (600–1500 MPa in DP600–DP980).
- **MRF achievable:** 10 – 25 %.
- **Application Hot-Spots:** B-pillars, rocker panels, crash rails (BMW 7-Series, Tata Harrier).

### 2.3.2 Aluminium Alloys

- **Why used:** ρ ≈ 2700 kg/m³ (≈ 1/3 of steel), high corrosion resistance due to Al₂O₃ passive film.
- **MRF achievable:** 30 – 50 %.
- **Common automotive grades:** AA5754 (5xxx — body panels), AA6111 (6xxx — outer panels, heat-treatable), AA7075 (7xxx — chassis, aerospace-grade).
- **Application Hot-Spots:** Hoods, doors, tailgates (Ford F-150 full-aluminium body), engine blocks, suspension arms.

### 2.3.3 Magnesium Alloys

- **Why used:** ρ ≈ 1740 kg/m³ (lowest of structural metals, ≈ 1/4 of steel, ≈ 2/3 of aluminium).
- **MRF achievable:** 50 – 70 % vs. steel.
- **Drawback:** Lower $E$ (~45 GPa), poor corrosion resistance, high-temperature creep.
- **Application Hot-Spots:** Steering wheel cores, instrument-panel beams, seat frames, gearbox housings (VW, GM).

### 2.3.4 Polymer-Matrix Composites (PMCs)

- **CFRP:** ρ ≈ 1600 kg/m³, $E$ ≈ 130–300 GPa, σ_uts ≈ 1500–3000 MPa.
- **GFRP:** ρ ≈ 1900 kg/m³, $E$ ≈ 25–45 GPa, σ_uts ≈ 500–1500 MPa.
- **MRF achievable:** 50 – 70 % (CFRP), 25 – 35 % (GFRP).
- **Application Hot-Spots:** Roof panels, leaf springs (Audi A2, Volvo XC90), driveshafts, crash structures (BMW i3 CFRP passenger cell).

### 2.3.5 Bio-Composites & Natural Fibres

- **Composition:** Flax / hemp / jute fibres (15–40 wt %) in PP / PLA / epoxy matrix.
- **ρ:** 1100 – 1500 kg/m³; **E:** 5 – 25 GPa; **σ_uts:** 50 – 250 MPa.
- **Application Hot-Spots:** Door trim panels, parcel shelves, headliners, seat backrests (Mercedes E-Class, Toyota Prius, BMW i3).
- **Sustainability edge:** 30 – 60 % lower embodied CO₂ vs. glass-fibre composites.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Kerala/Indian Context:**
> Natural-fibre composites use locally cultivated sisal and coir, supporting the *Make-in-India* and *Kerala State Bamboo & Coir Mission* policies. This is a high-yield KTU Part-A topic.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Crash Energy Management:** Lower mass reduces absolute kinetic energy at impact ($E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$), simplifying absorption structure design.
2. **EV Range:** Each 100 kg removed extends range by ~6–8 % (WLTP cycle) — equivalent to ~$4 worth of saved battery cells.
3. **Agility:** Reduces yaw inertia $I_{zz} = \sum m_i r_i^2$, improving transient handling.
4. **Brake Wear:** Lighter unsprung mass reduces thermal load on rotors.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mass Reduction Factor for an Equal-Strength Redesign

**Problem statement (typical KTU-style):**
A B-pillar currently made of mild steel (ρ_s = 7850 kg/m³, σ_uts,s = 350 MPa) is to be replaced by an aluminium alloy AA6111-T4 (ρ_a = 2700 kg/m³, σ_uts,a = 230 MPa) of the same volume. Calculate the **mass reduction factor (MRF)** and the **strength ratio (SR)**.

### Step 1 — Identify baseline mass and strength

$$
m_s = \rho_s \cdot V
$$

$$
F_{s,\,max} = \sigma_{uts,s} \cdot A
$$

The new component must withstand the same axial load $F_{max}$. If the new material has lower σ_uts, the cross-sectional area must be enlarged.

### Step 2 — Determine required area of the substitute

$$
A_a = \frac{F_{max}}{\sigma_{uts,a}} = \frac{\sigma_{uts,s} A_s}{\sigma_{uts,a}}
$$

### Step 3 — Form the area ratio

$$
\frac{A_a}{A_s} = \frac{\sigma_{uts,s}}{\sigma_{uts,a}} = \frac{350}{230} = 1.5217
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the new volume (assuming uniform thickness t, length L preserved)

$$
V_a = A_a \cdot L
$$

$$
V_s = A_s \cdot L
$$

$$
\frac{V_a}{V_s} = 1.5217
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the new mass

$$
m_a = \rho_a \cdot V_a = 2700 \times 1.5217 \cdot V_s
$$

$$
m_s = 7850 \cdot V_s
$$

### Step 6 — Form the Mass Reduction Factor

$$
\text{MRF} = \left(1 - \frac{m_a}{m_s}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
\frac{m_a}{m_s} = \frac{2700 \times 1.5217}{7850} = 0.5232
$$

$$
\text{MRF} = (1 - 0.5232) \times 100 = 47.68 \,\%
$$

> **Conclusion:** Even though aluminium's tensile strength is only 65 % of mild steel's, the redesign yields a **47.7 % mass reduction** for an equivalent load-bearing part.

## 3.2 Derivation: Equivalent Stiffness Mass Saving

A steel dashboard cross-beam (E_s = 210 GPa, ρ_s = 7850 kg/m³) is replaced by a magnesium beam (E_m = 45 GPa, ρ_m = 1740 kg/m³). Both beams are of rectangular section (b × h) and equal length L. Find the mass of the Mg beam such that bending stiffness $EI$ is identical.

### Step 1 — Equate bending stiffnesses

For a rectangular section, $I = \tfrac{b h^3}{12}$.

$$
E_s \cdot \frac{b_s h_s^3}{12} = E_m \cdot \frac{b_m h_m^3}{12}
$$

### Step 2 — Take the constraint that width is constant (b_s = b_m) and solve for the height ratio

$$
h_m^3 = \frac{E_s}{E_m} \cdot h_s^3
$$

$$
h_m = h_s \cdot \left(\frac{E_s}{E_m}\right)^{1/3} = h_s \cdot \left(\frac{210}{45}\right)^{1/3} = h_s \cdot (4.6667)^{0.3333}
$$

$$
h_m = h_s \cdot 1.6704
$$

### Step 3 — Compute volume ratio

$$
\frac{V_m}{V_s} = \frac{b_m h_m L}{b_s h_s L} = \frac{h_m}{h_s} = 1.6704
$$

### Step 4 — Compute mass ratio

$$
\frac{m_m}{m_s} = \frac{\rho_m \cdot V_m}{\rho_s \cdot V_s} = \frac{1740 \times 1.6704}{7850 \times 1} = 0.3703
$$

### Step 5 — Final mass saving

$$
\Delta m \;(\%) = (1 - 0.3703) \times 100 = 62.97 \,\%
$$

> **Conclusion:** Despite Mg's lower Young's modulus (≈ 1/5 of steel), an equivalent-stiffness redesign saves **~63 % mass** because the lower density more than compensates for the geometry expansion dictated by the cubic-root term.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Material Selection for a Typical Automotive Part

The table below is a **frequently-tested KTU analytical matrix**. Use it to justify material choice in long-answer questions.

| **Criterion** | **Weight (out of 5)** | **Mild Steel** | **AHSS** | **Al 6111** | **Mg AZ91** | **CFRP** | **Natural-Fibre PP** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Density (kg/m³) | 5 | 1 | 2 | 4 | 5 | 5 | 4 |
| Specific Strength | 5 | 1 | 3 | 3 | 3 | 5 | 1 |
| Formability | 3 | 5 | 3 | 4 | 2 | 1 | 4 |
| Crash Energy Absorbed | 4 | 3 | 5 | 3 | 2 | 4 | 1 |
| Corrosion Resistance | 3 | 1 | 2 | 4 | 1 | 5 | 3 |
| Recyclability | 4 | 5 | 5 | 5 | 4 | 2 | 3 |
| Cost (₹/kg, lower better) | 5 | 5 | 4 | 2 | 1 | 1 | 3 |
| **Weighted Score (max 145)** | – | 91 | 99 | 101 | 75 | 95 | 71 |

> [!NOTE]
> Reading: **Al 6111** emerges as the best compromise for *body panels*, while **AHSS** dominates the *crash-critical* zones. **CFRP** wins on specific strength and stiffness but loses on cost and recyclability.
