# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**Define the term "Mass Reduction Factor (MRF)" used in lightweight automotive design. State the MRF achievable by replacing mild steel with AA6111 aluminium alloy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Mass Reduction Factor (MRF)** is the percentage decrease in component mass achieved by substituting a reference material (typically mild steel, ρ = 7850 kg/m³) with a lighter alternative, while keeping the functional volume or the load-bearing capacity within design limits:

$$
\text{MRF (\%)} = \left(1 - \frac{\rho_{\text{new}}}{\rho_{\text{baseline}}}\right) \times 100
$$

Replacing mild steel with **AA6111-T4 aluminium (ρ ≈ 2700 kg/m³)** gives:

$$
\text{MRF} = \left(1 - \frac{2700}{7850}\right) \times 100 \approx 65.6 \,\% \quad \text{(equal volume basis)}
$$

In an *equal-strength* redesign (where the cross-section is enlarged to compensate for the lower σ_uts of Al vs. steel), the practical MRF is typically **40 – 50 %**, which is the figure adopted by KTU examiners.

*(1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark numerical evaluation.)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
**List any three advantages and two limitations of using Carbon-Fibre Reinforced Polymer (CFRP) in automotive body structures.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Advantages:**
1. Very high specific strength (σ/ρ ≈ 1100 MPa·m³/kg) and specific stiffness (E/ρ ≈ 90 GPa·m³/kg), enabling **50 – 70 % mass reduction** over steel.
2. Excellent fatigue and corrosion resistance, leading to a long service life in harsh underbody environments.
3. Design flexibility — fibres can be oriented along principal stress paths, reducing part count (e.g., CFRP leaf springs replace multi-piece steel assemblies).

**Limitations:**
1. **High cost** (₹ 3000–5000/kg) — currently restricted to premium and EV models.
2. **Poor recyclability** and long cure times; also requires capital-intensive Resin Transfer Moulding (RTM) infrastructure.

*(1 mark for any 3 advantages, 1 mark for any 2 limitations, 1 mark for overall presentation.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain, with a neat sketch, the classification of lightweight materials used in modern automotive applications. Discuss the role of **Advanced High-Strength Steels (AHSS)** in achieving crash-safety and weight reduction simultaneously. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A mild-steel suspension arm (ρ = 7850 kg/m³, σ_uts = 350 MPa) is to be replaced with an **AA6061-T6 aluminium arm (ρ = 2700 kg/m³, σ_uts = 310 MPa)** for the same load-carrying capacity. Calculate the **(i) mass reduction factor (MRF)** and **(ii) percentage saving in fuel economy** for an ICE vehicle. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A (a):**

**Step 1 — Classification of Lightweight Materials** *(3 marks)*

Refer to the hierarchical diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes. The classification is:

- **Metallic family:** AHSS, Aluminium alloys, Magnesium alloys.
- **Polymer/composite family:** CFRP, GFRP, SMC.
- **Bio-based family:** Natural-fibre reinforced PP, flax/PLA, hemp/epoxy.

**Step 2 — Role of AHSS in Crash Safety** *(3 marks)*

- AHSS grades (DP600 to DP1000) exhibit tensile strengths of **600 – 1000 MPa**, roughly **2 – 3×** that of mild steel.
- They retain the high **Young's modulus of steel (E ≈ 210 GPa)**, ensuring **bending and torsional stiffness** of the body-in-white is preserved.
- The **multi-phase microstructure (ferrite + martensite)** gives a high strain-hardening exponent, allowing the material to absorb **crash energy** through plastic deformation while resisting buckling.
- Applications: B-pillars, rocker panels, side-impact beams, bumper reinforcements (Tata Harrier, Hyundai Creta).

**Step 3 — Sketch** *(1 mark)*

A simple flow chart of the AHSS family showing the four principal grades (DP, TRIP, CP, MS) with their representative tensile strengths.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A (b):**

**Given:**
- ρ_s = 7850 kg/m³, σ_uts,s = 350 MPa
- ρ_a = 2700 kg/m³, σ_uts,a = 310 MPa
- Same load F and same length L

**Step 1 — Area ratio for equal load capacity** *(2 marks)*

$$
\frac{A_a}{A_s} = \frac{\sigma_{uts,s}}{\sigma_{uts,a}} = \frac{350}{310} = 1.1290
$$

**Step 2 — Volume and mass ratio** *(2 marks)*

$$
\frac{m_a}{m_s} = \frac{\rho_a \cdot V_a}{\rho_s \cdot V_s} = \frac{2700 \times 1.1290}{7850} = 0.3883
$$

**Step 3 — Mass Reduction Factor** *(1 mark)*

$$
\text{MRF} = (1 - 0.3883) \times 100 = 61.17 \,\%
$$

**Step 4 — Fuel-economy improvement (ICE)** *(2 marks)*

Using the EPA-validated relation 0.5 – 0.7 % FE gain per 1 % mass reduction:

$$
\Delta FE = 0.6 \times 61.17 = 36.7 \,\%
$$

(Use 0.5 for a conservative answer = 30.6 %; 0.7 for an optimistic answer = 42.8 %. KTU typically expects 0.5.)

> **Final answer:** MRF ≈ **61.2 %**, FE improvement ≈ **30 – 37 %** depending on the chosen coefficient.

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Compare the properties of **Magnesium, Aluminium, and Carbon-Fibre Reinforced Polymer (CFRP)** for use in automotive body-in-white structures. Prepare a comparative table covering density, Young's modulus, tensile strength, specific stiffness, specific strength, and typical cost. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the manufacturing processes used for fabricating **aluminium body panels** and **CFRP passenger cells**. Discuss why hybrid multi-material structures (steel + Al + CFRP) are increasingly adopted in modern vehicles. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B (a):**

**Comparative Table** *(5 marks)*

| **Property** | **Magnesium (AZ91)** | **Aluminium (AA6111-T4)** | **CFRP (UD T700/epoxy)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| ρ (kg/m³) | 1740 | 2700 | 1600 |
| E (GPa) | 45 | 70 | 150 |
| σ_uts (MPa) | 220 | 230 | 1800 |
| E/ρ (GPa·m³/kg) | 25.9 | 25.9 | 93.7 |
| σ/ρ (MPa·m³/kg) | 126.4 | 85.2 | 1125 |
| Approx. Cost (₹/kg) | 320 | 200 | 3500 |

**Key inferences** *(2 marks)*:
- **CFRP** is best in absolute and specific strength — ideal for crash and structural parts.
- **Aluminium** offers the best **balanced** property profile — best for body panels.
- **Magnesium** has the **lowest density of structural metals** but suffers from low $E$ and corrosion issues — used in non-structural castings.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B (b):**

**Aluminium Body Panels — Manufacturing** *(2 marks)*

1. **Stamping** in 5xxx (AA5754) or 6xxx (AA6111) coils, followed by **hemming** for door/hood edges.
2. **Resistance Spot Welding (RSW)** is replaced by **self-piercing rivets (SPR)** and **adhesive bonding** since Al's higher thermal conductivity and oxide layer make spot welding difficult.
3. **Heat treatment** (for 6xxx) is done after stamping in a low-temperature aging oven (paint-bake cycle, ~180 °C).

**CFRP Passenger Cell — Manufacturing** *(2 marks)*

1. **Resin Transfer Moulding (RTM):** dry carbon-fibre preforms placed in a closed mould, low-viscosity epoxy injected and cured.
2. **Compression Moulding of SMC-like carbon sheets** (used in BMW 7-Series roof segments).
3. **Prepreg lay-up + autoclave** for highest-performance parts (e.g., monocoque chassis of McLaren).

**Why Hybrid Multi-Material Design?** *(3 marks)*

- **Right-material, right-place:** Use **AHSS** for crash rails (energy absorption), **Al** for closure panels (mass + formability), and **CFRP** for the passenger cell (stiffness + weight).
- Balances **cost, performance, and recyclability** — a single material cannot meet all KPIs.
- Examples: **Audi A8 (ASF frame — Al + CFRP rear panel)**, **BMW i3 (CFRP Life Module + Al Drive Module)**, **Volvo XC90 (BORAS steel-Al hybrid)**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Do NOT confuse specific strength with specific stiffness.** A common 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Always state the baseline** ("compared to mild steel of density 7850 kg/m³") in your MRF answer.
> 3. **Fuel-economy coefficient:** Use **0.5** if the question says "conservative" and **0.7** if it says "aggressive / EPA validated". For BEVs the correct coefficient is **1.0**.
> 4. **Pipe-symbol trap:** Do NOT write `|x|` inside a markdown table — always use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` to avoid breaking the table layout (also a loss of 1–2 marks for sloppy formatting).
> 5. **CFRP cost comparison:** Always cite the order-of-magnitude difference (≈ 50 – 70× steel) when justifying hybrid design choices.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Lightweight materials** are categorised into **Metallic (AHSS, Al, Mg)**, **Polymer/Composite (CFRP, GFRP)**, and **Bio-based (natural-fibre PP)** families.
- **Mass Reduction Factor (MRF)** = $(1 - \rho_{new}/\rho_{steel}) \times 100$; baseline is mild steel at **7850 kg/m³**.
- **Specific Strength** = σ/ρ; **Specific Stiffness** = E/ρ — both are the two key lightweighting metrics.
- **AHSS (DP780, TRIP, MS)** offers 2–3× strength of mild steel with the same modulus, ideal for **crash-critical** zones.
- **Aluminium alloys (5xxx, 6xxx, 7xxx)** give **40 – 50 %** weight reduction; 6xxx is heat-treatable and used for outer body panels.
- **Magnesium (AZ91)** is the **lightest structural metal** (1740 kg/m³) but suffers from low $E$ and poor corrosion resistance.
- **CFRP** delivers the highest specific strength and stiffness; **GFRP** is a cheaper compromise; both require RTM or autoclave processing.
- **Natural-fibre composites** support the *Make-in-India / Kerala Coir Mission* narrative and reduce embodied CO₂ by 30 – 60 %.
- **Fuel-economy gain:** 0.5 – 0.7 % per 1 % mass reduction for ICE; **1.0 %** per 1 % for BEVs.
- **Hybrid multi-material design** (steel + Al + CFRP) is the **industry-standard** approach — no single material wins on all KPIs.
- **Key KTU mnemonics:**
  - *Density order (low to high):* **CFRP < Mg < GFRP < Flax/PP < Al < Steel < AHSS** (≈ equal to steel).
  - *Cost order (low to high):* **Steel < AHSS < Al < Mg < GFRP < Natural-fibre PP < CFRP**.
  - *Crash-energy rule:* $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$ — mass reduction lowers both fuel use and impact severity.
- **Common KTU exam keywords to use verbatim:** *Mass Reduction Factor, Specific Strength, Specific Stiffness, Body-in-White, Multi-material, Hot-stamping, RTM, AHSS, CAFE, ELV Directive, End-of-Life recyclability.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recycling and End-of-Life Vehicle Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET414 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Recycling and End-of-Life Vehicle Strategies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**End-of-Life Vehicle (ELV)** refers to any motorised road vehicle that has reached the end of its operational life and is designated for disposal, recovery, treatment, recycling, or shredding under the producer-responsibility framework. An ELV is formally classified when the vehicle is surrendered by its last registered owner, has been declared economically non-repairable (exceeding 30-50% of its market value), or has failed mandatory fitness certification for more than two consecutive testing cycles.

The **ELV Recycling Strategy** is a closed-loop, multi-stage industrial process governed by national legislation (e.g., EU Directive 2000/53/EC, Japan ELV Recycling Act 2005, India MoRTH ELV Notification 2024 draft) that mandates the systematic depollution, dismantling, shredding, and material recovery of discarded vehicles to achieve minimum **material recovery rate of 85%** and **reuse + recovery rate of 95%** by average vehicle mass.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight — The 3R+2R Framework**
> KTU 2024 Module 4 emphasises the 5-pillar ELV hierarchy: **Reduce** (lightweighting), **Reuse** (dismantled parts), **Recycle** (material recovery), **Recover** (energy from ASR), and **Remanufacture** (engine, transmission refurbishment).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital for Cars"

Imagine a **car as a patient that has lived a long life** and is now being admitted to a specialised hospital. Just like a hospital:

| Hospital Phase | ELV Phase | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Admission & Triage | Collection & Depollution | Drain hazardous fluids (blood/bile = oils, coolant, fuel, battery acid) |
| Diagnostic Check | Inventory of Reusable Parts | Identify healthy "organs" — engine, transmission, ECU, seats |
| Surgery | Dismantling | Carefully remove parts that can be transplanted to other "patients" |
| Incineration & Cremation | Shredding & ASR Processing | Crush the remainder and extract every gram of useful material |
| Recycling of body | Material Recovery | Melt metals, granulate plastics, regenerate glass |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why ELV Strategy Matters Globally**
> - World produces ~**80 million** new vehicles annually and retires ~**27 million** ELVs.
> - A single vehicle contains **150+ kg of plastics**, **750+ kg of ferrous metal**, and **30+ hazardous fluids**.
> - Improperly disposed ELVs leak heavy metals (Pb, Cd, Hg, Cr-VI) and hydrocarbons into soil and groundwater — a direct violation of the **Basel Convention (1989)** for transboundary hazardous waste movement.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Mandated by ELV Directive 2000/53/EC)

The three key performance indicators that examiners expect every KTU student to recall verbatim:

- **Reuse + Recycling Rate (R+R):** ≥ **85%** of average vehicle mass per year
- **Reuse + Recovery Rate (R+REC):** ≥ **95%** of average vehicle mass per year
- **Depollution Compliance:** 100% removal of fluids, battery, tyres, catalytic converter, airbags before shredding

**Units & Reference Constants Used Throughout Module 4:**

- Typical passenger car kerb mass: **1200 kg – 1500 kg**
- Ferrous metal fraction: **65 – 70%** (by mass)
- Non-ferrous metal fraction: **8 – 12%**
- Plastic fraction: **10 – 15%**
- Automotive Shredder Residue (ASR) target: **< 5%** of vehicle mass by 2025 (currently ~25%)
- Specific energy of ASR (Lower Heating Value, LHV): **~16 MJ/kg**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Operational Stages of ELV Treatment

The complete ELV value chain is decomposed into five sequential industrial stages. Understanding the logic of each stage is essential for both Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

**Stage 1 — Collection & Depollution (Pre-Treatment)**
- Vehicle is drained of all operational fluids: engine oil, transmission oil, brake fluid, power steering fluid, coolant, fuel, refrigerant (R-134a), washer fluid.
- Hazardous components physically removed: lead-acid battery, tyres, catalytic converter, airbags, mercury switches, electronic control units (ECUs).
- Air-conditioning refrigerant recovered using dedicated **EPA Section 82** certified recovery machines to prevent ozone-depleting substance release.
- Airbags are deployed in a controlled chamber with shielded operators (RACS — Remote Airbag Curation System) or electrically triggered in-situ.

**Stage 2 — Dismantling (Pre-Shredder Treatment)**
- Manual and robotic dismantling of high-value, easily separable components: engines, gearboxes, doors, bumpers, seats, headlamps, infotainment systems.
- Dismantled parts enter three streams:
  - **OEM Remanufacture** (engine blocks, alternators, starters)
  - **Aftermarket Reuse** (panels, trim, glass)
  - **Component Recycling** (radiators, aluminium wheels → secondary smelters)

**Stage 3 — Shredding (Mechanical Size Reduction)**
- The depolluted, partially-dismantled vehicle hulk is fed into a **rotor shear shredder** rotating at 400–800 rpm.
- Output is a heterogeneous mixture called **fluff** (size 10–100 mm).
- The shredder consumes approximately **0.4 – 0.6 kWh per vehicle** in electrical energy.

**Stage 4 — Post-Shredder Separation (Material Recovery)**
- Sequential separation using density, magnetism, and eddy currents:
  1. **Magnetic separation** → recovers ferrous metals (steel, cast iron)
  2. **Eddy current separation (ECS)** → recovers non-ferrous metals (Al, Cu, Zn)
  3. **Heavy media separation (HMS)** → separates dense plastics and glass
  4. **Optical/laser sorting** → separates polymers by resin type (PP, ABS, PA66)
  5. **Air classification / Zig-Zag classifier** → separates light ASR from heavy fraction

**Stage 5 — Recovery of Automotive Shredder Residue (ASR)**
- Light ASR fraction is processed for energy recovery via pyrolysis, gasification, or refuse-derived fuel (RDF) feed to cement kilns.
- Target: reduce landfilled ASR from 25% to < 5% of vehicle mass by 2030.

### 2.2 The "Why & How" Logic Behind Each Step

- **Why depollute first?** Hazardous fluids corrode shredding equipment, contaminate the recovered metal scrap, and cause uncontrolled environmental release of hydrocarbons and battery acid.
- **Why dismantle before shredding?** The market value of a refurbished engine (~₹25,000) far exceeds the value of its constituent metals (₹3,000). Early dismantling captures the highest economic and environmental value.
- **Why multi-stage separation?** No single physical property (density, magnetism, conductivity) can isolate all 6+ material streams — sequential separation achieves > 95% purity per fraction.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examiner-favourite numerical tools. Memorise the units.**

$$
\text{Reuse + Recycling Rate (R+R)} = \frac{M_{\text{reused}} + M_{\text{recycled}}}{M_{\text{vehicle}}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Reuse + Recovery Rate (R+REC)} = \frac{M_{\text{reused}} + M_{\text{recycled}} + M_{\text{energy\_recovered}}}{M_{\text{vehicle}}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Diversion from Landfill (\%)} = \frac{M_{\text{vehicle}} - M_{\text{ASR\_landfilled}}}{M_{\text{vehicle}}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Specific Energy of ASR Recovery} = \text{LHV}_{\text{ASR}} \times M_{\text{ASR}} = 16 \times M_{\text{ASR}} \;\; \text{[MJ]}
$$

$$
\text{Shredder Throughput} = \frac{M_{\text{vehicle}} \times 3600}{t_{\text{cycle}}} \;\; \text{[kg/hour]}
$$

$$
\text{Mass Balance (Shredder)}: \quad M_{\text{in}} = M_{\text{ferrous}} + M_{\text{non-ferrous}} + M_{\text{plastic}} + M_{\text{ASR}} + M_{\text{loss}}
$$

$$
\text{Carbon Footprint Avoidance} = \text{EF}_{\text{primary}} - \text{EF}_{\text{recycled}} = 1.99 - 0.39 = 1.60 \;\; \text{kg CO}_2\text{e/kg steel}
$$

### 2.4 Summary Table — High-Yield Reference Matrix

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reuse + Recycling Target | R+R | ≥ 85 | % by mass | EU ELV Directive 2000/53/EC |
| Reuse + Recovery Target | R+REC | ≥ 95 | % by mass | Energy recovery included |
| Ferrous metal fraction | m_Fe | 65 – 70 | % | Recovered by magnetic separation |
| Non-ferrous fraction | m_NF | 8 – 12 | % | Recovered by eddy current |
| Plastic fraction | m_Pl | 10 – 15 | % | Sorted by NIR/optical |
| ASR (landfill) target | m_ASR | < 5 | % | By 2030 (down from ~25%) |
| ASR LHV | LHV_ASR | 16 | MJ/kg | Refuse-derived fuel |
| Battery mass | m_bat | 12 – 25 | kg | Lead-acid dominant |
| Coolant volume | V_cool | 6 – 10 | L | Ethylene glycol based |
| Engine oil volume | V_oil | 4 – 6 | L | Mineral/synthetic |
| Fuel remaining | V_fuel | 5 – 60 | L | Depends on tank condition |
| AC refrigerant | m_ref | 0.5 – 1.2 | kg | HFC-134a dominant |
| Recycled steel EF saving | ΔEF | 1.60 | kg CO2e/kg | vs primary route |
| Recycled aluminium EF saving | ΔEF_Al | 9.0 | kg CO2e/kg | vs primary route |
| Shredder energy | E_shred | 0.4 – 0.6 | kWh/vehicle | Rotor shear type |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The carbon-footprint saving of recycling vehicle steel alone equals approximately **1.6 tonnes CO₂ avoided per ELV**. Scaled globally (27 million ELVs/year), the ELV industry prevents **~43 million tonnes of CO₂ emissions annually** — equivalent to taking 9.4 million passenger cars off the road for one year.

### 2.5 The Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) Framework

Under EPR, the vehicle manufacturer is legally and financially responsible for the end-of-life management of every vehicle they ever produced. This has driven the design philosophy known as **Design for Recycling (DfR)**:

- Single-polymer plastic components (avoiding PP/ABS blends that are inseparable)
- Easily removable bumpers and dashboards
- Material coding per ISO 1043 (plastics) and ISO 9453 (solders)
- Reduction of heavy-metal content (Pb < 0.1%, Cd < 0.01%, Hg < 0.1% per ELV Annex II)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Material Recovery Rate Calculation

> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style Numerical):**
> A passenger car of kerb mass **1500 kg** undergoes ELV treatment. The following mass distribution is recorded after the complete 5-stage process:
> - Reused parts (engine, transmission, doors): **180 kg**
> - Recycled ferrous metal: **945 kg**
> - Recycled non-ferrous metal: **120 kg**
> - Recycled plastics: **75 kg**
> - Energy-recovered ASR (sent to cement kiln): **105 kg**
> - Landfilled ASR: **75 kg**
>
> Calculate (a) the Reuse + Recycling Rate and (b) the Reuse + Recovery Rate, and verify ELV Directive compliance.

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1 — Identify Vehicle Mass and Recovered Masses**

The total vehicle mass entering the process:
$$
M_{\text{vehicle}} = 1500 \;\; \text{kg}
$$

Sum of all reused and recycled fractions:
$$
M_{\text{reused+recycled}} = 180 + 945 + 120 + 75 = 1320 \;\; \text{kg}
$$

Sum of reused, recycled, AND energy-recovered fractions:
$$
M_{\text{reused+recycled+energy}} = 1320 + 105 = 1425 \;\; \text{kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Reuse + Recycling Rate Formula**

$$
R+R = \frac{M_{\text{reused}} + M_{\text{recycled}}}{M_{\text{vehicle}}} \times 100
$$

Substituting the numerical values:
$$
R+R = \frac{1320}{1500} \times 100
$$

$$
R+R = 0.88 \times 100 = 88\%
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Reuse + Recovery Rate Formula**

$$
R+REC = \frac{1320 + 105}{1500} \times 100 = \frac{1425}{1500} \times 100 = 95\%
$$

**Step 4 — Verify Mass Balance (Sanity Check)**

$$
M_{\text{out,total}} = 180 + 945 + 120 + 75 + 105 + 75 = 1500 \;\; \text{kg}
$$

$$
M_{\text{out,total}} = M_{\text{vehicle}} = 1500 \;\; \text{kg} \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Step 5 — Compliance Verdict**

| Metric | Calculated | ELV Directive Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| R+R | 88% | ≥ 85% | **COMPLIANT** ✓ |
| R+REC | 95% | ≥ 95% | **COMPLIANT ✓ (borderline)** |
| Landfilled ASR | 5% | < 5% (2030 target) | **COMPLIANT ✓** |

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Energy Recovery from ASR

> **Problem Statement:**
> An ELV shredder plant processes **200 vehicles/day**, each of mass 1500 kg, with 25% of vehicle mass becoming ASR. The ASR has a Lower Heating Value (LHV) of 16 MJ/kg and is sent to a cement kiln with 35% thermal efficiency. Calculate the useful thermal energy recovered per day.

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1 — Mass of ASR Generated per Vehicle**

$$
M_{\text{ASR,vehicle}} = 0.25 \times 1500 = 375 \;\; \text{kg/vehicle}
$$

**Step 2 — Total ASR Mass per Day**

$$
M_{\text{ASR,daily}} = 200 \times 375 = 75000 \;\; \text{kg/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Chemical Energy in ASR (per day)**

$$
E_{\text{chemical}} = M_{\text{ASR,daily}} \times \text{LHV}_{\text{ASR}} = 75000 \times 16
$$

$$
E_{\text{chemical}} = 1{,}200{,}000 \;\; \text{MJ/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Thermal Efficiency of the Cement Kiln**

$$
E_{\text{useful}} = \eta_{\text{kiln}} \times E_{\text{chemical}} = 0.35 \times 1{,}200{,}000
$$

$$
E_{\text{useful}} = 420{,}000 \;\; \text{MJ/day}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ)**

$$
E_{\text{useful,kWh}} = \frac{420{,}000}{3.6} = 116{,}667 \;\; \text{kWh/day}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** This single shredder plant recovers energy equivalent to the daily consumption of approximately **3,900 Indian urban households** (~30 kWh/month) — a powerful argument for ELV energy recovery in the KTU board answer sheet.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Carbon Footprint Avoidance via Steel Recycling

> **Problem Statement:**
> A fleet of 100 ELVs (each 1500 kg) yields 65% of mass as recyclable steel. The emission factor for primary (blast furnace) steelmaking is 1.99 kg CO₂e/kg, and for recycled (EAF) steelmaking is 0.39 kg CO₂e/kg. Calculate the avoided CO₂ emissions.

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1 — Mass of Recycled Steel per Vehicle**

$$
M_{\text{steel,vehicle}} = 0.65 \times 1500 = 975 \;\; \text{kg/vehicle}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Mass of Recycled Steel (100 vehicles)**

$$
M_{\text{steel,total}} = 100 \times 975 = 97500 \;\; \text{kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Avoided Emission Factor per kg of Steel**

$$
\Delta \text{EF} = 1.99 - 0.39 = 1.60 \;\; \text{kg CO}_2\text{e/kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Avoided Emissions**

$$
\text{CO}_2 \text{ avoided} = M_{\text{steel,total}} \times \Delta \text{EF} = 97500 \times 1.60
$$

$$
\text{CO}_2 \text{ avoided} = 156{,}000 \;\; \text{kg CO}_2\text{e} = 156 \;\; \text{tonnes CO}_2\text{e}
$$

**Analytical Note:** This single 100-vehicle recycling campaign offsets the annual emissions of approximately **34 average Indian passenger cars** (~4.6 t CO₂/year each).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance of Recovery Routes

| Recovery Route | Recovery Yield | Energy Input | CO₂ Impact | Cost (₹/kg) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reuse (dismantled parts) | 100% per part | Very Low | Negligible | 80 – 150 |
| Remanufacture | 90 – 95% | Medium | Low | 120 – 200 |
| Material recycling (metals) | 95% | Medium | Moderate | 25 – 45 |
| ASR energy recovery | 25% mass → 100% energy | High (kiln) | Moderate | 8 – 15 |
| Landfilling (worst case) | 0% | None | High (long-term) | 3 – 6 (short-term cheap) |

> [!NOTE]
> **The KTU Validity Key:** Examiners award full marks (14/14) only when the answer quantifies the recycling and recovery rates with explicit mass-balance verification. Always close numerical problems with a one-line interpretation of compliance.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ELV Treatment Process — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block renders the complete end-to-end flow of an ELV through the authorised treatment facility (ATF), as required by Module 4. Every node uses alphanumeric identifiers (prefixed with letters) and clean uppercase text labels — no embedded markdown formatting — to ensure smooth rendering.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[STAGE 1A VEHICLE INTAKE AND OWNER VERIFICATION] --> A2[STAGE 1B WEIGHING AND DEPOLLUTION BAY]
    A2 --> A3[FLUID REMOVAL OIL FUEL COOLANT BRAKE]
    A2 --> A4[HAZARDOUS PART EXTRACTION BATTERY TYRES CATALYST AIRBAGS]
    A3 --> A5[STAGE 2 DISMANTLING LINE]
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> B1[STAGE 2A MANUAL DISMANTLING OEM REUSABLE PARTS]
    A5 --> B2[STAGE 2B ROBOTIC DISMANTLING ENGINES GEARBOXES]
    B1 --> C1[STAGE 3 SHREDDER FEED HULK]
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2[STAGE 3A ROTOR SHEAR SHREDDER 400 TO 800 RPM]
    C2 --> D1[STAGE 4 POST SHREDDER SEPARATION LINE]
    D1 --> D2[STAGE 4A MAGNETIC DRUM FERROUS RECOVERY]
    D1 --> D3[STAGE 4B EDDY CURRENT NON FERROUS RECOVERY]
    D1 --> D4[STAGE 4C OPTICAL SORTER POLYMER SEPARATION]
    D1 --> D5[STAGE 4D ZIG ZAG ASR AIR CLASSIFICATION]
    D2 --> E1[OUTPUT FERROUS SCRAP EAF STEELMAKING]
    D3 --> E2[OUTPUT ALUMINIUM COPPER ZINC INGOTS]
    D4 --> E3[OUTPUT POLYMER PELLETS INJECTION MOULDING]
    D5 --> F1[STAGE 5 ASR TREATMENT ROUTING]
    F1 --> F2[STAGE 5A PYROLYSIS OR GASIFICATION]
    F1 --> F3[STAGE 5B RDF FEED TO CEMENT KILN]
    F1 --> F4[STAGE 5C LANDFILL MINIMISED TARGET LT 5 PERCENT]
```

### 4.2 Schematic Legend & Engineering Rationale

| Node Cluster | Function | Engineering Equipment |
|---|---|---|
| A1 – A4 | Owner paperwork, weighing, fluid extraction | Drain racks, suction pumps, refrigerant recovery units |
| A5 – B2 | Dismantling and segregation | Pneumatic tools, robotic arms with vision, lifting rigs |
| C1 – C2 | Size reduction | Rotor shear shredder, hammer mill, granulator |
| D1 – D5 | Multi-stage separation | Magnetic drum, eddy current separator, NIR optical sorter, Zig-Zag air classifier |
| E1 – E3 | Recycled material output | Briquetted scrap, ingots, compounded polymer pellets |
| F1 – F4 | ASR final disposition | Pyrolysis reactor, gasifier, RDF feeder, sanitary landfill (last resort) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematic:** The two divergent pathways for ASR (pyrolysis vs. RDF vs. landfill) are not redundant — they are tiered by economic and environmental priority. Pyrolysis recovers monomers and carbon black; RDF substitutes fossil fuel; landfill is the unavoidable last-resort (currently ~25% but targeted to < 5%).

### 4.3 Material Flow Sankey (Textual Representation)

For KTU board exams, a textual Sankey representation often substitutes for graphical figures. The mass flow of a **1500 kg ELV** is summarised below:

```
[1500 kg VEHICLE INPUT]
   |-- 180 kg  --> REUSE (engine, transmission, doors, seats)        [12.0%]
   |-- 945 kg  --> FERROUS RECYCLING (steel, cast iron)             [63.0%]
   |-- 120 kg  --> NON-FERROUS RECYCLING (Al, Cu, Zn)               [8.0%]
   |-- 75 kg   --> PLASTIC RECYCLING (PP, ABS, PA66 pellets)        [5.0%]
   |-- 105 kg  --> ENERGY RECOVERY (ASR to cement kiln)             [7.0%]
   |-- 75 kg   --> LANDFILL (residual ASR)                          [5.0%]
   |___________
   TOTAL = 1500 kg ✓
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 2]**
> **Define an End-of-Life Vehicle (ELV) and list any four hazardous substances that must be removed during depollution.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **End-of-Life Vehicle (ELV)** is a motor vehicle that has reached the end of its useful life and is classified for disposal when it is surrendered by the last registered owner, declared uneconomical to repair (repair cost > 50% of market value), or has failed fitness certification. As per EU Directive 2000/53/EC, four hazardous substances that must be removed during depollution are:
1. **Engine and gear oil** (hydrocarbon pollutants)
2. **Battery acid** (sulphuric acid, lead)
3. **Air-conditioning refrigerant (HFC-134a)** (greenhouse gas, GWP = 1430)
4. **Catalytic converter** (contains Pt, Pd, Rh — precious and strategic metals)
*(Alternate accepted answers: brake fluid, coolant/ethylene glycol, airbags (sodium azide), mercury switches.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Repeat Paper]**
> **What is Automotive Shredder Residue (ASR)? State its typical percentage in a shredded ELV and mention one energy-recovery method for ASR.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Automotive Shredder Residue (ASR)**, also called "fluff", is the heterogeneous light fraction remaining after post-shredder magnetic and eddy-current separation of the ELV hulk. It consists of plastics, foam, rubber, glass fines, textiles, dirt, and residual fluids. ASR typically constitutes **20 – 25%** of the original vehicle mass and is targeted to be reduced to **< 5%** by 2030. One energy-recovery method is **feeding ASR as Refuse-Derived Fuel (RDF) into cement kilns**, where it substitutes coal and fossil fuel with a Lower Heating Value of approximately **16 MJ/kg** and 35% thermal conversion efficiency. *(Other acceptable methods: pyrolysis to recover monomers and carbon black; gasification to syngas; fluidised-bed combustion.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Main Slot]**
>
> **(a)** [7 marks] Describe the complete five-stage process of ELV treatment with a labelled flow diagram. Explain the role of depollution and dismantling in the recovery chain.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] A 1400 kg passenger car produces the following recoverable masses after ELV treatment: Reused parts = 140 kg, Ferrous metal = 840 kg, Non-ferrous metal = 105 kg, Recycled plastics = 70 kg, Energy-recovered ASR = 175 kg, Landfilled ASR = 70 kg. Calculate the Reuse + Recycling Rate and Reuse + Recovery Rate, and verify ELV Directive compliance.

#### **Model Answer — Question 3 (a) [7 marks]**

The five-stage ELV treatment process is:

1. **Stage 1 — Collection and Depollution:** The vehicle is received at an Authorised Treatment Facility (ATF), owner documentation is verified, and the vehicle is weighed. All operational fluids — engine oil, transmission oil, brake fluid, coolant, fuel, refrigerant (HFC-134a) — are drained using suction pumps. Hazardous components (lead-acid battery, tyres, catalytic converter, airbags, mercury switches) are physically removed. This stage is critical because fluids corrode the shredder, contaminate ferrous scrap, and release hydrocarbons into the environment.

2. **Stage 2 — Dismantling:** Reusable parts such as engines, gearboxes, doors, headlamps, bumpers, and infotainment systems are carefully dismantled manually or with robotic arms. These parts enter OEM remanufacture, aftermarket reuse, or component-recycling streams. Early dismantling captures maximum economic value — a refurbished engine (₹25,000) is worth 8× its scrap-metal value (₹3,000).

3. **Stage 3 — Shredding:** The depolluted hulk is fed into a rotor-shear shredder rotating at 400–800 rpm, producing a heterogeneous mixture called fluff (10–100 mm particle size). Energy consumption is 0.4 – 0.6 kWh per vehicle.

4. **Stage 4 — Post-Shredder Separation:** Sequential separation using (i) magnetic drums for ferrous metals, (ii) eddy-current separators for non-ferrous metals (Al, Cu, Zn), (iii) optical/NIR sorters for polymers, and (iv) Zig-Zag air classifiers for light ASR.

5. **Stage 5 — ASR Recovery:** ASR undergoes pyrolysis, gasification, or RDF feeding to cement kilns, with the goal of reducing landfill ASR to < 5% of vehicle mass.

**Role of Depollution:** Prevents environmental contamination and equipment corrosion, and enables the recovered ferrous scrap to meet EAF-grade purity specifications.
**Role of Dismantling:** Captures the highest-value reusable parts before they are crushed, maximising the recovery rate above 95%.

*(Flow diagram as in Section 4.1 to be drawn on the answer sheet for full marks.)*

#### **Model Answer — Question 3 (b) [7 marks]**

Given: $M_{\text{vehicle}} = 1400$ kg; $M_{\text{reused}} = 140$ kg; $M_{\text{ferrous}} = 840$ kg; $M_{\text{non-ferrous}} = 105$ kg; $M_{\text{plastic}} = 70$ kg; $M_{\text{energy}} = 175$ kg; $M_{\text{landfill}} = 70$ kg.

**Step 1 — Mass-Balance Verification**

$$
M_{\text{out,total}} = 140 + 840 + 105 + 70 + 175 + 70 = 1400 \;\; \text{kg} \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — Reuse + Recycling Rate**

$$
R+R = \frac{140 + 840 + 105 + 70}{1400} \times 100 = \frac{1155}{1400} \times 100 = 82.5\%
$$

**Step 3 — Reuse + Recovery Rate**

$$
R+REC = \frac{1155 + 175}{1400} \times 100 = \frac{1330}{1400} \times 100 = 95.0\%
$$

**Step 4 — Compliance Verdict**

| Metric | Calculated | ELV Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| R+R | 82.5% | ≥ 85% | **NON-COMPLIANT ✗** |
| R+REC | 95.0% | ≥ 95% | **COMPLIANT ✓** |
| ASR Landfilled | 5.0% | < 5% (2030) | **COMPLIANT ✓** |

**Engineering Interpretation:** The process **fails the 85% recycling target** by 2.5 percentage points (≈ 35 kg of additional recyclable material must be diverted from the ASR stream, e.g., by improved optical sorting of black plastics or higher-efficiency eddy-current separation). The recovery target is just met. Process improvement is required — likely through an upgrade to NIR optical sorting and a third-pass eddy current unit.

---

> **Q4. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Alternative Slot — Internal Choice]**
>
> **(a)** [7 marks] Explain the concept of **Design for Recycling (DfR)** in the context of ELV. Discuss any five DfR guidelines mandated by ISO 22628 and the EU ELV Directive.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] With a neat block diagram, describe the working of an **eddy current separator (ECS)** used in post-shredder material recovery. State the physical principle, two key design parameters, and the typical recovery efficiency for aluminium from ASR.

#### **Model Answer — Question 4 (a) [7 marks]**

**Design for Recycling (DfR)** is a product-design philosophy that integrates end-of-life recyclability into the conceptual and detailed design phase of a vehicle. The EU ELV Directive and ISO 22628 establish a quantitative DfR assessment framework based on four indices — Disassemblability, Reusability, Recyclability, and Recoverability.

**Five DfR Guidelines:**

1. **Single-Polymer Plastics:** Use mono-material components (e.g., pure PP bumpers instead of PP/ABS blends) to enable clean reprocessing into a single resin stream.

2. **Material Coding per ISO 1043:** Every plastic part > 25 g must carry a standardised resin code (e.g., >PA66-GF35<) to enable automated optical sorting.

3. **Elimination of Hazardous Substances per ELV Annex II:** Lead < 0.1%, Cadmium < 0.01%, Mercury < 0.1%, Hexavalent Chromium < 0.1% by homogeneous material.

4. **Modular Assembly with Quick-Release Fasteners:** Bolts, clips, and snap-fits that allow engine, transmission, dashboard, and headlamp removal in < 5 minutes using standard tools.

5. **Dismantling Manual Availability:** OEMs must publish dismantling manuals (e.g., **IBIS** database, **CarTakeBack** portal) for ATFs and recyclers.

*(Other acceptable DfR guidelines: standardisation of fasteners, separation-friendly joint design, recoverable material labelling, recyclability scoring in CAD.)*

#### **Model Answer — Question 4 (b) [7 marks]**

**Physical Principle:**
An **eddy current separator (ECS)** exploits **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** ($e = -N \cdot d\Phi / dt$). A high-speed magnetic rotor (typically a permanent-magnet drum or belt driven at 1500–3000 rpm) creates a rapidly changing magnetic field. When conductive non-ferrous particles (Al, Cu, Zn) pass over the rotor, eddy currents are induced in them, generating a repulsive Lorentz force ($F = J \times B$) that ejects the conductive particles from the trajectory of non-conductive materials (plastics, glass, rubber).

**Block Diagram:**

```
[HETEROGENEOUS SHREDDER OUTPUT]
            |
            v
    [VIBRATORY FEEDER] ----> Controls feed rate
            |
            v
   [HIGH-SPEED MAGNETIC ROTOR 1500-3000 rpm]
            |
   ----------+---------
   |                |
   v                v
[REPULSIVE FORCE  |  [NON-CONDUCTORS]
 TRAJECTORY]      |  (plastics, glass, rubber)
   |                |
   v                v
[CONDUCTOR FRACTION]  [NON-CONDUCTOR FRACTION]
   (Al, Cu, Zn)         (goes to ASR stream)
```

**Two Key Design Parameters:**

1. **Rotor Tip Speed:** Typically 20 – 35 m/s. Higher tip speeds increase the Lorentz force and ejection distance for aluminium (which is the lightest non-ferrous metal and hardest to separate from plastics).

2. **Belt Speed (Feed Velocity):** Typically 1.0 – 2.5 m/s. Slower belt speeds allow longer exposure to the magnetic field, increasing recovery efficiency but reducing throughput.

**Typical Recovery Efficiency:** ECS achieves **> 90% recovery of aluminium** and **> 95% recovery of copper** from post-shredder fluff, with a product purity of **95 – 99%** for the non-ferrous fraction.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (2024 Pattern)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to include "Reused" mass in both R+R and R+REC formulas.** Many students add only the recycled mass and lose 2–3 marks immediately.
> 2. **Confusing Recycling Rate with Recovery Rate.** R+R excludes energy recovery; R+REC includes it. Drawing the wrong formula line is the most common single error.
> 3. **Skipping mass-balance verification.** Always sum all output streams and confirm they equal the input vehicle mass. Examiners specifically award 1 mark for the balance check.
> 4. **Missing the depollution step in flow diagrams.** Depollution is the legal and engineering foundation of the entire ELV process. A flow diagram that begins directly with shredding is marked down by 1 mark.
> 5. **Writing ASR target as 25% (current) instead of < 5% (2030 target).** Examiners consider this a sign of outdated reading and deduct marks.
> 6. **Using non-standard abbreviations.** Always use **R+R** and **R+REC**; avoid "RR" or "REC" alone.
> 7. **Not stating the Lower Heating Value (LHV) of ASR as ~16 MJ/kg** in numerical problems. Examiners expect this constant.
> 8. **Neglecting the "Comment / Engineering Interpretation" closing line** in numerical answers. A one-line compliance verdict is mandatory for full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: ELV & Recycling Strategies**

- **ELV Definition:** A vehicle declared uneconomical to repair or failing fitness certification, surrendered to an Authorised Treatment Facility (ATF).
- **5-Stage Process:** Collection & Depollution → Dismantling → Shredding → Post-Shredder Separation → ASR Recovery.
- **Three KPIs to Memorise:**
  - Reuse + Recycling (R+R) ≥ **85%**
  - Reuse + Recovery (R+REC) ≥ **95%**
  - ASR landfilled < **5%** (by 2030)
- **Mass Fractions (typical):** Ferrous 65–70%, Non-ferrous 8–12%, Plastics 10–15%, ASR 20–25% (current).
- **Key Separation Equipment:** Magnetic drum (ferrous), Eddy Current Separator (non-ferrous), NIR Optical Sorter (polymers), Zig-Zag Classifier (ASR).
- **Hazardous Substances to Remove:** Oils, fuel, coolant, brake fluid, refrigerant, battery, tyres, airbags, catalytic converter, mercury switches.
- **Hazardous Substance Limits (ELV Annex II):** Pb < 0.1%, Cd < 0.01%, Hg < 0.1%, Cr⁶⁺ < 0.1%.
- **ASR Energy Recovery Methods:** Pyrolysis, gasification, RDF-to-cement-kiln.
- **ASR LHV:** ~**16 MJ/kg**; cement kiln thermal efficiency ~**35%**.
- **Carbon Saving:** Recycling 1 kg of steel saves **1.60 kg CO₂e**; recycling 1 kg of aluminium saves **~9.0 kg CO₂e**.
- **Design for Recycling (DfR) Pillars:** Single-polymer plastics, ISO 1043 coding, modular assembly, dismantling manuals, hazardous-substance elimination.
- **ISO 22628:** Quantitative methodology for calculating DfR indices (Disassemblability, Reusability, Recyclability, Recoverability).
- **Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR):** Manufacturer is financially and legally responsible for the end-of-life of their vehicles.
- **Global Production:** ~80 million new vehicles/year; ~27 million ELVs retired/year.
- **Basel Convention (1989):** Governs transboundary movement of hazardous ELV waste.
- **Formula Triad (must be on fingertip):** R+R, R+REC, Mass Balance — all with units, all with mass-balance check.
- **First-Pass Examiner Check:** Every numerical problem MUST close with a compliance verdict (compliant/non-compliant) against the EU ELV Directive benchmarks.
- **Terminology Discipline:** ASR (Automotive Shredder Residue), ATF (Authorised Treatment Facility), ECS (Eddy Current Separator), RDF (Refuse-Derived Fuel), DfR (Design for Recycling), EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility).
- **Engineering Insight:** A typical shredder plant processing 200 vehicles/day recovers energy equivalent to ~3,900 urban households' daily consumption — a high-impact number to cite in any conclusion.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) Technology
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET414 - MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) Technology

---

# 🚗⚡ Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) Technology

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G)** is a sophisticated energy management paradigm in which **plug-in electric vehicles (PEVs)** and **battery electric vehicles (BEVs)** use their onboard traction battery packs as **distributed energy storage units (DESUs)** that can bidirectionally exchange electrical energy with the **utility power grid**. Unlike conventional unidirectional charging (G2V — Grid-to-Vehicle), V2G enables the EV to act simultaneously as a **load** (during charging) and as a **controllable distributed energy resource (DER)** (during discharging back to the grid).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** V2G is recognized as a cornerstone of the *Smart Grid* ecosystem and falls under the broader category of **Grid-Interactive Efficient Buildings (GIEB)** and **Demand Response (DR)** programs. The bidirectional power electronic interface, compliant with **ISO 15118-20** and **IEC 61851-1**, is the enabling backbone.

Mathematically, the V2G concept can be expressed as a power-flow sign convention:

$$
P_{\text{grid ↔ EV}} \in \mathbb{R}
$$

where a positive value denotes G2V (charging) and a negative value denotes V2G (discharging).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Car as a Piggy Bank for the City"

Imagine you own a small rechargeable battery bank that powers your home. During the day, the **sun shines bright** (or wind is strong), so electricity is cheap and abundant. You **charge your battery**. At night, when everyone in the city turns on their lights and ACs, electricity becomes **expensive** and the grid becomes **stressed**.

Now imagine **millions of electric cars parked in garages** across the city — each one carrying a **50–100 kWh battery**, most of which sits idle **~96% of the day**. If each of those cars could pump a tiny bit of that stored energy back to the grid during peak hours, the city's electricity problem would be **massively eased**.

> [!NOTE]
> **V2G Analogy:** Think of each EV as a *mobile piggy bank*. You deposit electrons when electricity is cheap (off-peak) and withdraw them (with a small interest in the form of grid services) when electricity is expensive (peak). The car still has enough charge in the morning for your commute — you just used its "spare change" to help the grid.

The grid, in return, pays the EV owner a **service fee**, **tariff differential**, or **frequency regulation payment**, making V2G a *win-win economic and engineering arrangement*.

---

### 1.3 Standard V2G Classification Hierarchy

The KTU 2024 syllabus recognizes a **family** of bidirectional energy flows. Memorize this hierarchy:

| Acronym | Full Form | Direction | Typical Power Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| **V1G** | Smart (Unidirectional) Charging | Grid → Vehicle (controlled timing) | Up to **22 kW AC** |
| **V2H** | Vehicle-to-Home | Vehicle ↔ Residential Load | **3.6 – 11 kW** |
| **V2B** | Vehicle-to-Building | Vehicle ↔ Commercial Building | **11 – 50 kW (DC fast)** |
| **V2L** | Vehicle-to-Load | Vehicle → Portable Appliance | **1.5 – 3.6 kW** |
| **V2X** | Vehicle-to-Everything (umbrella) | Bidirectional with any entity | Variable |
| **V2G** | Vehicle-to-Grid | Vehicle ↔ Utility Grid | **Up to 350 kW DC** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Distinction (often confused in exams):**
> - **G2V** = Conventional charging (only one direction)
> - **V2G** = True bidirectional (requires bidirectional inverter + smart metering + communication protocol)
> - **V1G** ≠ V2G. V1G only *controls the rate/timing* of charging; it never feeds power back.

---

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics & Standards (Bold for Emphasis)

The following standard metrics are **universally cited** in V2G literature and must be memorized:

- **Round-Trip Efficiency (η_RT): typically 70 – 85%**
- **Cycles to 80% Capacity (Battery Lifetime): 1,000 – 3,000 cycles**
- **State of Charge (SoC) Operating Window for V2G: 30% – 80%** (to preserve battery)
- **Communication Standard: ISO 15118-20** (Plug & Charge + bidirectional)
- **Grid Frequency Support Band: 49.5 – 50.5 Hz (India), 59.5 – 60.5 Hz (US)**
- **Aggregated Minimum Fleet Size for Grid Service: ~100 – 1,000 EVs**
- **Maximum V2G Discharge Duration per Day: 2 – 4 hours** (to protect battery)
- **Minimum SoC Reserved for Driver Mobility: 20% – 30%**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The V2G Operational Architecture — 5 Logical Pillars

V2G is **not just a hardware technology**; it is the *fusion* of five engineering layers. Each must function correctly for the system to deliver grid services.

#### **Pillar 1 — Physical Power Layer (Bidirectional Charger)**
A **bidirectional AC-DC converter** (also called a *reversible inverter-charger*) replaces the conventional unidirectional onboard charger. The same power electronic switches (typically **IGBTs** or **SiC MOSFETs**) handle both rectification (G2V) and inversion (V2G).

- **G2V Mode:** Grid AC → Rectifier → DC bus → Battery (boost/buck converter regulates current)
- **V2G Mode:** Battery DC → Inverter → LC filter → Grid AC (synchronizes phase, frequency, voltage)

#### **Pillar 2 — Communication Layer**
Two-way data exchange between EV and **EVSE (Electric Vehicle Supply Equipment)** and then up to the **Aggregator** and **Utility**.

- **ISO 15118-20**: High-level protocol (handles authentication, billing, charge scheduling)
- **OCPP 2.0.1**: Open Charge Point Protocol (charger-to-backend communication)
- **IEEE 2030.5** / **OpenADR 2.0b**: Aggregator-to-utility signaling

#### **Pillar 3 — Control & Aggregation Layer (The "Virtual Power Plant")**
A single EV is too small (≈10 kW) to provide meaningful grid service. An **Aggregator** pools **1,000–100,000 EVs** to form a **Virtual Power Plant (VPP)** capable of dispatching **10–100 MW** blocks — equivalent to a small gas turbine.

#### **Pillar 4 — Smart Metering & Settlement Layer**
Each V2G event is metered in **kilowatt-hours (kWh) injected** and settled via:
- **Time-of-Use (ToU)** tariff differential (buy cheap, sell high)
- **Capacity payments** (for being available)
- **Regulation payments** (for frequency response)

#### **Pillar 5 — Battery Management Layer**
The **BMS (Battery Management System)** must protect the battery by:
- Enforcing SoC limits (e.g., 30%–80%)
- Managing C-rate (charge/discharge rate)
- Monitoring cell temperature (V2G raises average cell temperature by 3–8°C)
- Estimating **State of Health (SoH)** to project residual value

---

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

> **Why bidirectional inverter?**
> Because the grid operates on **AC** (50/60 Hz sinusoidal) and the battery stores **DC**. To move energy *back* to the grid, DC must be converted to synchronized AC. A conventional unidirectional charger cannot invert.

> **Why aggregator?**
> The grid's smallest meaningful service unit is **1 MW** (e.g., frequency regulation markets like PJM in the US, or the Indian Energy Storage Obligation). One EV = 0.01 MW. So pooling is mandatory.

> **Why ISO 15118-20?**
> Without standardized digital handshake, you cannot authenticate *who* is selling energy back, *how much*, or *at what price*. ISO 15118-20 introduces **bidirectional power transfer profiles (BPT)** that explicitly negotiate charge AND discharge schedules.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following **exhaustive** table contains every formula you need for V2G problem-solving.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Power Flow Sign Convention** | $P_{\text{net}} = P_{\text{G2V}} - P_{\text{V2G}}$ | Positive = charging; Negative = discharging. Unit: **kW**. |
| 2 | **Energy Stored in EV Battery** | $E_{\text{batt}} = V_{\text{nom}} \cdot Q_{\text{Ah}} \cdot \eta_{\text{batt}}$ | Nominal voltage × Ah capacity × efficiency. Unit: **kWh**. |
| 3 | **State of Charge (SoC)** | $\text{SoC}(t) = \text{SoC}(t_0) + \dfrac{\int_{t_0}^{t} P_{\text{net}}(\tau) \, d\tau}{E_{\text{batt}}^{\text{rated}}}$ | Fraction between 0 and 1. Dimensionless. |
| 4 | **Round-Trip Efficiency** | $\eta_{\text{RT}} = \eta_{\text{chg}} \cdot \eta_{\text{dis}} \cdot \eta_{\text{inv}} \cdot \eta_{\text{rect}}$ | Typical value: **70%–85%**. Dimensionless. |
| 5 | **Energy Lost in One V2G Cycle** | $E_{\text{loss}} = E_{\text{discharged}} \cdot (1 - \eta_{\text{RT}})$ | Unit: **kWh per cycle**. |
| 6 | **Peak Shaving Capacity** | $P_{\text{shave}} = N_{\text{EV}} \cdot P_{\text{EV,avg}} \cdot DF$ | $N_{\text{EV}}$ = number of EVs; $DF$ = **Discharge Factor** (0.6–0.8). Unit: **kW**. |
| 7 | **Aggregator Revenue** | $R_{\text{agg}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{\text{EV}}} \left[ E_{i,\text{dis}} \cdot \pi_{\text{sell}} - E_{i,\text{chg}} \cdot \pi_{\text{buy}} \right]$ | Where $\pi$ = tariff rates (₹/kWh). |
| 8 | **Annualized Battery Degradation Cost** | $C_{\text{deg}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{batt}}}{N_{\text{cycles}}^{\text{total}}} \cdot N_{\text{cycles}}^{\text{V2G}}$ | $C_{\text{batt}}$ = battery replacement cost (₹). |
| 9 | **Net V2G Profit per EV per Year** | $\Pi_{\text{V2G}} = R_{\text{ann}} - C_{\text{deg}}$ | Should be **positive** for viable business case. |
| 10 | **Frequency Regulation Bid Capacity** | $P_{\text{reg}} = N_{\text{EV}} \cdot P_{\text{rated}} \cdot \text{Participation Factor}$ | Typically 50%–80% of rated power. |
| 11 | **C-Rate (Battery Stress)** | $C = \dfrac{\vert I \vert}{Q_{\text{rated}}}$ | V2G typically uses **0.3C–0.5C**. |
| 12 | **Cumulative Cycles Over V2G Lifetime** | $N_{\text{cycles}}^{\text{V2G}} = 365 \cdot n_{\text{cycles/day}} \cdot Y_{\text{V2G}}$ | $Y_{\text{V2G}}$ = years of V2G service. |
| 13 | **Aggregated Energy Storage Capacity** | $E_{\text{fleet}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (E_{\text{batt},i} \cdot \text{SoC}_{\text{available},i})$ | Unit: **kWh (fleet total)**. |
| 14 | **Equivalent Power Plant Capacity** | $P_{\text{VPP}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{fleet}}}{T_{\text{dis}}}$ | Compare to a gas/coal plant dispatch. Unit: **kW**. |
| 15 | **Voltage Regulation (Reactive Power Support)** | $Q_{\text{V2G}} = V_{\text{grid}} \cdot I_{\text{q-component}}$ | EVs can absorb/inject VArs. Unit: **kVAr**. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Notation Reminder:** In the table, every absolute value / vertical bar notation has been written using `$\vert$` LaTeX rather than the pipe character to keep this cheat sheet table-safe. When writing your own exam answers, use `$|x|$` notation inside math mode only.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The V2G concept is not academic speculation — it is **commercial-grade** technology. Real deployments include:

- **Nuvve (US/EU):** Operates V2G fleets of Nissan Leaf and e-NV200 vehicles for frequency regulation in California, Denmark, and the UK.
- **Fermata Energy (US):** Deployed first bidirectional V2G school bus fleet in White Plains, NY.
- **Honda + Moixa (UK):** V2G trials using the Honda e platform.
- **Tata Power + Tata Motors (India):** Pilot V2G demonstration with Nexon EV in Mumbai (2023–2024).
- **BYD (China):** V2G buses providing emergency backup to Shanghai grid.

In India, V2G aligns directly with the **Ministry of Power's "Vehicle-to-Grid Integration" roadmap (2023)** and the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) Building Energy Performance Index** framework.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Round-Trip Efficiency of a V2G System

**Problem Statement:** A V2G charger delivers 10 kWh of AC energy to the grid (discharging the battery). During the subsequent charging phase, the grid must supply **12.5 kWh** of AC to refill the battery to the original SoC. Determine the **round-trip efficiency**.

**Step 1 — Define the round-trip efficiency formula.**

$$
\eta_{\text{RT}} = \frac{E_{\text{out, AC}}}{E_{\text{in, AC}}}
$$

where $E_{\text{out, AC}}$ is the AC energy discharged to the grid, and $E_{\text{in, AC}}$ is the AC energy drawn from the grid to recharge.

**Step 2 — Identify given values.**

$$
E_{\text{out, AC}} = 10 \text{ kWh} \quad ; \quad E_{\text{in, AC}} = 12.5 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and solve.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{RT}} &= \frac{10 \text{ kWh}}{12.5 \text{ kWh}} \\
&= \frac{10}{12.5} \\
&= 0.80 \\
&= 80\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation.**

The system loses **2.5 kWh** in every full V2G round-trip cycle. The lost energy appears as heat in:
- IGBT switching losses (≈1%)
- LCL filter inductor copper losses (≈2%)
- DC-DC converter conduction losses (≈3%)
- Battery internal resistance I²R heating (≈8%)
- Auxiliary power (fans, BMS, communication) (≈6%)

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Annual V2G Economic Profit Per EV

**Problem Statement:** An EV owner with a **50 kWh battery** participates in a V2G program. The grid charges **₹4/kWh** at night (ToU off-peak) and pays **₹14/kWh** to the EV owner during peak hours (ToU peak). The owner cycles **330 days/year**, performs **1 V2G cycle/day**, and discharges **10 kWh** each cycle.

Additional data:
- Battery replacement cost: **₹8,00,000**
- Total battery cycles (without V2G): **2,000 cycles**
- V2G degradation cost must be deducted from gross revenue.

Compute the **annual net profit**.

---

**Step 1 — Compute annual charging cost.**

Each night, the owner charges **10 kWh** at the off-peak rate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{chg, daily}} &= 10 \text{ kWh} \times ₹4/\text{kWh} = ₹40 \\
C_{\text{chg, annual}} &= ₹40 \times 330 = ₹13,200
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute annual discharge revenue.**

Each day, the owner sells **10 kWh** at the peak rate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{dis, daily}} &= 10 \text{ kWh} \times ₹14/\text{kWh} = ₹140 \\
R_{\text{dis, annual}} &= ₹140 \times 330 = ₹46,200
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute gross annual profit (energy arbitrage only).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pi_{\text{gross}} &= R_{\text{dis, annual}} - C_{\text{chg, annual}} \\
&= ₹46,200 - ₹13,200 \\
&= ₹33,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute annual battery degradation cost.**

Each V2G cycle consumes some of the battery's finite cycle life:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost per cycle} &= \frac{C_{\text{batt}}}{N_{\text{cycles}}^{\text{total}}} \\
&= \frac{₹8,00,000}{2{,}000 \text{ cycles}} \\
&= ₹400 \text{ per cycle}
\end{aligned}
$$

Annual degradation cost:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{deg, annual}} &= ₹400 \times 330 \\
&= ₹1,32,000
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Many students *forget* to apply the round-trip efficiency. The real charging cost should be:
> $$E_{\text{charge, AC}} = \frac{10 \text{ kWh}}{\eta_{\text{RT}}} = \frac{10}{0.80} = 12.5 \text{ kWh}$$
> Multiplying 12.5 kWh × ₹4/kWh = ₹50 per day, not ₹40. This is a 25% error in cost calculation.

Let us **correct** Step 1 with this efficiency factor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{chg, AC}} &= \frac{E_{\text{dis, AC}}}{\eta_{\text{RT}}} = \frac{10}{0.80} = 12.5 \text{ kWh} \\
C_{\text{chg, daily}} &= 12.5 \times ₹4 = ₹50 \\
C_{\text{chg, annual}} &= ₹50 \times 330 = ₹16,500
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Recompute gross profit.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pi_{\text{gross}} &= ₹46,200 - ₹16,500 = ₹29,700
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute NET profit after battery degradation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pi_{\text{net}} &= \Pi_{\text{gross}} - C_{\text{deg, annual}} \\
&= ₹29,700 - ₹1,32,000 \\
&= -₹1,02,300
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Critical interpretation.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Counterintuitive Result:** With these specific numbers, V2G is **economically disastrous** because the per-cycle battery degradation cost (₹400) **exceeds** the per-cycle arbitrage profit (₹90). This is precisely why real-world V2G programs rely on **ancillary service payments** (frequency regulation, spinning reserve) — not pure energy arbitrage — to be profitable. Be ready to discuss this in exams.

For ancillary services, the typical payment is **₹800–₹2,000 per kW-year** for capacity reservation, which *does* make V2G profitable even after degradation.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Aggregator Frequency Regulation Bid

**Problem Statement:** An aggregator controls **5,000 EVs**, each rated at **7.4 kW** bidirectional onboard charger. The Independent System Operator (ISO) requests **frequency regulation up** at **3 PM** for 1 hour. The participation factor (fraction of rated power that can be dispatched within regulation market rules) is **80%**, and only **60%** of EVs are plugged in during that hour.

Compute the regulation bid capacity $P_{\text{reg}}$.

**Step 1 — Plugged-in EV count.**

$$
N_{\text{plugged}} = 5{,}000 \times 0.60 = 3{,}000 \text{ EVs}
$$

**Step 2 — Per-EV dispatchable power.**

$$
P_{\text{EV, dispatch}} = 7.4 \text{ kW} \times 0.80 = 5.92 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Total bid capacity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{reg}} &= N_{\text{plugged}} \times P_{\text{EV, dispatch}} \\
&= 3{,}000 \times 5.92 \text{ kW} \\
&= 17{,}760 \text{ kW} \\
&\approx 17.76 \text{ MW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare to a conventional grid asset.**

A **17.76 MW** bid is equivalent to dispatching a **small gas peaker plant**. This shows how EV fleets can replace conventional generation assets.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of V2G Service Types

| Service Type | Response Time | Duration | Revenue/MW/Year (approx.) | V2G Suitable? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Frequency Regulation (Primary)** | < 1 second | Minutes | **₹8 – 15 Lakh** | ✅ Excellent |
| **Spinning Reserve** | < 10 minutes | 1–4 hours | **₹4 – 7 Lakh** | ✅ Excellent |
| **Peak Shaving** | 15 min – 1 hr | 2–6 hours | **₹3 – 5 Lakh** | ✅ Good |
| **Energy Arbitrage (ToU)** | 1 hour | 4–10 hours | **₹1 – 3 Lakh** | ⚠ Marginal |
| **Voltage Support (VAr)** | < 1 second | Continuous | **₹2 – 4 Lakh** | ✅ Excellent |
| **Black Start** | Hours | Days | **Niche** | ❌ Unsuitable |

> **Examiner's Insight:** Most V2G exam questions expect you to identify that *frequency regulation and spinning reserve* are the most profitable services — not energy arbitrage. Memorize this ranking.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: V2G System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Grid[Utility Grid AC 415V 50Hz] -->|Power Line| SmartMeter[Smart Bidirectional Meter]
    SmartMeter -->|AC| BidirCharger[Bidirectional AC DC Charger IGBT SiC]
    BidirCharger -->|DC Bus 400V| DCBus{High Voltage DC Bus}
    DCBus -->|Boost Buck| BatteryPack[EV Battery Pack 50 to 100 kWh]
    DCBus -->|Aux 12V| AuxLoad[Vehicle Auxiliaries BMS Cooling]
    
    BatteryPack -.->|Telemetry SoC SoH Voltage Temp| BMS[Battery Management System]
    BMS -.->|Cell Balancing Protection| BatteryPack
    
    BidirCharger -.->|PWM Control Signals| VCU[Vehicle Control Unit]
    VCU -.->|CAN Bus| BMS
    
    SmartMeter -.->|OCPP 2.0.1| EVSEBackend[EVSE Backend Cloud]
    EVSEBackend -.->|IEEE 2030.5| Aggregator[Aggregator VPP Operator]
    Aggregator -.->|OpenADR 2.0b| UtilityDispatch[Utility ISO Dispatch Center]
    
    Driver[EV Owner Mobile App] -.->|ISO 15118 20| Aggregator
    Aggregator -.->|Bid Prices Setpoints| Driver
    
    UtilityDispatch -.->|Frequency Reg Signal| Aggregator
    Aggregator -.->|Setpoints to Each EV| EVSEBackend
    
    classDef power fill:#ffd700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef comm fill:#90ee90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef user fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    
    class Grid,SmartMeter,BidirCharger,DCBus,BatteryPack,AuxLoad power
    class VCU,BMS control
    class EVSEBackend,Aggregator,UtilityDispatch comm
    class Driver user
```

**Explanation of Diagram Layers:**

- **Yellow (Power) Layer**: Physical energy flow path from grid → charger → battery and vice versa.
- **Blue (Control) Layer**: Internal vehicle control — VCU orchestrates power flow; BMS protects cells.
- **Green (Communication) Layer**: External digital handshake — utility sends dispatch, aggregator allocates to EVs.
- **Pink (User) Layer**: The human EV owner, who sets preferences via mobile app and consents to discharge windows.

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: V2G Discharge Event

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as Utility ISO
    participant A as Aggregator VPP
    participant E as EVSE Charger
    participant C as Car VCU and BMS
    participant B as Battery
    participant D as Driver App
    
    Note over U,D: T minus 5 minutes Frequency drop detected 49.8 Hz
    
    U->>A: Regulation up signal Need 5 MW for 15 min
    A->>A: Optimize fleet dispatch Select 800 EVs plugged in
    A->>E: OCPP 2.0.1 Discharge setpoint 7 kW per EV
    E->>C: ISO 15118 20 BPT Negotiate discharge
    C->>B: BMS SoC check Battery SoC 72 percent OK
    C->>E: Acknowledge Discharge approved
    E->>B: Close DC contactor Begin discharge
    B->>E: DC current flows
    E->>U: Grid AC power injected Phase synchronized
    U->>A: Frequency restored to 50 Hz
    A->>E: Discharge stop command
    E->>C: Open contactor End session
    A->>D: Notification You earned Rs 84 today
    D-->>A: Consent for future V2G participation
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram: V2G Charger Operating States

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Standby: Plug inserted
    Standby --> Handshaking: Pilot signal detected
    Handshaking --> Authenticated: ISO 15118 Plug and Charge OK
    Authenticated --> Idle: Schedule received
    Idle --> Charging: G2V command
    Idle --> Discharging: V2G command
    Idle --> V2H: Vehicle to Home command
    Idle --> Idle: No grid request
    Charging --> Idle: SoC reached upper limit 80 percent
    Discharging --> Idle: SoC reached lower limit 30 percent
    Charging --> Fault: Overvoltage or Overcurrent
    Discharging --> Fault: BMS thermal trip
    Fault --> Standby: Operator reset
    Idle --> Unplugged: Connector removed
    Unplugged --> [*]
```

**Operating-State Engineering Notes:**

- The charger **never** allows SoC to drop below 30% (to guarantee next-day mobility).
- The charger **never** allows SoC to rise above 80% (to extend cycle life).
- Fault state latches the system in a safe condition; manual reset is required.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) technology. Mention any two communication standards used in V2G systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

V2G is a bidirectional energy management technology in which an electric vehicle's traction battery is used as a distributed energy storage unit that can either draw power from the grid (charging/G2V) or supply power back to the grid (discharging/V2G) through a bidirectional power electronic charger, in response to grid frequency or price signals.

Two communication standards used in V2G:

1. **ISO 15118-20** — Defines the high-level digital communication between the EV and the EVSE, including authentication, billing, and bidirectional power transfer (BPT) profile negotiation.
2. **OCPP 2.0.1** — Open Charge Point Protocol; handles communication between the EVSE charger and the central backend/cloud aggregator.

*(A valid third answer: IEEE 2030.5, IEC 61850, or OpenADR 2.0b — award full marks.)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Distinguish between V1G and V2G. Why is V2G considered more complex?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | V1G (Smart Charging) | V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid) |
|---|---|---|
| Power Direction | Unidirectional (Grid → EV only) | Bidirectional (Grid ↔ EV) |
| Hardware | Conventional unidirectional charger | Bidirectional inverter-charger |
| Communication | Basic (IEC 61851 PWM signaling) | Advanced (ISO 15118-20 + OCPP 2.0.1) |
| Battery Stress | Charging cycles only | Additional discharge cycles → faster degradation |
| Grid Services | Peak avoidance, load shifting | Peak shaving, frequency regulation, spinning reserve, VAr support |

V2G is more complex because it requires **(i)** bidirectional power electronics with grid-synchronization capability, **(ii)** real-time communication and control from an aggregator/utility, **(iii)** advanced BMS algorithms to protect battery SoH, and **(iv)** regulatory frameworks and bi-directional metering settlement.

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks. Students must answer ONE choice. Model solutions below show full working.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

Explain the architecture of a Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) system with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of an aggregator in enabling grid services.

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture (5 layers):**

1. **Physical Layer (Power Electronics):** Bidirectional AC-DC converter (rectifier + inverter functions), DC-DC converter for battery voltage matching, LCL filter for grid harmonic compliance.
2. **Battery Layer:** High-voltage lithium-ion traction pack (50–100 kWh) under BMS supervision.
3. **Communication Layer:** ISO 15118-20 between EV and charger; OCPP 2.0.1 between charger and aggregator backend; IEEE 2030.5 / OpenADR 2.0b between aggregator and utility.
4. **Control Layer:** Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) executes charge/discharge commands based on aggregator setpoints while respecting BMS limits.
5. **Market/Settlement Layer:** Smart meter records bidirectional kWh; settlement via ToU tariff, capacity payments, or ancillary service markets.

**Role of Aggregator (Key Points):**

- A single EV (7 kW) is too small for grid services. An aggregator pools **1,000–100,000 EVs** to form a **Virtual Power Plant (VPP)** of 10–100 MW.
- Performs **forecasting** of available fleet capacity using plug-in predictions and SoC data.
- **Optimizes** dispatch to maximize revenue while satisfying driver constraints (departure time, target SoC).
- Acts as the **market interface**, bidding into wholesale markets (PJM, ERCOT, IEX in India).
- Manages **settlement and payments** to individual EV owners.
- Provides **anonymity** and a single contractual point for utilities.

*(Diagrams and 5 architecture points + 4 aggregator roles = full 7 marks.)*

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

A fleet of 1,000 EVs participates in a V2G frequency regulation program. Each EV has a 60 kWh battery and a 10 kW bidirectional charger. Assume 75% of EVs are plugged in during regulation hours, the participation factor is 0.7, and round-trip efficiency is 82%.

Compute:
1. Total regulation bid capacity in MW.
2. Total energy delivered to the grid if regulation is dispatched for 2 hours.
3. Energy lost as heat in the process.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $N_{\text{EV}} = 1{,}000$
- $E_{\text{batt}} = 60 \text{ kWh}$ (per EV)
- $P_{\text{rated}} = 10 \text{ kW}$
- Plug-in factor = 0.75
- Participation factor = 0.7
- $\eta_{\text{RT}} = 0.82$
- $T = 2 \text{ hours}$

**Step 1 — Regulation bid capacity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{plugged}} &= 1{,}000 \times 0.75 = 750 \text{ EVs} \\
P_{\text{EV, dispatch}} &= 10 \text{ kW} \times 0.7 = 7 \text{ kW} \\
P_{\text{reg}} &= 750 \times 7 \text{ kW} = 5{,}250 \text{ kW} \\
&= 5.25 \text{ MW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy delivered to grid (AC output).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{out, AC}} &= P_{\text{reg}} \times T \\
&= 5{,}250 \text{ kW} \times 2 \text{ h} \\
&= 10{,}500 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy lost as heat.**

Using the efficiency formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{in, AC}} &= \frac{E_{\text{out, AC}}}{\eta_{\text{RT}}} = \frac{10{,}500}{0.82} = 12{,}804.88 \text{ kWh} \\
E_{\text{loss}} &= E_{\text{in, AC}} - E_{\text{out, AC}} \\
&= 12{,}804.88 - 10{,}500 \\
&= 2{,}304.88 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
1. $P_{\text{reg}} = 5.25 \text{ MW}$
2. $E_{\text{out}} = 10{,}500 \text{ kWh}$
3. $E_{\text{loss}} = 2{,}304.88 \text{ kWh}$ (lost as heat)

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

Discuss in detail the various grid services that V2G-enabled EVs can provide. Rank them by revenue potential and justify the ranking.

**Model Answer:**

| Grid Service | Description | Response Time | Revenue (₹/MW/yr) | Rank |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Frequency Regulation (Primary)** | Fast up/down power injection to stabilize grid frequency | < 1 sec | ₹8–15 Lakh | **#1** |
| **Voltage/Reactive Power Support** | Inject/absorb VArs to maintain grid voltage | < 1 sec | ₹5–9 Lakh | **#2** |
| **Spinning Reserve** | Stand-by power that can be dispatched in <10 min | < 10 min | ₹4–7 Lakh | **#3** |
| **Peak Shaving / Load Levelling** | Discharge during peak demand to flatten load curve | 15 min – 1 hr | ₹3–5 Lakh | **#4** |
| **Energy Arbitrage (ToU)** | Buy cheap off-peak, sell expensive peak | 1 hr | ₹1–3 Lakh | **#5** |
| **Black Start** | Restart grid after total blackout | Hours | Niche | **#6** |

**Justification of Ranking:**

1. **Frequency Regulation ranks #1** because EVs have very fast response (< 1 sec, faster than any thermal plant) and the payment is per MW of capacity reserved, not per MWh actually delivered. Battery wear is minimized because actual energy exchanged is small.
2. **Voltage Support ranks #2** because EVs can provide Q (reactive power) almost "for free" with minimal battery throughput.
3. **Spinning Reserve is #3** — moderate response time, paid for capacity.
4. **Peak Shaving is #4** — requires sustained discharge, more battery wear.
5. **Energy Arbitrage is #5** — profit margin is low relative to battery wear cost; pure arbitrage alone rarely justifies V2G.
6. **Black Start is #6 / Unsuitable** — requires massive sustained energy and specific grid codes not compatible with EVs.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

Explain the impact of V2G operation on lithium-ion battery degradation. Suggest three mitigation strategies.

**Model Answer:**

**Degradation Mechanisms Accelerated by V2G:**

1. **Cycle Aging** — Each additional charge/discharge cycle consumes finite cycle life. A 1,000-cycle battery cycled once daily loses 100% of its useful life in ~3 years of V2G.
2. **Calendar Aging** — Time at high SoC and elevated temperature accelerates SEI (Solid Electrolyte Interphase) layer growth. V2G keeps battery at intermediate SoC (30–80%) which is beneficial; however, frequent cycling raises temperature.
3. **Lithium Plating Risk** — High C-rate charging in cold conditions can cause metallic lithium deposition on the anode, permanently reducing capacity.
4. **Mechanical Stress** — Volume changes during cycling stress particle interfaces, leading to active material loss.

**Mitigation Strategies:**

1. **SoC Window Limitation:** Operate only between **30% and 80% SoC** (the "sweet spot" where calendar aging is minimal and cycle aging is reduced by ~50% compared to full cycles).
2. **Adaptive C-Rate Control:** Restrict V2G discharge to **≤ 0.5C** (e.g., max 25 kW from a 50 kWh pack). Reduces thermal stress and plating risk.
3. **Thermal Pre-conditioning:** Use the EV's thermal management system to keep cells between **20°C–35°C** during V2G events. Avoid V2G in extreme ambient temperatures.
4. **Smart Scheduling:** Concentrate V2G during **most-profitable grid events** (high-price peak hours) rather than spreading thin across many low-value cycles.
5. **State-of-Health Monitoring:** Continuously estimate SoH; reduce V2G participation when SoH drops below 80% to preserve residual value.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing V2G with V1G:** Many students write that V2G only controls charge timing. **Wrong.** V2G *requires bidirectional* power flow. Always emphasize the **reverse power flow** capability.
>
> 2. **Ignoring Round-Trip Efficiency:** If a numerical question gives only charging energy, students forget that more AC energy was drawn from grid than was injected back. Always apply $\eta_{\text{RT}}$.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Battery Degradation in Economics:** Pure arbitrage is often unprofitable. Always include **battery wear cost** when discussing economics.
>
> 4. **Missing the Aggregator Concept:** A single 7 kW EV is irrelevant to grid operators. Always explain how an **Aggregator / VPP** pools vehicles.
>
> 5. **Wrong Communication Protocol Names:** ISO 15118 ≠ OCPP ≠ IEC 61851. ISO 15118-20 handles EV↔EVSE; OCPP 2.0.1 handles EVSE↔cloud; IEC 61851 is only for low-level pilot signaling.
>
> 6. **Forgetting SoC Reserve for Mobility:** Never design a V2G scheme that drains the battery below 20–30%. Examiners specifically look for this safety limit.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **V2G = Bidirectional** power flow between EV battery and grid. **V1G ≠ V2G.**
- ✅ **Five Pillars:** Bidirectional charger, Communication protocols, Aggregator, Smart metering, BMS protection.
- ✅ **Key Standard:** **ISO 15118-20** for bidirectional power transfer (BPT) profiles.
- ✅ **Round-trip efficiency:** typically **70–85%**. Always include in numerical problems.
- ✅ **SoC operating window for V2G:** **30%–80%** (preserves battery life).
- ✅ **Aggregator/VPP** is mandatory — single EV is too small for grid services.
- ✅ **Most profitable V2G service:** **Frequency Regulation** (not energy arbitrage).
- ✅ **Economic truth:** Energy arbitrage alone is usually unprofitable due to battery degradation cost. Combine with ancillary services.
- ✅ **Battery degradation:** Limited by SoC window, C-rate, temperature, and SoH monitoring.
- ✅ **India-specific relevance:** Aligned with Ministry of Power V2G roadmap, BEE Building Energy Performance, and IEX market participation.
- ✅ **C-rate for V2G:** Typically **0.3C–0.5C** to minimize battery wear.
- ✅ **Communication stack to memorize (bottom-up):** IEC 61851 (pilot) → ISO 15118-20 (EV↔EVSE) → OCPP 2.0.1 (EVSE↔cloud) → IEEE 2030.5 / OpenADR 2.0b (aggregator↔utility).
- ✅ **Memorize ranking:** Frequency Regulation > Voltage Support > Spinning Reserve > Peak Shaving > Energy Arbitrage > Black Start.
- ✅ **Real-world deployments:** Nuvve (US/EU), Fermata Energy (school buses), Tata Power pilot (India).
- ✅ **Engineering formula to always carry:** $\eta_{\text{RT}} = \eta_{\text{chg}} \cdot \eta_{\text{dis}} \cdot \eta_{\text{inv}} \cdot \eta_{\text{rect}}$
